topics.title: The Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is a prime-mover that converts the chemical energy of a liquid or gaseous fuel into useful mechanical work (rotational energy on the crankshaft) by burning (combusting) the fuel *inside* a closed working cylinder. Combustion produces high-temperature, high-pressure gases that expand against a movable piston, converting fluid pressure energy into linear motion and then into rotary motion via a crank-slider mechanism.

In the KTU 2024 Autotronics context, "The Engine" is treated not merely as a thermodynamic device but as an **electro-mechanical cyber-physical system** containing mechanical subsystems (cylinder block, piston, valves, crankshaft), fluid subsystems (intake, exhaust, fuel rail, lubrication, cooling), and electronic subsystems (sensors, ECM/ECU, actuators). It forms the *physical plant* that the Autotronics systems of the later modules (engine management, ignition, fuel injection, transmission, ABS, body electronics) observe, command, and regulate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The syllabus frames the engine through three lenses — (1) operating principle, (2) classification, and (3) performance metrics. Memorising these three classifications and three performance metrics is a guaranteed 7–10 marks in any Part A.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Engine as a Pump That Pumps Itself"

Think of an IC engine as a **bicycle pump that fills a sealed can and then uses the air pressure inside the can to push back on its own handle**.

1. As you push the pump handle down, air is *sucked in* from outside (this is the **intake stroke**).
2. A valve closes the can's mouth. The trapped air is squeezed (the **compression stroke**).
3. You then ignite a tiny drop of fuel with the squeezed air — *but you don't push*. The fuel's own explosion pushes the handle back up (the **power/expansion stroke** — the only stroke that does useful work).
4. Finally, a second valve opens and the burnt gas is pushed out (the **exhaust stroke**).

After four strokes, you have rotated the pump handle one full turn, and the cycle starts over. The engine is essentially a self-actuating pump: instead of you pumping, a **crankshaft-piston-slider mechanism** automates the push, and a **chemical spark** does the pushing instead of your muscle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world mapping:** The bicycle pump = piston, sealed can = combustion chamber, your hand = crank, fuel igniting spontaneously or via spark = combustion event, and burnt gas escaping = exhaust stroke. Every modern gasoline/diesel engine is built around this identical concept.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **γ (kappa)** | Specific heat ratio for air | **1.4** (ideal diatomic gas) |
| **R** | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| **CV** | Calorific value of petrol | **~44 MJ/kg** |
| **CV** | Calorific value of diesel | **~43 MJ/kg** |
| **ρ_air** | Density of intake air (STP) | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| **η_th,Otto** | Otto cycle ideal efficiency | Function of compression ratio r |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of IC Engines (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.1.1 By Cycle of Operation
- **Four-Stroke Cycle Engine** — completes one working cycle in **4 piston strokes (2 crank revolutions)**. Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust.
- **Two-Stroke Cycle Engine** — completes one working cycle in **2 piston strokes (1 crank revolution)**. Suction+Compression followed by Power+Exhaust.

### 2.1.2 By Type of Fuel & Ignition
- **Spark Ignition (SI) Engine** — uses petrol/gasoline. Combustion initiated by an **external spark plug** at a precisely timed instant (e.g., 10°–35° BTDC).
- **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine** — uses diesel. Combustion initiated by **auto-ignition** when fuel is injected into highly compressed hot air (compression ratio 14:1 to 22:1).

### 2.1.3 By Cooling Method
- **Air-cooled** — fins on cylinder block (e.g., small motorcycles).
- **Water-cooled** — coolant jacket with radiator + pump (most modern cars).

### 2.1.4 By Valve Location
- **Overhead Valve (OHV)** — camshaft in block, valves in head via pushrods.
- **Overhead Cam (OHC / DOHC)** — camshaft(s) in cylinder head, directly operating valves (most modern engines).

### 2.1.5 By Fuel Delivery (Autotronics focus)
- **Carburetted engines** — mechanical air-fuel metering.
- **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)** — one injector per cylinder, electronically pulsed by ECU.
- **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** — injects fuel directly into cylinder at high pressure (200 bar).

## 2.2 The Two Ideal Air-Standard Cycles

### 2.2.1 Otto Cycle (Models the SI/Petrol Engine)
Four internally reversible processes:
1. **1 → 2:** Isentropic (adiabatic) compression.
2. **2 → 3:** Constant-volume heat addition (combustion approximated as instantaneous at TDC).
3. **3 → 4:** Isentropic expansion (power stroke).
4. **4 → 1:** Constant-volume heat rejection (exhaust + intake cooling approximated).

### 2.2.2 Diesel Cycle (Models the CI/Diesel Engine)
1. **1 → 2:** Isentropic compression.
2. **2 → 3:** Constant-pressure heat addition (combustion as fuel is injected gradually).
3. **3 → 4:** Isentropic expansion.
4. **4 → 1:** Constant-volume heat rejection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two different cycles?** In a petrol engine, combustion is *nearly instantaneous* (spark → flame kernel → full burn in milliseconds at nearly constant volume). In a diesel engine, combustion is *gradual* (fuel injected progressively as the piston moves down), so the heat-addition process is more accurately modelled at constant pressure.

## 2.3 Performance & Geometric Parameters

Every KTU problem on this module hinges on these terms. The student must know the **exact word** and the **exact formula**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition / Practical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bore | **D** | Inner diameter of the cylinder (mm) |
| Stroke | **L** | Distance travelled by piston between TDC and BDC (mm) |
| Compression Ratio | **r** | Ratio of total cylinder volume to clearance volume (dimensionless) |
| Cut-off Ratio | **ρ** | Ratio of volumes at end and start of heat addition (Diesel cycle only) |
| Indicated Power | **IP** | Power developed inside the cylinder from gas pressure (kW) |
| Brake Power | **BP** | Power actually measured at the crankshaft output (kW) |
| Friction Power | **FP** | Power lost to friction, pumping, accessories (kW); IP = BP + FP |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | **sfc** | Fuel mass flow per unit power output (kg/kWh) |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | **η_bt** | Fraction of fuel chemical energy converted to brake work |
| Volumetric Efficiency | **η_v** | Ratio of actual inducted air mass to theoretical (displacement × air density) mass |
| Mechanical Efficiency | **η_m** | BP / IP |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable from first principles. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for any vertical-bar quantities.

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$ | Swept volume of one cylinder (m³) |
| 2 | $r = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio; $V_c$ = clearance volume |
| 3 | $\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle ideal thermal efficiency |
| 4 | $\eta_{th,Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle ideal thermal efficiency |
| 5 | $IP = \dfrac{p_{m,i} \, L \, A \, N \, K}{60}$ | Indicated Power (W); K=1/2 for 4-stroke, K=1 for 2-stroke; N in rpm; A in m² |
| 6 | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Brake Power (W); T in N·m, N in rpm |
| 7 | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency |
| 8 | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,act}}{\rho_{a} \, V_s \, \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot n_{cyl}}$ | Volumetric efficiency (4-stroke) |
| 9 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{BP}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption (kg/kWh) |
| 10 | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{BP \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency |
| 11 | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_s}$ | Mean effective pressure (Pa) |
| 12 | $T = \dfrac{5252 \cdot HP}{N}$ | Empirical torque formula (for quick problems; ft·lb, HP, rpm) |

**Boundary conditions & assumptions for all cycle formulas:**
- Working fluid = ideal gas, behaves as air throughout (air-standard cycle).
- All processes are internally reversible.
- Specific heats $c_p$ and $c_v$ are assumed **constant** at room temperature ($\gamma = 1.4$).
- No change in KE/PE of working fluid.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas drive real engineering decisions:
- **Compression ratio (r)** is limited by **knock** in SI engines (typically 8:1 to 12:1) and by **cylinder pressure** in CI engines (up to 22:1). The ECU controls knock via spark-retard and is the *primary* Autotronics function.
- **Volumetric efficiency (η_v)** dictates engine breathing; it is maximised via variable valve timing (VVT), variable intake manifolds, and turbocharging — all of which are ECU-actuated.
- **Brake thermal efficiency (η_bt)** sets the *useful energy boundary* for fuel-economy regulations (BS-VI, Euro 7). Modern direct-injection turbocharged engines achieve 38–42 % vs. the Otto ideal ceiling of ~65 %.
- **BSFC** maps the engine's "sweet spot" on its BSFC map, which is stored in the ECU as the calibration baseline for fuel injection quantity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

The Otto cycle consists of four ideal processes. Heat is added at constant volume (process 2 → 3) and rejected at constant volume (process 4 → 1). The compression (1 → 2) and expansion (3 → 4) are isentropic.

**Step 1 — Define the heat transfers.**

Heat added during combustion (constant volume, process 2 → 3):

$$
q_{in} = c_v (T_3 - T_2)
$$

Heat rejected during exhaust cooling (constant volume, process 4 → 1):

$$
q_{out} = c_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Define thermal efficiency.**

The thermal efficiency is defined as the fraction of heat input converted to net work:

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the heat expressions.**

$$
\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{c_v (T_4 - T_1)}{c_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply isentropic relations for processes 1 → 2 and 3 → 4.**

For the isentropic compression (1 → 2):

$$
\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

For the isentropic expansion (3 → 4):

$$
\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left( \dfrac{V_4}{V_3} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

The second equality holds because in the Otto cycle the volume ratio during expansion equals the volume ratio during compression (both equal to r).

**Step 5 — Cross-multiply the isentropic relations.**

From the compression relation:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

From the expansion relation:

$$
T_3 = T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the temperature differences.**

$$
T_3 - T_2 = T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1} (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into the efficiency expression.**

$$
\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma - 1} (T_4 - T_1)} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Efficiency increases monotonically with compression ratio r. Doubling r from 8 to 16 raises the ideal Otto efficiency from 56.5 % to 70.2 % — but real SI engines are knock-limited to r ≈ 10–12.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Indicated Power (IP)

The indicated power is the rate at which work is done by the gas pressure on the piston. We define the **indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP or p_mi)** as a hypothetical constant pressure that, if acting on the piston throughout the power stroke, would produce the same work as the actual varying pressure.

**Step 1 — Work per power stroke per cylinder.**

$$
W_{cycle} = p_{mi} \cdot A \cdot L
$$

where A = π D² / 4 is the cross-sectional area of the piston and L is the stroke length.

**Step 2 — Number of power strokes per second.**

For a 4-stroke engine, only one power stroke occurs every **2 revolutions per cylinder**. With N = engine speed in rpm and K a constant:

- 4-stroke: K = 1/2
- 2-stroke: K = 1

$$
n_{power} = \dfrac{N \cdot K}{60} \quad \text{[power strokes per second per cylinder]}
$$

**Step 3 — Indicated power per cylinder.**

$$
IP_{cyl} = W_{cycle} \cdot n_{power} = p_{mi} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot K}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Total indicated power for an n-cylinder engine.**

$$
IP = n_{cyl} \cdot p_{mi} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot K}{60}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{IP = \dfrac{p_{mi} \, L \, A \, N \, K \, n_{cyl}}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore = 80 mm, stroke = 90 mm, runs at 3000 rpm. The indicated mean effective pressure is 8 bar. Calculate the indicated power.

**Step 1 — Compute swept volume per cylinder.**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^{2} (0.090) = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Identify K for a 4-stroke engine.**

$$
K = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Convert IMEP to SI units.**

$$
p_{mi} = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 800{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Plug into the IP formula.**

$$
IP = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot K \cdot n_{cyl}}{60} = \dfrac{800000 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000 \times 0.5 \times 4}{60}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
800000 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} = 361.92
$$

$$
361.92 \times 3000 = 1{,}085{,}760
$$

$$
1{,}085{,}760 \times 0.5 \times 4 = 2{,}171{,}520
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by 60.**

$$
IP = \dfrac{2{,}171{,}520}{60} = 36{,}192 \text{ W} \approx 36.19 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{IP \approx 36.19 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always write the units at every step. A common mistake is using D in mm instead of m. The swept-volume formula demands SI units throughout — failing this loses 2 of 7 marks silently.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture of a Modern Electronically-Controlled IC Engine

The following Mermaid block diagram maps every physical and electronic subsystem of a modern fuel-injected petrol engine as a *cyber-physical system*, which is the KTU 2024 Autotronics perspective.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Cylinder Block and Head]
        A2[Piston and Connecting Rod]
        A3[Crankshaft and Flywheel]
        A4[Valvetrain IN and EX]
    end

    subgraph FLUID["FLUID AND AIR SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1[Air Intake Manifold]
        B2[Throttle Body Electronic]
        B3[Fuel Rail and Injectors]
        B4[Exhaust Manifold and Catalytic Converter]
        B5[Coolant Jacket and Radiator]
        B6[Oil Sump and Pump]
    end

    subgraph SENS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        C1[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP]
        C2[Camshaft Position Sensor CMP]
        C3[Manifold Absolute Pressure MAP]
        C4[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAF]
        C5[Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS]
        C6[Lambda Oxygen Sensor O2]
        C7[Knock Sensor KS]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER ECU"]
        D1[Engine Control Unit ECU]
        D2[Memory Maps Fuel and Ignition]
        D3[Diagnostic Trouble Codes DTC]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        E1[Fuel Injector Drivers]
        E2[Ignition Coil and Spark Plug]
        E3[Idle Air Control Valve]
        E4[Variable Valve Timing Solenoid]
    end

    MECH --> FLUID
    FLUID --> SENS
    SENS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT
    ACT --> MECH
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows clockwise. The mechanical subsystem drives the fluid subsystem (pumping air and fuel). The fluid subsystem produces measurable physical states that the **sensor layer** converts into electrical signals. The **ECU** runs embedded firmware against stored calibration maps and issues commands to the **actuator layer**, which in turn actuates the mechanical subsystem — closing the loop.

## 4.2 Mermaid P-V Diagram of the Otto Cycle

The pressure-volume diagram is the most frequently drawn figure in KTU cycle problems. The four corner states are labelled 1 (BDC start of compression), 2 (TDC end of compression), 3 (TDC end of combustion), and 4 (BDC end of expansion).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1((1)) -- isentropic compression --> s2((2))
    s2((2)) -- constant volume heat addition --> s3((3))
    s3((3)) -- isentropic expansion --> s4((4))
    s4((4)) -- constant volume heat rejection --> s1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid P-V Diagram of the Diesel Cycle

The Diesel cycle has constant-pressure heat addition (process 2 → 3) instead of constant-volume.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1((1)) -- isentropic compression --> t2((2))
    t2((2)) -- constant pressure heat addition --> t3((3))
    t3((3)) -- isentropic expansion --> t4((4))
    t4((4)) -- constant volume heat rejection --> t1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: Differentiate between a 4-stroke and a 2-stroke IC engine.**

**Model Answer (Expected Keywords for Full Marks):**

A 4-stroke IC engine completes one working cycle in **four piston strokes** (two full revolutions of the crankshaft), producing **one power stroke every two revolutions**. The four strokes are **intake, compression, power, and exhaust**, each performed as a dedicated stroke. Valves are operated by a dedicated **camshaft**, and the engine uses a **crankcase solely for lubrication**.

A 2-stroke IC engine completes one working cycle in **two piston strokes** (one revolution of the crankshaft), producing **one power stroke every revolution**. The functions of intake and exhaust are combined with the compression and power strokes respectively, typically using **ports in the cylinder wall** rather than poppet valves, and the **crankcase serves as the intake pump**. Consequently, for the same displacement and speed, a 2-stroke engine theoretically delivers **twice the power** of a 4-stroke but suffers from **lower thermal efficiency** and **higher emissions** due to charge-short-circuiting.

| Feature | 4-Stroke | 2-Stroke |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power strokes per revolution | 1 / 2 | 1 / 1 |
| Valve mechanism | Camshaft + poppet valves | Crankcase scavenging + ports |
| Lubrication | Wet sump + pump | Often total-loss oil mix |
| Thermal efficiency | Higher | Lower (charge loss) |
| Power-to-weight | Lower | Higher |

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Q: Define the term "compression ratio" of an IC engine and explain why it is limited to about 10:1 in petrol engines.**

**Model Answer:**

The **compression ratio (r)** of an IC engine is defined as the ratio of the **total cylinder volume when the piston is at Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)** to the **clearance volume when the piston is at Top Dead Centre (TDC)**:

$$
r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}
$$

In petrol (SI) engines, the compression ratio is limited to approximately **8:1 to 12:1** because higher compression ratios cause **engine knock (detonation)** — an uncontrolled, near-instantaneous auto-ignition of the end-gas ahead of the flame front. This produces a sharp pressure spike, audible "pinging," loss of power, and potential piston damage. Knocking is suppressed by (a) using higher-octane fuel, (b) **retarding spark timing** via the ECU (knock-control strategy in Autotronics), (c) using cooled exhaust gas recirculation (EGR), and (d) optimising chamber geometry. Diesel engines, in contrast, **require** high compression (14:1 to 22:1) because combustion is initiated by auto-ignition of the fuel-air mixture, not by a spark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[CHOOSE EITHER Question A OR Question B]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a P-V diagram, derive the expression for the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 75 mm and a stroke of 85 mm. Its compression ratio is 9:1. At a speed of 3600 rpm, the indicated mean effective pressure is 9 bar. Calculate the **(i)** indicated power, **(ii)** clearance volume per cylinder. Take γ = 1.4. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Otto Cycle Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions of the air-standard Otto cycle:** (1 mark)
1. The working fluid is a fixed mass of **ideal air** behaving as a perfect gas.
2. All processes are **internally reversible**.
3. Specific heats $c_p$ and $c_v$ remain **constant** at room-temperature values, hence γ = 1.4.
4. Combustion is replaced by **instantaneous heat addition** at constant volume.
5. Exhaust is replaced by **instantaneous heat rejection** at constant volume.
6. No change in kinetic or potential energy of the working fluid.

**P-V diagram:** (1 mark) — Four states 1 → 2 → 3 → 4, with 1–2 and 3–4 as isentropic curves, 2–3 and 4–1 as constant-volume lines. Draw as a clockwise closed loop with V-axis horizontal, P-axis vertical.

**Derivation:** (5 marks — see full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes; the key boxed result is:)

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- n_cyl = 4 (cylinders)
- Cycle = 4-stroke ⇒ K = 1/2
- D = 75 mm = 0.075 m
- L = 85 mm = 0.085 m
- r = 9
- N = 3600 rpm
- p_mi = 9 bar = 9 × 10⁵ Pa
- γ = 1.4

**(i) Indicated Power:** (4 marks)

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^{2} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
IP = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot K \cdot n_{cyl}}{60}
$$

$$
IP = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{5} \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.085 \times 3600 \times 0.5 \times 4}{60}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
9 \times 10^{5} \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} = 3976.2
$$

$$
3976.2 \times 0.085 = 337.98
$$

$$
337.98 \times 3600 = 1{,}216{,}728
$$

$$
1{,}216{,}728 \times 0.5 \times 4 = 2{,}433{,}456
$$

$$
IP = \dfrac{2{,}433{,}456}{60} = 40{,}557.6 \text{ W} \approx 40.56 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{IP \approx 40.56 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Clearance Volume per Cylinder:** (3 marks)

$$
V_s = A \cdot L = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.085 = 3.755 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 375.5 \text{ cm}^3
$$

From $r = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{V_s}{r - 1} = \dfrac{375.5}{9 - 1} = \dfrac{375.5}{8} = 46.94 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c \approx 46.94 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ per cylinder}}
$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Q: (a)** Compare the Otto and Diesel cycles on the basis of (i) process of heat addition, (ii) compression ratio, (iii) peak temperature, and (iv) thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, single-cylinder diesel engine has a bore of 100 mm, a stroke of 120 mm, and runs at 1500 rpm. The indicated mean effective pressure is 7 bar. The fuel consumption is 4 kg/hr and the calorific value is 42 MJ/kg. Calculate the **(i)** indicated power, **(ii)** indicated thermal efficiency, **(iii)** brake power if mechanical efficiency is 80 %. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Otto vs Diesel Comparison (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Otto Cycle (SI/Petrol) | Diesel Cycle (CI/Diesel) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Heat addition | **Constant volume** (combustion nearly instantaneous after spark) | **Constant pressure** (combustion gradual as fuel is injected) |
| (ii) Compression ratio | **8:1 to 12:1** (limited by knock) | **14:1 to 22:1** (must auto-ignite fuel) |
| (iii) Peak temperature | **Higher** at end of combustion due to constant-V heat addition | **Lower** peak for the same heat input; spread over a longer crank interval |
| (iv) Thermal efficiency | $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ — typically higher for same r due to earlier expansion |
| Ignition source | Spark plug | Auto-ignition by compression temperature |
| Fuel used | Petrol, high octane | Diesel, high cetane |
| Weight & cost | Lighter, cheaper | Heavier, robust (high cylinder pressures) |
| Applications | Passenger cars, two-wheelers | Trucks, buses, locomotives, ships |

(1 mark per row of comparison, with diagrams for both cycles: 1 mark reserved for the P-V diagrams.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Diesel Engine Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- 4-stroke, single cylinder ⇒ K = 1/2, n_cyl = 1
- D = 100 mm = 0.10 m
- L = 120 mm = 0.120 m
- N = 1500 rpm
- p_mi = 7 bar = 7 × 10⁵ Pa
- m_f = 4 kg/hr
- CV = 42 MJ/kg = 42 × 10⁶ J/kg
- η_m = 80 % = 0.80

**(i) Indicated Power:** (3 marks)

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
IP = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.120 \times 1500 \times 0.5 \times 1}{60}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
7 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 5497.8
$$

$$
5497.8 \times 0.120 = 659.74
$$

$$
659.74 \times 1500 = 989{,}610
$$

$$
989{,}610 \times 0.5 = 494{,}805
$$

$$
IP = \dfrac{494{,}805}{60} = 8246.75 \text{ W} \approx 8.247 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{IP \approx 8.247 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Indicated Thermal Efficiency:** (2 marks)

Heat input rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = \dfrac{4}{3600} \times 42 \times 10^{6} = 46{,}666.67 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta_{th,i} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{8246.75}{46{,}666.67} = 0.1767 = 17.67 \text{ \%}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,i} \approx 17.67 \%}
$$

**(iii) Brake Power:** (2 marks)

$$
BP = \eta_m \cdot IP = 0.80 \times 8246.75 = 6597.4 \text{ W} \approx 6.597 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{BP \approx 6.597 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant K:** 4-stroke → K = ½; 2-stroke → K = 1. Using K = 1 for a 4-stroke **doubles your IP** silently. This is the **#1 silent killer** in numericals.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Bore and stroke given in mm must be converted to m *before* substituting. Failing this costs 2 marks with no partial credit.
> 3. **Confusing BP and IP:** IP is power *inside the cylinder*; BP is power *at the crankshaft output*. Always check which one the question asks for.
> 4. **Skipping the P-V diagram:** Even in a 14-mark question, **drawing the P-V diagram is worth 1–2 marks**. Skipping it loses those marks gratuitously.
> 5. **Writing η = 1 − 1/r^γ:** Forgetting the **(γ − 1)** exponent is a typo that costs the full efficiency derivation marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **IC Engine** = internal combustion prime mover converting chemical energy of fuel into rotary mechanical work via a crank-slider mechanism.
- ✅ **Two ideal cycles** to memorise: **Otto** (constant-volume heat addition, SI engines) and **Diesel** (constant-pressure heat addition, CI engines).
- ✅ **Otto efficiency** = $1 - 1/r^{\gamma - 1}$; **Diesel efficiency** = $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$.
- ✅ **4-stroke K = ½, 2-stroke K = 1** — commit this to muscle memory.
- ✅ **IP formula:** $IP = p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K \cdot n_{cyl} / 60$ — units *must* be SI (Pa, m, rpm).
- ✅ **BP formula:** $BP = 2\pi N T / 60$ — T in N·m, N in rpm, output in Watts.
- ✅ **η_m = BP / IP**, **η_bt = (BP × 3600) / (m_f × CV)**, **η_v = actual air mass / theoretical air mass**.
- ✅ **Compression ratio limited by knock** in SI engines (~10:1); required to be high (~18:1) in CI engines.
- ✅ **γ for air = 1.4** (diatomic ideal gas); **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- ✅ Modern engines are **cyber-physical systems**: sensors → ECU → actuators → mechanical plant (the Autotronics context).
- ✅ **Key sensors** to know: CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, CTS, O2 (lambda), KS.
- ✅ **Key actuators** to know: fuel injectors, ignition coils, idle air control, VVT solenoid.
- ✅ **Knock control**, **lambda control**, **fuel mapping**, and **ignition timing** are the four primary ECU control loops you must be able to *name and describe*.
- ✅ Always draw the **P-V diagram** in cycle questions — it is a guaranteed 1–2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Components of an Automotive Electronic System

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Electronic Components (Autotronics Components):** The fundamental electromechanical and solid-state devices used in modern vehicles to **sense** physical/chemical parameters, **process** the acquired data, and **actuate** mechanical or electrical subsystems. These components form the building blocks of every modern subsystem such as Engine Control, Antilock Braking System (ABS), Electronic Stability Program (ESP), Airbag Systems, and Body Control Modules (BCM).

In the KTU 2024 OEMRT831 syllabus, Module 1 categorizes these components into three functional families:

1. **Sensors** – Input devices (transducers) converting physical quantities into electrical signals.
2. **Actuators** – Output devices converting electrical commands into physical action.
3. **Control / Processing Units** – Microcontroller-based decision-making hardware (ECU/ECM/TCM).

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Human Body Analogy**
> Imagine driving a car is like the human body performing a task. The **sensors** are the *five senses* (eyes → camera/light sensor, skin → temperature/pressure sensor, ears → knock/microphone sensor). The **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** is the *brain* that processes sensory data and decides what to do. The **actuators** are the *muscles* (fuel injectors squirt fuel, spark plugs ignite, throttle valves open). Without any one of these, the "body" of the car cannot function intelligently.

### Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember

- **Standard Automotive Voltage (Nominal):** **12 V DC** (Battery rating **12.6 V** fully charged, charging system **13.5 V – 14.5 V**).
- **CAN Bus Standard Speed:** **500 kbps** (high-speed CAN, ISO 11898).
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +125 °C** for under-hood electronics.
- **Sensor Supply Voltage (Vref):** Typically **5 V DC** (from ECU regulated rail).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Three-Family Architecture of Autotronics

### Family 1 — SENSORS (Input Devices)

A sensor is a **transducer** that converts a physical parameter into a measurable electrical quantity.

#### A. Temperature Sensors
- **NTC Thermistor (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* as temperature rises. Used in coolant temperature, intake air temperature, oil temperature.
- **PTC Thermistor:** Resistance *increases* with temperature (used as current limiters).
- **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):** Linear, platinum-based, very accurate.

#### B. Pressure Sensors
- **Piezoelectric type:** Generates voltage when stressed (dynamic pressure – knock sensor).
- **Piezoresistive (Wheatstone bridge) type:** Resistance changes with strain (MAP – Manifold Absolute Pressure sensor).

#### C. Position / Speed Sensors
- **Variable Reluctance (VR) sensor:** Generates AC voltage proportional to *rate of change* of flux (used for crankshaft/camshaft position).
- **Hall Effect sensor:** Output voltage switches between 0 V and 5 V when a ferromagnetic blade passes (camshaft position, wheel speed in some ABS).
- **Potentiometric (Throttle Position Sensor – TPS):** Resistive divider produces voltage proportional to angle.

#### D. Gas / Chemical Sensors
- **Zirconia Oxygen Sensor (Lambda Sensor):** Generates voltage (0.1 V lean to 0.9 V rich) based on O₂ differential across a ceramic membrane.
- **Wideband O₂ Sensor:** Uses a pump cell to maintain stoichiometric balance; output current.
- **NOx Sensor:** Electrochemical cell sensing nitrogen oxides.

#### E. Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor
- **Hot-wire type:** Maintains a hot wire at a fixed temperature differential; the current required is proportional to air mass flow.

### Family 2 — ACTUATORS (Output Devices)

| Actuator | Working Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Solenoid (Fuel Injector)** | Electromagnetic coil lifts a needle valve against spring force | Petrol/diesel fuel metering |
| **Stepper Motor (Idle Air Control)** | Pulses to a stator rotate a permanent magnet rotor in discrete steps | Bypasses throttle plate for idle RPM control |
| **DC Motor (Throttle Body)** | Brush-commutated or BLDC motor actuates throttle plate | Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / Drive-by-Wire) |
| **Relay** | Low-current coil controls a high-current mechanical switch | Headlamp, horn, fuel pump circuits |
| **Piezo Injector** | Piezo crystal stack elongates when voltage applied | Common-rail diesel direct injection |
| **Ignition Coil** | Mutual induction steps up 12 V to 25–40 kV | Spark generation |

### Family 3 — CONTROL UNITS (Processing Devices)

- **ECU (Engine Control Unit):** 32-bit microcontroller (Infineon TriCore, NXP/Freescale MPC5xxx, Renesas RH850). Operating clock **80–300 MHz**, Flash **1–8 MB**, RAM **128 KB – 1 MB**.
- **TCM (Transmission Control Module):** Dedicated ECU for automatic gearboxes.
- **BCM (Body Control Module):** Handles lighting, wipers, central locking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECU Operating Principle (Closed-Loop Feedback)**
> The ECU continuously executes: *Read Sensors → Compute via Look-up Tables / Algorithms → Drive Actuators → Wait fixed loop time (typically **5 ms – 10 ms** for engine management) → Repeat.*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NTC Thermistor (Steinhart–Hart simplified) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | $R_T$ = resistance at T, $B$ = beta constant (K), $T$ = absolute temp (K) |
| 2 | Voltage Divider (used in TPS, MAF signal) | $V_{out} = V_{ref}\cdot\dfrac{R_{sense}}{R_{sense}+R_{fixed}}$ | $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$, $V_{out}$ = 0–5 V output |
| 3 | Piezo Sensor Charge Output | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = piezoelectric constant (C/N), $F$ = force (N) |
| 4 | Variable Reluctance Sensor AC Output | $V_{rms} = k \cdot \omega \cdot \dfrac{d\Phi}{d\theta}$ | $\omega$ = rotational speed (rad/s) |
| 5 | Lambda Sensor Stoichiometric EMF | $V_{lambda} = \dfrac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2,\,ref}}{p_{O_2,\,exh}}\right)$ | $F$ = Faraday constant (96485 C/mol), R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) |
| 6 | Battery EMF (Charging Circuit) | $V_{system} = V_{battery} - I\cdot R_{cable}$ | Loop equation for cranking |
| 7 | Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{supply}}{R_{coil}}$ | Ohm's law applied to coil |
| 8 | Engine RPM from Crank Sensor | $RPM = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N_{teeth}}$ | $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz), $N_{teeth}$ = teeth on reluctor ring (typically 60 − 2) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are evaluated *in real-time* inside the ECU's Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) channels and timer-capture inputs. The Hall sensor pulse train enters the ECU's *Input Capture* peripheral, which measures period and computes engine speed every **crank revolution**.

## 2.3 Why These Components Matter

- A modern mid-segment car contains **70 – 150 sensors** and **80 – 100 actuators** (Bosch Automotive Handbook reference class).
- Failure of a single sensor (e.g., MAF) degrades fuel economy by **10 % – 15 %** and increases emissions beyond **BS-VI / Euro 6** limits.
- This is why KTU emphasizes the *input-process-output* triad in Module 1 — every later module (engine management, ABS, EV systems) reuses these primitives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — NTC Thermistor Resistance at Any Temperature

### Given
- Reference resistance at $T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$ (25 °C): $R_0 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$
- Beta constant: $B = 3950\ \text{K}$
- We need $R_T$ at $T = 353.15\ \text{K}$ (80 °C)

### Step-by-Step

Start from the standard NTC thermistor model:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the reciprocal of T:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{353.15} \approx 2.8317 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reciprocal of T₀:**

$$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} \approx 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket term:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = (2.8317 - 3.3540)\times 10^{-3} = -0.5223\times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by B:**

$$B \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-0.5223 \times 10^{-3}) \approx -2.063$$

**Step 5 — Take the exponential:**

$$e^{-2.063} \approx 0.1271$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by R₀:**

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \times 0.1271 \approx 1271\ \Omega \approx 1.27\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> **Conclusion:** As temperature rose from 25 °C to 80 °C, resistance *dropped* from 10 kΩ to 1.27 kΩ — confirming the **NTC (negative coefficient)** behaviour.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Engine RPM from a 60 − 2 Tooth Crankshaft Sensor

### Given
- Crankshaft reluctor ring: **60 − 2 = 58 teeth** (60 slots, 2 missing for reference)
- Hall sensor pulse frequency: $f = 4200\ \text{Hz}$ at a given engine speed

### Step-by-Step

$$RPM = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N_{teeth}}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$RPM = \frac{60 \times 4200}{58}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator:**

$$60 \times 4200 = 252{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**

$$RPM = \frac{252{,}000}{58} \approx 4344.83\ \text{rpm}$$

> **Interpretation:** The crankshaft is rotating at roughly **4345 rpm**. The 60 − 2 pattern provides **two missing teeth** every revolution, which the ECU uses to identify top-dead-centre (TDC) reference.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lambda Sensor EMF (Nernst Equation)

The zirconia oxygen sensor behaves as a **solid-state electrochemical cell**. The open-circuit voltage is governed by the Nernst equation:

$$V_{lambda} = \frac{R \cdot T}{4F} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\,ref}}{p_{O_2,\,exh}}\right)$$

**Constants:**
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$
- Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio gives $\frac{p_{O_2,\,ref}}{p_{O_2,\,exh}} \approx 10^{10}$
- Exhaust gas temperature $T = 873\ \text{K}$ (600 °C)

**Step 1 — Multiply R·T:**

$$R \cdot T = 8.314 \times 873 \approx 7258\ \text{J/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Divide by 4F:**

$$\frac{R \cdot T}{4F} = \frac{7258}{4 \times 96485} = \frac{7258}{385940} \approx 0.01881\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of pressure ratio:**

$$\ln(10^{10}) = 10 \cdot \ln(10) \approx 10 \times 2.3026 = 23.026$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$V_{lambda} = 0.01881 \times 23.026 \approx 0.433\ \text{V}$$

> **Interpretation:** A stoichiometric mixture gives approximately **0.45 V** at 600 °C exhaust temperature — a textbook figure for narrow-band O₂ sensors (lean < 0.3 V, rich > 0.7 V).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection

| Parameter | Thermistor | Piezoelectric | Hall Effect | VR (Inductive) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Resistance | Charge / Voltage | Digital pulse | AC sine pulse |
| Power required | Yes (excitation) | None (self-gen) | Yes (5 V supply) | None (self-gen) |
| Frequency response | Low (slow) | Very high | High | Medium-high |
| Temperature range | −40 °C to 300 °C | Wide | Wide | Very wide |
| Typical use | Coolant temp | Knock detection | Cam position, wheel speed | Crank position (older) |
| ECU interface | ADC input | Charge amp + ADC | Timer capture | Comparator + Timer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Automotive Electronics Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Temperature Sensor NTC] --> ECU
    S2[Pressure Sensor MAP] --> ECU
    S3[Oxygen Sensor Lambda] --> ECU
    S4[Crank Position Hall] --> ECU
    S5[Camshaft Position Hall] --> ECU
    S6[Throttle Position TPS] --> ECU
    S7[Knock Sensor Piezo] --> ECU
    S8[MAF Sensor Hot Wire] --> ECU

    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU 32 bit MCU]

    ECU --> A1[Fuel Injector Solenoid]
    ECU --> A2[Ignition Coil]
    ECU --> A3[Idle Stepper Motor]
    ECU --> A4[Throttle Body DC Motor]
    ECU --> A5[Fuel Pump Relay]
    ECU --> A6[Purge Control Valve]

    BAT[12 V Battery] --> REG[Voltage Regulator 5 V Vref]
    REG --> S1
    REG --> S2
    REG --> S6
    REG --> ECU
```

## 4.2 Sensor-to-ECU Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PH[Physical Quantity eg Pressure] --> TR[Transducer Element eg Diaphragm]
    TR --> SC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    SC --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 12 bit]
    ADC --> MCU[ECU Microcontroller Processing]
    MCU --> OUT[Actuator Driver H bridge]
    OUT --> ACT[Physical Actuator Motion]
```

## 4.3 ECU Closed-Loop Control Cycle (Engine Management)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Read all sensor inputs ADC and Timer] --> B[Compute target fuel pulse using map]
    B --> C[Drive injector open for t_inj ms]
    C --> D[Wait loop period 10 ms typical]
    D --> A
```

## 4.4 Relay Driver Circuit (Typical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[ECU GPIO Pin 5 V Logic] --> D1[Flyback Diode 1N4007]
    MCU --> R[Base Resistor 1 kohm]
    R --> Q[Transistor NPN BC547]
    Q --> RL[Relay Coil 12 V 80 mA]
    RL --> BATT[12 V Battery]
    Q --> GND[Ground 0 V]
    RL --> SW[Relay Contacts SPDT]
    SW --> LOAD[High Current Load eg Headlamp]
```

## 4.5 Sensor Hierarchy by Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG[Engine Management]
        E1[MAP / MAF]
        E2[CKP / CMP]
        E3[ECT / IAT]
        E4[TPS]
        E5[O2 Sensor]
        E6[Knock Sensor]
    end
    subgraph CHS[Chassis Control]
        C1[Wheel Speed Sensor]
        C2[Steering Angle Sensor]
        C3[Yaw Rate Gyro]
        C4[Lateral Accelerometer]
    end
    subgraph BDY[Body Electronics]
        B1[Rain Light Sensor]
        B2[Parking Ultrasonic]
        B3[Battery Current Sensor]
        B4[Cabin Temperature]
    end
    ENG --> ECU1[ECM]
    CHS --> ECU2[ABS ESP ECU]
    BDY --> ECU3[BCM]
    ECU1 --> CAN[CAN Bus 500 kbps]
    ECU2 --> CAN
    ECU3 --> CAN
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**List any three temperature sensors used in an automotive engine management system and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** – NTC thermistor mounted at the thermostat housing; used to compute cold-start enrichment and ignition timing advance.
2. **Intake Air Temperature (IAT) Sensor** – NTC thermistor in the intake manifold; used for air-mass correction and to prevent detonation at high under-hood temperatures.
3. **Exhaust Gas Temperature (EGT) Sensor** – High-temperature thermocouple or RTD placed before/after the turbocharger or DPF; used to protect the turbo and trigger DPF regeneration.

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Hall Effect sensor and a Variable Reluctance (VR) sensor used for crankshaft position sensing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hall Effect Sensor | Variable Reluctance Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Power supply | Needs 5 V / 12 V DC | Self-generating (no supply) |
| Output | Square wave 0 V – 5 V | AC sine wave |
| Zero-speed detection | Yes (DC output) | No (output → 0 at 0 rpm) |
| ECU interface | Direct digital input | Needs comparator + rectifier |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**. List the major sub-blocks and describe the function of each. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **piezoelectric knock sensor** with the help of a circuit diagram showing signal conditioning. State how the ECU interprets the knock signal. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ECU Architecture:**

The ECU is built around a 32-bit microcontroller and consists of the following sub-blocks:

1. **Microcontroller Core (CPU)** – Executes the control algorithm (interrupt-driven, every **5–10 ms**).
2. **Flash Memory (1–8 MB)** – Stores calibration maps, look-up tables (ignition timing, fuel maps).
3. **RAM (128 KB – 1 MB)** – Stores volatile variables during execution.
4. **Analog Inputs (ADC, 12-bit, multiplexed)** – Reads thermistor, MAP, TPS, O₂, battery voltage.
5. **Digital Inputs** – Hall sensors, frequency inputs (CKP), switch inputs.
6. **Output Drivers** – Low-side/high-side drivers for injectors, coils, relays. Often **smart-power ICs** with diagnostic feedback.
7. **Communication Interfaces** – CAN, LIN, K-Line (ISO 9141), FlexRay (legacy), Ethernet (newer).
8. **Power Supply** – 12 V input → 5 V and 3.3 V regulated rails.
9. **Watchdog & Safety** – Monitors software execution; resets MCU on fault.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[12 V Supply] --> REG[5 V and 3.3 V Regulators]
    REG --> CPU[32 bit CPU]
    CPU --> FLASH[Flash Memory]
    CPU --> RAM[RAM]
    CPU --> ADC[ADC 12 bit]
    CPU --> DIN[Digital Inputs Hall and VR]
    CPU --> OUT[Smart Power Outputs]
    CPU --> COM[CAN and LIN Transceiver]
```

**(b) Piezoelectric Knock Sensor Working:**

- A **piezoelectric crystal** (lead zirconate titanate – PZT) is mechanically coupled to the engine block via a stud.
- When cylinder combustion creates abnormal pressure oscillations (knock frequency **6 kHz – 15 kHz** for petrol engines), the crystal generates a charge **Q = d · F**.
- This charge is converted to a voltage via a **charge amplifier** (often inside the ECU).
- The signal passes through a **band-pass filter** (centre frequency ≈ **7 kHz**, Q-factor ≈ 5) to isolate knock from mechanical noise.
- The filtered signal is fed to the ECU's ADC. The ECU computes a window-integrated RMS amplitude and compares against a **knock threshold map** (RPM vs load).
- If threshold is exceeded → ignition timing is retarded by a few degrees until knock disappears.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write that the knock sensor is *mounted on the cylinder head*. Correct mounting is on the **engine block** between cylinder 2 and 3 (for inline-4 engines) so it can detect vibration from any cylinder. Mounting on the head restricts detection to that cylinder only — and you lose 2 marks here.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **zirconia oxygen (lambda) sensor** with a suitable diagram. State the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol and the corresponding sensor output. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With a circuit diagram, describe the operation of a **fuel injector driver circuit** inside an ECU. Explain peak-and-hold current strategy. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Zirconia Lambda Sensor:**

- Construction: a thimble of **zirconia ceramic (ZrO₂)** coated inside and outside with porous platinum electrodes.
- One side is exposed to **ambient air** (reference, pO₂ ≈ 21 %).
- The other side is exposed to **exhaust gas** (varying pO₂).
- At temperatures above **350 °C**, zirconia becomes an **oxygen-ion conductor**.
- Oxygen ions migrate from high-pO₂ side to low-pO₂ side, generating an EMF given by the **Nernst equation** derived in §3.3.
- Output: **0.1 V (lean)** ↔ **0.9 V (rich)**, with a sharp step at the stoichiometric point (λ = 1, AFR ≈ **14.7:1** for petrol).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR[Ambient Air Reference pO2 21 percent] --> PT1[Inner Pt Electrode]
    PT1 --> ZR[Zirconia Thimble ZrO2]
    ZR --> PT2[Outer Pt Electrode]
    EXH[Exhaust Gas pO2 varies] --> PT2
    PT1 --> V[Voltage Output 0.1 to 0.9 V]
    PT2 --> V
```

**(b) Fuel Injector Driver — Peak-and-Hold Strategy:**

A solenoid injector has inductance (L ≈ 5 mH) and resistance (R ≈ 2–4 Ω). To open the needle, the ECU must first produce a strong **pull-in current** (peak) of around **8–10 A** for **1–2 ms**, then reduce to a **hold current** of **2–3 A** to keep the valve open without overheating the coil.

**Driver Circuit:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECU[ECU MCU Timer PWM] --> DRV[Smart Power Driver TLE8880 etc]
    DRV --> COIL[Injector Coil L and R]
    COIL --> FLY[Flyback Diode]
    COIL --> ECU_R[Current Sense Resistor 10 milliohm]
    ECU_R --> ADC[ADC Feedback to MCU]
    DRV --> BAT[12 V Battery]
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. ECU outputs **PWM duty ≈ 80 %** for 1.5 ms → coil current ramps to **8 A** (peak).
2. Current-sense resistor feedback triggers ECU to drop PWM to **20–25 % duty** → current settles at **2.5 A** (hold).
3. End of injection: PWM = 0 → magnetic field collapses, flyback diode conducts, injector needle closes (response time **< 0.5 ms**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students draw the flyback diode *across the coil backwards*. The cathode must connect to the **12 V supply side**, anode to the driver side — this is the orientation that protects against the **back-EMF** of ~ **60 V** generated at switch-off. Drawing it the wrong way loses 2 marks and can fail the safety-related CO assessment criterion.

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Autotronics components split into **Sensors, Actuators, ECU**.
- ✅ Automotive nominal system voltage is **12 V DC**; sensor supply typically **5 V**.
- ✅ **NTC thermistor** resistance drops with temperature (Steinhart–Hart / Beta equation).
- ✅ **Hall sensor** needs DC supply; **VR sensor** is self-generating but cannot detect zero speed.
- ✅ **Zirconia O₂ sensor** uses the **Nernst equation**; switches at **~0.45 V** at stoichiometry (AFR **14.7:1** petrol).
- ✅ **Crank RPM** is computed as $RPM = 60 f / N_{teeth}$ from a **60 − 2** reluctor ring.
- ✅ ECU loop time = **5–10 ms**; reads sensors → computes → drives actuators → repeats.
- ✅ **Peak-and-hold** injector driver: peak ≈ **8 A**, hold ≈ **2–3 A**, flyback diode orientation is critical.
- ✅ Modern car uses **CAN bus (500 kbps)** for inter-ECU communication (ISO 11898).
- ✅ Always remember the **70+ sensor count** of a modern vehicle (good KTU "general awareness" point).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive Train
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Drive Train

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drive Train (Powertrain) Definition:** The drive train (or powertrain) of a vehicle is the complete assembly of components that transmit the engine's generated power to the driving wheels. It encompasses the **clutch, transmission (gearbox), propeller shaft, universal joints, differential, final drive, axles (live and dead), and wheels**, working in synchrony to deliver tractive effort while allowing variations in torque, speed, and direction.

In the context of **Autotronics** (OEMRT831), the drive train is not merely a mechanical system; it is a **mechatronic sub-system** heavily integrated with **electronic control units (ECUs)**, **sensors**, and **actuators**. The modern drive train is monitored and controlled by:

- **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**
- **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)**
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**
- **Traction Control System (TCS)**
- **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The 2024 Scheme highlights the *electronic* and *control* aspects of the drive train. Students are expected to understand both the conventional mechanical layout **and** the modern electronically-managed systems (e.g., automatic transmissions, CVT, DCT, torque-on-demand 4WD).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine the human body:
- The **engine** is the **heart** (generates raw power in the form of blood/energy).
- The **drive train** is the **circulatory system** (arteries, veins, capillaries) — it **channels, regulates, and distributes** the energy to the parts that actually do work (the wheels = muscles).
- The **ECU/TCU** is the **brain and nervous system** — it decides *when* and *how much* energy to deliver based on driver demand (brain signals from accelerator pedal).

**Plain-English Intuition:**
> When you press the accelerator, the engine spins fast but produces power in a narrow RPM band. The wheels, however, need different amounts of torque and speed for different situations (climbing a hill, cruising on a highway, reversing). The drive train is the **mechanical translator** that converts the engine's raw rotational output into *exactly* the torque-speed combination the wheels need, *exactly* when they need it.

| Real-World Analogy | Drive Train Component |
|---|---|
| Clutch pedal | Disconnecting engine from gearbox when shifting |
| Gear ratios in a bicycle | Low gear = high torque for hills, high gear = high speed for flat roads |
| Differential on a car turning | Inner wheel travels less distance than outer wheel |
| Bicycle chain | Transmits power from pedals to rear wheel |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** Always remember — the drive train does **three core jobs**:
> 1. **Torque Multiplication** (gearbox, final drive)
> 2. **Direction Reversal** (reverse gear)
> 3. **Speed Equalization Between Wheels** (differential)

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Wheel Torque ($T_w$):** Measured in **N·m (Newton-metres)**.
- **Engine Speed ($N_e$):** Measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**.
- **Vehicle Speed ($V$):** Measured in **km/h** (or m/s in SI).
- **Gear Ratio ($i$):** Dimensionless ratio (output speed / input speed).
- **Overall Drive Ratio ($i_o$):** Product of transmission ratio and final drive ratio.
- **Tyre Rolling Radius ($r_r$):** Measured in **metres (m)**, typically 0.25 – 0.40 m for passenger cars.
- **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):** Dimensionless, range 0.1 (ice) to 1.0 (dry asphalt).

---

## 1.4 Functional Block Diagram of a Drive Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine / Internal Combustion Engine] --> CL[Clutch]
    CL --> GB[Gearbox / Transmission]
    GB --> PS[Propeller Shaft]
    PS --> DIFF[Differential Unit]
    DIFF --> FD[Final Drive]
    FD --> AX[Axles Half Shafts]
    AX --> WH[Driving Wheels]
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] -.controls.-> ENG
    TCU[Transmission Control Unit TCU] -.controls.-> GB
    ECU -.monitors.-> CL
    TCU -.monitors.-> DIFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **RWD Layout shown.** In FWD vehicles, the differential and final drive are integrated into the transaxle at the front. In AWD/4WD, additional transfer case and front drive shafts are added.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Component-by-Component Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Clutch

The clutch is a **friction coupling device** that allows the driver (or TCU) to engage or disengage the engine from the gearbox. It solves a critical problem: the engine is always rotating when running, but the wheels and gears must be stationary when the vehicle is stopped or when changing gears.

**Types of Clutches (KTU 2024 Focus):**

| Type | Actuation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Single-plate dry friction clutch | Mechanical / Hydraulic | Most passenger cars |
| Multi-plate clutch | Hydraulic / Electromagnetic | Motorcycles, high-performance cars |
| Centrifugal clutch | Automatic (centrifugal force) | Scooters, go-karts |
| Torque converter | Hydraulic (automatic transmission) | Automatic transmission vehicles |
| Electromagnetic clutch | Electric current | A/C compressors, hybrid vehicles |

**Operating Principle:**
The clutch has three states:
1. **Engaged (foot off pedal):** Pressure plate clamps the friction disc against the flywheel → 100% torque transfer.
2. **Slipping (pedal partially pressed):** Partial contact → controlled torque transfer (used during start-up and gear shifts).
3. **Disengaged (pedal fully pressed):** Friction disc separated from flywheel → zero torque transfer.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hook:** The clutch must transmit **maximum engine torque** without slipping. The number of friction surfaces is $n$ (single-plate = 2, dual-plate = 4). The total clutch capacity is $n \times \mu \times W \times r_m$, where $r_m$ is the mean friction radius.

---

### 2.1.2 The Gearbox (Transmission)

The gearbox is a **torque-speed converter** that provides multiple gear ratios to keep the engine operating in its **power band** across varying vehicle speeds.

**Types of Gearboxes (KTU 2024 – Critical):**

| Type | Full Name | Control | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| MT | Manual Transmission | Driver (shift lever) | Clutch pedal required |
| AT | Automatic Transmission | Hydraulic (TCU) | Torque converter, planetary gears |
| AMT | Automated Manual Transmission | Electro-hydraulic | Robotised manual gearbox |
| CVT | Continuously Variable Transmission | Electronic | Pulley-and-belt infinite ratios |
| DCT | Dual-Clutch Transmission | Electronic | Two clutches, two gear sets |
| EVT | Electric Variable Transmission | Electronic | Used in hybrids (Toyota THS) |

**Why a Gearbox is Necessary:**
- The ICE produces peak torque only in a narrow RPM range (~2000–4000 rpm for diesel, ~3000–5500 rpm for petrol).
- The vehicle, however, needs to operate from **0 km/h to 200+ km/h**.
- The gearbox provides discrete (or continuous, in CVT) ratios to **multiply torque at low speeds** and **reduce engine speed at high cruising speeds**.

---

### 2.1.3 The Propeller Shaft & Universal Joints

The propeller shaft is a **rotating tubular shaft** that transmits power from the gearbox (at the front of a RWD car) to the differential (at the rear axle). It must accommodate:
- **Length changes** (rear axle moves up/down with suspension travel) → **slip joint / spline**.
- **Angular changes** (rear axle is not always parallel to gearbox output) → **universal joint (U-joint)** or **constant-velocity (CV) joint**.

**Universal Joint Velocity Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
The output shaft of a single U-joint does **not** rotate at a constant angular velocity. For an input angle $\theta_1$ and output angle $\theta_2$:

$$\tan(\theta_1) = \tan(\phi) \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

where $\phi$ is the angle of the driving yoke and $\alpha$ is the joint angle. This non-uniformity is why **CV joints** are used in front wheel drive (FWD) half-shafts.

---

### 2.1.4 The Differential

The differential is a brilliant **epicyclic gear mechanism** that:
1. Splits engine torque between the two driving wheels.
2. Allows the two wheels to rotate at **different speeds** when cornering.

**Why different speeds are needed:**
When a vehicle turns, the **outer wheel** travels a **larger radius** than the **inner wheel**. If both wheels were rigidly connected, one would slip, causing tyre wear, instability, and loss of cornering grip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Shafts of a Differential:**
> - **Input (sun/crown) shaft** — receives power from the propeller shaft via the ring gear.
> - **Two output (side gear) shafts** — drive the left and right wheels.
> - The **cross-pin / planet carrier** — rotates when the wheels are turning at different speeds.

**Differential Ratio (Final Drive Ratio, $i_0$):**
$$i_0 = \frac{\text{Number of teeth on ring gear}}{\text{Number of teeth on pinion gear}}$$

Typical values: **3.0 to 4.5** for passenger cars, **5.0+** for trucks.

---

### 2.1.5 Types of Differentials (Autotronics Focus)

| Type | Locking Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Open Differential | None | Standard passenger cars |
| Locking Differential | Manual or electric actuator | Off-road vehicles |
| Limited-Slip Differential (LSD) | Clutches / viscous fluid / Torsen gears | Performance cars |
| Electronic Limited-Slip Differential (eLSD) | ECU-controlled multi-plate clutch | Modern AWD/performance cars |
| Torque-Vectoring Differential | Twin clutches per wheel | High-end AWD (Audi Sport, BMW M) |

---

### 2.1.6 Axles

- **Live Axle (Rigid Axle):** Both wheels connected by a single rigid beam. Used in trucks, off-roaders, older rear-wheel-drive cars.
- **Independent (Dead) Axle:** Each wheel is suspended and driven independently. Used in modern FWD cars and premium RWD cars. The half-shafts contain **CV joints** (Rzeppa or Tripod type).

---

### 2.1.7 Wheels and Tyres

The **wheel** consists of the **rim** and the **tyre**. The **rolling radius** $r_r$ of the tyre is critical for all drive train calculations.

**Tyre Size Notation (P-metric):**
`P 205/55 R 16`
- `P` = Passenger car
- `205` = Section width in **mm**
- `55` = Aspect ratio (sidewall height = 55% of width)
- `R` = Radial construction
- `16` = Rim diameter in **inches**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = 0.377 \times \dfrac{r_r \times N_e}{i_t \times i_0}$ | Vehicle speed from engine RPM | $V$ in km/h, $N_e$ in rpm, $r_r$ in m |
| 2 | $T_w = T_e \times i_t \times i_0 \times \eta_t$ | Wheel torque from engine torque | All in N·m |
| 3 | $N_w = \dfrac{N_e}{i_t \times i_0}$ | Wheel RPM from engine RPM | All in rpm |
| 4 | $i_0 = \dfrac{Z_{ring}}{Z_{pinion}}$ | Final drive ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r_r}$ | Tractive force at the wheel | $F_t$ in N |
| 6 | $R_r = \mu \times W$ | Rolling resistance | N |
| 7 | $F_a = m \times a$ | Acceleration force (Newton's 2nd law) | N |
| 8 | $F_g = m \times g \times \sin(\theta)$ | Grade climbing force | N |
| 9 | $P_w = T_w \times \omega_w$ | Power at the wheels | W (Watts) |
| 10 | $\omega_w = \dfrac{2\pi \times N_w}{60}$ | Wheel angular velocity | rad/s |
| 11 | $\eta_t = 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95$ | Drivetrain efficiency (typical) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $i_t = \dfrac{Z_{driven}}{Z_{driver}}$ | Transmission gear ratio | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $\theta_{inner} \times r_{inner} = \theta_{outer} \times r_{outer}$ | Ackermann geometry / differential | Radians × m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Joint Equation (Precise):**
> Let $\alpha$ = joint angle, $\phi$ = driving yoke angle. Then:
> $$\tan(\phi) = \frac{\sin(\theta_2)}{\cos(\alpha)}$$
> and
> $$\tan(\theta_1) = \tan(\phi) \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drive train forms the **spine of vehicle dynamics**. Every system in a modern car — from **adaptive cruise control** to **electronic stability control (ESC)** — relies on drive train sensor data:

- **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS):** Used by ABS, TCS, ESP.
- **Crankshaft Position Sensor:** Inputs to ECU for fuel injection timing.
- **Transmission Speed Sensors:** Inputs to TCU for shift scheduling.
- **Torque Management:** ECU/TCU coordinate to deliver exactly the right torque to each wheel in **torque-vectoring AWD systems** (e.g., Audi Quattro, Subaru Symmetrical AWD).

In **electric vehicles (EVs)**, the drive train is dramatically simplified — typically a single-speed reduction gearbox driving each axle. This is a hot topic in **KTU 2024 elective papers** and **viva voce**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Vehicle Speed from Engine RPM

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU drive train numerical problems. We will derive it from first principles.

### Given:
- Engine speed: $N_e$ in **rpm**
- Transmission gear ratio: $i_t$ (dimensionless)
- Final drive ratio: $i_0$ (dimensionless)
- Tyre rolling radius: $r_r$ in **metres**

### To Find:
- Vehicle speed $V$ in **km/h**

---

### Step 1: Convert Engine RPM to Wheel RPM

The transmission and final drive both **reduce** the rotational speed (and correspondingly **multiply** the torque). The total speed reduction ratio is $i_t \times i_0$.

$$N_w = \frac{N_e}{i_t \times i_0}$$

**Explanation:** If $i_t = 3$ (1st gear) and $i_0 = 4$ (final drive), then for every 1 revolution of the engine, the wheel makes $\frac{1}{12}$ of a revolution.

---

### Step 2: Calculate the Linear Distance Travelled per Wheel Revolution

The distance travelled by the vehicle in one complete wheel revolution equals the **circumference of the tyre**:

$$C = 2 \pi r_r$$

where $C$ is in **metres** and $r_r$ is in **metres**.

---

### Step 3: Calculate Linear Velocity (in m/s)

Velocity = (Revolutions per second) × (Distance per revolution)

$$V_{m/s} = \frac{N_w}{60} \times 2\pi r_r$$

**Explanation:** $N_w$ is in rpm, so we divide by 60 to get rev/s. Each rev covers $2\pi r_r$ metres.

---

### Step 4: Substitute $N_w$ from Step 1

$$V_{m/s} = \frac{N_e}{60 \times i_t \times i_0} \times 2\pi r_r$$

$$V_{m/s} = \frac{2\pi r_r N_e}{60 \times i_t \times i_0}$$

---

### Step 5: Convert from m/s to km/h

Multiply by 3.6 (since 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h):

$$V_{km/h} = \frac{2\pi r_r N_e \times 3.6}{60 \times i_t \times i_0}$$

$$V_{km/h} = \frac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 3.6 \times r_r \times N_e}{60 \times i_t \times i_0}$$

$$V_{km/h} = \frac{22.619 \times r_r \times N_e}{60 \times i_t \times i_0}$$

$$V_{km/h} = \frac{0.37699 \times r_r \times N_e}{i_t \times i_0}$$

$$\boxed{V = 0.377 \times \frac{r_r \times N_e}{i_t \times i_0}}$$

---

### Step 6: Numerical Verification

**Problem:** A car has $r_r = 0.32$ m, $i_t = 3$ (1st gear), $i_0 = 4.1$. Engine runs at 3000 rpm. Find vehicle speed.

**Solution:**

$$V = 0.377 \times \frac{0.32 \times 3000}{3 \times 4.1}$$

$$V = 0.377 \times \frac{960}{12.3}$$

$$V = 0.377 \times 78.049$$

$$\boxed{V = 29.42 \text{ km/h}}$$

**Interpretation:** At 3000 rpm in 1st gear, the car moves at ~29.4 km/h. This is consistent with real vehicles, where 1st gear is used for slow-speed launch.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wheel Torque from Engine Torque

### Given:
- Engine torque: $T_e$ in N·m
- Transmission ratio: $i_t$
- Final drive ratio: $i_0$
- Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_t$ (typical 0.85–0.92)

### Step 1: Torque Multiplication Across the Gearbox

Gear ratios multiply torque in proportion to the speed reduction:

$$T_{gb} = T_e \times i_t \times \eta_{gb}$$

where $\eta_{gb}$ is the gearbox efficiency (~0.95).

### Step 2: Torque Multiplication Across the Final Drive

$$T_w = T_{gb} \times i_0 \times \eta_{fd}$$

### Step 3: Combine Both Steps

$$T_w = T_e \times i_t \times i_0 \times \eta_{gb} \times \eta_{fd}$$

Defining $\eta_t = \eta_{gb} \times \eta_{fd}$:

$$\boxed{T_w = T_e \times i_t \times i_0 \times \eta_t}$$

**Note on direction:** The reverse gear inverts the direction of rotation, so $T_{reverse} = -T_w$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Tractive Force at the Wheel–Road Interface

The **tractive force** $F_t$ is the force that pushes the vehicle forward. It is generated at the contact patch between the tyre and the road.

### Step 1: Torque Produces a Tangential Force

The wheel torque acts at the rolling radius of the tyre:

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_r}$$

### Step 2: Equivalently, Using Engine Torque

Substituting the derived expression for $T_w$:

$$F_t = \frac{T_e \times i_t \times i_0 \times \eta_t}{r_r}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

**Problem:** $T_e = 200$ N·m, $i_t = 2.5$, $i_0 = 3.7$, $\eta_t = 0.88$, $r_r = 0.31$ m. Find $F_t$.

**Solution:**

$$F_t = \frac{200 \times 2.5 \times 3.7 \times 0.88}{0.31}$$

$$F_t = \frac{1628}{0.31}$$

$$\boxed{F_t = 5251.6 \text{ N}}$$

This force is what accelerates the car, climbs hills, and overcomes rolling resistance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Forces Acting on a Vehicle

The tractive force $F_t$ must overcome **three resistance forces** for the vehicle to move:

| Force | Symbol | Formula | Dominant At |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rolling Resistance | $F_r$ | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$ | All speeds |
| Aerodynamic Drag | $F_a$ | $F_a = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ | High speeds |
| Gradient (Climb) | $F_g$ | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Hills |
| Acceleration | $F_{acc}$ | $F_{acc} = m \cdot a$ | Transients |

Where:
- $\mu_r$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (0.01–0.02 for car tyres on asphalt)
- $W$ = vehicle weight in N
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (0.25 for modern sedans)
- $A$ = frontal area in m²
- $V$ = vehicle velocity in m/s
- $\theta$ = gradient angle in radians

### Equation of Motion (KTU High-Yield):

$$F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g + F_{acc}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T_e \cdot i_t \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_r} = \mu_r m g + 0.5 \rho C_d A V^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Differential Speed Ratio During Cornering

### Given:
- Steering angle: $\delta$ (in radians)
- Wheelbase: $L$
- Track width: $t$

### Step 1: Turning Radius of the Vehicle's Centre of Gravity

Using the **Ackermann steering geometry**:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan(\delta)}$$

### Step 2: Inner and Outer Wheel Turning Radii

$$R_{inner} = R - \frac{t}{2}$$

$$R_{outer} = R + \frac{t}{2}$$

### Step 3: Speed Ratio Between Wheels

Since all four wheels must roll without slipping:

$$\frac{V_{inner}}{V_{outer}} = \frac{R_{inner}}{R_{outer}} = \frac{R - t/2}{R + t/2}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

**Problem:** $L = 2.5$ m, $t = 1.5$ m, $\delta = 0.2$ rad (~11.5°). Find the speed ratio.

**Solution:**

$$R = \frac{2.5}{\tan(0.2)} = \frac{2.5}{0.2027} = 12.33 \text{ m}$$

$$R_{inner} = 12.33 - 0.75 = 11.58 \text{ m}$$

$$R_{outer} = 12.33 + 0.75 = 13.08 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{11.58}{13.08} = 0.885$$

**Interpretation:** The inner wheel must rotate at 88.5% of the outer wheel's speed. The differential handles this automatically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Complete RWD Vehicle Drive Train Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT[Front of Vehicle]
        ENG[Engine ECU Controlled]
        CLU[Clutch Single Plate Dry]
        TR1[Transmission Gearbox MT or AT]
    end

    subgraph MIDDLE[Centre Tunnel]
        PROP[Propeller Shaft]
        UNI[Universal Joints U Joints]
        SLIP[Slip Joint Spline]
    end

    subgraph REAR[Rear Axle Assembly]
        PIN[Pinion Gear]
        RING[Ring Gear Final Drive]
        DIFFC[Differential Carrier]
        SG1[Side Gear Left]
        SG2[Side Gear Right]
        HALF1[Half Shaft Left]
        HALF2[Half Shaft Right]
    end

    subgraph WHEELS[Driving Wheels]
        WHL1[Rear Left Tyre]
        WHL2[Rear Right Tyre]
    end

    ENG --> CLU
    CLU --> TR1
    TR1 --> PROP
    PROP --> UNI
    UNI --> SLIP
    SLIP --> PIN
    PIN --> RING
    RING --> DIFFC
    DIFFC --> SG1
    DIFFC --> SG2
    SG1 --> HALF1
    SG2 --> HALF2
    HALF1 --> WHL1
    HALF2 --> WHL2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for Exams:** When drawing drive train layouts in KTU exams, always label power-flow direction with **arrows**, and annotate each component with its **function in one line**.

---

## 4.2 FWD Vehicle with Transaxle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG2[Engine Transverse Mount] --> CLU2[Clutch]
    CLU2 --> GB2[Manual Gearbox]
    GB2 --> TRX[Transaxle Differential + Final Drive Integrated]
    TRX --> CV1[Left CV Joint Rzeppa]
    TRX --> CV2[Right CV Joint Rzeppa]
    CV1 --> FW1[Front Left Wheel]
    CV2 --> FW2[Front Right Wheel]
    ECU2[TCU Transmission Control Unit] -.-> TRX
    ABS2[ABS Module] -.monitors.-> FW1
    ABS2 -.monitors.-> FW2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FWD vs RWD:** FWD eliminates the propeller shaft and places the differential inside the gearbox (transaxle). This improves packaging, reduces weight, and provides better traction under acceleration. The penalty is **torque steer** under hard acceleration.

---

## 4.3 AWD / 4WD with Transfer Case

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENG3[Engine] --> CLU3[Clutch]
    CLU3 --> TCASE[Transfer Case]
    TCASE --> FR_DIFF[Front Differential]
    TCASE --> RR_PROP[Propeller Shaft to Rear]
    RR_PROP --> RR_DIFF[Rear Differential]
    FR_DIFF --> F_LH[Front Left Half Shaft]
    FR_DIFF --> F_RH[Front Right Half Shaft]
    RR_DIFF --> R_LH[Rear Left Half Shaft]
    RR_DIFF --> R_RH[Rear Right Half Shaft]
    F_LH --> F_LW[Front Left Wheel]
    F_RH --> F_RW[Front Right Wheel]
    R_LH --> R_LW[Rear Left Wheel]
    R_RH --> R_RW[Rear Right Wheel]
    AWD_ECU[AWD ECU with Multiple Clutch Packs] -.controls.-> TCASE
    AWD_ECU -.controls.-> FR_DIFF
    AWD_ECU -.controls.-> RR_DIFF
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"Differentiate between Part-time 4WD, Full-time AWD, and On-demand AWD."* — Part-time 4WD is for off-road only (not safe on dry tarmac), Full-time AWD always drives all wheels (with a centre differential), On-demand AWD uses electronics to engage rear wheels when slip is detected.

---

## 4.4 Clutch Engagement States (Functional Block)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Engaged
    Engaged --> Slipping: Pedal Partially Pressed
    Slipping --> Disengaged: Pedal Fully Pressed
    Disengaged --> Slipping: Pedal Released Partially
    Slipping --> Engaged: Pedal Released Fully
    Engaged --> [*]: Engine Off

    note right of Engaged
        100% Torque Transfer
        Vehicle Moves
    end note

    note right of Slipping
        Controlled Slip
        Used During Launch
        and Gear Shifts
    end note

    note left of Disengaged
        Zero Torque Transfer
        Gear Change Possible
    end note
```

---

## 4.5 Torque Flow in a Planetary Gear Set (Automatic Transmission)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sun Gear] <--> PLANET[Planet Gears 3 or 4]
    PLANET --> RING2[Ring Gear Internal Teeth]
    PLANET -.rotates on.-> CARRIER[Planet Carrier]
    SUN --> OUT1[Output Option 1]
    CARRIER --> OUT2[Output Option 2]
    RING2 --> OUT3[Output Option 3]
    SUN -.input.-> IN1
    CARRIER -.input.-> IN2
    RING2 -.input.-> IN3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept for AT:** In a planetary gear, **any one** member can be the input, **another** the output, and the **third** is held stationary (or driven). This gives multiple gear ratios in a compact package. The TCU controls **clutch packs and bands** to lock the appropriate member, producing forward, reverse, or neutral.

---

## 4.6 Differential Operation During Cornering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STRAIGHT[Straight Line Driving]
        S1[Both Wheels Rotate at SAME Speed]
        S1 --> S2[Cross Pin DOES NOT Rotate]
    end

    subgraph TURN[Cornering]
        T1[Outer Wheel Rotates FASTER]
        T2[Inner Wheel Rotates SLOWER]
        T1 --> T3[Cross Pin ROTATES]
        T2 --> T3
        T3 --> T4[Side Gears Rotate at Different Speeds]
    end

    STRAIGHT --> TURN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Differential Limitation:** When one wheel loses grip (e.g., on ice), the open differential sends *all* the torque to the slipping wheel. This is why **LSD** and **locking differentials** are used in performance and off-road vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Drive Train". List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

> A drive train is the group of components in a vehicle that transmits power from the engine to the driving wheels. Its main components are:
> 1. Clutch
> 2. Gearbox (Transmission)
> 3. Propeller shaft
> 4. Universal joints
> 5. Differential
> 6. Final drive (ring and pinion)
> 7. Axles (live or half shafts)
> 8. Driving wheels and tyres

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for components]**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the function of a differential in an automobile? Why is it necessary during cornering?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Function:** The differential splits the engine torque between the two driving wheels and, at the same time, allows them to rotate at **different speeds** when required.
>
> **Necessity during cornering:** When a vehicle turns, the outer wheel covers a longer path than the inner wheel in the same time. If both wheels were rigidly connected, the inner wheel would have to slip (scrub), causing tyre wear, instability, and loss of cornering grip. The differential accommodates this by allowing the outer wheel to rotate faster than the inner wheel, while still delivering torque to both.

**[Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks for function + 1.5 marks for necessity]**

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a single-plate clutch. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A single-plate clutch consists of:
1. **Flywheel** — attached to the engine crankshaft, acts as one friction surface.
2. **Clutch plate (friction disc)** — has friction linings on both sides, splined to the gearbox input shaft.
3. **Pressure plate** — bolted to the flywheel via clutch springs (or diaphragm spring), provides clamping force.
4. **Clutch springs / diaphragm spring** — provide axial clamping force.
5. **Release bearing** — pushes against the pressure plate fingers when the clutch pedal is pressed.
6. **Operating lever / fork** — converts pedal motion to release bearing motion.

**Working:**
- **Engaged Position (Pedal Released):** The clutch springs force the pressure plate against the friction disc, clamping it tightly against the flywheel. The engine torque is transmitted to the gearbox input shaft via the splines. The vehicle moves.
- **Disengaged Position (Pedal Pressed):** The release bearing pushes against the diaphragm spring fingers, which pivot and pull the pressure plate away from the friction disc. The disc is no longer clamped, and the engine is disconnected from the gearbox. Gear changes can now be made.
- **Slipping (Pedal Partially Pressed):** Partial clamping force → friction disc slips against the flywheel → controlled torque transfer, used during vehicle launch.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FW[Flywheel Engine Side] --> FD[Friction Disc Splined to Gearbox Shaft]
    FD --> PP[Pressure Plate]
    PP --> DS[Diaphragm Spring]
    DS --> RB[Release Bearing]
    RB --> LF[Lever Fork]
    LF --> CP[Clutch Pedal]
    GBS[Gearbox Input Shaft] -.splined.-> FD
```

**Clutch Capacity Equation (Additional Credit):**

$$T_{max} = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot r_m$$

where $n$ = number of friction surfaces (= 2 for single-plate), $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $W$ = axial clamping force, $r_m$ = mean radius of friction lining.

---
