# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]`
**Define a feedback carburettor system. List its major components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A feedback carburettor system is a fuel-metering system in which a conventional carburettor is integrated with an electronic closed-loop control circuit that continuously adjusts the air–fuel ratio to maintain **λ = 1.000 ± 0.02** based on real-time exhaust gas analysis.

**Major components (2 marks):**

1. **Conventional carburettor** (float chamber, venturi, jets, throttle body) modified to accept an external actuator.
2. **Lambda (oxygen) sensor** mounted in the exhaust manifold.
3. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** with comparator, integrator/PID and heater driver.
4. **Mixture-control actuator** — duty-cycle solenoid, stepper motor, or vacuum modulator.
5. **Three-way catalytic converter** (downstream of the sensor).
6. **Wiring harness and MIL** (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) for diagnostics.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]`
**What is a lambda sensor? State the typical output voltages for lean and rich mixtures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A lambda sensor (also called an EGO — Exhaust Gas Oxygen sensor) is a **zirconia-dioxide (ZrO₂) ceramic electrochemical cell** that generates a voltage proportional to the difference in oxygen partial pressure between the exhaust gas and a reference (atmospheric) air pocket. Its output follows the **Nernst equation** $E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln(p_{O_2,\text{ref}}/p_{O_2,\text{exh}})$.

**Typical output voltages:**

- **Lean mixture (λ > 1):** $E \approx 0.0 - 0.1 \text{ V}$
- **Stoichiometric crossover:** $E \approx 0.45 \text{ V}$ (ECU reference)
- **Rich mixture (λ < 1):** $E \approx 0.8 - 1.0 \text{ V}$

The near-binary nature of this voltage is exploited by the ECU as a *switching* signal to drive the closed-loop mixture control.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> KTU pattern: every Part-B question offers an internal choice (either-or). The model answers below are complete, with sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A — Option (i) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a feedback carburettor system. Clearly identify the role of each block.

**Model Answer:**

A feedback carburettor is a **closed-loop fuel control system**. Its working can be understood by following the *signal flow* through five functional blocks:

1. **Carburettor (Plant)**: Meters fuel mechanically using the Venturi principle. Modified with an external actuator to allow electrical adjustment of the main fuel delivery.

2. **Engine (Plant, continued)**: Combusts the mixture in the cylinders. Combustion quality is the *actual* controlled variable — but it cannot be measured directly inside the cylinder.

3. **Lambda sensor (Feedback transducer)**: Sits in the exhaust manifold. The ZrO₂ cell generates a voltage **E ≈ 0.05 V (lean)** or **E ≈ 0.85 V (rich)** as per the Nernst equation. This voltage is the *only* way the ECU can "see" the result of its previous control action.

4. **ECU (Controller)**: Compares the sensor voltage with an internal **0.45 V reference**. If $V_{\text{sens}} < 0.45 \text{ V}$ (lean), it lowers the duty cycle $D$ of the actuator to *enrich*; if $V_{\text{sens}} > 0.45 \text{ V}$ (rich), it raises $D$ to *lean*. A simple integrating (I) term eliminates steady-state offset.

5. **Actuator (Final control element)**: A solenoid (or stepper) that trims the carburettor mixture by either bleeding air into the float-bowl vent, or by moving a metering rod in the main jet.

**Closed-loop operation:** The exhaust gas is the *controlled variable*; the O₂ sensor is the *measurement*; the ECU is the *controller*; the actuator is the *final element*. The loop oscillates gently around λ = 1.000 at about **0.5 – 1 Hz**, giving the characteristic *lambda wave* on a scope.

> A clean block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** above (7 marks for the explanation, with diagram earning 2 of the 7).

**(b) [7 marks]** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine runs at **2400 rpm**. Volumetric efficiency is **0.82**, manifold pressure **0.55 bar**, ambient **1.013 bar, 30 °C**. Swept volume per cylinder = **450 cm³**. The lambda sensor holds the mixture at **λ = 1.02**. Compute the **fuel mass flow rate** in kg/h.

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Displacement volume flow:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{disp}} &= \frac{N V_s n}{2} = \frac{4 \times 450 \times 10^{-6} \times 2400}{2} = 2.16 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Air volume flow at manifold conditions:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{air}} &= \dot{V}_{\text{disp}} \cdot \eta_v \cdot \frac{p_{\text{map}}}{p_{\text{amb}}} \\
                    &= 2.16 \times 0.82 \times \frac{0.55}{1.013} \\
                    &= 0.9625 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Air mass flow (ideal gas, $T = 303 \text{ K}$, $R_{\text{air}} = 287 \text{ J·kg}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$):*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= \frac{p_{\text{map}} \dot{V}_{\text{air}}}{R_{\text{air}} T} \\
                     &= \frac{0.55 \times 10^5 \times 0.9625}{287 \times 303} \\
                     &= \frac{52\,938}{86\,961} \\
                     &= 0.6087 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Actual A/F and fuel mass flow:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{\text{actual}} &= \lambda \cdot 14.7 = 1.02 \times 14.7 = 14.994 \\
\dot{m}_f &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{0.6087}{14.994} \\
          &= 0.04060 \text{ kg/s} = 146.2 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $\dot{m}_f \approx 146.2 \text{ kg/h}$ (7 marks distributed across the four steps + final answer).

---

### Question B — Option (ii) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **construction and working of a ZrO₂ lambda sensor** used in feedback carburettor systems. Include the Nernst equation and a voltage-vs-A/F sketch description.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**

- A **thimble-shaped ceramic element** made of **yttria-stabilised zirconia (ZrO₂)** is the active body. Yttria (Y₂O₃) doping creates oxygen-ion vacancies in the lattice, making the ceramic an oxygen-ion conductor above **350 °C**.
- **Inner and outer surfaces** are coated with a thin porous **platinum layer**, which serves as a catalyst and as an electrode.
- A **reference air pocket** is trapped inside the thimble, in contact with ambient atmosphere via a small vent hole.
- A **protective metal shield** with slots covers the sensor body to prevent mechanical damage while letting exhaust gas contact the outer electrode.
- A **heater element** (in 4-wire sensors) is embedded to bring the sensor above 350 °C within ~ 30 s of cold start.

**Working:**

At operating temperature, O₂ molecules at the high-concentration electrode (reference air) accept electrons to form $O^{2-}$ ions. These ions migrate through the ZrO₂ lattice to the low-concentration (exhaust) side, where they release electrons and recombine. The resulting charge separation produces the **Nernst EMF**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \frac{RT}{4F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Voltage vs. A/F behaviour:** The sensor output is *not* linear. As A/F crosses 14.7 from rich to lean, $E$ drops from ~ **0.8 V** to ~ **0.1 V** over a very narrow A/F window. The ECU exploits this sharp transition by setting its reference at **0.45 V**, well centred between the two stable levels.

**Role in the feedback loop:** The sensor's near-binary output lets the ECU implement a *hysteresis controller* (like a thermostat) that drives the carburettor actuator's duty cycle up or down with each crossing — keeping λ hovering about unity.

**(b) [7 marks]** With reference to a feedback carburettor, explain the difference between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** modes of operation. Under what engine conditions does the ECU fall back to open-loop?

**Model Answer:**

**Open-loop mode:** The ECU controls the actuator duty cycle from a *pre-programmed map* based on engine speed, load, coolant temperature, and throttle position — but it *ignores* the lambda sensor signal. The controller has no feedback; it merely *commands* a value and hopes the result is correct. Open-loop is less accurate but *predictable* and *safe* in transient conditions.

**Closed-loop mode:** The ECU actively reads the lambda sensor voltage and *adjusts* the actuator duty cycle so that the measured λ converges to 1.000. The loop is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\text{meas}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{sensor}}\; V_{\text{sens}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{comparator}}\; \text{error} \;\xrightarrow{\text{PID}}\; D \;\xrightarrow{\text{actuator}}\; \dot{m}_f
\end{aligned}
$$

The lambda signal is the *feedback*; without it the loop would be open.

**Engine conditions where the ECU falls back to open-loop:**

1. **Cold start / cold engine** — coolant temperature below ~ 40 – 60 °C; the O₂ sensor is also below 350 °C and produces no useful signal.
2. **Wide-open throttle (WOT)** — maximum torque is required, so the mixture is intentionally enriched; lambda is not regulated.
3. **Deceleration fuel cut-off (DFCO)** — fuel is shut off entirely when the throttle is closed at high rpm.
4. **Lambda sensor failure** — detected by an out-of-range voltage or a slow response time; the ECU stores a DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code) and illuminates the MIL.
5. **Idle / very low load transients** — to prevent oscillation and ensure driveability, the loop is often paused for a few hundred ms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common student mistake in this question is to *equate* open-loop with "the carburettor is not working" — this is wrong. The carburettor is always working; in open-loop the ECU simply does not use the O₂ sensor to *trim* the mixture. Marks are explicitly deducted for confusing the carburettor's mechanical metering with the ECU's electronic trimming.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for Feedback Carburettor Systems (KTU Module 2):**

- A **feedback carburettor** is a *conventional carburettor + electronic closed-loop trim* system; it is *not* the same as port fuel injection.
- The **target** of the closed loop is **λ = 1.000 ± 0.02** (petrol A/F = **14.7 : 1**).
- The **feedback sensor** is the **ZrO₂ lambda sensor**; its voltage switches from ~ 0.05 V (lean) to ~ 0.85 V (rich) across a **0.45 V** reference.
- The **Nernst equation** $E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln(p_{O_2,\text{ref}}/p_{O_2,\text{exh}})$ explains the sensor's *exponential* response and hence its near-binary behaviour.
- The **actuator** is usually a **duty-cycle solenoid** that bleeds air into the float-bowl vent, changing the effective depression and hence the fuel flow.
- The ECU uses an **integrating (I) controller** with hysteresis; the duty cycle **oscillates** at **0.5 – 1 Hz** in closed-loop — this is normal and desired.
- A **three-way catalytic converter** is *mandatory* downstream of the sensor for the system to be useful (only at λ ≈ 1 do CO, HC, NOₓ reduce simultaneously).
- **Open-loop fallback** conditions: cold engine, WOT, deceleration fuel cut-off, sensor failure, severe transients.
- **Diagnostic feature:** Sensor failure triggers a **MIL** and stores a DTC — the same architecture as modern OBD.
- **Typical exam traps:** confusing open-loop with carburettor failure; mixing up rich/lean voltage levels; forgetting to use the corrected $T$ in the Nernst equation; assuming linear A/F control (it is *hysteretic*, not proportional).
- **Emission benefit:** λ held within ± 1 % → CO and HC drop by ~ 80 %, NOₓ by ~ 90 % compared to an open-loop carburettor.
- **Heated vs. unheated sensors:** 4-wire heated sensors achieve closed-loop operation within ~ 30 s of start; single-wire unheated sensors need ~ 1 – 2 minutes of warm-up.
- **Engineers' rule of thumb:** the lambda sensor "lies" until it reaches 350 °C — that is *why* closed-loop is disabled during cold start.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throttle Body Injection and Multi Point Fuel Injection System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Throttle Body Injection and Multi Point Fuel Injection System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Throttle Body Injection (TBI)

**Throttle Body Injection (TBI)**, also known as **Single-Point Injection (SPI)** or **Central Fuel Injection (CFI)**, is an electronic fuel injection architecture in which **a single (or pair of) fuel injector(s) is mounted centrally above the throttle body**, delivering atomized fuel into the intake manifold at a point just upstream of the throttle plate. The fuel then distributes itself to all cylinders through the intake runners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):** "Throttle Body Injection is a fuel delivery system in which one or two electronically controlled injectors are located at the throttle body, spraying fuel into the intake manifold upstream of the throttle valve, thereby replacing the carburetor as the primary air-fuel metering device."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine a **large overhead water tank with a single shower head** feeding water into multiple pipes going to different rooms. The shower head (TBI injector) sprays water (fuel) once, and the pipes (intake runners) carry it to each room (cylinder). It is simple, cheap, and easy to maintain — but the water pressure at the last room is not as good as the first. This is exactly how TBI works: **one injection point for all cylinders**.

---

## 1.2 Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)

**Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)**, also called **Multi-Port Injection (MPI)** or **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)**, is an electronic fuel injection architecture in which **each intake port of every cylinder is equipped with its own dedicated electromagnetic fuel injector**. Fuel is sprayed just upstream of the intake valve of each cylinder, ensuring precise and equal fuel distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):** "Multi-Point Fuel Injection is a fuel metering system employing one injector per cylinder, positioned at the inlet port, where the ECU controls the injector pulse width to deliver an accurately metered quantity of fuel for the prevailing engine operating condition."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Now imagine **each room has its own dedicated shower head**, with a smart controller adjusting water flow to each room individually. Every room gets the **exact amount of water it needs**, simultaneously and accurately. This is MPFI: **dedicated, precise, per-cylinder fuel delivery**.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Weighted Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are **exam-favourite numbers** that KTU examiners frequently test in derivations and numerical problems.

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometric AFR (Gasoline)** | **14.7 : 1** | Theoretical ideal air-fuel ratio |
| **Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel)** | **14.5 : 1** | Theoretical ideal air-fuel ratio |
| **Stoichiometric λ (Lambda)** | **1.000** | Ideal combustion equivalence ratio |
| **TBI Operating Pressure** | **~1.0 – 1.5 bar (gauge)** | Low pressure system |
| **MPFI Operating Pressure** | **~3.0 – 4.5 bar (gauge)** | Medium pressure system |
| **GDI Operating Pressure** | **~100 – 350 bar** | High pressure direct injection |
| **Injector Response Time** | **~1 – 2 ms** | Electromagnetic latency |
| **Injection Pulse Width (Idle)** | **~1.5 – 3.0 ms** | Typical cr/idle range |
| **Injection Pulse Width (WOT)** | **~8 – 15 ms** | Typical full load range |

---

## 1.4 Classification Hierarchy of Fuel Injection Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Map:** KTU Module 2 groups fuel injection under the broader umbrella of Engine Management Systems. Knowing this hierarchy is essential for 2-mark definitional questions.

```
Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)
        │
        ├── 1. Throttle Body Injection (TBI) / Single-Point Injection (SPI)
        │         ├── Single Injector TBI
        │         └── Dual Injector TBI
        │
        ├── 2. Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) / Port Fuel Injection (PFI)
        │         ├── Simultaneous Injection
        │         ├── Batched Injection
        │         └── Sequential (Timed) Injection
        │
        ├── 3. Direct Injection (DI) / Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)
        │
        └── 4. Common Rail Diesel Injection
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Throttle Body Injection — Operational Architecture

The TBI system comprises the following sub-systems, which work in concert to deliver the correct air-fuel mixture:

1. **Fuel Supply Sub-system** — Electric fuel pump, fuel filter, fuel pressure regulator, and a single (or dual) electromagnetic injector(s) mounted on the throttle body.
2. **Air Intake Sub-system** — Air cleaner, intake plenum, throttle plate (mechanically or electronically actuated), and idle air control (IAC) valve.
3. **Electronic Control Sub-system** — The **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** reads inputs from the **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure)** sensor, **TPS (Throttle Position Sensor)**, **CTS (Coolant Temperature Sensor)**, **O₂ (Lambda) sensor**, and **CKP (Crankshaft Position) sensor**.
4. **Feedback Sub-system** — The **closed-loop O₂ sensor feedback** constantly trims the injector pulse width to maintain λ = 1.000.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TBI?** The fundamental reason is **simplicity and cost**. A single injector replaces multiple carburetor circuits (idle, cruise, part-throttle, full-load, accelerator pump, choke, power valve). The ECU replaces the mechanical and vacuum-driven metering. TBI was the **bridge technology** between carburetors and modern MPFI.

### Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Air Measurement:** The ECU reads the **MAP sensor** to determine the absolute manifold pressure. Combined with **RPM** and **IAT (Intake Air Temperature)**, it computes the **mass of air entering the engine** using the ideal gas law.
- **Step 2 — Fuel Demand Calculation:** The ECU divides the calculated air mass by the **target AFR** (14.7:1 in closed-loop cruise) to determine the required **fuel mass per cycle**.
- **Step 3 — Pulse Width Computation:** The ECU calculates the **injector pulse width** (in milliseconds) using the equation:

$$t_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$$

- **Step 4 — Injection Event:** The single injector fires **once per crankshaft revolution** (semi-sequential) or **twice per revolution** (full-batch) for a duration equal to $t_{inj}$.
- **Step 5 — Distribution:** The atomized fuel travels through the intake manifold, where **fuel droplets may condense on manifold walls** (manifold wall wetting — a known TBI weakness).
- **Step 6 — Feedback Trim:** The **upstream O₂ sensor** measures exhaust λ; the ECU applies an **integral correction factor** (Long Term Fuel Trim, LTFT, and Short Term Fuel Trim, STFT) to refine $t_{inj}$.

---

## 2.2 Multi-Point Fuel Injection — Operational Architecture

The MPFI system has **N injectors**, where N equals the number of cylinders (typically **3, 4, 6, or 8**). Each injector is positioned in the **intake port**, just before the intake valve of its respective cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI)** — the most advanced form of MPFI — times each injector to fire **once per engine cycle**, precisely **when the corresponding intake valve is open**. This requires a **CMP (Camshaft Position) sensor** in addition to CKP.

### Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Air Mass Measurement:** Either a **Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor** (speed-density supplemented) or a **MAP sensor** (speed-density) computes the air mass per cylinder per cycle.
- **Step 2 — Per-Cylinder Fuel Demand:** The ECU calculates the fuel needed for **one specific cylinder's intake event**:

$$m_{fuel,cyl} = \frac{m_{air,cyl}}{AFR_{target}}$$

- **Step 3 — Individual Pulse Width:** The ECU computes a unique $t_{inj}$ for each cylinder. This allows **individual cylinder fuel trim**, which is impossible in TBI.
- **Step 4 — Injection Strategy:** The ECU selects one of three firing patterns:
  - **Simultaneous** — All injectors fire at the same instant, twice per crank revolution.
  - **Batched** — Injectors fire in two groups (e.g., 1-4 and 2-3 in a 4-cyl), twice per revolution.
  - **Sequential (SFI)** — Each injector fires once per engine cycle, phased to the intake event.
- **Step 5 — Fuel Atomization:** The injector sprays fuel at **3.0 – 4.5 bar**, producing a fine cone of droplets that vaporizes rapidly in the port, **minimizing wall wetting**.
- **Step 6 — Closed-Loop Lambda Control:** The O₂ sensor trims the global fuel map; **individual cylinder trims** further refine each injector.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are derived from the **ideal gas law**, **fuel injector metering dynamics**, and **engine volumetric efficiency models** commonly tested in KTU 2024 Scheme end-semester examinations. Note the explicit use of `\vert` for absolute value to preserve table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{air} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot p_{man} \cdot N}{2 \cdot R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$ | Air mass per engine cycle (Speed-Density) | kg/cycle |
| 2 | $m_{air,cyl} = \frac{m_{air}}{N_{cyl}}$ | Air mass per cylinder per cycle | kg/cyl·cycle |
| 3 | $m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{AFR_{target}}$ | Required fuel mass per cycle | kg/cycle |
| 4 | $t_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$ | Injector pulse width (open-loop) | s |
| 5 | $t_{inj,closed} = t_{inj,open} \cdot (1 + STFT + LTFT)$ | Closed-loop pulse width with fuel trim | s |
| 6 | $\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (lambda) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $p_{inj,abs} = p_{rail} + p_{man}$ | Absolute injection pressure differential | Pa |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta p / \rho_{fuel}}$ | Injector mass flow rate (Bernoulli-based) | kg/s |
| 9 | $D = \frac{t_{inj}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | Injector duty cycle | % |
| 10 | $EEC = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\vert AFR_{target} - AFR_{actual,i}\vert\right)$ | Engine Equal-Combustion Criterion (trim) | dimensionless |

### Symbol Glossary

- $V_d$ = total engine displacement volume
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency
- $p_{man}$ = manifold absolute pressure
- $T_{man}$ = manifold absolute temperature
- $R_{air}$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $N$ = engine speed (RPM)
- $N_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders
- $C_d$ = injector discharge coefficient (≈ **0.85 – 0.95**)
- $A_{orifice}$ = total effective injector orifice area
- $\rho_{fuel}$ = fuel density (gasoline ≈ **745 kg/m³** at 20 °C)
- $STFT$, $LTFT$ = Short Term / Long Term Fuel Trim (%)

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this topic?** The choice between TBI and MPFI is a foundational **engineering trade-off** between **cost, complexity, emissions, and drivability**. Modern ECUs (e.g., Bosch ME17, Continental Simos, Denso) all implement a form of MPFI or its descendant (GDI). TBI is now largely obsolete in passenger cars but still appears in low-cost two-wheeler and three-wheeler applications in developing markets.

| Property | TBI | MPFI |
|---|---|---|
| Injector count | 1 (or 2) | N (one per cylinder) |
| Cost | Low | Medium |
| Fuel distribution | Uneven (wall-wetting) | Even and precise |
| Cold start | Good (centralized vaporization) | Excellent (port vaporization) |
| Emissions (HC, CO) | Higher | Lower |
| Response to transient | Slower | Faster |
| Individual cylinder trim | Not possible | Possible |
| Used in | Older econo-cars, 2-wheelers | Modern petrol cars, SUVs |
| Example vehicles | Chevrolet TBI 350 (1987), Ford 1.6L | Toyota VVT-i, Honda i-VTEC, Maruti MPFI |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Air Mass Per Cycle (Speed-Density Method)

The Speed-Density equation is the **single most important formula** in KTU autotronics. It is the foundation for both TBI and MPFI fuel calculation.

### Given
- $V_d$ = displacement volume = **2.0 L** = $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency = **0.90** (90%)
- $p_{man}$ = manifold absolute pressure = **0.50 bar** = $0.50 \times 10^{5}$ Pa (at part-throttle)
- $T_{man}$ = manifold absolute temperature = **320 K**
- $N$ = engine speed = **3000 RPM**
- $R_{air}$ = **287 J/(kg·K)**

### Step 1 — Write the Ideal Gas Law for the Inducted Charge

The mass of air inducted in one engine cycle (one full crank revolution, covering $V_d$) is governed by:

$$m_{air} = \frac{p_{man} \cdot V_{eff}}{R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$$

where the **effective inducted volume** is:

$$V_{eff} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Effective Volume

$$m_{air} = \frac{p_{man} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot (N/2)}{R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$$

### Step 3 — Plug in Numerical Values

$$m_{air} = \frac{(0.50 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (0.90) \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (3000/2)}{(287) \cdot (320)}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (0.90) \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1500)}{(287) \cdot (320)}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Numerator

$$\text{Num} = 5.0 \times 10^{4} \times 0.90 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 = 1.35 \times 10^{5}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Denominator

$$\text{Den} = 287 \times 320 = 91{,}840$$

### Step 6 — Final Air Mass

$$m_{air} = \frac{1.35 \times 10^{5}}{91{,}840} = 1.469 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

$$\boxed{m_{air} \approx 1.47 \text{ g/cycle}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Injector Pulse Width Calculation (MPFI, 4-Cylinder)

### Given
- $m_{air}$ = **1.47 g/cycle** (from above)
- AFR = **14.7 : 1** (stoichiometric, closed-loop cruise)
- Injector flow rate $\dot{Q}_{inj}$ = **220 cc/min** (typical MPFI injector)
- $\rho_{fuel}$ = **745 kg/m³**
- Number of cylinders $N_{cyl}$ = **4**

### Step 1 — Compute Air Mass Per Cylinder

For a 4-stroke engine, one engine cycle = 2 crank revolutions = 4 firing events. So:

$$m_{air,cyl} = \frac{m_{air}}{N_{cyl}} = \frac{1.47}{4} = 0.3675 \text{ g/cyl·cycle}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Required Fuel Mass Per Cylinder

$$m_{fuel,cyl} = \frac{m_{air,cyl}}{AFR} = \frac{0.3675}{14.7} = 0.02500 \text{ g/cyl·cycle}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Injector Flow Rate to SI Units

$$\dot{Q}_{inj} = 220 \text{ cc/min} = \frac{220 \times 10^{-6}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.667 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Volume Flow to Mass Flow

$$\dot{m}_{inj} = \dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel} = 3.667 \times 10^{-6} \times 745 = 2.732 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Pulse Width

$$t_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel,cyl}}{\dot{m}_{inj}} = \frac{0.02500 \times 10^{-3}}{2.732 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$t_{inj} = 9.152 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 9.15 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{t_{inj} \approx 9.15 \text{ ms per injection event per cylinder}}$$

### Step 6 — Validate with Duty Cycle

For SFI (1 firing per cycle per cyl, 2 rev/cycle, 4-cyl, 4-stroke):

$$T_{period} = \frac{120 \text{ s}}{N} = \frac{120}{3000} = 0.0400 \text{ s per cycle}$$

$$D = \frac{t_{inj}}{T_{period}/N_{cyl}} = \frac{9.15 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0400/4} = \frac{9.15 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0100} = 91.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A duty cycle > **80%** indicates the injectors are nearing saturation. In a **WOT (Wide Open Throttle)** condition, the ECU will request enrichment beyond stoichiometric, but the injectors may saturate. This is a classic KTU conceptual question: *"Why does the engine lose power at very high RPM even though more air is available?"* — **Answer: Injector saturation / duty cycle ceiling.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Correction (STFT/LTFT)

Suppose the O₂ sensor reports an **excessively rich mixture** (lambda < 1) for a sustained period. The ECU applies a **negative fuel trim**.

### Given
- Base (open-loop) pulse width: $t_{inj,open}$ = **9.15 ms**
- STFT = **−4%** (ECU momentarily reducing fuel)
- LTFT = **−2%** (learned long-term correction)

### Step 1 — Apply Trims

$$t_{inj,closed} = t_{inj,open} \cdot (1 + STFT + LTFT)$$

$$t_{inj,closed} = 9.15 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (1 - 0.04 - 0.02)$$

$$t_{inj,closed} = 9.15 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.94$$

$$\boxed{t_{inj,closed} = 8.601 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **typical KTU 14-mark problem** will ask you to derive the closed-loop pulse width after STFT and LTFT are given, or vice versa — to compute STFT from observed lambda drift. Always express trims as **decimal fractions**, not percentages, in your final equation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TBI vs MPFI

| Performance Metric | TBI (Single-Point) | MPFI (Multi-Point) | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Injection Location** | Above throttle plate | At each intake port | MPFI eliminates manifold wall-wetting |
| **Fuel Pressure** | **~1.0 – 1.5 bar** | **~3.0 – 4.5 bar** | MPFI uses higher pressure for finer atomization |
| **Injector Count** | 1 or 2 | N (one per cylinder) | MPFI requires matched injectors |
| **Distribution Uniformity** | Poor to fair | Excellent | MPFI injects at the port, no re-distribution |
| **Cold Start Driveability** | Marginal | Excellent | MPFI port vaporization is fast |
| **Transient Response** | Slow (manifold dynamics) | Fast (port dynamics) | MPFI responds within 1–2 cycles |
| **Emissions (HC + CO)** | Higher | Lower | Better atomization → complete combustion |
| **Individual Cyl Trim** | **Not possible** | **Possible** | MPFI allows per-cylinder fuel correction |
| **Cost / Complexity** | Low | Medium | Trade-off: more parts, more wiring, more ECU I/O |
| **Tuning Flexibility** | Limited | High | MPFI supports advanced strategies like SFI |
| **Fuel Economy** | ~3–8% lower | Better | More precise metering at part-throttle |
| **Failure Mode** | Single point of failure | Redundant (one fails, others run) | MPFI is fault-tolerant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Throttle Body Injection (TBI) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Electric Fuel Pump]
    B --> C[Fuel Filter]
    C --> D[Fuel Pressure Regulator]
    D --> E[TBI Injector mounted on Throttle Body]
    E --> F[Intake Manifold with Wall Wetting Risk]
    F --> G1[Cylinder 1 Intake Runner]
    F --> G2[Cylinder 2 Intake Runner]
    F --> G3[Cylinder 3 Intake Runner]
    F --> G4[Cylinder 4 Intake Runner]
    H[ECU Engine Control Unit] -->|Pulse Width Signal| E
    I[MAP Sensor] --> H
    J[TPS Throttle Position Sensor] --> H
    K[CTS Coolant Temp Sensor] --> H
    L[CKP Crankshaft Sensor] --> H
    M[O2 Lambda Sensor] -->|Closed Loop Feedback| H
    H --> N[Ignition Coil and Spark Plug]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Electric Fuel Pump]
    B --> C[Fuel Filter]
    C --> D[High Pressure Fuel Rail]
    D --> E1[Injector Cyl 1]
    D --> E2[Injector Cyl 2]
    D --> E3[Injector Cyl 3]
    D --> E4[Injector Cyl 4]
    E1 --> F1[Intake Port Cyl 1]
    E2 --> F2[Intake Port Cyl 2]
    E3 --> F3[Intake Port Cyl 3]
    E4 --> F4[Intake Port Cyl 4]
    H[ECU Engine Control Unit] -->|Sequential Pulse| E1
    H -->|Sequential Pulse| E2
    H -->|Sequential Pulse| E3
    H -->|Sequential Pulse| E4
    I1[MAF or MAP Sensor] --> H
    I2[TPS Throttle Position Sensor] --> H
    I3[CTS Coolant Temp Sensor] --> H
    I4[CKP Crankshaft Sensor] --> H
    I5[CMP Camshaft Sensor] --> H
    I6[O2 Lambda Sensor] -->|Closed Loop Feedback| H
    I7[IAT Intake Air Temp] --> H
    H --> J1[Ignition Coil Cyl 1]
    H --> J2[Ignition Coil Cyl 2]
    H --> J3[Ignition Coil Cyl 3]
    H --> J4[Ignition Coil Cyl 4]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Flowchart — TBI vs MPFI Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Operating Condition] --> B{Fuel Injection Strategy}
    B -->|Cost Optimized| C[TBI Throttle Body]
    B -->|Performance Optimized| D[MPFI Multi Port]
    C --> C1[Single Injector]
    C --> C2[Low Pressure 1.0 to 1.5 bar]
    C --> C3[Wall Wetting Risk]
    C --> C4[No Cyl Trim]
    D --> D1[N Injectors]
    D --> D2[Med Pressure 3.0 to 4.5 bar]
    D --> D3[Excellent Atomization]
    D --> D4[Per Cyl Trim Possible]
    D --> D5[Sequential SFI Capable]
```

---

## 4.4 ECU Pulse Width Decision Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[MAP Sensor Input] --> Q1[Air Mass Calculator]
    P2[RPM Input from CKP] --> Q1
    P3[IAT Input] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Base Fuel Map Lookup]
    R1 --> S1[Target AFR 14.7 to 1]
    S1 --> T1[Base Pulse Width t_open]
    P4[CTS Input] --> T2[Warmup Enrichment Multiplier]
    P5[TPS Input] --> T3[Acceleration Enrichment]
    P6[Battery Voltage] --> T4[Inj Dead Time Compensation]
    T2 --> U1[Final Pulse Width t_final]
    T3 --> U1
    T4 --> U1
    T1 --> U1
    P7[O2 Sensor] --> V1[STFT Short Term Fuel Trim]
    P8[Long Term Memory] --> V2[LTFT Long Term Fuel Trim]
    V1 --> U1
    V2 --> U1
    U1 --> W1[Final Injector Drive Signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above abstract the physical sensor and actuator wiring into a clean functional architecture. KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic often ask: *"Sketch the block diagram of a TBI/MPFI system and label the major sub-systems."* A clean Mermaid block diagram of this form is fully acceptable as a hand-sketch substitute, provided all functional blocks are correctly labelled.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **"With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Throttle Body Injection (TBI) system. Mention any three advantages of TBI over a conventional carburetor."** **[3 Marks]**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 60–80 words):

TBI uses **a single electronically controlled injector** mounted on the throttle body, spraying atomized fuel into the intake manifold just above the throttle plate. The **ECU** computes the required **pulse width** from inputs like **MAP**, **TPS**, **CTS**, and **CKP**, then fires the injector accordingly. The **O₂ sensor** provides closed-loop feedback for precise air-fuel ratio (λ = 1.000) control.

**Three advantages over carburetor:**
1. **Better air-fuel ratio accuracy** under all operating conditions.
2. **Improved cold-start and warm-up driveability** due to ECU-managed enrichment.
3. **Lower emissions** (HC, CO) through closed-loop lambda control.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **"Differentiate between Throttle Body Injection (TBI) and Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) systems. Mention any four points of difference."** **[3 Marks]**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Form Expected):

| Sl. No. | Parameter | TBI | MPFI |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Injector Location** | On throttle body | At each intake port |
| 2 | **Number of Injectors** | One (or two) | One per cylinder (N) |
| 3 | **Fuel Pressure** | 1.0 – 1.5 bar | 3.0 – 4.5 bar |
| 4 | **Fuel Distribution** | Uneven (wall-wetting) | Uniform (port atomization) |
| 5 | **Individual Cyl Trim** | Not possible | Possible |
| 6 | **Cost & Complexity** | Low | Higher |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulation**, Module 2 carries one full 14-mark question with **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part sub-question carries **7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Pattern]**

> **Part (a)** — *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system. Discuss the different injection strategies used in MPFI.* **[7 Marks]**
>
> **Part (b)** — *A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine running at 3000 RPM has a displacement of 2.0 L and volumetric efficiency of 88%. The manifold pressure is 0.6 bar (absolute) and the temperature is 330 K. The fuel injector has a flow rate of 200 cc/min and fuel density is 745 kg/m³. Calculate the injector pulse width required for stoichiometric operation (AFR = 14.7).* **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Working of MPFI (4 Marks):**
In MPFI, **one fuel injector is provided at the intake port of each cylinder**, just upstream of the intake valve. The fuel pump supplies pressurized fuel (3.0 – 4.5 bar) to a **common fuel rail**, from which each injector draws. The **ECU** computes the required fuel mass from inputs of **MAF/MAP**, **TPS**, **CTS**, **IAT**, **CKP**, and **CMP**. The ECU then drives each injector with a precisely timed **pulse width signal** so that the correct mass of atomized fuel is sprayed directly into the port, where it vaporizes rapidly before the intake event.

**Injection Strategies (3 Marks):**
1. **Simultaneous Injection:** All N injectors fire at the same instant, twice per crank revolution. Simplest, lowest-cost, no CMP needed.
2. **Batched Injection:** Injectors fire in 2 (or more) groups. For a 4-cyl, groups are 1-4 and 2-3, fired twice per revolution. Slight improvement over simultaneous.
3. **Sequential (Timed) Injection (SFI):** Each injector fires **once per engine cycle**, precisely phased to the **intake valve opening** of its cylinder. Requires **CMP sensor**. Best transient response, best emissions, supports individual cylinder trim.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks — Full Step-by-Step]:

**Step 1 — Given Data**

$$V_d = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3, \quad \eta_{vol} = 0.88, \quad p_{man} = 0.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

$$T_{man} = 330 \text{ K}, \quad N = 3000 \text{ RPM}, \quad R_{air} = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

$$N_{cyl} = 4, \quad \dot{Q}_{inj} = 200 \text{ cc/min}, \quad \rho_{fuel} = 745 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad AFR = 14.7$$

**Step 2 — Air Mass Per Cycle (Speed-Density)**

$$m_{air} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot p_{man} \cdot N}{2 \cdot R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{0.88 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \times 10^{5} \times (3000/2)}{2 \times 287 \times 330}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{0.88 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.0 \times 10^{4} \times 1500}{189{,}420}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{158{,}400}{189{,}420} = 8.363 \times 10^{-1} \text{ g/cycle}$$

Wait — re-evaluating. Note: the standard formula is $m_{air} = \eta_{vol} V_d p_{man} N / (2 R_{air} T_{man})$, which is the form for **mass per revolution** (one cycle of 4-stroke = 2 revolutions; but here "cycle" often means one revolution's worth of inducted mass). For 4-stroke:

$$m_{air,total\_per\_2rev} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot p_{man}}{R_{air} \cdot T_{man}} = \frac{0.88 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \times 10^{5}}{287 \times 330}$$

$$m_{air,per\_2rev} = \frac{105.6}{94{,}710} = 1.115 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 1.115 \text{ g per 2 rev (4 firing events)}$$

**Step 3 — Air Mass Per Cylinder Per Firing**

$$m_{air,cyl} = \frac{1.115}{4} = 0.2787 \text{ g/cyl·firing}$$

**Step 4 — Fuel Mass Per Cylinder Per Firing**

$$m_{fuel,cyl} = \frac{m_{air,cyl}}{AFR} = \frac{0.2787}{14.7} = 0.01896 \text{ g/cyl·firing}$$

**Step 5 — Injector Mass Flow Rate (SI)**

$$\dot{Q}_{inj} = 200 \text{ cc/min} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{inj} = \dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} \times 745 = 2.483 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Injector Pulse Width**

$$t_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel,cyl}}{\dot{m}_{inj}} = \frac{0.01896 \times 10^{-3}}{2.483 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.636 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{inj} \approx 7.64 \text{ ms}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **Part (a)** — *Explain the concept of closed-loop lambda control in a TBI system. How do STFT and LTFT help maintain λ = 1.000? Describe with a block diagram.* **[7 Marks]**
>
> **Part (b)** — *A TBI injector has a flow rate of 1.5 g/s at 1.2 bar rail pressure. The engine requires 0.06 g of fuel per injection event. Calculate: (i) the open-loop pulse width, (ii) the corrected pulse width if the O₂ sensor reports a sustained lean condition with STFT = +6% and LTFT = +3%, and (iii) the percentage change in effective AFR.* **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Closed-Loop Lambda Control (3 Marks):**
The **O₂ (lambda) sensor** mounted in the exhaust manifold generates a voltage of approximately **0.1 V (lean)** to **0.9 V (rich)**, switching sharply at **λ = 1.000** (stoichiometric). This signal is fed to the ECU, which compares it to an internal reference of 0.45 V. If the mixture is lean, the ECU **increases** the injector pulse width; if rich, it **decreases** the pulse width. This oscillation is the **closed-loop STFT correction**.

**STFT vs LTFT (2 Marks):**
- **STFT (Short-Term Fuel Trim):** A **real-time, rapidly changing** correction (typically ±10%), reset every few seconds. It directly responds to the O₂ sensor signal.
- **LTFT (Long-Term Fuel Trim):** A **slowly learned, persistent** correction (typically ±20%), stored in the ECU's **non-volatile KAM (Keep Alive Memory)**. It compensates for **aging, drift, vacuum leaks**, and **injector wear**.

**Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
ECU → Base Pulse Width Map → STFT Multiplier → LTFT Multiplier → Final Drive Signal → Injector; O₂ sensor → ECU (feedback).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1 — Given Data**
- $\dot{m}_{inj} = 1.5 \text{ g/s}$
- $m_{fuel,req} = 0.06 \text{ g/event}$
- $STFT = +6\%$, $LTFT = +3\%$

**Step 2 — Open-Loop Pulse Width**

$$t_{open} = \frac{m_{fuel,req}}{\dot{m}_{inj}} = \frac{0.06}{1.5} = 0.040 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Closed-Loop Pulse Width**

$$t_{closed} = t_{open} \cdot (1 + STFT + LTFT)$$

$$t_{closed} = 40 \times (1 + 0.06 + 0.03) = 40 \times 1.09 = 43.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Fuel Delivered (Closed-Loop)**

$$m_{fuel,eff} = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot t_{closed} = 1.5 \times 43.6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0654 \text{ g/event}$$

**Step 5 — New Effective AFR**

$$AFR_{eff} = \frac{m_{air,cyl}}{m_{fuel,eff}} = \frac{0.06 \times 14.7}{0.0654} = \frac{0.882}{0.0654} = 13.49$$

**Step 6 — Percentage Change in AFR**

$$\Delta AFR\,\% = \frac{AFR_{eff} - AFR_{stoich}}{AFR_{stoich}} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta AFR\,\% = \frac{13.49 - 14.7}{14.7} \times 100\% = -8.23\%$$

$$\boxed{\Delta AFR\,\% \approx -8.23\% \text{ (rich correction)}}$$

The negative sign confirms the ECU **enriched** the mixture to compensate for the lean condition detected by the O₂ sensor — a classic **rich-shift trim**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for 4-stroke engines** in the speed-density formula: $m_{air} = \eta_{vol} V_d p_{man} N / (2 R_{air} T_{man})$. The "2" in the denominator is non-negotiable. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up units** of injector flow rate (cc/min vs g/s vs kg/s). Always convert to a single SI unit system before dividing. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing STFT and LTFT** as additive percentages on the AFR instead of on the pulse width. The trim is applied to **$t_{open}$**, not to AFR directly. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows or labels**. KTU examiners deduct marks if functional flow direction is unclear. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Failing to mention the CMP sensor** requirement for Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI). Marks lost: **1 mark**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this as your **last-night revision checklist** before the KTU ESE exam. Every line below is a high-yield bullet.

- ⭐ **TBI = Single-Point Injection = Central Fuel Injection** — one (or two) injector(s) on throttle body.
- ⭐ **MPFI = Multi-Point Injection = Port Fuel Injection** — one injector per cylinder, at the port.
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric AFR for petrol = 14.7 : 1**, $\lambda = 1.000$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- ⭐ **TBI fuel pressure ≈ 1.0 – 1.5 bar**; **MPFI fuel pressure ≈ 3.0 – 4.5 bar**; **GDI ≈ 100 – 350 bar**.
- ⭐ The **Speed-Density equation** $m_{air} = \eta_{vol} V_d p_{man} N / (2 R_{air} T_{man})$ is the **single most-tested formula** in this module.
- ⭐ **Injector pulse width** $t_{inj} = m_{fuel} / (\dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel})$ — convert all units to SI.
- ⭐ **Closed-loop correction:** $t_{closed} = t_{open} \cdot (1 + STFT + LTFT)$.
- ⭐ **STFT** is real-time and resets quickly; **LTFT** is learned and persistent (stored in KAM).
- ⭐ **MPFI injection strategies** in increasing order of sophistication: **Simultaneous → Batched → Sequential (SFI)**.
- ⭐ **SFI requires a CMP sensor** for cylinder identification; simultaneous and batched do not.
- ⭐ **MPFI advantages over TBI**: better atomization, no wall-wetting, individual cylinder trim, lower emissions, faster transient response.
- ⭐ **TBI advantages over MPFI**: lower cost, simpler wiring, fewer components, easier diagnostics.
- ⭐ **Duty cycle** $D = t_{inj} / T_{period} \times 100\%$. Values above 80% indicate **injector saturation** and explain WOT power loss.
- ⭐ **Wall-wetting** in TBI is the main cause of cold-start and transient driveability issues.
- ⭐ The **O₂ sensor** switches sharply at λ = 1.000, generating a voltage between **0.1 V (lean)** and **0.9 V (rich)** — the basis of closed-loop control.
- ⭐ For **KAM (Keep Alive Memory)**: store LTFT, learned idle values, and adaptive parameters. Erasing KAM resets the ECU to its factory fuel map.
- ⭐ **Modern vehicles** have all migrated from TBI to MPFI/SFI. TBI is now considered a **legacy technology**, replaced by **GDI** (Gasoline Direct Injection) in newer vehicles.
- ⭐ **In your KTU answer scripts**, always state the assumption of **ideal gas behaviour** when using the speed-density formula. Examiners reward explicit assumptions.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Throttle Body Injection & Multi-Point Fuel Injection System**
> *Total estimated study time: 6–8 hours | KTU ESE weightage: ~14 marks (one full 14-mark question)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
