<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: INJECTION SYSTEM CONTROLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Injection System Controls** refer to the integrated electronic, electro-mechanical, and electro-hydraulic subsystems within an engine management unit (ECU/ECM) that govern the precise metering, atomization, pressurization, and timed delivery of fuel into the combustion chamber of a spark-ignition (SI) or compression-ignition (CI) engine. The objective is to maintain the air-fuel ratio (AFR) at its chemically optimal **stoichiometric value (14.7:1 for gasoline)**, synchronize injection events with the crankshaft position, and dynamically compensate for transient operating conditions such as cold start, acceleration, deceleration, and altitude variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Outcome *CO2 – Analyze electronic fuel injection and ignition subsystems*, the topic *Injection System Controls* mandates understanding of closed-loop / open-loop control, lambda feedback, injection timing modes (synchronous/asynchronous), and pulse-width computation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Syringe"

Imagine you are pouring exactly the right amount of medicine into a patient's IV drip every second, but the patient's metabolism (engine load) keeps changing — sometimes running a marathon (hard acceleration), sometimes sleeping (idle), and sometimes shivering in cold (cold start). You cannot just deliver a *fixed* amount. You need:

- **Sensors** (eyes, fingers) to read heart rate, body temperature, blood oxygen.
- **A controller (brain/ECU)** to compute exactly how much medicine to release.
- **An injector (syringe)** that physically delivers fuel.
- **A feedback sensor (oxygen sensor / lambda probe)** that tells the brain whether too much or too little fuel was delivered last time, so the next shot can be corrected.

This is precisely what an *Injection System Controller* does — it is a **closed-loop, real-time, multi-variable control system** regulating a chemical reaction (combustion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (remember for numerical problems):**
> - Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = **14.7 : 1** (by mass)
> - Stoichiometric AFR for diesel = **14.5 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric AFR for LPG = **15.5 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric AFR for CNG (methane) = **17.2 : 1**
> - Universal Gas Constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Molar mass of air **M_air = 28.97 g/mol**
> - Lambda (λ) = Actual AFR / Stoichiometric AFR, with **λ = 1.000** at stoichiometry.
> - Lower Heating Value of gasoline ≈ **44 MJ/kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of an Injection Control System

An electronic fuel injection (EFI) system is structured into four functional layers, each governed by the ECU:

**Layer 1 — Input (Sensing Layer):**
- Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) → engine speed (N) and piston position.
- Manifold Absolute Pressure sensor (MAP) → engine load.
- Mass Air Flow sensor (MAF) or Air-Flow Meter (vane-type / hot-wire).
- Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) — potentiometric or Hall-effect.
- Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS / CLT).
- Intake Air Temperature Sensor (IAT).
- Oxygen Sensor (Lambda probe, narrow-band or wide-band).
- Cam Position Sensor (CMP) for sequential injection.

**Layer 2 — Computation (ECU Strategy Layer):**
The ECU executes a real-time algorithm combining **base fuel map**, **enrichment maps**, and **feedback corrections**.

**Layer 3 — Output (Actuation Layer):**
- Solenoid or piezo-electric **fuel injector(s)**.
- **Fuel pump relay** and **fuel pressure regulator**.
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** or electronic throttle body.

**Layer 4 — Feedback Layer:**
- **Closed-loop mode:** O2 sensor signal (0.1–0.9 V) toggles ECU between lean (lean correction → +) and rich (→ −) trim. The **long-term fuel trim (LTFT)** and **short-term fuel trim (STFT)** are stored in adaptive memory.

### 2.2 Control Strategies — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop

| Feature | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | ECU computes injection based on pre-calibrated 3-D maps (RPM × Load) without feedback. | ECU uses O2 sensor feedback to continuously correct fuel delivery. |
| **Operating regime** | Cold start, wide-open throttle (WOT), deceleration fuel cut-off (DFCO), high-load enrichment. | Steady cruise, idle, light load — where λ ≈ 1 must be tightly held. |
| **Accuracy** | ±10% AFR error tolerable. | ±1% AFR error achievable. |
| **KTU Board Weightage** | **High (3–7 marks)** | **High (3–7 marks)** |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application in Injection Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{\text{actual}}}{AFR_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio dimensionless | — | Lambda feedback regulation |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Mass flow rate of fuel required | kg/s | Base fuel computation |
| 3 | $T_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, per cycle}}}{q_{\text{inj}} \cdot N / 2}$ | Injection pulse width | seconds (ms) | Direct injector actuation time |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot P_{\text{man}} \cdot M_{\text{air}}}{R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}$ | Air mass per cycle (Speed-Density) | kg | Load-sensing fuel strategy |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{\text{air, hot-wire}} = K_{\text{HW}} \cdot \dfrac{V_s \cdot \Delta T}{R_s}$ | King's Law (hot-wire MAF) | kg/s | MAF-based fuel strategy |
| 6 | $\eta_v = f(N,\, P_{\text{man}},\, T_{\text{man}})$ | Volumetric efficiency map | fraction | Look-up table input |
| 7 | $AFR_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{2\,(M_{\text{O}_2}/M_{\text{CH}_n}) \cdot 32/M_{\text{CH}_n} \cdot M_{\text{air}}}{...}$ | Theoretical derivation | ratio | Stoichiometry |
| 8 | $T_{\text{inj,total}} = T_{\text{base}} \cdot F_{\text{cool}} \cdot F_{\text{air}} \cdot F_{\text{accel}} \cdot F_{\text{bat}} \cdot F_{\text{STFT}}$ | Compensated pulse-width | ms | Real-time fuel strategy |
| 9 | $P_{\text{rail,CR}} = P_{\text{min}} + \Delta P_{\text{dyn}}$ | Common rail pressure regulation | bar | Diesel injection control |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{pilot}} + Q_{\text{main}} + Q_{\text{post}} = Q_{\text{total}}$ | Multi-stage diesel injection summation | mm³/stroke | Diesel pilot/post injection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In KTU exams, students lose marks when they confuse $\lambda$ with AFR. $\lambda = 1$ means stoichiometric. $\lambda < 1$ means **rich mixture** (power); $\lambda > 1$ means **lean mixture** (economy/NOx reduction).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Injection system controls form the cornerstone of every modern OEM powertrain because they directly govern:
- **Emissions compliance** (Euro 6/BS-VI) — closed-loop lambda control keeps the **three-way catalytic converter (TWC)** at peak efficiency.
- **Fuel economy** — precisely metered injection optimizes indicated thermal efficiency.
- **Driveability** — transient enrichments prevent hesitation and stalling.
- **Cold-start emissions** — the **flex-fuel compensator** enriches AFR when CHT < 40 °C.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Gasoline (C₈H₁₈)

We treat gasoline as **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)** for chemical balance. Complete combustion in dry air:

$$
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a \left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + d\,\text{N}_2
$$

**Step 1 — Carbon balance:**

Atoms of C in reactants = atoms in products.

$$
1 \cdot 8 = b \cdot 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; b = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen balance:**

Atoms of H in reactants = atoms in products.

$$
1 \cdot 18 = c \cdot 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; c = 9
$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen balance:**

Atoms of O in reactants = atoms in products (only O₂ contributes).

$$
2a = 2b + c
$$

$$
2a = 2(8) + 9 = 25 \;\Longrightarrow\; a = 12.5
$$

**Step 4 — Nitrogen balance (inert):**

$$
d = 3.76 \cdot a = 3.76 \times 12.5 = 47.0
$$

So the balanced equation is:

$$
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47.0\,\text{N}_2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass of air required:**

Mass of air = mass of O₂ + mass of N₂.

$$
m_{\text{air}} = a \cdot M_{\text{O}_2} + a \cdot 3.76 \cdot M_{\text{N}_2}
$$

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 12.5 \times 32 + 12.5 \times 3.76 \times 28
$$

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 400.0 + 1316.0 = 1716.0 \;\text{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute mass of fuel:**

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = 8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 96 + 18 = 114 \;\text{g}
$$

**Step 7 — Stoichiometric AFR:**

$$
AFR_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{1716.0}{114.0} = 15.05
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The accepted textbook value of **14.7 : 1** is averaged across paraffinic, olefinic, and aromatic real-gasoline fractions. KTU examiners typically expect the student to write **"14.7 for gasoline, 14.5 for diesel"** in the answer.

### 3.2 Derivation: Injection Pulse-Width (T_inj) from Speed-Density Method

**Given:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder SI engine, displacement $V_d = 1.6$ L, MAP $P_{\text{man}} = 0.5$ bar (absolute), manifold temperature $T_{\text{man}} = 320$ K, $\eta_v = 0.85$, target $\lambda = 1.0$, stoichiometric AFR = 14.7, injector static flow $q_{\text{inj}} = 200$ cc/min at 3 bar, engine speed $N = 3000$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Air mass per cylinder per cycle (Speed-Density):**

$$
m_{\text{air, cyl}} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot P_{\text{man}} \cdot M_{\text{air}}}{R \cdot T_{\text{man}} \cdot n_{\text{cyl}}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{air, cyl}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{5} \cdot 0.02897}{8.314 \cdot 320 \cdot 4}
$$

$$
m_{\text{air, cyl}} = \frac{1.970 \times 10^{0}}{1.0642 \times 10^{4}} = 1.852 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg/cycle/cyl}
$$

**Step 2 — Required fuel mass per cycle (λ = 1):**

$$
m_{\text{fuel, cyl}} = \frac{m_{\text{air, cyl}}}{AFR_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{fuel, cyl}} = \frac{1.852 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 1.260 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kg/cycle/cyl}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert fuel mass to volume using gasoline density (ρ = 745 kg/m³):**

$$
V_{\text{fuel, cyl}} = \frac{m_{\text{fuel, cyl}}}{\rho_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{1.260 \times 10^{-5}}{745} = 1.692 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Convert injector flow rate to SI:**

$$
q_{\text{inj}} = 200\;\text{cc/min} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{60}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Pulse-width:**

$$
T_{\text{inj}} = \frac{V_{\text{fuel, cyl}}}{q_{\text{inj}}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{inj}} = \frac{1.692 \times 10^{-8}}{3.333 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.076 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} = 5.076\;\text{ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The ECU will typically round this to ~5.1 ms and may apply compensation multipliers: $T_{\text{inj, final}} = T_{\text{base}} \cdot F_{\text{cool}} \cdot F_{\text{air}} \cdot F_{\text{accel}} \cdot F_{\text{bat}} \cdot F_{\text{STFT}}$, where each factor lies between 0.8 and 1.5.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hot-Wire MAF Output (King's Law)

The hot-wire mass air flow sensor works by maintaining a constant temperature difference $\Delta T$ between a heated sensing element and the incoming air. The heat loss $Q$ to the airflow is:

$$
Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

The convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$ follows King's Law:

$$
h = a + b \cdot \sqrt{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}
$$

For a constant $\Delta T$, electrical power input $P_e$ balances convective loss:

$$
P_e = I^2 R_s = (a + b\sqrt{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}) \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

Solving for the mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \left( \frac{P_e - a \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{b \cdot A \cdot \Delta T} \right)^{2}
$$

The ECU then uses this $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$ directly in the fuel equation (Equation 2 in the Formula Sheet) — this is the **MAF-based strategy**, preferred for its directness, but vulnerable to MAF contamination.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Control Strategies

| Parameter | **Alpha-N (TPS × RPM)** | **Speed-Density (MAP × RPM)** | **MAF (Airflow Sensor)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Sensor | TPS + CKP | MAP + IAT + CKP | MAF + CKP |
| Air-Mass Equation | Implicit (3-D map) | $P_{\text{man}} \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v / (R\,T_{\text{man}})$ | Direct from sensor |
| Accuracy at Low Load | Poor (TPS noisy) | Good | Excellent |
| Accuracy at High Load | Excellent (TPS steady) | Good (η_v error) | Excellent |
| Transient Response | Slow | Fast (uses MAP) | Fastest |
| Back-pressure Sensitivity | None | Moderate (uses MAP) | Sensitive |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical Application | Race / Motorsport | Most OEM port injection | Premium OEM, GDI |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Lambda Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Pedal Input] --> B[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    A1[Crankshaft Position] --> C[CKP Sensor]
    A2[Manifold Air] --> D[MAP Sensor]
    A3[Coolant Temp] --> E[CLT Sensor]
    A4[Exhaust Oxygen] --> F[Lambda O2 Sensor]

    B --> ECU
    C --> ECU
    D --> ECU
    E --> ECU
    F --> ECU
    ECU[ECU / Engine Control Module]

    ECU --> G1[Base Fuel Map Lookup]
    G1 --> G2[Coolant Enrichment F_cool]
    G2 --> G3[Air Temp Correction F_air]
    G3 --> G4[Acceleration Enrichment F_accel]
    G4 --> G5[Battery Voltage Correction F_bat]
    G5 --> G6[STFT Lambda Trim]
    G6 --> H[Pulse Width T_inj]
    H --> I[Fuel Injector Solenoid]
    I --> J[Intake Manifold Combustion]
    J --> K[Exhaust Gas]
    K --> F

    ECU --> L[Fuel Pump Relay]
    L --> M[Electric Fuel Pump]
    M --> N[Pressurized Fuel Rail]
    N --> I
```

### 4.2 Multi-Stage Diesel Common-Rail Injection Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECU Receives Accelerator Pedal and CKP] --> B[Compute Q_total from Rail Pressure and Maps]
    B --> C1[Pilot Injection Q_pilot]
    B --> C2[Main Injection Q_main]
    B --> C3[Post Injection Q_post]
    C1 --> D1[SOI_pilot at -8 to -12 deg BTDC]
    C2 --> D2[SOI_main at 0 to 4 deg BTDC]
    C3 --> D3[SOI_post at 60 to 120 deg ATDC]
    D1 --> E[Common Rail Pressure 250 to 2000 bar]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Pressure Regulator SCV on HP Pump]
    E --> G[Piezo Injector Actuation]
    G --> H[Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I[Pressure Sensor in Rail Feedback]
    I --> A
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Operating Condition] --> B{Is Engine Cold Start?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Open Loop F_cool Enrichment]
    B -- No --> D{Is Throttle WOT?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Open Loop Power Enrichment Lambda 0.78 to 0.85]
    D -- No --> F{Is Deceleration Fuel Cutoff Active?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Open Loop Zero Injection]
    F -- No --> H[Closed Loop Lambda Target 1.000]
    H --> I{O2 Sensor Warmed Up and in Range?}
    I -- Yes --> J[STFT and LTFT Active]
    I -- No --> K[Open Loop Default Map]
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning Chain of Narrow-Band Lambda Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Exhaust Gas in Tailpipe] --> B[Zirconia Lambda Probe 0.1V Lean 0.9V Rich]
    B --> C[Reference Comparator 0.45V Threshold]
    C --> D[Inverting Amplifier]
    D --> E[Low Pass Filter 1Hz Cutoff]
    E --> F[ECU ADC Input Channel]
    F --> G[STFT Increments or Decrements]
    G --> H[Adaptive LTFT Memory]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Equivalence Ratio (λ)" and state its value at stoichiometric, rich, and lean operating conditions of a gasoline engine.**

**Model Answer:**

The equivalence ratio (lambda) is defined as the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the chemically correct stoichiometric air-fuel ratio:

$$
\lambda = \frac{AFR_{\text{actual}}}{AFR_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

- At stoichiometry: **λ = 1.000** (AFR = 14.7 for gasoline)
- Rich mixture: **λ < 1.000** (excess fuel, e.g., λ = 0.85 for WOT)
- Lean mixture: **λ > 1.000** (excess air, e.g., λ = 1.05 for cruise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Mark Tip:** Always mention the stoichiometric AFR value along with λ. Examiners penalize missing units/context.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three major differences between open-loop and closed-loop control strategies used in electronic fuel injection systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | No feedback sensor required. | Uses oxygen (lambda) sensor feedback. |
| 2 | Used during cold start, WOT, DFCO. | Used during idle, cruise, light load. |
| 3 | AFR accuracy ±10% acceptable. | AFR accuracy ±1% achievable. |
| 4 | Operates from pre-calibrated 3-D maps. | ECU computes STFT and LTFT adaptively. |
| 5 | Faster computation, no sensor lag. | Slower due to feedback delay (~1 s). |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **closed-loop electronic fuel injection system** based on the speed-density method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a displacement of 1.8 L, running at 3500 rpm. The MAP sensor reads 0.6 bar (absolute), manifold air temperature is 340 K, volumetric efficiency is 88%, injector flow rate is 220 cc/min, and the target lambda is 1.0. Compute the **fuel injection pulse width per cylinder per cycle** in milliseconds. Take gasoline density as 745 kg/m³. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
A speed-density EFI system computes the air mass entering each cylinder using the **ideal gas equation** based on MAP, IAT, displacement, and volumetric efficiency. The fuel injector pulse width is then derived by dividing the required fuel mass by the injector flow rate.

**Working (5 marks):**

1. **Sensing Stage:** MAP sensor measures $P_{\text{man}}$ (absolute, bar), IAT sensor measures $T_{\text{man}}$ (K), CKP sensor determines $N$ (rpm) and crank angle, CLT measures coolant temperature.

2. **Air-Mass Computation:** ECU applies the speed-density equation:
$$
m_{\text{air, cyl}} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot P_{\text{man}} \cdot M_{\text{air}}}{R \cdot T_{\text{man}} \cdot n_{\text{cyl}}}
$$

3. **Base Fuel Calculation:** Using $\lambda = 1$ and stoichiometric AFR = 14.7:
$$
m_{\text{fuel, base}} = \frac{m_{\text{air, cyl}}}{14.7}
$$

4. **Correction Multipliers:** ECU multiplies base fuel by $F_{\text{cool}}, F_{\text{air}}, F_{\text{accel}}, F_{\text{bat}}$ from look-up tables stored in ROM.

5. **Pulse-Width Generation:** ECU generates a 5–12 ms square wave to the **solenoid injector**, which delivers atomized fuel upstream of the intake valve (port injection).

6. **Feedback Stage:** The downstream O2 sensor (heated, narrow-band) produces a voltage flipping between 0.1 V (lean) and 0.9 V (rich). ECU adds **STFT** of ±10% and updates **LTFT** adaptive memory.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above) — should be drawn for full marks.**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**

$V_d = 1.8\;\text{L} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3$, $N = 3500\;\text{rpm}$, $P_{\text{man}} = 0.6\;\text{bar} = 0.6 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa}$, $T_{\text{man}} = 340\;\text{K}$, $\eta_v = 0.88$, $n_{\text{cyl}} = 4$, $q_{\text{inj}} = 220\;\text{cc/min}$, $\lambda = 1.0$, $\rho_{\text{fuel}} = 745\;\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Air mass per cylinder per cycle:**

$$
m_{\text{air, cyl}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.88 \times 0.6 \times 10^{5} \times 0.02897}{8.314 \times 340 \times 4}
$$

$$
m_{\text{air, cyl}} = \frac{2.7544}{11307} = 2.436 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel mass per cylinder per cycle (λ = 1):**

$$
m_{\text{fuel, cyl}} = \frac{2.436 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 1.657 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel volume per cylinder per cycle:**

$$
V_{\text{fuel, cyl}} = \frac{1.657 \times 10^{-5}}{745} = 2.225 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Injector flow rate in SI:**

$$
q_{\text{inj}} = \frac{220 \times 10^{-6}}{60} = 3.667 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Pulse width:**

$$
T_{\text{inj}} = \frac{V_{\text{fuel, cyl}}}{q_{\text{inj}}} = \frac{2.225 \times 10^{-8}}{3.667 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.067 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} = \mathbf{6.07\;\text{ms}}
$$

**Final Answer:** The required injector pulse width is **≈ 6.07 ms per cylinder per cycle**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **multi-stage injection strategy** in a modern common-rail diesel engine. Explain pilot, main, and post injections with their typical crank-angle timings. (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between **port fuel injection (PFI)**, **direct injection (GDI)**, and **common-rail direct injection (CRDI)** systems. State at least four distinguishing parameters. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

A **Common-Rail Diesel Injection (CRDI)** system uses a high-pressure accumulator (rail) maintained at 250–2000 bar by a radial-piston pump. The ECU controls a **piezo-electric injector** to dispense fuel in multiple precisely-timed events per combustion cycle:

1. **Pilot Injection (Pre-Injection):**
   - Crank angle: **−8° to −12° BTDC**.
   - Quantity: **1–4 mm³** per stroke.
   - Purpose: Pre-heat combustion chamber, reduce combustion noise (diesel knock), lower peak pressure rise rate, reduce **NOx and soot**.

2. **Main Injection:**
   - Crank angle: **0° to +4° ATDC** (TDC region).
   - Quantity: **Bulk of fuel** (10–50 mm³).
   - Purpose: Main energy release for torque output. ECU varies timing and quantity with load/speed for optimal BSFC.

3. **Post Injection (After-Injection):**
   - Crank angle: **+60° to +120° ATDC**.
   - Quantity: **1–5 mm³**.
   - Purpose: Raise exhaust temperature to regenerate the **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)**, and enable **NOx reduction in SCR** by providing unburned HC for thermal management.

4. **Optionally: Late Post-Injection** for further DPF regeneration at +200° ATDC.

The ECU determines **Q_total = Q_pilot + Q_main + Q_post** from pedal position, CKP, and rail pressure feedback.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

| Parameter | **PFI (Port FI)** | **GDI (Gasoline DI)** | **CRDI (Diesel Common-Rail)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Injection Location | Intake port (upstream of intake valve) | Directly into cylinder | Directly into cylinder |
| Injection Pressure | 3–5 bar | 100–350 bar | 1500–2000 bar |
| Fuel Type | Gasoline | Gasoline | Diesel |
| Atomization | Wall-guided / spray-guided | Spray-guided | Spray-guided / wall-guided |
| Combustion Mode | Homogeneous (λ≈1) | Stratified (lean) or homogeneous | Diffusion (compression ignition) |
| Pilot/Post Injection | No (single event) | Limited (multi-hole) | Yes (pilot, main, post) |
| Cold Start | Easy (port pre-warming) | Difficult (wall wetting) | Excellent (glow plug assist) |
| Typical Application | Conventional SI engines | Modern downsized SI engines | BS-VI / Euro-VI diesel engines |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark-Deduction Causes):**
> 1. **Confusing port injection (PFI) with GDI:** PFI injects upstream of the intake valve; GDI injects directly into the cylinder. Many students mix this up — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing λ < 1 and λ > 1 directions:** Always state "rich" for λ < 1 and "lean" for λ > 1 explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert injector flow rate from cc/min to m³/s** in pulse-width numericals — this is a 1-mark deduction trap.
> 4. **Omitting the closed-loop block diagram** when the question says "with neat block diagram" — examiners allocate 2 marks for the diagram alone.
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of the three-way catalytic converter** in the closed-loop system — it's the reason closed-loop keeps λ = 1.000 ± 0.01.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **AFR Stoichiometric Values:** Gasoline = 14.7, Diesel = 14.5, LPG = 15.5, CNG = 17.2, Methanol = 6.4, Ethanol = 9.0.
- **Lambda = 1** ⇒ Stoichiometric; **λ < 1** ⇒ Rich; **λ > 1** ⇒ Lean.
- **Speed-Density equation** uses **MAP, IAT, displacement, η_v** — preferred for cost-sensitive OEM applications.
- **MAF (hot-wire)** measures air directly using King's Law — most accurate but contamination-prone.
- **Alpha-N** uses TPS × RPM — fastest transient response, used in motorsport.
- **Closed-loop** is enabled only when the O2 sensor reaches ~300 °C (heated lambda probe).
- **STFT** = Short Term Fuel Trim (immediate, ±10% per second).
- **LTFT** = Long Term Fuel Trim (slow adaptive, ±25% over minutes).
- **DFCO** = Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off, active when TPS = 0% and RPM > 1500.
- **Cold-start enrichment** can push AFR to **λ = 0.6** at −20 °C ambient.
- **WOT enrichment** targets **λ ≈ 0.78–0.85** for maximum power (air-fuel ratio 11.5:1 for gasoline).
- **Common-rail diesel** uses 1500–2000 bar rail pressure and **pilot + main + post** injection strategy.
- **DPF regeneration** is achieved by **post-injection** raising exhaust temperature to 600 °C.
- **GDI stratified charge** operates at λ = 1.6–3.0 in light load for fuel economy.
- **Injector pulse width** is typically 2–15 ms in port injection, 0.5–3 ms in GDI.
- **Piezo injectors** can execute up to **7 injection events per cycle** (vs. 3 for solenoid).
- **Fuel rail pressure** must be maintained within **±2%** of setpoint for repeatable injection quantity.
- **WUE** = Warm-Up Enrichment factor, $F_{\text{WUE}} = 1.4$ at 0 °C → 1.0 at 80 °C coolant.
- **Battery voltage correction** compensates for injector solenoid's slower response at low V_bat.
- **TWC** = Three-Way Catalytic Converter — reduces CO, HC, NOx simultaneously only when λ = 1.000 ± 0.01.
- **OBD-II readiness flags** for catalyst, O2 sensor, EGR, EVAP are all gated by closed-loop fuel control activation.
- **Typical exam traps:** using R = 287 J/(kg·K) for dry air (correct) vs. R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) (also correct, but units must be consistent).
- **Closed-loop catalyst efficiency** requires the O2 sensor to be **after** the TWC as well as **before** it (post-cat O2 sensor monitors O₂ storage).
- **Knock sensor** is not part of fuel injection but integrates with injection **retard** strategy on SI engines.
- **Start of Injection (SOI)** in diesel is mapped against **rail pressure × RPM × pedal position** in a 3-D map.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantage of Electronic Ignition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantage of Electronic Ignition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

An **Electronic Ignition System (EIS)** is an automotive ignition architecture in which the primary circuit switching (interrupting the current through the ignition coil's primary winding) is performed by **solid-state semiconductor devices** — such as **bipolar junction transistors (BJT)**, **Darlingtons**, or **Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs)** — rather than by mechanically operated **breaker contact points**. The trigger signal for the semiconductor switch is derived from sensors (magnetic pick-up, Hall-effect, optical, or ECU-controlled) that detect the engine's crankshaft / camshaft position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** In the context of the *Autotronics* module, "advantage" is evaluated not just as a feature list, but in terms of measurable engineering parameters — secondary voltage, spark energy, timing accuracy, emissions, and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are flicking a light switch on and off **5,000 times per minute** using your fingers. After a few hours, your fingers get tired, the contacts wear out, sparks appear at the switch, and the timing becomes irregular.

Now imagine a **silent, invisible electronic robot** doing the same switching — perfectly timed, never tired, never wearing out, and capable of producing a much stronger "flick" each time. That robot is your **electronic ignition switch** (transistor or IGBT), and the light is the spark inside the engine cylinder.

In short:
- **Mechanical points** = tired fingers
- **Transistor / IGBT** = tireless, precise electronic robot

---

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Conventional (Points) | Electronic Ignition |
|---|---|---|
| Secondary Voltage | **15 – 20 kV** | **30 – 40 kV** |
| Spark Duration | **~ 0.5 ms** | **1 – 2 ms (multi-spark)** |
| Dwell Angle | **Fixed (mechanical)** | **Variable / ECU-controlled** |
| Contact Bounce | **Present** | **Eliminated** |
| Service Interval | **~ 20,000 km** | **> 1,00,000 km** |
| Ignition Energy | **20 – 30 mJ** | **40 – 100+ mJ** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **secondary voltage** of an inductive ignition system is governed by **Faraday's Law** of electromagnetic induction, where the rate of change of flux linkage $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ directly controls the induced EMF.

---

### Why "Advantage" is a Quantitative Topic (Not Just Descriptive)

In KTU 2024 scheme evaluations, the word *advantage* is treated as an **engineering performance comparison** against the baseline (conventional Kettering ignition). Each advantage must be justified with a *cause-effect* link, e.g., *elimination of contact bounce → stable secondary voltage → complete combustion → reduced HC emissions*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Physics of Why Electronic Ignition "Wins"

The fundamental physics of an inductive ignition system is governed by the energy stored in the primary coil's magnetic field when the switch is closed, and the rapid collapse of that field when the switch is opened. The energy available at the spark plug is:

$$E_{spark} = \frac{1}{2} \, L_p \, I_p^2$$

Where:
- $L_p$ = inductance of the primary winding (typically **5 – 8 mH**)
- $I_p$ = primary current at the moment of switch opening (limited by ballast resistor / ECU)

The induced secondary voltage is given by Faraday's law:

$$V_s = -N_s \, \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

Where $N_s$ is the secondary turns count (typically **10,000 – 25,000 turns** for a step-up ratio of ~1:100).

---

### 2.2 Eight Structural Advantages — Cause-Effect Analysis

#### Advantage 1 — Higher Secondary Voltage (Stronger Spark)
- **Cause:** Transistors switch off **faster** (in microseconds) than mechanical points (in milliseconds). The faster $dI/dt$ produces a **higher $d\Phi/dt$**, hence higher $V_s$.
- **Effect:** Confidently ignites lean / EGR-diluted / high-RON fuel-air mixtures, allowing better fuel economy.

#### Advantage 2 — Increased Spark Energy & Duration
- **Cause:** Dwell angle is electronically optimized. The system holds primary current at saturation level for just the right duration.
- **Effect:** Spark lasts **2 – 4 times longer**, ensuring complete combustion of the air-fuel mixture, especially at idle and cruise.

#### Advantage 3 — Accurate, Adaptive Ignition Timing
- **Cause:** Electronic modules (or full ECUs) can compute ideal timing using inputs from **crankshaft position sensor**, **MAP sensor**, **MAF sensor**, **knock sensor**, **coolant temperature sensor**.
- **Effect:** Optimum spark advance at every operating point → **5 – 12 %** improvement in fuel efficiency and **~ 15 – 25 %** reduction in tailpipe emissions.

#### Advantage 4 — Elimination of Contact Point Wear
- **Cause:** No mechanical rubbing contacts. Wear is reduced to zero in the high-current switching path.
- **Effect:** **No periodic contact-point replacement**, no dwell angle adjustment, lower long-term maintenance cost.

#### Advantage 5 — Elimination of Contact Bounce
- **Cause:** Mechanical points "bounce" momentarily on opening, producing multiple low-energy sparks before settling.
- **Effect:** A **single, clean, high-energy spark** per firing event — reduces misfires and RF interference.

#### Advantage 6 — Better High-Speed Performance
- **Cause:** At 6000+ RPM, mechanical points cannot follow the rapid cam profile reliably. Transistors switch in **< 10 μs**.
- **Effect:** Reliable ignition up to **8000 – 10,000 RPM** — supports modern high-revving engines.

#### Advantage 7 — Reduced Emissions
- **Cause:** Complete combustion + accurate timing = lower unburnt **HC**, **CO**, and **NOx**.
- **Effect:** Compliance with **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and equivalent Euro norms.

#### Advantage 8 — Compatibility with Engine Management Systems
- **Cause:** Output is a clean square-wave digital signal suitable for ECU processing.
- **Effect:** Enables features like **knock control**, **cold-start enrichment**, **idling stabilization**, and **integrated OBD-II diagnostics**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spark Energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | Energy delivered to spark plug | **mJ** |
| 2 | Secondary Voltage | $V_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Voltage step-up in secondary | **kV** |
| 3 | Turns Ratio | $n = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Step-up ratio (typical **~ 1:100**) | dimensionless |
| 4 | Dwell Time | $t_d = \dfrac{\theta_d \cdot 60}{360 \cdot N}$ | Duration of primary current buildup | **ms** |
| 5 | Primary Time Constant | $\tau_p = \dfrac{L_p}{R_p}$ | Current rise time constant | **ms** |
| 6 | Spark Duration | $t_s = \dfrac{L_s}{R_s}$ | Duration of spark discharge | **μs – ms** |
| 7 | Ignition Advance | $\theta_a = f(N, \text{Load}, T, \text{Knock})$ | Variable spark advance function | **° BTDC** |
| 8 | Switching Loss | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{ce} I_p t_f f_{sw}$ | Heat dissipated in transistor | **W** |

> [!NOTE]
> All boundary conditions assume: 12 V nominal battery, **firing frequency** = $\dfrac{N \cdot \text{cyl}}{2 \cdot 60}$ Hz for a 4-stroke engine, and ambient temperature $\le 125^\circ C$ for the IGBT junction.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Electronic Ignition Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Passenger cars** | Fuel economy, BS-VI compliance, smooth idle |
| **Motorcycles** | Compact CDI modules, high-RPM operation |
| **Racing / F1** | Coil-on-Plug (COP), up to **10,000 RPM** operation |
| **Aerospace piston engines** | Reliable high-altitude ignition with lean mixtures |
| **Standby Gensets** | Auto-start reliability, low maintenance |
| **Marine outboards** | Corrosion-resistant, no contact erosion from humidity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why a Faster Switch Produces Higher Secondary Voltage

We start from the primary circuit's energy storage in the coil and apply Faraday's Law.

**Step 1 — Primary current at the moment of opening (steady state):**
For an RL circuit with DC supply $V_b$, after a long time, the inductor behaves like a short circuit (ignoring ballast). The primary current is:

$$I_p = \frac{V_b}{R_p}$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic flux linkage in primary winding:**
$$\Phi_p = L_p \cdot I_p = L_p \cdot \frac{V_b}{R_p}$$

**Step 3 — Rate of change of flux linkage at the instant of switch opening:**
The flux collapses from $\Phi_p$ to **0** in a time $t_{off}$ (the switch-off / collapse time).

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\frac{\Phi_p}{t_{off}} = -\frac{L_p \, V_b}{R_p \cdot t_{off}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Faraday's Law to the secondary winding:**
$$V_s = -N_s \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the derivative:**
$$V_s = N_s \cdot \frac{L_p \, V_b}{R_p \cdot t_{off}}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the transformer turns ratio:**
The primary and secondary are wound on the same magnetic core, so $\Phi_{primary} = \Phi_{secondary}$ (same flux). The secondary voltage can also be expressed as:

$$V_s = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_p \cdot \frac{\tau_{build}}{t_{off}}$$

Where $\tau_{build}$ is the current build-up time before the switch opens, and $t_{off}$ is the collapse time.

**Step 7 — Compare the two systems quantitatively:**

$$\frac{V_{s,\text{electronic}}}{V_{s,\text{conventional}}} = \frac{t_{off,\text{conventional}}}{t_{off,\text{electronic}}}$$

**Step 8 — Plug in real-world values:**

- Mechanical points: $t_{off,\text{conv}} \approx 1 \times 10^{-3}$ s = **1 ms**
- Transistor / IGBT: $t_{off,\text{elec}} \approx 1 \times 10^{-6}$ s = **1 μs**

**Step 9 — Calculate the ratio:**

$$\frac{V_{s,\text{electronic}}}{V_{s,\text{conventional}}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 1000$$

**Step 10 — Reality check with non-idealities:**
In practice, secondary voltage is limited by arcing inside the distributor cap (~ **35 kV**) and losses. So the effective ratio is around **2× to 2.5×**, not 1000×, giving the well-known **30 – 40 kV** for electronic systems versus **15 – 20 kV** for mechanical points.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Quantitative Comparison Table

| Performance Parameter | Mechanical (Kettering) Points | Transistor-Assisted Ignition (TAI) | Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI) | Distributor-Less (DLI / COP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Secondary Voltage** | 15 – 20 kV | 30 – 35 kV | 35 – 45 kV | 35 – 45 kV |
| **Spark Energy** | 20 – 30 mJ | 40 – 60 mJ | 50 – 100 mJ | 60 – 120 mJ |
| **Spark Duration** | ~ 0.5 ms | ~ 1.5 ms | ~ 0.1 ms (sharp) | ~ 2 ms (multi-spark) |
| **Dwell Control** | Fixed | Variable | Fixed (CDI) | ECU-controlled |
| **Contact Wear** | High (every 20k km) | None | None | None |
| **High-Speed Limit** | ~ 5500 RPM | ~ 7500 RPM | ~ 10,000 RPM | ~ 9000 RPM |
| **Emission Compliance** | BS-II / pre-Euro | BS-IV | BS-IV | BS-VI / Euro 6 |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Reverse Polarity Protection** | Not needed | Required | Required | Required (ECU) |
| **Common Use** | Vintage bikes / cars | 1980s – 90s cars | Two-wheelers | Modern cars |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU question asks *"Why does CDI give a sharper spark but shorter duration?"* — the answer lies in the energy source: CDI stores energy in a **capacitor** ($E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$), discharging it almost instantly, while inductive systems (TAI, DLI) discharge the **coil's magnetic energy** over a longer time.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Solved)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine runs at 3000 RPM. The ignition coil has $L_p = 6$ mH and primary resistance $R_p = 3 \Omega$ (cold). Battery voltage = 12 V.

**(a) Find the primary current at the moment of opening (after saturation).**
$$I_p = \frac{V_b}{R_p} = \frac{12}{3} = 4 \text{ A}$$

**(b) Calculate the energy stored in the primary winding.**
$$E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 4^2 = 0.048 \text{ J} = 48 \text{ mJ}$$

**(c) If the turns ratio is 1:200 and the switch-off time is 1 μs, find the peak secondary voltage.**
$$V_s = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_p \cdot \frac{\tau_{build}}{t_{off}} \approx 200 \times 12 \times \frac{1\text{ ms}}{1\, \mu s} \cdot \frac{1}{1000} \text{ (saturation correction)}$$

The simplified result, accounting for losses and saturation, yields $\approx$ **30 kV**, which is consistent with typical electronic ignition outputs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modern Electronic Ignition System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the signal and power flow inside a typical electronically controlled ignition system. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric, and labels contain only raw uppercase text (no markdown formatting) to remain safe under all Mermaid renderers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    batt1[BATTERY 12V DC]
    ignSwitch[IGNITION SWITCH]
    ecmUnit[ENGINE CONTROL UNIT ECU]
    crankSensor[CRANKSHAFT POSITION SENSOR]
    camSensor[CAMSHAFT POSITION SENSOR]
    mapSensor[MANIFOLD ABSOLUTE PRESSURE SENSOR]
    knockSensor[KNOCK SENSOR]
    coolantSens[COOLANT TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
    driverStage[IGNITION DRIVER MODULE IGBT GATE DRIVER]
    coilA[IGNITION COIL PRIMARY WINDING]
    coilB[IGNITION COIL SECONDARY WINDING]
    sparkA[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 1]
    sparkB[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 2]
    sparkC[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 3]
    sparkD[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 4]
    feedbackPath[FEEDBACK KNOCK AND MISFIRE SIGNAL]

    batt1 --> ignSwitch
    ignSwitch --> ecmUnit
    ignSwitch --> driverStage
    crankSensor --> ecmUnit
    camSensor --> ecmUnit
    mapSensor --> ecmUnit
    coolantSens --> ecmUnit
    knockSensor --> feedbackPath
    feedbackPath --> ecmUnit
    ecmUnit --> driverStage
    driverStage --> coilA
    coilA --> coilB
    coilB --> sparkA
    coilB --> sparkB
    coilB --> sparkC
    coilB --> sparkD
```

---

### 4.2 Comparative Flow — Conventional vs Electronic Ignition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[CONVENTIONAL KETTERING SYSTEM]
        a1[CAM OPERATED BREAKER POINTS] --> a2[IGNITION COIL PRIMARY]
        a2 --> a3[CONDENSER 0.2 TO 0.3 MICROFARAD]
        a3 --> a1
        a2 --> a4[DISTRIBUTOR ROTOR]
        a4 --> a5[SPARK PLUGS]
    end

    subgraph B[ELECTRONIC IGNITION SYSTEM]
        b1[CRANKSHAFT OR HALL SENSOR] --> b2[ECU OR IGNITION MODULE]
        b2 --> b3[IGBT OR DARLINGTON TRANSISTOR]
        b3 --> b4[IGNITION COIL PRIMARY]
        b4 --> b5[SECONDARY WINDING]
        b5 --> b6[SPARK PLUGS DIRECT OR VIA DISTRIBUTOR]
    end

    A -.REPLACED BY.-> B
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Electronic Ignition Type to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startQ[APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS]
    q1{IS COST THE PRIMARY CONSTRAINT?}
    q2{IS THE ENGINE 2 STROKE OR 2 CYLINDER?}
    q3{IS MAXIMUM RPM ABOVE 7000?}
    q4{IS EMISSION COMPLIANCE BS VI MANDATORY?}
    out1[USE TAI]
    out2[USE CDI]
    out3[USE DLI OR COP]
    out4[USE COIL ON PLUG WITH ECU]

    startQ --> q1
    q1 -->|YES| q2
    q1 -->|NO| q4
    q2 -->|YES| out2
    q2 -->|NO| out1
    q4 -->|YES| q4a{IS HIGH RPM REQUIRED}
    q4a -->|YES| out4
    q4a -->|NO| out3
```

---

### 4.4 Energy Storage & Release Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1[T1 PRIMARY CURRENT BEGINS TO RISE IN COIL]
    t2[T2 DWELL TIME ENDS IGBT SWITCHES OFF]
    t3[T3 SECONDARY VOLTAGE PEAKS AT 30 TO 40 KV]
    t4[T4 SPARK DISCHARGE OCCURS AT PLUG GAP]
    t5[T5 PLASMA CHANNEL DECAYS MULTI SPARK MODE]

    t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dwell time (T1 → T2) in an electronic system is **dynamically computed** by the ECU based on battery voltage, engine RPM, and temperature. In a mechanical system, dwell is **fixed** by lobe profile and can become insufficient at high RPM — a major disadvantage that electronic systems eliminate entirely.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Set B]
**List any four advantages of an electronic ignition system over the conventional contact-point ignition system.**

**Model Answer (4 Advantages — 3 marks):**

1. **Higher secondary voltage (30 – 40 kV)** at the spark plug due to faster semiconductor switching, producing a much stronger spark.
2. **Accurate and adaptive ignition timing** controlled by ECU using multiple sensor inputs (crankshaft, MAP, knock sensor).
3. **No mechanical contact wear**, so no periodic replacement of contact points and no dwell angle adjustment is required.
4. **Better high-speed performance and lower emissions**, enabling compliance with BS-VI norms and improved fuel economy.

(Any 4 of the 8 advantages discussed in Section 2.2 will fetch full 3 marks.)

---

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary Exam]
**Explain why the elimination of contact point bounce is considered a major advantage of electronic ignition systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Contact point bounce occurs in mechanical ignition systems because the spring-loaded points physically oscillate after opening due to cam profile impact. Each bounce produces a **false low-energy spark**, leading to:

- Multiple weak sparks instead of one strong spark → **incomplete combustion** and increased **HC emissions**.
- **Mis-firing** at high RPM, since multiple bounces can occur within one dwell period.
- **RF / EMI interference** with radio and electronic systems.

In electronic ignition, a transistor or IGBT switches in **< 10 μs** with no mechanical movement, producing a **clean, single, high-energy spark** per firing event. This guarantees complete combustion, lower emissions, and stable idle quality — particularly important for meeting modern emission standards.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Set A]
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **Transistor-Assisted Ignition (TAI)** system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare TAI and **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** systems in terms of working principle, spark characteristics, advantages, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Transistor-Assisted Ignition (TAI)** system replaces the contact breaker with a **power transistor (Darlington or IGBT)** that switches the primary current of the ignition coil.

**Components and Signal Flow:**

1. **Trigger Mechanism:** A magnetic pick-up (reluctor) or Hall-effect sensor mounted near the distributor shaft generates a small AC or digital pulse synchronized with the engine's camshaft rotation. (1 mark)
2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU) / Ignition Module:** The trigger pulse is shaped and amplified. The ECU computes the **required dwell time** based on battery voltage and RPM. (1 mark)
3. **Driver Stage (Transistor Switch):** When the trigger pulse arrives, the transistor turns **OFF**, abruptly interrupting the primary current through the coil. (1 mark)
4. **Ignition Coil:** The collapsing magnetic field induces a high voltage (Faraday's Law) in the secondary winding, stepping it up from 12 V to **30 – 40 kV**. (1 mark)
5. **Distributor and Spark Plug:** The high-voltage pulse is routed to the correct cylinder's spark plug, where it ionizes the air-fuel gap and initiates combustion. (1 mark)
6. **Dwell Control:** When the trigger signal ends, the transistor turns **ON** again, allowing primary current to build up for the next firing. (1 mark)
7. **Feedback and Diagnostics:** Modern TAI systems send a confirmation signal back to the ECU (IGF / IGN feedback) for misfire detection. (1 mark)

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Comparison Aspect | TAI (Transistor-Assisted) | CDI (Capacitor Discharge) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Storage** | In **primary coil inductance** $L_p$ | In **charging capacitor** $C$ (typically 1 – 2 μF) |
| **Energy Equation** | $E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ |
| **Switching Device** | IGBT or Darlington transistor | **Thyristor (SCR)** triggered by sensor |
| **Spark Type** | Long, hot, inductive spark (~ 1 – 2 ms) | Short, sharp, capacitive spark (~ 0.1 ms) |
| **Spark Voltage** | 30 – 35 kV | 35 – 45 kV |
| **Spark Energy** | 40 – 60 mJ | 50 – 100 mJ |
| **Cold-Start Performance** | Better, due to longer spark | Good, due to high voltage overcoming fouling |
| **High-Speed Performance** | Good up to ~ 7500 RPM | Excellent up to ~ 10,000 RPM |
| **Typical Applications** | 4-stroke cars, BS-IV / Euro 4 era | 2-stroke motorcycles, scooters, racing engines |
| **Advantages** | Better idle quality, dwell optimized | Lighter coils, simpler triggering |
| **Disadvantages** | Coil heating at sustained high RPM | Capacitor voltage drops at low RPM |

(7 marks distributed: 2 for working, 2 for spark characteristics, 2 for advantages, 1 for application)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Set B]
**(a)** Derive an expression for the **secondary voltage** of an inductive ignition system and show that faster switching produces higher spark voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **knock sensor** in an electronic ignition system and explain how it enables **closed-loop ignition timing control**. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation of Secondary Voltage:**

**Step 1:** When the primary switch is closed for dwell time $t_d$, primary current rises to:

$$I_p = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left(1 - e^{-t_d / \tau_p}\right)$$

For saturated operation $t_d \gg \tau_p$, so $I_p \approx \dfrac{V_b}{R_p}$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** The magnetic flux linkage at the moment of opening is:

$$\Phi = L_p \cdot I_p$$ (1 mark)

**Step 3:** When the switch opens in time $t_{off}$, the flux collapses linearly (idealized) from $\Phi$ to 0:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\frac{\Phi}{t_{off}} = -\frac{L_p \, I_p}{t_{off}}$$ (1 mark)

**Step 4:** Applying Faraday's Law to the secondary winding with $N_s$ turns:

$$V_s = -N_s \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = N_s \cdot \frac{L_p \, I_p}{t_{off}}$$ (1 mark)

**Step 5:** Rearranging with $V_b = I_p \cdot R_p$ substituted:

$$V_s = \frac{N_s}{R_p \cdot t_{off}} \cdot L_p \cdot V_b$$ (1 mark)

**Step 6:** Since $L_p$ and $V_b$ are system constants, **$V_s$ is inversely proportional to switch-off time $t_{off}$**. (1 mark)

**Step 7:** Faster switching (smaller $t_{off}$) → higher $V_s$. A transistor that switches in **1 μs** versus mechanical points in **1 ms** yields 1000× higher theoretical voltage, practically **~ 2×** due to losses — confirming the advantage. (1 mark)

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Role of the Knock Sensor in Closed-Loop Ignition Control:**

1. **Knock (Detonation) Definition:** Uncontrolled auto-ignition of end-gas in the combustion chamber produces high-frequency pressure oscillations (5 – 15 kHz) known as engine knock. Knock reduces efficiency and can mechanically damage pistons. (1 mark)
2. **Knock Sensor Operation:** A **piezoelectric accelerometer** mounted on the engine block converts the vibration signal into a proportional voltage. The ECU band-pass filters this signal (typically 5 – 15 kHz) to isolate knock from normal engine noise. (2 marks)
3. **Closed-Loop Control Algorithm:**
   - The ECU compares the knock sensor output to a **calibrated knock threshold map** indexed by RPM and load.
   - If knock is detected, the ECU **retards ignition timing** by **1 – 5° BTDC** in small steps (e.g., 0.5° per detection).
   - If no knock is detected for a period, the ECU **advances timing slowly** back toward the optimal map value.
   - This creates a **closed feedback loop** (sensor → ECU → actuator → engine → sensor). (2 marks)
4. **Engineering Benefit:** This advantage is **impossible in mechanical ignition systems** because points-based systems have no sensor input and no way to dynamically alter timing. (1 mark)
5. **Real-world Outcome:** Knock-sensor-equipped engines can run on lower-octane fuel and still achieve maximum efficiency, while protecting the engine from damage — a clear advantage of electronic ignition. (1 mark)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations (Autotronics Module 2):**
> 1. **Listing advantages without justification** — KTU examiners award marks only for the *cause-effect link*. Writing *"higher voltage"* alone fetches **0 marks**; writing *"higher voltage because faster $dI/dt$ in transistor switching produces greater $d\Phi/dt$"* fetches full credit.
> 2. **Confusing TAI with CDI** — these are two **fundamentally different** systems. TAI stores energy in an **inductor**, CDI stores it in a **capacitor**. Mixing them up costs full 7 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong circuit** — when asked for a block diagram of an electronic ignition, students often draw the **mechanical contact-breaker circuit**. Always include the **transistor / IGBT switch** in the primary circuit.
> 4. **Forgetting the knock-sensor feedback loop** in Part B questions on closed-loop control — without it, the answer is incomplete and loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring BS-VI / Euro norms context** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes emission compliance. Failing to link an advantage to **emission reduction** is considered an incomplete answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electronic Ignition** = semiconductor-switched (BJT / Darlington / IGBT) replacement of mechanical breaker points in the primary ignition circuit.
- **Primary advantages:** Higher secondary voltage (30 – 40 kV), longer / sharper spark, accurate adaptive timing, no contact wear, no contact bounce, excellent high-RPM performance, lower emissions, ECU compatibility.
- **Governing equation:** $V_s = N_s \cdot \dfrac{L_p \, I_p}{t_{off}}$ — secondary voltage is **inversely proportional to switch-off time**, so faster transistors yield stronger sparks.
- **Energy equation (inductive):** $E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$. **Energy equation (capacitive CDI):** $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$.
- **Dwell angle** is **fixed** in mechanical systems but **variable and optimized** in electronic systems — a critical advantage.
- **Types to remember:** TAI (transistor-assisted), CDI (capacitor discharge), DLI (distributor-less), COP (coil-on-plug), EI (electronic ignition with full ECU).
- **Knock sensor feedback** enables **closed-loop ignition timing control** — impossible in mechanical systems.
- **Emission compliance:** BS-VI / Euro 6 is achievable only with electronic (not mechanical) ignition systems.
- **Cost vs Benefit trade-off:** Mechanical systems are cheaper but obsolete; electronic systems dominate modern automotive applications.
- **Maintenance interval** jumps from ~ 20,000 km (points) to > 1,00,000 km (electronic) — significant cost-of-ownership advantage.
- **Most common exam pitfall:** describing advantages *qualitatively* without *quantitative* cause-effect reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Group and Sequential Injection Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Group and Sequential Injection Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI / MPI)** architecture, the Electronic Control Unit (ECU) must decide **when** to energise each of the engine's solenoid injectors relative to the **crankshaft angle** and the **intake valve events** of every cylinder. The two dominant non-simultaneous strategies covered in Module 2 are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Group Injection (GFI / Batch-Fire Injection)** — The engine's injectors are partitioned into two (or sometimes three) electrical groups. All injectors belonging to the same group are energised **simultaneously** by the ECU driver stage, and each group is fired once every **360°** of crankshaft rotation. A 4-cylinder engine thus produces **two injection events per engine cycle (720°)**, regardless of which cylinder is on its induction stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential Injection (SFI / Timed Injection)** — Each individual injector is fired **separately and precisely at the intake stroke of its own cylinder**. There are exactly **N_cyl** injection events per **720°** crank cycle, and the timing of every pulse is referenced to that cylinder's **intake-valve-opening** event. This requires a **Camshaft Position (CMP) sensor** in addition to the Crankshaft Position (CKP) sensor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter"

Imagine a four-table restaurant with a single waiter (the ECU) carrying four plates of food (fuel doses):

- **Sequential Injection** → A **meticulous waiter** who delivers each plate to the correct table **exactly when that customer sits down** (the intake stroke). The food is hot, properly portioned, and untouched by anyone else.
- **Group Injection** → A **less-fussy waiter** who drops two plates on two tables at the **same moment**, even though only one of those customers has just sat down. The other plate sits on the second table for a few hundred milliseconds until that customer arrives. It still works — but the food has been sitting on the counter (intake manifold wall) for a moment, and some may have cooled or stuck.

The "meticulous" strategy costs slightly more in ECU complexity and one extra sensor, but the **fuel-air mixing quality, idle stability, and emissions** are demonstrably superior — which is why every modern passenger car uses sequential injection.

## 1.3 Why These Two Strategies Exist (Historical Context)

Early electronic MPFI systems of the **1980s (Bosch L-Jetronic, Motronic 1.x)** were built with cheap, low-resolution trigger wheels and **no cam sensor**. The ECU could only know *where the crank was*, not *which cylinder was on its intake stroke*. Group injection was the natural solution because it did not require that information — it simply fired Group 1 every 180° and Group 2 every 180° offset. When cam-position sensors became affordable and motronic firmware became powerful enough in the **1990s (Motronic M2.x, ME-Motronic)**, sequential injection became standard. Group mode is therefore retained in modern ECUs as a **limp-home fallback** when the CMP sensor fails (open-loop, reduced-accuracy operation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric** — The **injection event period** between two consecutive injector firings in a 4-cylinder engine:
> - Group mode → **180°** of crankshaft rotation
> - Sequential mode → **180°** of crankshaft rotation (but distributed across the correct cylinders)

## 1.4 Scope of This Module

This chapter covers: (i) the operational difference between GFI and SFI, (ii) the hardware required (CKP, CMP, driver stages, injector types), (iii) the timing mathematics (injection pulse width, crank-angle scheduling), (iv) fault-mode behaviour, and (v) comparative performance against KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation criteria.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Architecture Common to Both Strategies

Both GFI and SFI share an identical **sensor front-end and injector set**. The ECU differentiates between the two strategies purely in **firmware**, by re-routing its driver-stage firing schedule. The shared architecture is:

| Subsystem | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Position sensing | **CKP sensor** (inductive / Hall) | Provides **crank angle** and **engine speed (RPM)** — 60-2 or 36-1 tooth wheels typical |
| Position sensing | **CMP sensor** (Hall) | Identifies which cylinder is on compression stroke — **mandatory for SFI**, optional for GFI |
| Air metering | **MAP** (speed-density) or **MAF** (hot-wire) | Provides **m_air** to the calculation unit |
| Corrective feedback | **Lambda (O₂) sensor** — narrowband or wideband | Closed-loop AFR trim |
| Drivers | N-channel MOSFET **low-side drivers** (one per injector in SFI; shared per group in GFI) | Switches the 12 V injector solenoid to ground |
| Actuator | Electromagnetic solenoid injector (peak-and-hold) | Converts electrical pulse → metered fuel pulse |

## 2.2 Group Injection — Operating Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Group firing logic (4-cylinder):** Cylinders 1 & 3 form **Group A**; cylinders 2 & 4 form **Group B**. Both groups fire on **every 360°** of crank rotation, but **180° apart** from each other (so an event happens every 180°).

**Why it works even without a cam sensor:** Because every cylinder goes through an induction stroke once per 720°, and the engine's **firing order is 1-3-4-2** (typical), the crankshaft encounters a cylinder needing fuel every 180°. The two-group scheme happens to align with this — a happy coincidence exploited in early systems.

**Pulse-width in group mode:**
- Each injector in the group still delivers fuel for **one** cylinder's induction event.
- Therefore, the pulse width per injector is **identical to the sequential pulse width**; only the **time of firing** differs.
- Each injector fires **2× per 720°** in GFI, whereas in SFI it fires only **1× per 720°**.

**Wall-wetting problem in GFI:**
- If Injector 1 fires at the moment Cyl 3 is opening its intake valve, the fuel destined for Cyl 1 must **wait in the intake port** until Cyl 1's valve opens — typically 180°–360° later.
- During this wait, fuel may **condense on the port wall**, causing transient air-fuel excursions during **cold start, tip-in, and tip-out transients**.

## 2.3 Sequential Injection — Operating Principle

In SFI mode, the ECU maintains an internal **cylinder counter** that is initialised on every CMP-sensor falling edge (or rising edge, depending on trigger pattern). On every CKP reference pulse, the ECU:

1. **Identifies** which cylinder is next to begin its intake stroke.
2. **Calculates** the crank angle until intake-valve opening (IVO) for that cylinder.
3. **Schedules** the corresponding injector firing that many crank degrees in the future.
4. **Outputs** the correct pulse width to that specific driver's MOSFET.

**Software block for SFI (typical C-code abstraction in ECU firmware):**

```text
on CKP_trigger_isr( angle )
   if (angle == scheduled_angle[cyl_index]) {
       driver[cyl_index].fire( pulse_width_us );
       cyl_index = (cyl_index + 1) mod N_cyl;
   }
```

**Consequence:** Each injector fires exactly **once per 720°** of crank rotation, and that single event is **synchronised to its cylinder's induction event**. No fuel is ever "stored" in the port.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical questions on injection systems.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of air per cylinder per cycle | $m_{air}$ | $\rho_{air} \cdot V_{d,cyl} \cdot \eta_{v}$ | kg | $V_{d,cyl}=V_d/N_{cyl}$ |
| 2 | Mass of fuel per cylinder per cycle | $m_{fuel}$ | $m_{air} / AFR_{target}$ | kg | Stoich petrol: $AFR_s = 14.7$ |
| 3 | Injection pulse width | $T_{pw}$ | $m_{fuel} / \dot{m}_{inj}$ | s | $\dot{m}_{inj}$ in kg/s |
| 4 | Time per engine cycle (4-stroke) | $T_{cyc}$ | $120 / N$ | s | N in RPM |
| 5 | Injection events per cycle | GFI: $N_{evt}$ | $2$ | — | For 4-cyl, 2 groups |
| 6 | Injection events per cycle | SFI: $N_{evt}$ | $N_{cyl}$ | — | One per cylinder |
| 7 | Crank angle between SFI events | $\theta_{sfi}$ | $720° / N_{cyl}$ | deg | = 180° for 4-cyl |
| 8 | Crank angle between GFI events | $\theta_{gfi}$ | $360° / N_{groups}$ | deg | = 180° for 2 groups |
| 9 | Injector duty cycle | $D$ | $T_{pw} \cdot N \cdot N_{cyl} / (60 \cdot 2)$ | — | Should remain < 0.85 |
| 10 | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (petrol) | $AFR_s$ | $\mathbf{14.7 : 1}$ | — | Standard constant |
| 11 | Energy density of petrol (LHV) | $LHV$ | $\mathbf{44 \text{ MJ/kg}}$ | J/kg | Standard constant |
| 12 | Crank angle per second | $\omega_{crank}$ | $6N$ | deg/s | N in RPM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Equation (1) + (2) + (3) is the complete **fuel map calculation pipeline** used in every production ECU. Equation (9) is used by diagnostic tools to detect **injector clogging** (duty cycle drifts upward at constant load as $\dot{m}_{inj}$ falls).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Trade-offs

| Parameter | Group Injection | Sequential Injection |
|---|---|---|
| CMP sensor required | **No** | **Yes** |
| ECU firmware complexity | Low | Medium–High |
| Fuel wall-wetting | **Significant** | Negligible |
| Cold-start HC emissions | Higher | Lower by **10–20%** |
| Idle stability (RPM fluctuation) | $\pm 25$ RPM | $\pm 8$ RPM |
| Throttle tip-in response | Slight hesitation | Crisp |
| Cost | Lower | Marginally higher (CMP + wiring) |
| Use in production | Mostly retired (2000s) | **Universal in modern cars** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When asked to "compare" GFI and SFI, examiners expect you to list **at least four** of the above points and explicitly link each to a **physical mechanism** (e.g., "Idle stability is better in SFI *because* fuel is delivered just before IVO, so wall-wetting transients are eliminated").

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Air Mass per Cylinder per Cycle

**Given:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine with total displacement $V_d$, running at $N$ RPM with volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ at the current operating point.

**Step 1 — Displacement per cylinder:**
In a 4-stroke engine, total displacement $V_d$ is shared equally between the $N_{cyl}$ cylinders (for an inline configuration):

$$V_{d,cyl} = \frac{V_d}{N_{cyl}}$$

This is the **geometric** volume swept by one piston from BDC to TDC.

**Step 2 — Air density at manifold conditions:**
At intake-manifold pressure $p_{man}$ and temperature $T_{man}$, using the ideal-gas relation:

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{p_{man}}{R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$$

For standard atmospheric conditions ($p_{man} = 1.0$ bar, $T_{man} = 300$ K, $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K)):

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{101325}{287 \times 300} \approx 1.177 \text{ kg/m}^3 \approx \mathbf{1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric efficiency correction:**
The actual mass of air inducted is less than the geometric displacement would suggest, because of throttling, residual exhaust gas, and heat transfer. This is captured by $\eta_v$ (a dimensionless factor, typically $0.3$ at idle, $0.85$ at WOT):

$$m_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{d,cyl} \cdot \eta_v$$

> **Conversion logic:** We multiply the geometric swept volume by the density to get the *theoretical* mass if it were filled with ambient air, then scale by $\eta_v$ to account for the fact that **the cylinder is not completely filled** with fresh charge at the actual intake conditions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Fuel Mass per Cylinder per Cycle

**Step 4 — Air-fuel ratio target:**
The driver demand and the closed-loop lambda controller combine to demand a target ratio $AFR_{target}$. For stoichiometric combustion in a petrol engine:

$$AFR_s = 14.7 : 1$$

**Step 5 — Mass of fuel per cycle per cylinder:**
By definition of the air-fuel ratio:

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{AFR_{target}}$$

The ECU computes this **once per cylinder per cycle** (4 times per 720° crank rotation in a 4-cyl engine).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Injection Pulse Width

**Step 6 — Injector mass flow rate:**
A solenoid injector is essentially a calibrated orifice. At a fixed rail pressure $\Delta p$ (typically 3–4 bar for port injection), the steady-state mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \Delta p}$$

where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.9$ for sharp-edged orifices) and $A_{eff}$ is the effective nozzle area. Manufacturers publish this as a single number, e.g., $\dot{m}_{inj} = 2.0$ g/s at 3 bar for a typical small port injector.

**Step 7 — Required pulse width:**
For a pulse of duration $T_{pw}$ to deliver mass $m_{fuel}$:

$$T_{pw} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj}}$$

This is the **fundamental pulse-width equation** that the ECU's PWM driver must implement. Every injection strategy (GFI, SFI, simultaneous) reduces to selecting *when* and *how long* to fire each driver with this exact pulse width.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number and Spacing of Injection Events

**Step 8 — Time per complete 4-stroke cycle:**
A 4-stroke engine completes one cycle every **two** crank revolutions. With N in revolutions per minute:

$$T_{cyc} = \frac{2 \times 60}{N} = \frac{120}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 9 — Injection-event count:**

$$
N_{events} = \begin{cases} N_{cyl} \cdot N_{groups} = N_{cyl} \times 1 = N_{cyl} & \text{(SFI)} \\ N_{groups} \times 2 = 2 \cdot N_{groups} & \text{(GFI, 2 fires per 720° per group)} \end{cases}
$$

For our 4-cyl, 2-group engine: $N_{events} = 4$ for SFI, $N_{events} = 4$ for GFI (2 events per group × 2 groups), but **distributed differently** in time.

**Step 10 — Crank-angle between events:**

$$
\theta_{spacing} = \begin{cases} 720° / N_{cyl} = 180° & \text{(SFI, 4-cyl)} \\ 360° / N_{groups} = 180° & \text{(GFI, 2 groups)} \end{cases}
$$

**Crucial observation:** Both strategies space events at exactly **180° crank angle apart**. The difference is **which cylinder** receives fuel at each event — and that is what makes SFI thermodynamically and chemically superior.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern — 7-mark derivation question):**

**Given data:**
- 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine
- Total displacement $V_d = 1.6$ L
- Engine speed $N = 2500$ RPM
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.70$
- Air density $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Target AFR = 14.7 (stoichiometric)
- Injector flow rate $\dot{m}_{inj} = 2.0$ g/s at 3.0 bar rail pressure

**Step 1 — Compute displacement per cylinder:**

$$V_{d,cyl} = \frac{1.6 \text{ L}}{4} = 0.4 \text{ L} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute air mass per cylinder per cycle:**

$$m_{air} = 1.2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.70 = 3.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 336 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute fuel mass per cycle:**

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{336 \text{ mg}}{14.7} = 22.86 \text{ mg} \approx 22.9 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pulse width:**

$$T_{pw} = \frac{22.86 \text{ mg}}{2000 \text{ mg/s}} = 0.01143 \text{ s} = 11.43 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Compute time per cycle:**

$$T_{cyc} = \frac{120}{2500} = 0.048 \text{ s} = 48 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Compute duty cycle:**

$$D = \frac{T_{pw}}{T_{cyc}} = \frac{11.43}{48} = 0.2381 = 23.81\%$$

**Step 7 — Compare GFI and SFI event count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{events,SFI} &= 4 \text{ per } 720°, \text{ each } 11.43 \text{ ms} \\
N_{events,GFI} &= 4 \text{ per } 720°, \text{ each } 11.43 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total fuel mass delivered per cycle** is identical:
$$m_{fuel,total} = 4 \times 22.86 = 91.43 \text{ mg/cycle per engine (4-cyl)}$$

**Step 8 — Average fuel flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel,avg} = \frac{91.43 \text{ mg}}{48 \text{ ms}} = 1.905 \text{ g/s} \approx 114.3 \text{ g/min} \approx 6.86 \text{ kg/h}$$

This corresponds to a **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** of approximately $300$ g/kWh for a small 1.6 L engine at this load point — a realistic mid-range cruise value.

**Verification (engine-out power estimate):**
$$P_{fuel} = 1.905 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \times 44 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} = 83.8 \text{ kW (fuel energy rate)}$$

If brake thermal efficiency is $\eta_{bt} = 0.30$, brake power $\approx 25$ kW, which is consistent with a 1.6 L engine at 2500 RPM at partial load. **Numbers check out.**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — ECU Fuel-Injection Control Block Architecture

The following mermaid block diagram maps the **signal flow** from physical sensors through the ECU firmware layers to the injector solenoids. Both GFI and SFI share this identical hardware — only the **Mode Selector block** routes the driver-stage outputs differently.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SENSE_CKP[Crank Position Sensor CKP - 60 minus 2] --> ECU_IN[ECU Input Capture Timer]
    SENSE_CMP[Cam Position Sensor CMP - Hall] --> ECU_IN
    SENSE_MAP[MAP Sensor - Manifold Absolute Pressure] --> ECU_IN
    SENSE_TPS[Throttle Position Sensor TPS] --> ECU_IN
    SENSE_CTS[Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS] --> ECU_IN
    SENSE_LAM[Lambda Oxygen Sensor - Wideband] --> ECU_IN

    ECU_IN --> CALC_AIR[Air Mass Calculator m_air]
    CALC_AIR --> CALC_FUEL[Fuel Mass Calculator m_fuel per cyl]
    CALC_FUEL --> PULSE_W[Pulse Width Engine T_pw]
    SENSE_LAM --> LAMBDA_CTRL[Closed Loop Lambda Trim]
    LAMBDA_CTRL --> PULSE_W

    PULSE_W --> MODE_SEL[Injection Strategy Selector]
    MODE_SEL --> SFI_BLK[Sequential Mode Firmware]
    MODE_SEL --> GFI_BLK[Group Mode Firmware]

    SFI_BLK --> DRV1[Driver MOSFET Inj 1]
    SFI_BLK --> DRV2[Driver MOSFET Inj 2]
    SFI_BLK --> DRV3[Driver MOSFET Inj 3]
    SFI_BLK --> DRV4[Driver MOSFET Inj 4]
    GFI_BLK --> DRV1
    GFI_BLK --> DRV2
    GFI_BLK --> DRV3
    GFI_BLK --> DRV4

    DRV1 --> INJ1[Solenoid Injector Cyl 1]
    DRV2 --> INJ2[Solenoid Injector Cyl 2]
    DRV3 --> INJ3[Solenoid Injector Cyl 3]
    DRV4 --> INJ4[Solenoid Injector Cyl 4]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Injection-Strategy Decision Flowchart

Modern ECUs automatically fall back to GFI mode under specific fault conditions. The decision logic implemented in the firmware is:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENG_START[Engine Cranking / Key On] --> CMP_CHK{CMP Signal Valid}
    CMP_CHK -- No --> FAULT_LOG[Log DTC P0340 - Camshaft Position Sensor Circuit]
    FAULT_LOG --> GFI_MODE[Enter Group Injection Mode]
    CMP_CHK -- Yes --> WARM_CHK{Engine Coolant Above 20 deg C}
    WARM_CHK -- No --> STRAT_ENRICH[Use Enriched Start Map with SFI]
    STRAT_ENRICH --> SFI_MODE[Normal Sequential Mode]
    WARM_CHK -- Yes --> SFI_MODE
    SFI_MODE --> RUN_LOOP{Continuous Operation}
    RUN_LOOP --> LAMBDA_OK{Closed Loop Lambda OK}
    LAMBDA_OK -- Yes --> RUN_LOOP
    LAMBDA_OK -- No --> DIAG[Run O2 Sensor Diagnostic]
    DIAG --> SENSOR_FAULT{Wideband Sensor Plausibility}
    SENSOR_FAULT -- Fault --> LIMP[Limp Home - Group Mode with Fixed Map]
    SENSOR_FAULT -- OK --> RUN_LOOP
    GFI_MODE --> LIMP
    LIMP --> RUN_LOOP
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Cylinder Firing and Injection Event Timeline (720° Cycle)

This is a **functional architecture flow** showing the relationship between crankshaft position, cylinder events, and injection events in **Sequential mode vs Group mode** for a firing order 1-3-4-2.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SEQ720["SEQUENTIAL INJECTION MODE - 4 events per 720 deg"]
        D0A[0 deg TDC Cyl 1 Power] --> D180A[180 deg TDC Cyl 3 Power]
        D180A --> D360A[360 deg TDC Cyl 4 Power]
        D360A --> D540A[540 deg TDC Cyl 2 Power]
        D540A --> D720A[720 deg Return to TDC Cyl 1]
        EV1A[Inj 1 Pulse at 360 deg - Cyl 1 IVO] -. fires .-> D360A
        EV3A[Inj 3 Pulse at 540 deg - Cyl 3 IVO] -. fires .-> D540A
        EV4A[Inj 4 Pulse at 0 deg next cycle - Cyl 4 IVO] -. fires .-> D0A
        EV2A[Inj 2 Pulse at 180 deg - Cyl 2 IVO] -. fires .-> D180A
    end
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph GRP720["GROUP INJECTION MODE - 2 group events per 720 deg"]
        D0B[0 deg TDC Cyl 1 Power] --> D180B[180 deg TDC Cyl 3 Power]
        D180B --> D360B[360 deg TDC Cyl 4 Power]
        D360B --> D540B[540 deg TDC Cyl 2 Power]
        D540B --> D720B[720 deg Return to TDC Cyl 1]
        EV_G1[Group A Pulse at 0 deg - Inj 1 and 3 simultaneous] -. fires .-> D0B
        EV_G2[Group B Pulse at 360 deg - Inj 2 and 4 simultaneous] -. fires .-> D360B
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams above:** In SFI, each injector fires **at the Intake-Valve-Opening of its own cylinder**, which is **360° after** that cylinder's power stroke TDC. In GFI, both Group A injectors (Cyl 1 and Cyl 3) fire together at Cyl 1's power-stroke TDC, even though Cyl 3 is on its power stroke and Cyl 1 has **already passed** its induction event.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Differentiate between Group Injection and Sequential Injection in MPFI systems.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Question Pattern)]**
**Cognitive Level:** Understand | **CO Mapping:** CO2

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Group Injection (GFI) | Sequential Injection (SFI) |
|---|---|---|
| Injector firing | All injectors in a group fire **simultaneously** | Each injector fires **individually** |
| Trigger reference | Only **CKP** sensor required | **CKP + CMP** sensors both required |
| Injection events per 720° | **2** (one per group) for 4-cyl | **4** (one per cylinder) |
| Synchronisation with intake event | **Asynchronous** (may not match IVO) | **Synchronised** to each cylinder's IVO |
| Wall-wetting | Present (fuel pools in port) | Negligible |
| Use case | Limp-home mode, older systems | All modern passenger cars |

*(Award 1 mark for each of three correct contrasting points.)*

---

### **Q2.** Why is a Camshaft Position (CMP) sensor mandatory in Sequential Fuel Injection but not in Group Injection?
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Question Pattern)]**
**Cognitive Level:** Analyze | **CO Mapping:** CO2

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In SFI, the ECU must know **which specific cylinder** is about to begin its induction stroke, because it has to fire **only that cylinder's injector** at the correct crank angle. The CMP sensor provides this absolute phase reference (Cyl 1 TDC compression vs TDC exhaust). Without it, the ECU cannot disambiguate between two crankshaft revolutions and would risk firing injectors at the wrong valve events (e.g., delivering fuel during the exhaust stroke).

In GFI, the injectors fire in **fixed groups every 180°** crank angle, irrespective of which cylinder is on its induction stroke. Fuel may pool in the port for the "wrong" cylinder, but no fuel is ever delivered during a non-intake stroke. Hence, the CMP sensor is **not functionally required** for GFI. *(3 marks — 1 for SFI justification, 1 for GFI justification, 1 for the consequence-of-failure statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each Part-B question below offers an **internal choice** as per KTU ESE pattern. Attempt **either** the whole of Choice A **or** Choice B.

---

### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI)** with a neat block diagram. List **four advantages** of SFI over Group Injection. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyze | **CO Mapping:** CO2, CO3

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** SFI is a multi-point injection strategy in which the ECU fires each individual injector **once per 720°**, precisely timed to the intake stroke of its assigned cylinder.

2. **Block-diagram description (3 marks):** Refer to the ECU architecture in Section 4.1. The CKP sensor provides crank-angle triggers via the input-capture timer. The CMP sensor is interrogated on every revolution to **reset and maintain a 4-position cylinder counter** in firmware. The Air Mass Calculator (using MAP or MAF) and the closed-loop Lambda controller feed the Fuel Mass Calculator, which outputs the required fuel mass $m_{fuel}$ per cylinder per cycle. The Pulse-Width Engine converts this to milliseconds using $\dot{m}_{inj}$. A scheduling table in firmware contains the **crank-angle offsets** at which each cylinder's injector should fire (typically 360° after that cylinder's power TDC, i.e., at intake TDC). The driver-stage MOSFETs (one per injector) are energised individually.

3. **Four advantages over GFI (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
   - **Lower HC emissions** during cold start and idle (no port-wall fuel accumulation).
   - **Better idle stability** ($\pm 8$ RPM vs $\pm 25$ RPM in GFI).
   - **Crisp throttle tip-in response** (fuel arrives just-in-time at IVO).
   - **Improved part-load fuel economy** (3–5% gain on NEDC cycle).
