4. **Disadvantage / limitation (1 mark):** Requires a CMP sensor and more sophisticated firmware; higher ECU cost.

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a displacement of 1.2 L, runs at 3000 RPM, with $\eta_v = 0.75$, $\rho_{air} = 1.18$ kg/m³, target AFR = 14.5, and injector flow rate = 1.8 g/s. Calculate: **(i)** air mass per cylinder per cycle, **(ii)** fuel mass per cycle, **(iii)** injection pulse width, and **(iv)** injector duty cycle. **(7 marks)**
**Cognitive Level:** Apply | **CO Mapping:** CO3

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Air mass per cylinder per cycle (2 marks):**
$$V_{d,cyl} = \frac{1.2 \text{ L}}{4} = 0.3 \text{ L} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$
$$m_{air} = 1.18 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.75 = 2.655 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 265.5 \text{ mg}$$

**(ii) Fuel mass per cycle per cylinder (1.5 marks):**
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{265.5}{14.5} = 18.31 \text{ mg}$$

**(iii) Injection pulse width (2 marks):**
$$T_{pw} = \frac{18.31 \text{ mg}}{1800 \text{ mg/s}} = 0.01017 \text{ s} = 10.17 \text{ ms}$$

**(iv) Injector duty cycle (1.5 marks):**
$$T_{cyc} = \frac{120}{3000} = 0.040 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}$$
$$D = \frac{10.17}{40} = 0.2543 = 25.43\%$$

**Answer verification:** A duty cycle of ~25% at 3000 RPM is within the normal operating window (<85% limit), confirming the injector is sized correctly for this engine. *(+1 mark for the verification comment.)*

---

### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a suitable timing diagram, explain the **Group Injection (GFI)** technique. Compare its performance with sequential injection. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Cognitive Level:** Understand / Evaluate | **CO Mapping:** CO2, CO3

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** In GFI, the engine's injectors are wired into two (or three) electrical groups. Each group is fired by a single driver stage; all injectors in the group are energised simultaneously, once per 360° of crankshaft rotation.

2. **Timing diagram — 720° cycle, 4-cyl firing order 1-3-4-2 (3 marks):**

| Crank Angle | 0° | 180° | 360° | 540° | 720° |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder | 1 (Power) | 3 (Power) | 4 (Power) | 2 (Power) | 1 (IVO) |
| **Group A** (Inj 1, 3) | **FIRE** | — | — | — | — |
| **Group B** (Inj 2, 4) | — | — | **FIRE** | — | — |
| **SFI (for contrast)** | — | Inj 2 fires | — | Inj 4 fires | Inj 1 fires |

Note: In GFI, Group A fires at 0° crank (during Cyl 1's power stroke), but Cyl 1's actual induction does not begin until 360° later. Fuel for Cyl 1 therefore pools in the intake port — the **wall-wetting phenomenon**.

3. **Comparison (3 marks — 1.5 each for the two systems):**

   - **Wall-wetting** — Severe in GFI (fuel dwells 180–360° crank in the port), negligible in SFI.
   - **Emissions (cold-start HC, idle CO)** — SFI gives **10–20% reduction** in HC during cold start; GFI is worse.
   - **Idle quality** — SFI RPM fluctuation $\pm 8$ RPM vs GFI $\pm 25$ RPM.
   - **Throttle tip-in** — SFI delivers fuel "just-in-time"; GFI shows transient lean/rich spikes.
   - **Hardware cost** — GFI is cheaper (no CMP, simpler driver). SFI requires CMP sensor.

**(b)** A technician reports that the engine ECU has logged fault code **P0340 (Camshaft Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction)**. **(i)** Identify the strategy the ECU will fall back to. **(ii)** List **three observable symptoms** the driver might experience. **(iii)** Justify why the ECU does not shut off fuel injection entirely. **(7 marks)**
**Cognitive Level:** Analyze / Evaluate | **CO Mapping:** CO3, CO4

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Fallback strategy (2 marks):**
The ECU will switch to **Group Injection (GFI) mode** with a pre-stored open-loop fuel map. The CMP sensor signal is ignored; injection timing is derived solely from the CKP sensor, firing Group A every 360° and Group B every 360° offset. A Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) P0340 is stored in non-volatile memory, and the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** is illuminated.

**(ii) Three observable symptoms (3 marks — 1 each):**
- **MIL illuminated** on the instrument cluster.
- **Rough idle** with RPM fluctuations of $\pm 25$ RPM and possible misfire codes (P0300–P0304).
- **Increased fuel consumption** by approximately 5–10% and **elevated tailpipe HC** during warm-up.
- *(Acceptable alternative: cold-start hesitation, tip-in stumble, or stalling.)*

**(iii) Justification for not shutting off injection (2 marks):**
If the ECU shut off fuel entirely, the engine would **stall immediately**, leaving the vehicle stranded in traffic — a serious safety hazard. Group injection is a **degraded but functional** mode: it produces higher emissions and worse driveability, but it allows the driver to reach a service centre under their own power. This is a classic example of a **limp-home (fail-soft)** design philosophy mandated by OBD-II regulations (SAE J2012 / ISO 15031).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "injection events per cycle" with "number of injectors":** Students often write "GFI has 4 events per cycle" or "SFI has 2 events per cycle" — both wrong. GFI has 2 group-events per cycle, SFI has N_cyl events per cycle.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2-revolution factor in a 4-stroke:** The cycle time is $120/N$, **not** $60/N$. This single error cascades into a wrong duty cycle. Always state "4-stroke" explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up $V_d$ with $V_{d,cyl}$:** Total displacement vs per-cylinder displacement. Most pulse-width errors originate here.
> 4. **Using $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³ for non-standard conditions:** At 3000 m altitude or under boost, density changes significantly. Always state the assumed $p_{man}$ and $T_{man}$.
> 5. **Stating that SFI is "more efficient":** SFI improves **part-load fuel economy** by 3–5%, but at WOT the thermodynamic efficiency is identical (both strategies deliver the same $m_{fuel}$). Be precise.
> 6. **Skipping the role of the lambda sensor:** The pulse-width is **trimmed** by the closed-loop lambda controller (typically $\pm 25\%$). Forgetting this loses 1 mark in any closed-loop numerical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **GFI = Group (or Batch) Fire Injection** — injectors in groups, all injectors in a group fire **simultaneously**, 2 events per 720° for 4-cyl, requires **only CKP** sensor.
- ✅ **SFI = Sequential (or Timed) Fuel Injection** — each injector fires **individually**, one event per cylinder per 720°, requires **CKP + CMP** sensors.
- ✅ **Pulse width equation** — $T_{pw} = m_{fuel} / \dot{m}_{inj}$ where $m_{fuel} = \rho_{air} \cdot (V_d / N_{cyl}) \cdot \eta_v / AFR$.
- ✅ **Cycle time (4-stroke)** — $T_{cyc} = 120 / N$ seconds (N in RPM). Remember the factor of 2.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR for petrol** — **14.7 : 1** (energy density LHV = **44 MJ/kg**).
- ✅ **Crank angle between SFI events in 4-cyl** — exactly **180°**.
- ✅ **Crank angle between GFI events in 4-cyl (2 groups)** — exactly **180°** (same spacing, different cylinders).
- ✅ **Wall-wetting** is the central physical disadvantage of GFI — fuel pools in the intake port.
- ✅ **SFI benefits** — Lower HC, better idle, crisper tip-in, 3–5% better part-load FE.
- ✅ **P0340 DTC** → ECU falls back to GFI limp-home mode; MIL is illuminated; OBD-II mandates this.
- ✅ **Duty cycle** $D = T_{pw} / T_{cyc}$ should be kept **< 85%** to allow injector closure time and prevent coil overheating.
- ✅ **Injectors** are peak-and-hold solenoid type in modern systems; driver MOSFETs switch the low side; flyback diodes are mandatory.
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ varies from ~0.3 (idle, throttled) to ~0.85 (WOT, naturally aspirated) and >1.0 (turbocharged at boost).
- ✅ **MAP sensor** dominates in speed-density ECUs (Honda, older Ford); **MAF sensor** dominates in alpha-N ECUs (Bosch, modern Ford).
- ✅ **Closed-loop lambda trim** adjusts $T_{pw}$ by typically $\pm 25\%$ in real time using the wideband O₂ sensor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of the Fuel System Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working of the Fuel System Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel System** in an automobile is an integrated assembly of mechanical, electrical, and electronic components designed to **store, filter, pressurize, meter, and deliver the correct quantity of atomized fuel** into the engine's combustion chambers at the precisely controlled timing dictated by the Engine Control Unit (ECU). In the context of **Autotronics (OEMRT831)**, the fuel system is a *cyber-physical subsystem* where electromechanical actuators (pumps, injectors) and sensors (MAF, MAP, lambda, rail-pressure) operate in closed-loop synchrony with the ECU to maintain the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (AFR ≈ 14.7:1 for gasoline / 14.5:1 for diesel under lambda = 1 closed-loop control)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 2**
> The fuel system is no longer treated as a purely mechanical aggregate. The 2024 scheme expects students to map *every component* to its **electronic counterpart (sensor/actuator)** and explain the **closed-loop feedback architecture** governing it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Human Circulatory System

Imagine the fuel system as the **human circulatory system**:

| Human Body | Automotive Fuel System |
|---|---|
| Heart | Electric Fuel Pump (pressurizes) |
| Arteries & Veins | Fuel Lines / High-Pressure Rails |
| Kidneys (filter blood) | Fuel Filter |
| Lungs (mix O₂ with blood) | Intake Manifold / Combustion Chamber |
| Brain (decides oxygen rate) | Engine Control Unit (ECU) |
| Capillaries (controlled release) | Fuel Injectors |

Just as the brain monitors oxygen demand and adjusts heart rate, the **ECU monitors engine load, RPM, and air mass**, then modulates the **injector pulse width** and **fuel pump duty cycle** to deliver the right "blood" (fuel) at the right time.

## 1.3 Classification of Fuel Systems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to *compare* these systems. Memorize the classification first.

1. **Carbureted System (CV)** – Venturi-based, mechanically metered. Obsolete in modern BS-VI / Euro 6 vehicles.
2. **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI / SPFI)** – One injector per cylinder, port injection.
3. **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDi / DI)** – Injector sprays directly into the cylinder.
4. **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** – Used in diesel engines, very high rail pressure (**1350–2500 bar**).
5. **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** – Generic term covering MPFI/GDi controlled by ECU.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Stoichiometric AFR (Petrol):** **14.7 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel):** **14.5 : 1**
- **MPFI Rail Pressure:** **2.5 – 4.0 bar**
- **GDi Rail Pressure:** **100 – 350 bar**
- **CRDi Rail Pressure:** **1350 – 2500 bar**
- **Fuel Tank Working Pressure:** **0.2 – 0.5 bar** (with EVAP vent valve)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Modern MPFI System

The system can be decomposed into **four functional stages**:

1. **Storage & Suction Stage** – Fuel Tank + In-tank Electric Pump
2. **Filtration & Pressurization Stage** – Fuel Filter + Pressure Regulator + Fuel Rail
3. **Metering & Atomization Stage** – ECU + Fuel Injectors + Throttle Body
4. **Feedback Stage** – Lambda Sensor + MAF/MAP Sensor → ECU (closed loop)

## 2.2 Component-by-Component Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Fuel Tank
- Constructed of **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** or **steel** (older vehicles).
- Houses the **in-tank submersible electric pump** and a **fuel level sender unit** (float + variable resistor / rheostat).
- Incorporates an **EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control)** vent valve and charcoal canister to capture HC vapours — a **BS-VI mandatory** feature.

### 2.2.2 Electric Fuel Pump
- **Two main types:**
  * **Roller-vane pump** (most common, 40–80 L/h @ 3 bar)
  * **Gerotor pump** (higher efficiency, used in GDi)
- Powered by **12 V DC** via a relay controlled by the ECU.
- Pump speed is regulated by a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signal** that varies the duty cycle from ~10 % (idle) to 100 % (WOT).
- A **non-return (check) valve** in the pump head maintains residual line pressure after engine shut-off for **hot-restart capability**.

### 2.2.3 Fuel Filter
- Pleated paper element with porosity of **10–25 µm**.
- Must be replaced every **20,000 – 40,000 km** (manufacturer specific).
- Causes a permissible **pressure drop ≤ 50 kPa** at rated flow.

### 2.2.4 Fuel Pressure Regulator
- **Diaphragm-type** mechanical regulator (older MPFI) **or** ECU-controlled solenoid regulator (modern).
- Maintains a **constant pressure differential** across the injector tip — typically **2.5–3.5 bar** above manifold pressure.
- Excess fuel returns to the tank via the **return line**.

### 2.2.5 Fuel Injectors
- **Solenoid-type** (most common) or **Piezoelectric** (direct injection).
- ECU controls the **injection pulse width (t_inj)** in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- Flow rate is measured in **cc/min** or **lb/hr** at a standard test pressure (usually 3 bar for MPFI).
- The injector has two distinct phases:
  * **Opening time (~1.5 ms):** Needle lifts, fuel starts spraying.
  * **Linear (metering) phase:** Fuel flow is proportional to pulse width.

### 2.2.6 Throttle Body
- Houses the **throttle plate** controlling intake air mass.
- Modern systems use **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / Drive-by-Wire)** — no mechanical cable. The ECU commands a **DC motor** to position the throttle based on Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS) input.

### 2.2.7 Engine Control Unit (ECU)
- The "brain" of the fuel system.
- Computes the **base pulse width** using the formula:
  * `T_base = (M_air / (AFR_stoich × Injector_Flow_Rate))`
- Applies **closed-loop correction** based on the **lambda (O₂) sensor** voltage (0.1 V = lean, 0.9 V = rich).
- Uses **adaptive fuel trims (Long Term Fuel Trim – LTFT, Short Term Fuel Trim – STFT)** to compensate for aging, altitude, and component wear.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars below use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (Petrol) | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ | – | Mass ratio |
| 2 | Lambda (Excess Air Factor) | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | – | $\lambda = 1$ → stoich; $\lambda > 1$ → lean; $\lambda < 1$ → rich |
| 3 | Base Injection Pulse Width | $T_{inj} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}}$ | ms (after × 60) | $\dot{m}_{air}$ in kg/s, $\dot{m}_{inj}$ in kg/s |
| 4 | Volumetric Fuel Flow | $Q = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | $A$ = cross-section, $v$ = flow velocity |
| 5 | Injector Volumetric Flow | $Q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | m³/s | $C_d \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ |
| 6 | Pressure Drop in Pipe (Darcy-Weisbach) | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | $f$ = friction factor |
| 7 | Pump Power (Hydraulic) | $P_{pump} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W | For sizing the pump motor |
| 8 | Energy from Fuel (per cycle) | $E_f = m_f \cdot LHV$ | J | LHV ≈ 44 MJ/kg for petrol |
| 9 | Indicated Power (Ideal Otto) | $P_i = \eta_{th} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV$ | W | $\eta_{th} \approx 0.30 - 0.38$ |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (Fuel line) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | – | $Re < 2300$ → laminar; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent |
| 11 | Pump Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{displacement}}$ | – | Typically 0.85–0.95 |
| 12 | Mass Air Flow (Speed-Density) | $\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{man}}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{man}}$ | kg/s | MAP-based calculation |
| 13 | Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Readout | $P_{man} = P_{atm} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_d}{\dot{V}_{intake}}\right)$ | kPa | For naturally aspirated engines |
| 14 | Lambda Sensor Voltage Range | $V_{\lambda} \in \vert 0 \text{ V}, 1 \text{ V} \vert$ | V | Switching type – narrow band |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emissions Compliance:** Every component of the fuel system exists primarily to satisfy **BS-VI / Euro 6** norms. A lean-burn or rich-mix deviation of even **±1 %** can push HC/CO/NOx emissions beyond legal limits.
- **Fuel Economy:** Modern adaptive ECUs use **long-term fuel trims** to compensate injector ageing, improving **km/L** by up to **8–12 %** over the vehicle's lifetime.
- **Cold-Start Performance:** ECU enriches the mixture to **AFR ≈ 9:1** during cranking, then leans it out as the engine warms — a phenomenon called **"cold-start enrichment"** controlled by the **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Injector Pulse Width from First Principles

**Given:**
- Mass air flow into one cylinder per intake stroke: $\dot{m}_{air}$ (kg/s)
- Target lambda value: $\lambda = 1$ (stoichiometric)
- Injector mass flow rate at test pressure: $\dot{m}_{inj}$ (kg/s)
- Number of cylinders: $n$
- Engine speed: $N$ (rev/s)

**Find:** The base injection pulse width $T_{inj}$ for one cylinder per cycle.

---

### Step 1 – Define the target fuel mass per cycle

For one combustion event (one cycle in one cylinder), the air mass inducted is:

$$
m_{air,\,cycle} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{N \cdot n / 2}
$$

> **Reasoning:** A 4-stroke engine completes one firing event per cylinder every **2 revolutions**. With $n$ cylinders firing at $N$ revolutions/sec, the total firings per second is $N \cdot n / 2$. Dividing total air mass flow by this count gives the air mass per firing.

### Step 2 – Apply the stoichiometric relation

The required fuel mass is:

$$
m_{f,\,cycle} = \frac{m_{air,\,cycle}}{AFR_{stoich}}
$$

> **Reasoning:** At $\lambda = 1$, the ratio of air to fuel mass equals the stoichiometric AFR (14.7 for petrol). Hence, fuel mass = air mass ÷ 14.7.

### Step 3 – Convert fuel mass to injection time

The injector delivers fuel at a constant rate $\dot{m}_{inj}$ when energized. So the time it must stay open is:

$$
T_{inj} = \frac{m_{f,\,cycle}}{\dot{m}_{inj}}
$$

### Step 4 – Combine all steps

$$
T_{inj} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{N \cdot n / 2 \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}}
$$

Simplifying the factor of 2:

$$
T_{inj} = \frac{2 \cdot \dot{m}_{air}}{N \cdot n \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}}
$$

> **Note:** In practice, the ECU multiplies this base pulse by a battery-voltage correction factor, a coolant-temp enrichment factor, and a closed-loop trim factor from the lambda sensor.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Style)

> **Problem [Part B Style – 7 Marks]:**
> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **3000 RPM** with a total mass air flow of **0.06 kg/s**. Each injector delivers **2.67 × 10⁻⁴ kg/s** at 3 bar. Calculate the **base injection pulse width** per cylinder. Take $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$.

**Given:**
- $N = 3000/60 = 50$ rev/s
- $n = 4$
- $\dot{m}_{air,\,total} = 0.06$ kg/s
- $\dot{m}_{inj} = 2.67 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s
- $AFR = 14.7$

**Step 1 – Air mass per cylinder per cycle:**

$$
m_{air,\,cycle} = \frac{0.06}{50 \cdot 4 / 2} = \frac{0.06}{100} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 – Required fuel mass:**

$$
m_{f,\,cycle} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 4.08 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 – Pulse width:**

$$
T_{inj} = \frac{4.08 \times 10^{-5}}{2.67 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1528 \text{ s} = 152.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Result:** $T_{inj} \approx 152.8$ ms (base value; actual ECU value would be smaller after applying closed-loop correction back toward stoich).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Drop in a Fuel Line (Darcy–Weisbach)

To size the fuel pump and ensure injector tip pressure stays above specification, we need the line loss.

**Given:** Pipe length $L$, diameter $D$, fluid density $\rho_f \approx 750$ kg/m³ (petrol), velocity $v$, friction factor $f$ (Moody chart or Colebrook equation).

**Equation:**

$$
\Delta P_{line} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho_f \, v^2}{2}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The Darcy–Weisbach equation is an empirical fit for fully-developed pipe flow derived from dimensional analysis of the Navier–Stokes equations.
2. For $Re < 2300$ (laminar), $f = 64 / Re$.
3. For $Re > 4000$ (turbulent), use the **Colebrook–White** implicit relation:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the pipe roughness.

**Sample calculation:** $\varepsilon = 0.05$ mm, $D = 8$ mm, $v = 2$ m/s, $L = 2$ m, $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³.

$$
Re = \frac{750 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.008}{0.0005} = 24000
$$

This is turbulent. From Moody chart, $f \approx 0.028$.

$$
\Delta P = 0.028 \cdot \frac{2}{0.008} \cdot \frac{750 \cdot 4}{2} = 0.028 \cdot 250 \cdot 1500 = 10500 \text{ Pa} = 0.105 \text{ bar}
$$

> **Insight:** Even a modest 8 mm fuel line causes ~0.1 bar loss. The pump must therefore supply rail pressure **+ 0.1 bar** more than the regulator set-point.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Likely KTU Theory Question)

| Feature | Carburetor | MPFI | GDi | CRDi |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Metering | Venturi (mech.) | ECU + Inj. | ECU + Inj. | ECU + Inj. |
| Injection Point | Intake manifold | Port | In-cylinder | In-cylinder |
| Rail Pressure | Atmospheric | 2.5–4 bar | 100–350 bar | 1350–2500 bar |
| AFR Control | Approximate | Closed loop | Closed + Strat. | Always lean (λ>1) |
| Cold Start | Choke | ECU enrich. | ECU enrich. | Glow plug + ECU |
| Atomization Quality | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Emissions Compliance | Pre-BS-II | BS-IV | BS-VI | BS-VI |
| Service Complexity | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern MPFI Fuel System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank\n15 to 60 L HDPE] --> B[In-Tank Electric Pump\nRoller Vane Type]
    B --> C[Fuel Strainer\n80 micron mesh]
    C --> D[Fuel Filter\nPleated Paper 10-25 um]
    D --> E[High Pressure Fuel Line\n8 mm Nylon]
    E --> F[Fuel Rail\nCommon Manifold]
    F --> G1[Injector 1]
    F --> G2[Injector 2]
    F --> G3[Injector 3]
    F --> G4[Injector 4]
    G1 --> H1[Intake Port Cyl 1]
    G2 --> H2[Intake Port Cyl 2]
    G3 --> H3[Intake Port Cyl 3]
    G4 --> H4[Intake Port Cyl 4]
    F -.Return Line.-> R[Pressure Regulator\n3.0 bar]
    R --> A
    H1 --> I[Combustion Chamber\nCylinders 1 to 4]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Exhaust Manifold]
    J --> K[Lambda O2 Sensor]
    K --> L[ECU]
    L -- PWM Signal --> B
    L -- Pulse Width --> G1
    L -- Pulse Width --> G2
    L -- Pulse Width --> G3
    L -- Pulse Width --> G4
    M[MAF or MAP Sensor] --> L
    N[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> L
    O[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> L
    P[Throttle Position Sensor] --> L
```

> **Reading Guide:** Follow the **solid arrows** for fuel flow path and the **dashed arrows** for electronic feedback. The ECU (L) is the central decision node.

---

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Lambda Control – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver Pedal Input] --> B[ECU Target AFR Map\nLambda 1.0 in cruise]
    B --> C[Injector Pulse Width\nCalculation]
    C --> D[Solenoid Injector\nOpens for T_inj ms]
    D --> E[Combustion Chamber\nAir + Fuel]
    E --> F[Exhaust Gas]
    F --> G[Lambda O2 Sensor\n0.1 V to 0.9 V]
    G --> H[Signal Conditioner\nin ECU]
    H --> I{Is AFR greater than 14.7}
    I -- Yes Lean --> J[Increase Pulse Width]
    I -- No Rich --> K[Decrease Pulse Width]
    J --> C
    K --> C
```

> **Note:** The **oxygen sensor** acts as the *feedback transducer*. The ECU executes this loop **every 100 ms** during closed-loop operation (warm engine).

---

## 4.3 Functional Stages of a CRDi System (Diesel Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Feed Pump\nGerotor 0.5 to 2 bar]
    B --> C[Fuel Filter\nWater Separator + 5 um]
    C --> D[High Pressure Pump\n3 Piston Radial]
    D -- 1350 to 2500 bar --> E[Common Rail\nAccumulator]
    E --> F1[Injector Cyl 1]
    E --> F2[Injector Cyl 2]
    E --> F3[Injector Cyl 3]
    E --> F4[Injector Cyl 4]
    F1 --> G1[Cyl 1 Combustion]
    F2 --> G2[Cyl 2 Combustion]
    F3 --> G3[Cyl 3 Combustion]
    F4 --> G4[Cyl 4 Combustion]
    H[Rail Pressure Sensor\nPCB Piezo] --> I[ECU]
    I -- Solenoid Cmd --> F1
    I -- Solenoid Cmd --> F2
    I -- Solenoid Cmd --> F3
    I -- Solenoid Cmd --> F4
    I -- SCV Command --> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain the function of any four major components of an MPFI fuel system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – Approx. 50 words):**

1. **Electric Fuel Pump:** Submersible roller-vane pump in the tank; delivers fuel at 3–4 bar to the rail. *(1 m)*
2. **Fuel Filter:** Pleated paper element of porosity 10–25 µm; removes particulates to protect injectors. *(1 m)*
3. **Pressure Regulator:** Diaphragm-type; maintains constant 3 bar differential across injector tip. *(0.5 m)*
4. **Fuel Injectors:** Solenoid-operated; ECU modulates pulse width to meter fuel into the intake port. *(0.5 m)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "stoichiometric air–fuel ratio"? State its value for petrol and diesel engines.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (AFR)** is the chemically correct mass ratio of air to fuel at which **complete combustion** occurs with no excess of either reactant. *(2 marks)*
- Petrol: **14.7 : 1** *(0.5 m)*
- Diesel: **14.5 : 1** *(0.5 m)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Module 2 contains two questions. **Answer any ONE full question.** Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q3(a) [7 Marks – KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system. List the major sensors and actuators used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**
The in-tank electric pump draws fuel from the tank and pushes it through the fuel filter to the fuel rail. The pressure regulator holds rail pressure at ~3 bar. The ECU, based on inputs from the MAF/MAP, TPS, CTS, and crank position sensors, computes the injection pulse width and energises each solenoid injector once per engine cycle. The injectors spray atomised fuel onto the back of the intake valve (port injection). The lambda sensor in the exhaust provides closed-loop correction.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
[Refer SECTION_4 Diagram 4.1 above – the same Mermaid flow may be redrawn on paper.]

**Sensors and Actuators (1 Mark):**
- **Sensors:** MAF, MAP, TPS, CTS, Crank Position, Lambda O₂
- **Actuators:** Fuel Pump Relay, Injector Coils, Pressure Regulator

#### Q3(b) [7 Marks – KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**A 4-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine runs at 2400 RPM. The total air flow is 0.048 kg/s. The injector flow rate is 240 cc/min at 3 bar (density of petrol = 750 kg/m³). Compute the base injection pulse width per cylinder per cycle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert engine speed.**
$N = 2400 / 60 = 40$ rev/s

**Step 2: Air mass per cylinder per cycle (4-stroke, 2 rev/cycle).**
Total firing events per second = $(N \cdot n) / 2 = (40 \cdot 4)/2 = 80$
$m_{air, cycle} = 0.048 / 80 = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg

**Step 3: Required fuel mass (AFR = 14.7).**
$m_{f, cycle} = (6.0 \times 10^{-4}) / 14.7 = 4.082 \times 10^{-5}$ kg

**Step 4: Convert injector flow to mass basis.**
Volumetric flow = $240$ cc/min $= 240 \times 10^{-6} / 60 = 4.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
Mass flow = $4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 750 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s

**Step 5: Compute pulse width.**
$T_{inj} = (4.082 \times 10^{-5}) / (3.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.0136$ s = **13.6 ms**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mistakes in Pulse-Width Numericals:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the denominator of the cycle mass calculation. A 4-stroke engine has **1 firing per 2 revolutions**, not 1 per revolution. Many students write $m = \dot{m}_{air} / (N \cdot n)$ and lose **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Unit conversion of injector flow.** Always check if the given flow is *cc/min* or *cc/s* or *kg/s*. Mismatched units cost **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rev/s.** Final answer in **ms** is expected. Write "ms" explicitly.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

#### Q4(a) [7 Marks – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the working of a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system used in diesel engines. Compare its rail pressure and atomization with MPFI.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of CRDi (5 Marks):**
In a CRDi system, the **feed pump** (low-pressure gerotor pump) draws diesel from the tank and pushes it through a fuel filter (with water separator) to a **high-pressure pump** (typically a 3-piston radial pump driven by the camshaft). The high-pressure pump generates **1350–2500 bar** of pressure and charges the **common rail** (an accumulator). The rail has a **rail-pressure sensor** and a **pressure-control valve (SCV – Suction Control Valve)**. The ECU meters fuel by energising the **solenoid injectors**, which are triggered twice per cycle — first a small **pilot injection** (~2 mm³) for combustion noise reduction, then the **main injection** for power. Modern piezo injectors can perform **up to 9 injections per cycle** for emissions shaping.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | MPFI | CRDi |
|---|---|---|
| Rail Pressure | 2.5 – 4 bar | 1350 – 2500 bar |
| Atomization | Good | Excellent (sub-µm droplets) |
| Injection Phases | 1 per cycle | 1 to 9 per cycle |
| Engine Type | Petrol (Otto) | Diesel (CI) |

#### Q4(b) [7 Marks – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Derive an expression for the volumetric flow rate through a fuel injector. A GDi injector has an orifice diameter of 0.25 mm and a discharge coefficient of 0.85. The pressure drop across the injector is 120 bar. Calculate the mass flow rate. Take $\rho_{petrol} = 750$ kg/m³.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream side of the injector (1) and the orifice exit (2):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

Since the upstream cross-section is much larger than the orifice, $v_1 \approx 0$.

Rearranging:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

To account for viscous and contraction losses, introduce the **discharge coefficient** $C_d$:

$$
v_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

Volumetric flow rate $Q$ is velocity × area:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}
$$

**Calculation (3 Marks):**
- $D = 0.25$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-8}$ m²
- $\Delta P = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1.2 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $C_d = 0.85$
- $\rho = 750$ kg/m³

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \times \sqrt{2 \times 750 \times 1.2 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2 \times 750 \times 1.2 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{10}} = 1.342 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.342 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 6.587 \times 10^{-3} = 5.60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 5.6 \text{ g/s per injector}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall – Injector Flow Numericals:**
> 1. **Mixing up bar and Pascal.** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa. Students often write 120 Pa instead of 1.2 × 10⁷ Pa, giving a wildly wrong answer.
> 2. **Using diameter instead of radius** in the area formula. $\pi/4 \cdot D^2$ – do not use $\pi r^2$ incorrectly.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_d$.** Without $C_d$, the result is for an *ideal* nozzle. Real flow is ~15 % lower. Always mention $C_d$ in the formula even if the value is 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these 15 bullet points the night before the exam.**

- ☐ **Stoichiometric AFR:** 14.7 (petrol) and 14.5 (diesel) – *by mass*.
- ☐ **MPFI rail pressure:** 2.5–4 bar; **GDi:** 100–350 bar; **CRDi:** 1350–2500 bar.
- ☐ **Lambda (λ) = 1** is the closed-loop target in petrol engines under cruise/WOT.
- ☐ **Lambda sensor output:** 0.1 V (lean) ↔ 0.9 V (rich); switching narrow-band sensor.
- ☐ **Injector pulse width formula:** $T_{inj} = 2 \dot{m}_{air} / (N \cdot n \cdot AFR \cdot \dot{m}_{inj})$ for a 4-stroke engine.
- ☐ **4-stroke cycle rule:** **1 firing per 2 revolutions** – the factor of 2 in the denominator.
- ☐ **Discharge coefficient of injector:** $C_d \approx 0.7 - 0.9$.
- ☐ **Injector flow equation:** $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$.
- ☐ **Darcy–Weisbach pressure loss:** $\Delta P = f (L/D) (\rho v^2 / 2)$ – used for fuel line sizing.
- ☐ **Fuel pump duty cycle** is PWM-controlled by ECU; 10 % idle → 100 % WOT.
- ☐ **Pressure regulator** is downstream of rail and returns excess fuel to tank.
- ☐ **Cold-start enrichment** drives AFR to ~9:1 until coolant reaches 60 °C.
- ☐ **EVAP system** captures fuel vapours via charcoal canister – mandatory under BS-VI.
- ☐ **MAF / MAP sensor** is the primary input to ECU's base pulse-width calculation.
- ☐ **Closed-loop correction** uses **STFT (Short Term Fuel Trim)** and **LTFT (Long Term Fuel Trim)** as multiplicative corrections to the base pulse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Electronic Ignition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Electronic Ignition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔋 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic Ignition System (EIS)** is an automotive ignition architecture in which the primary current in the ignition coil is interrupted by a **solid-state electronic switching device** (transistor, thyristor, or Hall-effect based control module) rather than by the mechanical opening of contact breaker points. The control signal is generated by a triggering mechanism such as a magnetic pickup, Hall-effect sensor, or optical encoder, and processed by an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** to deliver precisely timed, high-energy sparks to the combustion chamber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Anchor**
> Per the OEMRT831 Module 2 syllabus, an electronic ignition system replaces the *mechanical cam-and-contact breaker* with an *electronic trigger and switch*, with the fundamental aim of producing a **higher secondary voltage (typically 25 kV – 40 kV)** and a **longer, more consistent spark duration** across the entire engine RPM band.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Switchboard Story 💡

Imagine an old **mechanical fan regulator** that uses a rotating knob with metallic contacts rubbing against a resistance coil. With time, the contacts **pit, oxidize, spark, and wear out**, and the fan speed becomes erratic. Now replace that with a **solid-state dimmer** that uses a tiny electronic chip to switch the current thousands of times per second — *no moving contacts, no wear, no spark at the switch, perfectly consistent output*. 

A conventional ignition is the **mechanical regulator**. An electronic ignition is the **solid-state dimmer**. The mechanical breaker points physically open and close thousands of times per minute under high current — they **arc, wear, bounce, and drift in timing**. Electronic switching does the same job in **microseconds, silently, and with no contact degradation**.

## 1.3 The Core Engineering Premise

The fundamental purpose of upgrading to electronic ignition is captured in one sentence:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Premise of EIS:** Deliver the *right amount of spark energy*, at the *exact right moment*, with *zero mechanical wear*, across the *entire operating envelope* of the engine — from cold cranking at **–10 °C** to wide-open throttle at **6,000 RPM**.

| Parameter | Conventional Ignition | Electronic Ignition |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Element | Contact Breaker Points | Transistor / Darlington / IGBT |
| Triggering Element | Cam + Mechanical Adjustment | Magnetic / Hall / Optical Pickup |
| Spark Voltage | 15 – 20 kV | 25 – 40 kV |
| Spark Duration | ~0.1 – 0.3 ms | ~1.0 – 2.0 ms (constant) |
| Contact Wear | Yes (major issue) | None |
| Timing Accuracy | ±2° – 4° crankshaft | ±0.5° – 1° crankshaft |
| Maintenance Interval | 10,000 – 15,000 km | 50,000+ km |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of EIS Advantage

The advantages of electronic ignition systems are best understood by grouping them into **seven engineering pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific failure mode of the conventional Kettering system.

### Pillar 1 — Superior Spark Energy & Duration
- The **dwell angle** in a mechanical system is limited because longer contact closure requires a *wider cam lobe* that physically cannot fit at high RPM.
- In electronic systems, **dwell is controlled electronically** as a *time interval* (not an angle), so the coil can be **fully saturated** at all engine speeds.
- This yields a **spark duration 3× – 5× longer** than mechanical systems, ensuring complete combustion of lean and rich mixtures.

### Pillar 2 — Precise Ignition Timing
- Mechanical advance mechanisms (centrifugal + vacuum) are prone to **hysteresis, lag, and wear**.
- Electronic systems use **mapped ignition timing** stored in the ECU, indexed by **RPM, MAP, MAF, coolant temperature, and knock sensor feedback**.
- Timing accuracy improves from **±2° – 4°** to **±0.5°** crankshaft angle.

### Pillar 3 — Higher Secondary Voltage Availability
- The primary circuit inductance and switching speed of a transistor (turn-off time **< 10 µs**) generate a **dI/dt** rate that is **10× – 20× higher** than mechanical points.
- This produces secondary voltages in the range of **25 kV – 40 kV** versus the **15 kV – 20 kV** of conventional systems.

### Pillar 4 — No Contact Wear → Extended Service Life
- Mechanical points suffer **pitting, oxidation, and rubbing-block wear** every time they open under inductive load.
- The transistor switch has **no moving parts** and operates in a sealed environment, leading to a service life often exceeding the vehicle's operational lifetime.

### Pillar 5 — Improved Cold-Start & Idling Performance
- At low temperatures and cranking RPM (**80 – 150 RPM**), the mechanical system's points barely close long enough to saturate the coil.
- Electronic systems maintain **constant energy output** even at cranking speeds because the dwell time is electronically managed.

### Pillar 6 — Reduced Emissions & Better Fuel Economy
- More complete combustion (due to longer spark) → lower **HC and CO emissions**.
- Precise timing → eliminates **retarded/advanced timing losses** → typical fuel economy improvement of **5% – 12%**.

### Pillar 7 — Compatibility with Engine Management Systems
- Electronic ignition is the **prerequisite for closed-loop knock control, EGR integration, and OBD-II diagnostics**.
- Mechanical systems cannot interface with sensors, making them incompatible with modern emission norms (BS-VI / Euro 6).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Takeaway:** When answering "list the advantages" type questions, structure the answer under these seven pillars. Examiners award marks for *grouping* related advantages under meaningful headings rather than presenting a flat bullet list.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{coil} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot L_p \cdot I_p^{2}$ | Stored magnetic energy in primary coil | Maximum spark energy available |
| 2 | $V_s = -L_p \cdot \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | Induced secondary voltage | Spark plug firing voltage |
| 3 | $I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p} \right)$ | Primary current build-up (transient) | Determines minimum dwell time |
| 4 | $I_{p,\max} = \dfrac{V_b}{R_p}$ | Steady-state primary current | Coil saturation current |
| 5 | $t_{dwell} \geq \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - k}\right)$ | Minimum dwell for current ratio $k \cdot I_{p,\max}$ | Electronic dwell control |
| 6 | $\eta_{ign} = \dfrac{E_{spark}}{E_{coil}} \times 100\%$ | Ignition energy transfer efficiency | System quality metric |
| 7 | $V_s = N \cdot L_p \cdot \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | Secondary voltage with turns ratio $N$ | Standard ignition equation |

**Standard Constant for KTU problems:**
- Battery voltage: **$V_b = 12 \text{ V}$**
- Primary inductance: **$L_p \approx 5 \text{ mH – 10 mH}$**
- Primary resistance: **$R_p \approx 0.5 \text{ Ω – 3 Ω}$**
- Turns ratio ($N_p : N_s$): **typically $1 : 60$ to $1 : 100$**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **automotive industry**, electronic ignition is the foundational technology that enabled:
1. **Catalytic converters** to work efficiently (requiring precise air-fuel ratio control).
2. **Turbocharged and direct-injection engines** to run safely (requiring knock-sensor-based timing retard).
3. **Hybrid powertrains** to integrate ICE start-stop cycles (requiring instantaneous, reliable spark at all times).
4. **OBD-II diagnostics** to monitor ignition misfires in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Every gasoline-powered vehicle manufactured post-2010 globally uses some form of **distributor-less electronic ignition (DLI)** or **coil-on-plug (COP)** electronic system — both evolutionary descendants of the basic electronic ignition principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Primary Current Build-up (The Heart of Dwell Control)

**Problem Context (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):**
*A 12 V battery is connected through a transistor switch to an ignition coil with primary inductance $L_p = 6 \text{ mH}$ and primary resistance $R_p = 2 \text{ Ω}$. Calculate (i) the steady-state primary current, (ii) the time required to reach 90% of steady-state current, and (iii) the energy stored in the coil at that moment.*

### Step 1: Write the Governing Differential Equation

When the transistor switch is closed, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the primary circuit gives:

$$V_b = I_p R_p + L_p \frac{dI_p}{dt}$$

This is a first-order linear ODE describing an **RL charging circuit**.

### Step 2: Apply the Standard RL Transient Solution

For an RL circuit with zero initial current ($I_p(0) = 0$), the solution is well-known:

$$I_p(t) = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p} \right)$$

**Logic of conversion:** The term $e^{-R_p t / L_p}$ decays exponentially. As $t \rightarrow \infty$, the exponential term vanishes and current reaches its DC steady-state value.

### Step 3: Calculate the Steady-State Primary Current

Substituting $t \rightarrow \infty$:

$$I_{p,\max} = \frac{V_b}{R_p} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ A}$$

**Logic:** With negligible inductance effect at steady state, the coil acts as a pure resistor.

### Step 4: Calculate the Time to Reach 90% of Steady-State Current

We need $I_p(t) = 0.9 \cdot I_{p,\max}$:

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p}$$

$$e^{-R_p t / L_p} = 0.1$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{R_p t}{L_p} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$

$$t_{90\%} = \frac{2.3026 \cdot L_p}{R_p} = \frac{2.3026 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$t_{90\%} = 6.908 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.91 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Stored Magnetic Energy at $t = t_{90\%}$

The current at $t = t_{90\%}$ is:

$$I_p(t_{90\%}) = 0.9 \times 6 = 5.4 \text{ A}$$

The stored energy is:

$$E_{coil} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times (5.4)^{2}$$

$$E_{coil} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 29.16 = 87.48 \text{ mJ}$$

### Step 6: KTU Insight — Why This Matters for Electronic Ignition

In a **mechanical system at 6,000 RPM**, the points close for only:

$$t_{close} = \frac{\text{dwell angle}}{360°} \times \frac{60}{N} = \frac{30°}{360°} \times \frac{60}{6000} = 833 \, \mu s = 0.83 \text{ ms}$$

This is **less than 1 ms** — the coil cannot reach 90% saturation. The current might be only:

$$I_p(0.83 \text{ ms}) = 6 \left( 1 - e^{-2 \times 0.83 \times 10^{-3} / 6 \times 10^{-3}} \right) = 6 \left( 1 - e^{-0.277} \right) = 6 \times 0.242 = 1.45 \text{ A}$$

This is only **24% of steady state** — explaining the **weak spark at high RPM** in conventional systems.

In an **electronic system**, the ECU extends dwell time to ensure full saturation, delivering **full 87.48 mJ of energy at all RPM**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Secondary Voltage (Spark Voltage)

### Step 1: Define the Induced EMF Equation

When the transistor opens, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a voltage:

$$V_s = -N \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \cdot L_p \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt}$$

where $N$ is the **turns ratio** $N_s / N_p$.

### Step 2: Estimate dI/dt for Electronic vs Mechanical

**For electronic switch (transistor turn-off time $\approx 5 \, \mu s$):**

$$\frac{dI_p}{dt} = \frac{6 \text{ A}}{5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \text{ A/s}$$

**For mechanical points (opening time $\approx 100 \, \mu s$ due to contact bounce):**

$$\frac{dI_p}{dt} = \frac{6}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 6 \times 10^{4} \text{ A/s}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Secondary Voltages

With $N = 80$, $L_p = 6 \text{ mH}$:

**Electronic:**

$$V_s = 80 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} = 576{,}000 \text{ V} = 576 \text{ kV (theoretical)}$$

In practice, the **spark plug arc-over at ~25 kV** clips the voltage — but this guarantees a *strong, reliable spark* even with fouled plugs.

**Mechanical:**

$$V_s = 80 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 \times 10^{4} = 28{,}800 \text{ V} = 28.8 \text{ kV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Insight:** The key takeaway is **not the raw voltage** but the *availability of margin*. Electronic systems deliver 25 kV with comfortable margin even under adverse conditions (high cylinder pressure, lean mixture, cold plug), whereas mechanical systems hover right at the breakdown threshold of the plug gap.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Ready)

| Performance Parameter | Conventional (Points) | Electronic (EIS) | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Spark Energy (mJ) | 20 – 30 | 80 – 120 | **4×** |
| Spark Duration (ms) | 0.1 – 0.3 | 1.0 – 2.0 | **5×** |
| Secondary Voltage (kV) | 15 – 20 | 25 – 40 | **2×** |
| Timing Accuracy (° crank) | ±2° – 4° | ±0.5° | **4× – 8×** |
| Dwell Control | Mechanical (cam) | Electronic (ECU) | **Adaptive** |
| Service Interval (km) | 10,000 | 50,000+ | **5×** |
| Cold-Start Spark Quality | Poor (weak) | Excellent (constant) | **Critical** |
| Compatibility with Sensors | None | Full (MAP, MAF, Knock) | **Enabling** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electronic Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery 12V] --> SW[Transistor Switch Module]
    SW --> COIL[Ignition Coil Lp and Np:Ns]
    COIL -->|High Voltage kV| SP1[Spark Plug Cyl 1]
    COIL --> SP2[Spark Plug Cyl 2]
    COIL --> SP3[Spark Plug Cyl 3]
    COIL --> SP4[Spark Plug Cyl 4]
    TRIG[Trigger Pickup Hall or Magnetic] --> ECU[Electronic Control Unit ECU]
    SENS[Engine Sensors MAP MAF TPS Knock] --> ECU
    ECU -->|Dwell and Timing Control Signal| SW
    ECU -->|Ignition Advance Map| DWELL[Dwell Controller]
    DWELL --> SW
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU:** The ECU receives the trigger pulse from the pickup and, based on mapped data from sensors, generates a *variable-width dwell pulse* to the transistor. The transistor then switches the primary coil current at the precise crank angle, producing the high-voltage secondary discharge at the correct plug.

## 4.2 Comparative Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Engine] --> B{Type of Ignition System}
    B -->|Conventional Points| C1[Cam lobe lifts contact arm]
    C1 --> D1[Contact opens under inductive load]
    D1 --> E1[Arc and wear occur at contacts]
    E1 --> F1[Primary current decays slowly]
    F1 --> G1[Weak secondary voltage generated]
    G1 --> H1[Spark duration short and inconsistent]
    B -->|Electronic Ignition| C2[Trigger sensor detects crank position]
    C2 --> D2[ECU calculates timing and dwell]
    D2 --> E2[ECU sends base signal to transistor]
    E2 --> F2[Transistor switches off in microseconds]
    F2 --> G2[Fast dI by dt produces high kV]
    G2 --> H2[Strong long spark of constant energy]
    H1 --> I[Result: Poor combustion and high emissions]
    H2 --> J[Result: Complete combustion and low emissions]
```

## 4.3 Signal Conditioning & Dwell Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CRK[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> SHAP[Signal Shaping and Amplification]
    SHAP --> ECU1[ECU Mapped Advance Calculation]
    KNK[Knock Sensor] --> DET[Knock Detection Circuit]
    DET --> ECU1
    MAP[MAP Sensor] --> ECU1
    ECU1 --> COMP[Dwell Comparator]
    COMP --> DRV[Base Driver for Power Transistor]
    DRV --> PWR[Power Darlington or IGBT Switch]
    PWR --> PRIM[Primary Winding of Ignition Coil]
    PRIM --> SEC[Secondary Winding]
    SEC --> ARC[Spark Plug Arc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of an electronic ignition system," include the **closed-loop knock feedback path** as shown above. Examiners in the 2024 scheme specifically reward students who depict the *sensor → ECU → actuator* loop, demonstrating an understanding of modern closed-loop control rather than a simple open-loop trigger.

## 4.4 Energy Flow Schematic (EIS vs Conventional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ConventionalPath[Conventional Energy Path]
        EB1[Battery] --> EP1[Primary Coil]
        EP1 --> ECAM1[Cam and Contact Points]
        ECAM1 -->|Losses 30 percent| ESP1[Spark Energy 20 mJ]
    end
    subgraph ElectronicPath[Electronic Energy Path]
        EB2[Battery] --> EP2[Primary Coil]
        EP2 --> ETR2[Transistor Switch]
        ETR2 -->|Losses less than 5 percent| ESP2[Spark Energy 100 mJ]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any three advantages of electronic ignition systems over conventional contact-breaker ignition systems.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
1. **Higher and more consistent spark energy** — Electronic dwell control ensures the coil reaches full magnetic saturation at all engine speeds, unlike mechanical systems where the dwell angle is limited at high RPM.
2. **No contact wear and reduced maintenance** — The transistor replaces mechanical contact points, eliminating pitting, oxidation, and the need for periodic gap adjustment.
3. **Improved ignition timing accuracy** — ECU-mapped timing (using MAP, MAF, and knock sensors) provides accuracy within ±0.5° crankshaft, compared to ±2°–4° in mechanical advance mechanisms. *(1 mark each)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is spark duration longer in an electronic ignition system?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
The spark duration in an electronic ignition system is longer (typically **1.0 – 2.0 ms** vs **0.1 – 0.3 ms** in conventional systems) because:
1. The **dwell period is controlled electronically** as a function of RPM and battery voltage, ensuring the coil is *fully saturated* before discharge, providing higher stored energy ($E = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{2}$).
2. The transistor's **fast switching** maintains secondary current flow through the plug arc for a longer interval since there is no contact bounce or arc-quenching at the primary switch.
3. The **higher stored energy** sustains the arc across the plug gap even as the cylinder pressure rises during combustion, prolonging the discharge. *(1 mark per point)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully**
> Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** on this topic by:
> - Writing generic "advantages" without **quantitative justification** (e.g., not stating "25 kV vs 15 kV").
> - Confusing **electronic ignition** with **distributor-less ignition (DLI)** or **coil-on-plug (COP)** — these are *types* of EIS, not separate systems.
> - Forgetting to mention the **knock sensor feedback loop** when discussing timing accuracy.
> - Skipping the **energy formula** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{2}$ when explaining spark energy.

---

### **Question 3 — Choice A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an electronic ignition system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the advantages of electronic ignition systems over conventional ignition systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working of Electronic Ignition System:**

**Step 1 — Trigger Generation (2 marks):**
A magnetic pickup sensor (reluctor) placed near a timing disc on the crankshaft generates a small **AC voltage pulse** each time a tooth passes the magnet. This pulse marks the precise crank position. Alternative: Hall-effect sensor produces a digital square wave.

**Step 2 — ECU Processing (2 marks):**
The trigger pulse is fed to the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**. The ECU consults its **ignition advance map**, indexed by:
- Engine RPM (from crankshaft sensor frequency)
- Engine load (from MAP / MAF sensor)
- Coolant temperature
- Knock sensor feedback

The ECU then computes the **ignition advance angle** (typically 10° – 40° BTDC) and the **required dwell time** to saturate the coil.

**Step 3 — Transistor Switching (2 marks):**
The ECU outputs a **base drive signal** to a power transistor (Darlington pair or IGBT). During the dwell interval, the transistor is ON and primary current builds in the coil. At the precise crank angle, the transistor switches OFF in **< 10 µs**, interrupting the primary current.

**Step 4 — High-Voltage Generation and Spark (1 mark):**
The sudden collapse of magnetic flux induces a high voltage in the secondary (≈ 25 kV – 40 kV), which arcs across the spark plug gap, igniting the air-fuel mixture.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Detailed Advantages of Electronic Ignition Systems:**

| # | Advantage | Quantitative / Technical Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Higher spark energy** | $E_{coil} = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{2}$ reaches **80 – 120 mJ** vs 20 – 30 mJ in points; constant across all RPM. |
| 2 | **Longer spark duration** | **1 – 2 ms** vs 0.1 – 0.3 ms — ensures complete combustion of lean mixtures. |
| 3 | **Higher secondary voltage** | **25 – 40 kV** vs 15 – 20 kV — guaranteed arc-over even with fouled plugs. |
| 4 | **Precise timing control** | ECU-mapped accuracy of **±0.5°** crank vs ±2° – 4° mechanical; incorporates knock retard. |
| 5 | **No contact wear** | Transistor has **no moving parts**; service interval extends from 10,000 km to 50,000+ km. |
| 6 | **Better cold-start performance** | Electronic dwell maintains coil saturation at cranking speeds (80 – 150 RPM). |
| 7 | **Lower emissions & better economy** | 5% – 12% fuel economy improvement; HC and CO reduced by 20% – 40%. |
| 8 | **Enables modern features** | Compatible with knock control, EGR, OBD-II misfire detection, and start-stop systems. |

**Concluding Statement (compulsory for full marks):**
Thus, electronic ignition systems provide superior performance, reliability, and emissions compliance, making them the universal standard in all post-1990 gasoline vehicles. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 3 — Choice B (Alternative Question for Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: (a)** Compare conventional and electronic ignition systems with respect to spark energy, spark duration, timing accuracy, maintenance, and emission levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ignition coil has a primary inductance of 8 mH and primary resistance of 1.5 Ω. It is connected to a 12 V battery through an electronic switch. Calculate: (i) the maximum primary current, (ii) the time to reach 95% of maximum current, and (iii) the energy stored in the coil at that instant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (each row carries ~0.5 – 1 mark):**

| Parameter | Conventional Ignition | Electronic Ignition | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spark Energy | 20 – 30 mJ | 80 – 120 mJ | EIS **4× higher** |
| Spark Duration | 0.1 – 0.3 ms | 1.0 – 2.0 ms | EIS **5× longer** |
| Timing Accuracy | ±2° – 4° | ±0.5° | EIS **4× – 8× better** |
| Maintenance | Every 10,000 km | 50,000+ km | EIS **5× lower** |
| Emissions | Higher HC & CO | 20% – 40% lower | EIS **cleaner** |
| Cold Starting | Weak spark | Strong spark | EIS **superior** |
| Cost | Low | Moderate-High | Conventional **cheaper** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Despite higher initial cost, the long-term benefits of EIS in performance, durability, and environmental compliance make it the unequivocal choice for modern vehicles.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $L_p = 8 \text{ mH} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$
- $R_p = 1.5 \text{ Ω}$
- $V_b = 12 \text{ V}$

**Part (i): Maximum Primary Current (2 marks)**

$$I_{p,\max} = \frac{V_b}{R_p} = \frac{12}{1.5} = 8 \text{ A}$$

**Part (ii): Time to Reach 95% of Maximum Current (3 marks)**

Setting $I_p(t) = 0.95 \cdot I_{p,\max}$:

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p}$$

$$e^{-R_p t / L_p} = 0.05$$

$$-\frac{R_p t}{L_p} = \ln(0.05) = -2.9957$$

$$t_{95\%} = \frac{2.9957 \times L_p}{R_p} = \frac{2.9957 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5}$$

$$t_{95\%} = \frac{23.966 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5} = 15.98 \text{ ms}$$

**Part (iii): Energy Stored at 95% Saturation (2 marks)**

$$I_p(t_{95\%}) = 0.95 \times 8 = 7.6 \text{ A}$$

$$E_{coil} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times (7.6)^{2}$$

$$E_{coil} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 57.76 = 231.04 \text{ mJ}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- (i) $I_{p,\max} = 8 \text{ A}$
- (ii) $t_{95\%} \approx 15.98 \text{ ms}$
- (iii) $E_{coil} \approx 231.04 \text{ mJ}$ *(1 mark reserved for unit correctness and proper final boxed answer)*

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 OEMRT831 Module 2**

- ✅ **Electronic Ignition** = solid-state switch + electronic trigger (no moving contact points).
- ✅ **Spark Voltage:** 25 kV – 40 kV (EIS) vs 15 kV – 20 kV (Conventional).
- ✅ **Spark Duration:** 1 – 2 ms (EIS) vs 0.1 – 0.3 ms (Conventional).
- ✅ **Spark Energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{2}$; EIS delivers 80 – 120 mJ.
- ✅ **Timing Accuracy:** ±0.5° (EIS) vs ±2° – 4° (Conventional) — enabled by ECU mapping + knock feedback.
- ✅ **Dwell Control:** Electronic (time-based) vs Mechanical (angle-based cam lobe).
- ✅ **No Contact Wear:** Transistor switches have no moving parts; service life > 50,000 km.
- ✅ **Cold-Start:** EIS maintains coil saturation even at 80 – 150 RPM cranking speed.
- ✅ **Emissions:** EIS reduces HC/CO by 20% – 40% via complete combustion and precise timing.
- ✅ **Fuel Economy:** 5% – 12% improvement over contact-breaker systems.
- ✅ **Enabling Technology:** EIS is the foundation for knock control, EGR, OBD-II, and start-stop systems.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $V_s = N \cdot L_p \cdot \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ — explains why faster switching = higher voltage.
- ✅ **Master Time Constant:** $\tau = L_p / R_p$ — defines the minimum dwell for full saturation.
- ✅ **Types of EIS to mention in long answers:** Transistor-Assisted Contact (TAC), Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI), and Distributor-Less Ignition (DLI).
- ✅ **Always end Part B answers** with a concluding line on why EIS is the modern standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CI Engine Management: Parameters Affecting Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CI Engine Management: Parameters Affecting Combustion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine Combustion Management** refers to the coordinated electronic and mechanical control of fuel injection, air handling, and in-cylinder thermodynamic conditions that govern the auto-ignition, premixed burn, and diffusion-controlled phases of combustion in a diesel (or HCCI/RCCI) power plant. The principal *parameters affecting combustion* in a CI engine are those inputs that the Engine Control Unit (ECU) modulates in real time — namely **injection timing (SOI)**, **injection pressure**, **injection quantity/duration**, **number and phasing of pilot/main/post injections**, **EGR rate**, **swirl ratio**, **boost pressure**, **intake air temperature**, and **fuel rail pressure** — together with the engine's geometrically fixed parameters such as **compression ratio**, **combustion chamber geometry**, and **fuel properties (cetane number, viscosity, volatility)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Under "CI Engine Management", the board specifically tests the *cause-effect relationship* between each control parameter and the three combustion phases — **Ignition Delay, Premixed Combustion, and Diffusion (Mixing-Controlled) Combustion**. Marks are routinely awarded for correctly identifying *which phase* a given parameter dominantly affects. ## 1.2 The Three Phases of CI Combustion Every CI combustion event is partitioned into three thermodynamically distinct stages: | Phase | Duration (crank angle) | Governing Phenomenon | Dominant Parameters | |---|---|---|---| | **Phase 1 – Ignition Delay** | 0.5°–15° CA | Physico-chemical preparation: atomization, vaporization, mixing, pre-flame reactions | Cetane number, compression temp/pressure, EGR, injection pressure, swirl | | **Phase 2 – Premixed Combustion** | Rapid (10°–40° CA) | Uncontrolled burning of fuel vapor accumulated during delay | Injection timing, ignition delay, spray atomization | | **Phase 3 – Diffusion Combustion** | Slow (40°–~80° CA) | Rate controlled by mixing of fuel jet with surrounding air | Injection pressure, spray geometry, swirl, EGR, load | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker & Sprayer":** > Imagine lighting a campfire in a strong gusty wind. (1) You first arrange the wood (fuel) and let the ambient heat warm it — this is the **Ignition Delay** phase. (2) When a single match is struck, all the pre-heated wood bursts into flames almost simultaneously — this is the **Premixed Combustion** (the loud "diesel knock"). (3) Thereafter, only the wood that mixes with oxygen as the wind blows continues to burn — this is the slow **Diffusion Combustion**. > The **ECU** is the campfire master: it decides *when* to add wood (injection timing), *how finely* to chop it (injection pressure → atomization), and *how much* wind to allow (EGR rate). Poor tuning gives either smoke (incomplete diffusion burn) or a violent flare-up (excessive premixed burn = high noise & NOx). ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Autotronics Modern CI engines (Common-Rail Diesel, SCR-equipped, Euro-VI/BS-VI compliant) use **Closed-Loop Combustion Control**. Sensors such as the **in-cylinder pressure sensor (piezoelectric)**, **needle-lift sensor**, **ion-current sensor**, and **lambda/NOx sensors** feed the ECU, which adjusts the parameters in real time to simultaneously minimize **NOx, PM (soot), BSFC, and noise**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Recap:** > - Stoichiometric A/F ratio for diesel (C₁₂H₂₃): **A/F_st ≈ 14.5 : 1** > - Diesel always runs **lean (φ < 1)**, typically φ = 0.2 – 0.8 > - Cetane Number (CN) of standard diesel: **CN ≈ 45–55** > - Compression ratio of CI engine: **CR = 14 : 1 to 22 : 1** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Parameter-by-Parameter Cause-Effect Analysis ### 2.1.1 Injection Timing (Start of Injection – SOI) - **Definition:** Crank-angle position (° bTDC) at which the injector needle begins to lift and fuel exits the nozzle. - **Effect:** - **Advanced SOI** (more ° bTDC) → longer ignition delay → more fuel accumulates → **louder premixed burn**, **higher peak pressure (P_max)**, **higher NOx**, **lower smoke**. - **Retarded SOI** → shorter delay → less premixed burn → **lower P_max**, **lower NOx**, **higher smoke & BSFC**, **higher HC & CO**. - **Typical optimal:** 8°–12° bTDC at rated load and speed. ### 2.1.2 Injection Pressure (Common-Rail Pressure) - Modern Common-Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) systems operate between **200 bar (idle)** and **2000 bar (peak)**. - **Effect:** - Higher P_inj → finer atomization (smaller Sauter Mean Diameter, SMD) → faster vaporization → **shorter ignition delay**, **lower soot**, **lower BSFC**. - Trade-off: extremely high pressure (>2000 bar) increases **in-cylinder penetration** causing wall-wetting → **higher HC & PM**. ### 2.1.3 EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) - Dilutes intake charge with inert combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, N₂). - **Effect:** - Lowers flame temperature → **drastically reduces NOx** (up to 50–70%). - Reduces O₂ partial pressure → **lengthens ignition delay** → more premixed fuel accumulation → slight rise in noise. - Excessive EGR (>35%) → **smoke increases sharply**, BSFC rises. ### 2.1.4 Swirl Ratio (S_R) - $S_R = \dfrac{\omega_{swirl}}{\omega_{engine}}$ — tangential rotation of intake air. - **Effect:** - Improves air-fuel mixing → **shorter diffusion burn duration**, **lower smoke**, **higher thermal efficiency**. - Excessive swirl → excessive heat loss to walls → **lower η_th**. ### 2.1.5 Compression Ratio (CR) - Higher CR → higher end-of-compression temperature (T₃) and pressure (P₃) → **shorter ignition delay**, **higher thermal efficiency**, but **higher peak pressure and NOx**. ### 2.1.6 Boost Pressure (Turbocharging) - Higher intake manifold pressure → higher **p_O₂** → shorter ignition delay, more complete combustion, higher specific output. ### 2.1.7 Multiple Injection Strategies A modern CRDi ECU may schedule up to **9 injections per cycle**: - **Pilot** (small SOI very early) → reduces ignition delay duration → softer combustion → **lower noise & NOx**. - **Main** (primary energy release). - **After/Post** (very late) → soot oxidation on the expansion stroke → **lower PM**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** Most 14-mark KTU questions either (a) ask you to *derive* the heat-release equation from the first law, or (b) ask you to compute the Weibe parameters from cylinder-pressure data. Memorise the table below. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Ignition Delay (Arrhenius model)** | $\tau_{id} = A \cdot p_{O_2}^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$ | $p_{O_2}$ in MPa; T in K; $E_a$ ≈ 30–80 kJ/mol; $n \approx 1\text{–}2$ | | 2 | **Weibe Mass-Burned Fraction** | $x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a \left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$ | $\theta_0$: start of combustion (°CA); $\Delta\theta$: burn duration; $m$: shape factor | | 3 | **Net (Apparent) Heat Release Rate** | $\dfrac{dQ_n}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\gamma-1} V \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$ | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$; $\theta$: crank angle | | 4 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{W_b} \times 10^3$ | g/kWh | | 5 | **Indicated Mean Effective Pressure** | $\text{IMEP} = \dfrac{W_i}{V_d}$ | bar | | 6 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{W_b}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | $\phi < 1$: lean; $\phi > 1$: rich | | 8 | **Cetane Number – Ignition Delay Correlation** | $\tau_{id} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{CN}^k}$ | $k \approx 0.5\text{–}1.0$ | | 9 | **Sauter Mean Diameter (atomization)** | $\text{SMD} \propto \dfrac{(\sigma\,\rho_l)^{0.5}}{\rho_g^{0.5} \cdot \Delta P^{0.5}}$ | $\sigma$: surface tension; $\Delta P$: rail-nozzle ΔP | | 10 | **Swirl Ratio** | $S_R = \dfrac{\omega_{swirl}}{\omega_{engine}}$ | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraint on Equivalence Ratio for Diesel:** Diesel always operates **φ = 0.2 – 0.8** (overall lean). Local equivalence ratio in the spray varies widely — fuel-rich pockets cause **soot**, fuel-lean pockets cause **NOx**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The relationships above are the foundation of **Engine Calibration Maps** stored in the ECU's non-volatile memory. Each map is a 3D look-up table indexed by (RPM, Pedal Position, Coolant Temp) that outputs the optimum values of SOI, rail pressure, EGR valve duty cycle, and VGT (Variable Geometry Turbine) vane position. Without mastery of these cause-effect chains, no modern OEM can pass a **BS-VI / Euro-VI emission certification**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apparent Heat Release Rate from First Law We begin with the **open-system first-law energy equation** applied to the cylinder contents, assuming uniform pressure and temperature at each crank angle: $$\frac{dQ_{B}}{d\theta} - \frac{dW}{d\theta} = \frac{dU}{d\theta}$$ Substituting $W = P\,dV$ (boundary work) and $U = m\,c_v\,T$, with the ideal-gas relation $P\,V = m\,R\,T$: $$\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = P \frac{dV}{d\theta} + \frac{dU}{d\theta}$$ Differentiating $P V = m R T$ with respect to $\theta$ (holding mass $m$ constant during the closed part of the cycle): $$\frac{d(PV)}{d\theta} = m R \frac{dT}{d\theta} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P \frac{dV}{d\theta} + V \frac{dP}{d\theta} = m R \frac{dT}{d\theta}$$ Since $c_v = \dfrac{R}{\gamma - 1}$, we have $\dfrac{dU}{d\theta} = m c_v \dfrac{dT}{d\theta} = \dfrac{m R}{\gamma-1} \dfrac{dT}{d\theta}$. Substituting: $$\frac{dU}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{\gamma-1}\left( P \frac{dV}{d\theta} + V \frac{dP}{d\theta} \right)$$ Substituting back into the first law: $$\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = P \frac{dV}{d\theta} + \frac{1}{\gamma-1}\left( P \frac{dV}{d\theta} + V \frac{dP}{d\theta} \right)$$ Factoring the $P\,\dfrac{dV}{d\theta}$ terms: $$\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P \frac{dV}{d\theta} + \frac{1}{\gamma-1} V \frac{dP}{d\theta}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **Apparent (Gross) Heat Release Rate** equation. To obtain the **Net (Cumulative) Heat Release**, integrate $dQ_B/d\theta$ over the combustion interval. The negative of $dQ_B/d\theta$ after peak pressure gives the **heat transfer rate to walls**, which must be subtracted in a "net" model. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weibe Function Origin and Properties The **Weibe function** is a statistical distribution function (originally used for fatigue-life analysis) adapted to model the cumulative mass fraction burned. It satisfies the empirical requirements: $x_b(\theta_0) = 0$ at combustion onset, and $x_b \to 1$ as $\theta \to \infty$. Define $\eta = \dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}$ (normalized crank angle, dimensionless). The Weibe cumulative distribution is: $$x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp(-a\,\eta^{m+1})$$ Differentiating with respect to $\theta$ gives the **mass burning rate (MBR)**: $$\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,\eta^{m}\,\exp(-a\,\eta^{m+1})$$ Setting the MBR derivative to zero to find the crank angle of maximum mass-burn rate: $$\frac{d^2 x_b}{d\theta^2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta_{max} = \left(\frac{m}{a(m+1)}\right)^{1/(m+1)}$$ For a typical diesel, $a = 5$, $m = 2$, giving $\eta_{max} \approx 0.41$, i.e., peak mass-burn rate occurs roughly **41% into the combustion duration** — confirming the asymmetric, rapid-rise/slow-tail shape of diesel combustion. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cetane Number Effect on Ignition Delay Empirically, the ignition delay $\tau_{id}$ scales inversely with cetane number raised to a small exponent: $$\tau_{id} \approx K \cdot \text{CN}^{-k}, \quad k \approx 0.6\text{ (typical)}$$ **Worked Numerical Example:** Given two fuels with CN = 40 and CN = 55, find the ratio of their ignition delays (assume $k = 0.6$). $$\frac{\tau_{id,1}}{\tau_{id,2}} = \left(\frac{\text{CN}_2}{\text{CN}_1}\right)^{0.6} = \left(\frac{55}{40}\right)^{0.6}$$ Computing: $$\frac{\tau_{id,1}}{\tau_{id,2}} = (1.375)^{0.6}$$ $$\ln(1.375) = 0.3185$$ $$0.6 \times 0.3185 = 0.1911$$ $$e^{0.1911} = 1.2106$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\tau_{id,1}}{\tau_{id,2}} \approx 1.21}$$ **Interpretation:** A higher-cetane fuel (CN 55) reduces ignition delay by **~17%** compared to CN 40. This directly translates into lower premixed-combustion pressure rise and lower noise — the reason modern high-speed automotive diesels demand CN ≥ 51. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Engine Outputs > [!NOTE] > **How to read this matrix:** "+" = parameter increase *increases* the output; "−" = parameter increase *decreases* the output. "↑↑" = strong positive correlation. | Parameter ↑ | P_max | NOx | Soot/PM | BSFC | Noise | η_th | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **SOI advanced** | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↑ | | **Injection Pressure** | ↑ | ↑ | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑↑ | | **EGR rate** | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | | **Cetane Number** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | | **Compression Ratio** | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑↑ | | **Swirl Ratio** | — | — | ↓ | ↓ | — | ↑ | | **Boost Pressure** | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑↑ | | **Intake Air Temp** | — | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | — | ↓ | | **Pilot Injection Quantity** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | | **Coolant Temperature** | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ## 3.5 Complete Worked Numerical Problem **Problem (typical KTU 14-mark type):** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder CI engine has bore $D = 0.085$ m, stroke $L = 0.090$ m, and rated speed $N = 3000$ rpm. At full load, indicated work per cycle is $W_i = 380$ J/cyl, brake work is $W_b = 320$ J/cyl, fuel mass per cycle per cyl is $m_f = 0.022$ g, calorific value CV = 43 MJ/kg. Calculate: (a) IMEP, (b) BMEP, (c) indicated thermal efficiency, (d) BSFC. **Solution:** Displacement per cylinder: $$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.085)^2 (0.090)$$ $$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.007225 \times 0.090 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.5025 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$V_d = 5.107 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 510.7 \text{ cm}^3$$ **(a) IMEP:** $$\text{IMEP} = \frac{W_i}{V_d} = \frac{380}{5.107 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.44 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 7.44 \text{ bar}$$ **(b) BMEP:** $$\text{BMEP} = \frac{W_b}{V_d} = \frac{320}{5.107 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.27 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 6.27 \text{ bar}$$ **(c) Indicated Thermal Efficiency:** Heat supplied per cycle: $$Q_{in} = m_f \times \text{CV} = 0.022 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} \times 43 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg} = 946 \text{ J}$$ $$\eta_{it} = \frac{W_i}{Q_{in}} = \frac{380}{946} = 0.4017 = 40.17\%$$ **(d) BSFC:** Number of power strokes per second per cylinder (4-stroke: $N/2$ cycles/sec/cyl): $$\text{Cycles/s} = \frac{3000}{2 \times 60} = 25 \text{ cycles/s/cyl}$$ Fuel mass flow per cylinder: $$\dot{m}_f = m_f \times \text{cycles/s} = 0.022 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ Brake power per cylinder: $$P_b = W_b \times 25 = 320 \times 25 = 8000 \text{ W} = 8 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} \times 10^{3} = \frac{5.5 \times 10^{-4}}{8000} \times 10^{3} = 6.875 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{3}$$ $$\boxed{\text{BSFC} = 0.0688 \text{ g/kJ} \;\; \text{or} \;\; 247.5 \text{ g/kWh}}$$ > [!NOTE] > A typical modern BSFC for an automotive CI engine is **200–220 g/kWh**. A value of 247.5 g/kWh is acceptable for a non-turbocharged, naturally-aspirated baseline. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1 — Three Phases of CI Combustion (P-θ Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1: IGNITION DELAY (tau_id)"] A1[SOI: Injector Opens] --> A2[Fuel Atomization] A2 --> A3[Fuel Vaporization] A3 --> A4[Air-Fuel Mixing] A4 --> A5{Premixed Reactants Ready?} A5 -->|Yes| A6[Auto-Ignition] end subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2: PREMIXED BURN"] A6 --> B1[Rapid Heat Release] B1 --> B2[Peak Pressure P_max] B2 --> B3[High dP/dtheta] end subgraph PHASE3["PHASE 3: DIFFUSION BURN"] B3 --> C1[Spray-Entrained Mixing] C1 --> C2[Slow Heat Release] C2 --> C3[Burn End: theta_b] end C3 --> D1[Expansion Stroke] D1 --> E1[Exhaust Valve Opens] style PHASE1 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style PHASE2 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style PHASE3 fill:#e1eaff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Figure 2 — CI Engine Parameter Control Architecture (ECU Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSORS["INPUT SENSORS"] S1[Crankshaft Position Sensor] S2[Camshaft Position Sensor] S3[Rail Pressure Sensor] S4[Cylinder Pressure Sensor] S5[Lambda / NOx Sensor] S6[Coolant Temp Sensor] S7[Intake Air Temp Sensor] S8[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] end ECU{CENTRAL ECU
Microcontroller + ASIC} subgraph ACTUATORS["OUTPUT ACTUATORS"] M1[Solenoid / Piezo Injector] M2[Rail Pressure Control Valve] M3[EGR Valve Stepper Motor] M4[VGT Vane Actuator] M5[Throttle Valve Motor] M6[Glow Plug Relay] end S1 --> ECU S2 --> ECU S3 --> ECU S4 --> ECU S5 --> ECU S6 --> ECU S7 --> ECU S8 --> ECU ECU -->|PWM Injection Signal| M1 ECU -->|Pressure Demand| M2 ECU -->|EGR Duty Cycle| M3 ECU -->|Vane Position| M4 ECU -->|Throttle Angle| M5 ECU -->|Cold-Start Pulse| M6 ECU -.->|Closed-Loop Feedback| S4 ECU -.->|Closed-Loop Feedback| S5 style SENSORS fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style ACTUATORS fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000 style ECU fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Figure 3 — Cause-Effect Chain of Parameters on Combustion Outputs ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Injection Timing SOI] --> O1[NOx] P1 --> O2[Smoke] P1 --> O3[Noise] P2[Injection Pressure] --> O1 P2 --> O2 P2 --> O4[BSFC] P3[EGR Rate] --> O1 P3 --> O2 P3 --> O4 P3 --> O5[P_max] P4[Swirl Ratio] --> O2 P4 --> O4 P4 --> O6[Thermal Efficiency] P5[Cetane Number] --> O3 P5 --> O7[Ignition Delay] P7[Compression Ratio] --> O7 P7 --> O6 P6[Pilot Injection] --> O3 P6 --> O1 P6 --> O2 style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style P2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style P3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style P4 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style P5 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style P6 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style P7 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style O1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style O2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style O3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style O4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style O5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style O6 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style O7 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 ``` ##
