- **CCA (Cold Cranking Amps):** The maximum current a fully charged battery can deliver for **30 seconds** at **−18 °C** while maintaining terminal voltage **≥ 7.2 V** for a 12 V battery. *(1 Mark)*
- **RC (Reserve Capacity):** The duration in minutes that a fully charged battery at **27 °C** can sustain a load of **25 A** before its terminal voltage drops below **10.5 V**. *(1 Mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **Main electrical power distribution architecture** of a modern automobile. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A starter motor draws **200 A** through a copper cable of **one-way length 1.2 m**. The maximum allowable voltage drop in the starter circuit is **0.4 V**. Determine:
  (i) The minimum required cable cross-section.
  (ii) The nearest standard cable size.
  (iii) The power loss in the cable during cranking.
*(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Main Electrical Power Distribution Architecture *(7 Marks)*

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Battery 12V] --> B[Main Fuse Box PDC]
    B --> C[Ignition Switch]
    B --> D[Relay Bank]
    C --> E[Starter Solenoid]
    C --> F[Ignition Coil]
    D --> G[Headlights]
    D --> H[Fuel Pump]
    D --> I[Fan Motor]
    B --> J[ECU Main Feed]
    J --> K[Sensors]
    J --> L[Actuators]
    A -.-> M[Chassis Ground]
    E -.-> M
    F -.-> M
    K -.-> M
    L -.-> M
```

**Explanation (4 Marks):**
The Main electrical power distribution system begins at the **12 V Main battery**, which serves as the primary energy reservoir. Power flows through the **Main Fuse & Relay Box (PDC — Power Distribution Center)**, where each branch is protected by an appropriately rated fuse. *(1 Mark)*

The **Ignition Switch** directs battery voltage to the starter circuit (crank position) and to the ignition coil / ECU (run position). The **Relay Bank** allows low-current ECU signals to switch high-current loads like headlights, fuel pump, and radiator fans. *(1 Mark)*

The **ECU** receives a clean, regulated power feed from the PDC and distributes commands to sensors (input) and actuators (output). *(1 Mark)*

All current returns to the battery negative terminal via the **Main Ground Network**, comprising engine-to-chassis braided straps, body ground points, and dedicated ECU grounds. *(1 Mark)*

---

#### Part (b) — Cable Sizing Calculation *(7 Marks)*

**Given:**
- $I = 200\ A$
- $L_{one-way} = 1.2\ m$, so $L_{total} = 2 \times 1.2 = 2.4\ m$
- $\Delta V_{max} = 0.4\ V$
- $\rho_{copper} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$

**Step (i): Minimum cable cross-section *(3 Marks)***

From Ohm's Law:

$$R_{max} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{I} = \frac{0.4}{200} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega$$

From the resistance formula:

$$A_{min} = \frac{\rho \times L_{total}}{R_{max}} = \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 2.4}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$A_{min} = \frac{4.138 \times 10^{-8}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.069 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2$$

$$A_{min} = 20.69\ mm^2$$

**Step (ii): Nearest standard cable size *(2 Marks)***

The next standard cable size above 20.69 mm² is **25 mm²** (commonly referred to as "4 AWG" in US convention or "20 mm² nominal" in European DIN 72551).

**Step (iii): Power loss during cranking *(2 Marks)***

Using the standard 25 mm² cable ($A = 25 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2$):

$$R_{actual} = \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 2.4}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.655 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega$$

$$P_{loss} = I^2 \times R_{actual} = (200)^2 \times 1.655 \times 10^{-3} = 66.2\ W$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> (1) Many students **forget to double the length** for the return path — this alone costs 1 Mark.
> (2) Students often **confuse $mm^2$ with $m^2$** in unit conversion — losing 1 Mark.
> (3) Selecting the *nearest standard size* must be from a *named standard* (DIN/ISO/SAE), not a random value.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, explain the **Main battery charging circuit** of an automobile, including the role of the voltage regulator. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A vehicle has the following **constant electrical loads** during ignition-OFF parked operation: ECU (3 A), alarm (0.8 A), infotainment standby (2 A). Calculate the **minimum battery capacity (in Ah)** required to sustain these loads for **72 hours** without dropping below **60 % State of Charge (SoC)**, assuming the battery's rated capacity is available at this discharge rate. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Main Battery Charging Circuit *(7 Marks)*

**Schematic (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Crankshaft] -->|Belt Drive| B[Alternator Rotor]
    B --> C[3-Phase Stator Winding]
    C --> D[Rectifier Bridge 6-Diode]
    D --> E[Voltage Regulator]
    E --> F[Main Battery 12V]
    F --> G[Electrical Loads]
    E --> H[Field Winding]
    H --> B
```

**Explanation (4 Marks):**
The **alternator** is driven by the engine crankshaft via a serpentine belt. Its 3-phase stator winding produces an AC voltage proportional to engine RPM. The **rectifier bridge** (six diodes in a full-wave Graetz configuration) converts the AC output to DC. *(1 Mark)*

The **Main battery** is connected in parallel with the alternator output. During engine operation, the alternator supplies current to both recharge the battery and power the live loads. *(1 Mark)*

The **voltage regulator** samples battery terminal voltage and modulates the **field winding current** (rotor excitation) to maintain output voltage between **13.8 V and 14.4 V**. *(1 Mark)*

If battery voltage falls below the setpoint, the regulator increases field current → higher alternator output → charging. If voltage exceeds 14.4 V, the regulator reduces field current → alternator output drops → protects battery from overcharging. *(1 Mark)*

---

#### Part (b) — Battery Capacity Calculation *(7 Marks)*

**Given:**
- ECU current = 3 A (continuous)
- Alarm current = 0.8 A (continuous)
- Infotainment standby = 2 A (continuous)
- Duration = 72 hours
- Maximum Depth of Discharge = 100% − 60% = 40%

**Step 1 — Compute total continuous load current *(2 Marks)***

$$I_{total} = 3 + 0.8 + 2 = 5.8\ A$$

**Step 2 — Compute total charge consumption *(2 Marks)***

$$Q_{consumed} = I_{total} \times t = 5.8 \times 72 = 417.6\ Ah$$

**Step 3 — Apply Depth of Discharge constraint *(3 Marks)***

Allowing discharge to 60% SoC means only **40%** of rated capacity may be used:

$$Q_{rated} = \frac{Q_{consumed}}{DoD_{max}} = \frac{417.6}{0.40} = 1044\ Ah$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):**
> (1) Many students confuse **SoC** with **DoD** — remember: $SoC + DoD = 100\%$.
> (2) Continuous loads over 72 hours are **unrealistic** without auxiliary charging; mention this in your answer for bonus marks.
> (3) Always state **assumptions** explicitly in your solution (e.g., "assuming Peukert's correction is negligible at low discharge rate").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Main" Automotive Electrical System**

- ☐ The **Main** system comprises: **Battery + Harness + PDC + Ground Network**.
- ☐ Standard nominal voltage = **12 V DC**; charging voltage = **13.8 – 14.4 V**.
- ☐ **CCA** = current at −18 °C for 30 s with V ≥ 7.2 V (12 V battery).
- ☐ **RC** = minutes at 25 A until V drops below 10.5 V at 27 °C.
- ☐ **Voltage drop budget** in starter circuit: **≤ 0.5 V** (≤ 1 % of system voltage).
- ☐ **Cable sizing formula:** $A_{min} = \dfrac{\rho \times 2L \times I}{\Delta V_{max}}$ — **never forget to double L**.
- ☐ **Copper resistivity:** $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$ at 20 °C.
- ☐ **Maximum current density rule:** 6 A/mm² (continuous), 10 A/mm² (intermittent).
- ☐ **Ground is as critical as positive feed** — over 60% of auto electrical faults are ground-related.
- ☐ **Peukert's equation** explains why high-discharge batteries deliver less effective Ah.
- ☐ **Alternator charging loop**: Bulk → Absorption → Float (state machine).
- ☐ **SoC + DoD = 100 %** — memorize the relationship.
- ☐ **KTU expects** neatly drawn block diagrams + labelled units + clear stepwise derivations.
- ☐ **Standard cable sizes** to memorize: 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70 mm².
- ☐ Always **explicitly state assumptions** before solving numericals to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Automotive Electronic Systems** refer to the integrated suite of microprocessor-controlled subsystems in a modern vehicle that extend beyond basic engine management to encompass **drive-by-wire architectures**, **vehicle dynamics control**, **active safety systems**, **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)**, and **in-vehicle networking protocols**. These systems form the cyber-physical backbone of contemporary automobiles, enabling real-time sensing, computation, and actuation with deterministic latency budgets typically below **10 ms** for safety-critical loops.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, "Advanced" denotes the second-generation control layer where **sensor fusion**, **closed-loop feedback**, **fail-safe redundancy**, and **multiplexed communication** supersede the purely mechanical and hydraulic subsystems of legacy vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEMRT831 — Module 2):**
> Advanced automotive electronic control subsystems — Electronic Throttle Control (ETC), Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Traction Control System (TCS), Antilock Braking System (ABS), Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), and in-vehicle networking protocols such as **CAN**, **LIN**, and **FlexRay**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a **bicycle**. Your hands grip the handlebar, your legs push pedals, and your brain makes constant micro-adjustments every fraction of a second:

- **Eyes** = Sensors (wheel speed, steering angle, yaw rate)
- **Brain** = ECU (Electronic Control Unit)
- **Hands on handlebar** = Actuators (throttle, brakes, steering motor)
- **Spinal cord (instant reflex loop)** = CAN bus carrying signals at high speed

Now imagine the bicycle could **think for you** — it would automatically slow down on a wet patch, steer gently to keep you upright, and prevent the wheel from skidding during emergency braking. That is exactly what an *advanced automotive electronic system* does: it replaces the human reflex arc with sensors, ECUs, and actuators communicating over a high-speed digital nervous system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Touchpoint:** A modern mid-segment car (e.g., Hyundai Creta, Toyota Hyryder) contains roughly **70–100 ECUs**, communicates over **2–5 CAN buses**, and generates up to **25 GB of data per hour** from sensors — all to keep the driver safe, efficient, and comfortable.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Typical Value | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|
| CAN bus bit rate | **500 kbps** (high-speed CAN) | ISO 11898 |
| FlexRay bandwidth | **10 Mbps** | FlexRay Consortium |
| ECU clock frequency | **40 – 300 MHz** | Automotive-grade (Infineon AURIX, NXP S32K3) |
| ABS control loop latency | **< 15 ms** | ISO 26262 ASIL-D |
| Vehicle bus voltage | **12 V ± 0.5 V** (conventional), **48 V** (mild hybrid) | ISO 7637 |
| Operating temperature range | **−40 °C to +125 °C** | AEC-Q100 Grade 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The deterministic latency bound of **10 ms** is the *hard real-time deadline* that distinguishes automotive-grade microcontrollers from consumer-grade MCUs. Missing this deadline in a brake-by-wire system can be catastrophic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture Decomposition

The advanced automotive electronics stack can be decomposed into **four interacting layers**:

### Layer 1 — Sensor Substrate (Physical Domain)
Converts mechanical/thermal/chemical quantities into electrical signals.

- **Wheel Speed Sensors**: Variable Reluctance (VRS) or Hall-effect, generating a square wave whose frequency is proportional to wheel angular velocity $\omega_w$.
- **Steering Angle Sensor**: Hall-effect or magneto-resistive, output range typically $\pm 540°$ (1.5 turns lock-to-lock).
- **Yaw Rate Sensor**: MEMS gyroscope, range $\pm 100°\!\/\text{s}$.
- **Lateral Acceleration Sensor**: MEMS accelerometer, range $\pm 1.5\,g$.
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**: Dual-track Hall sensor for redundancy.
- **Radar/Lidar/Camera**: For ADAS — typical object detection range **0 – 250 m** (77 GHz radar).

### Layer 2 — ECU / Processing Domain
Runs the control algorithms with deterministic scheduling.

- **Powertrain ECU** (Engine + Transmission).
- **Chassis ECU** (ABS, ESC, EPS — Electric Power Steering).
- **Body Control Module (BCM)**.
- **ADAS Domain Controller** (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ5).

### Layer 3 — Communication Backbone
Multiplexed serial buses reduce wiring harness weight from **> 70 kg** (point-to-point) to **< 25 kg** (multiplexed).

- **CAN** (Controller Area Network) — dominant in powertrain/chassis.
- **LIN** (Local Interconnect Network) — low-speed body electronics (mirrors, seats).
- **FlexRay** — high-bandwidth, time-triggered, used in safety-critical X-by-wire.
- **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** — emerging for ADAS and infotainment.

### Layer 4 — Actuator Domain
Converts electrical commands back into mechanical action.

- **Drive-by-Wire Throttle Body**: DC motor driving a gear-reduced throttle plate.
- **Brake-by-Wire**: Electro-hydraulic or fully electric caliper actuator.
- **Steer-by-Wire**: Motor on the rack, no mechanical column link.

---

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Control Theory (Why and How)

Every advanced subsystem is a **negative feedback control system**. The canonical structure is:

```
Desired Input  →  [Comparator]  →  [Controller C(s)]  →  [Plant P(s)]  →  Output
                                       ↑                                          ↓
                                       └──────────  [Sensor H(s)]  ←──────────────┘
```

### How it works in an Electronic Throttle Control (ETC):
1. The driver presses the accelerator pedal.
2. The **Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APP)** converts pedal angle $\theta_p$ into two redundant voltage signals (typically **0.5 V – 4.5 V**).
3. The ECU computes the desired throttle plate angle $\theta_{t,\text{des}}$ from a pre-calibrated **pedal map**.
4. The **TPS** (Throttle Position Sensor) reads the actual angle $\theta_{t,\text{act}}$.
5. The error $e(t) = \theta_{t,\text{des}} - \theta_{t,\text{act}}$ is fed into a **PI controller** (Proportional-Integral) to command the throttle DC motor voltage.
6. The DC motor drives the throttle plate through a reduction gear (typically **16:1 to 22:1**).
7. The loop closes at a **bandwidth of 10–20 Hz**, fast enough to feel instantaneous but filtered against high-frequency noise.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorise the symbols, units, and the physical meaning of each equation.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Domain / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r}$ | Wheel angular velocity | Wheel speed sensor | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v - \omega_w r}{v}$ | **Slip ratio** (braking $\lambda>0$) | ABS / TCS | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_{xb} = \mu(\lambda)\,N$ | Longitudinal brake friction force | Tire-road friction model | N |
| 4 | $\mu(\lambda) = k_1 \lambda - k_2 \lambda^2 + k_3 \lambda^3$ | Pacejka-like simplified friction | ABS controller | dimensionless |
| 5 | $a_y = \dfrac{v^2}{R}$ | Lateral acceleration in steady-state turn | ESC reference calc | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 6 | $\dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{v \cdot \delta}{L + K_u v^2}$ | Reference yaw rate (bicycle model) | ESC target | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 7 | $J \dot{\omega}_w = T_m - T_{brake} - r F_x$ | Wheel rotational dynamics | ABS / TCS | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 8 | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p (1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s})}{1}$ | PI controller transfer function | ETC / ESC | — |
| 9 | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{bit}}$ | Bit time on CAN bus | CAN timing | s |
| 10 | $\text{Slip angle } \alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v_y}{v_x}\right)$ | Tire slip angle | Vehicle dynamics | rad |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Equation 2** (slip ratio $\lambda$) is the *cornerstone* of ABS — the controller drives $\lambda$ to a value near **0.10 – 0.20** where the friction coefficient $\mu$ peaks, preventing wheel lock.
- **Equation 6** (reference yaw rate) lets ESC compute the *desired* yaw rate from vehicle speed $v$ and steering angle $\delta$; the actual measured yaw rate is then compared, and differential braking is applied to correct understeer/oversteer.
- **Equation 8** (PI controller) is the *workhorse* in ETC — proportional gain gives fast response, integral gain eliminates steady-state throttle tracking error.

---

## 2.4 Fail-Safe & Redundancy Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Automotive safety-critical systems must satisfy **ASIL-D** (Automotive Safety Integrity Level D), the highest tier under **ISO 26262**. This mandates:
> - **Dual-channel redundancy** in sensor signal paths.
> - **Plausibility checks** between redundant signals.
> - **Graceful degradation** to limp-home mode on fault detection.
> - **Diagnostic coverage ≥ 99 %** for single-point faults.

For example, in ETC the two APP sensor signals must track each other within **± 5 %**, otherwise the ECU declares a fault and reverts to **limp-home idle** (fixed throttle angle ~ $8°$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slip Ratio in ABS / TCS

The slip ratio $\lambda$ quantifies the *difference between the linear velocity of the tire contact patch and the vehicle's translational velocity*. It is the single most important variable in brake and traction control.

### Step 1 — Define the rotational velocity at the wheel contact patch

If the wheel rotates at angular velocity $\omega_w$ with effective rolling radius $r$, the velocity of the contact patch tangential to the road is:

$$
v_{\text{rot}} = \omega_w \cdot r
$$

### Step 2 — Compare with vehicle translational velocity

Let $v$ be the longitudinal velocity of the vehicle's center of mass measured by the ESP sensor or estimated from the non-slipping wheel.

### Step 3 — Form the difference

$$
\Delta v = v - v_{\text{rot}}
$$

This is the amount by which the tire is sliding relative to the road surface.

### Step 4 — Normalize by the appropriate reference velocity

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\max(v,\, v_{\text{rot}})}
$$

Convention used in KTU-recommended textbooks:
- **Braking phase:** $v > v_{\text{rot}}$, so $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, with $\lambda = 1$ meaning full wheel lock.
- **Traction phase (acceleration):** $v_{\text{rot}} > v$, so $\lambda \in [-1, 0]$, with $\lambda = -1$ meaning full wheel spin.

This can be written compactly as:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{v - \omega_w r}{\max(v,\, \omega_w r)}
$$

### Step 5 — Physical interpretation

- $\lambda = 0$: tire rolls freely, no slip → maximum longitudinal force available only at the rolling resistance limit.
- $\lambda \approx 0.10$ – $0.20$: peak friction coefficient region → **optimal braking/tracking point**.
- $\lambda = 1$: tire is locked, friction drops to sliding value, vehicle skids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Board Pattern):** When a 14-mark problem states *"Compute slip ratio given wheel speed and vehicle speed"*, always state (i) the regime (braking vs traction), (ii) the formula used, (iii) the numerical substitution, and (iv) the physical interpretation. Full marks require the interpretation step.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reference Yaw Rate for ESC

ESC maintains directional stability by ensuring the *measured yaw rate* matches the *desired yaw rate* predicted by a simplified **bicycle model**.

### Step 1 — Set up the bicycle model

Treat the vehicle as a rigid body with mass $m$, wheelbase $L$, and understeer gradient $K_u$. The front wheel is steered by angle $\delta$, the rear wheel is fixed.

### Step 2 — Apply the linearized lateral dynamics

In steady-state cornering (constant $\dot{\psi}$ and small slip angles), the yaw rate is governed by:

$$
\dot{\psi} \cdot \left( 1 + K_u\, \dfrac{v^2}{g\,L} \right) = \dfrac{v\, \delta}{L}
$$

where $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

### Step 3 — Solve for the reference yaw rate

$$
\dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{v\, \delta}{L + K_u\, v^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the form: $v^2$ appears in the denominator multiplied by $K_u$. This makes the yaw rate **saturation-limited** — beyond a critical speed $v_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{L / K_u}$, the vehicle physically cannot achieve the driver-requested yaw rate, and ESC must intervene via differential braking.

### Step 4 — Convert into lateral acceleration for cross-check

The implied steady-state lateral acceleration is:

$$
a_{y,\text{ref}} = v \cdot \dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{v^2\, \delta}{L + K_u\, v^2}
$$

The vehicle can sustain lateral accelerations up to roughly $\mu\, g \approx 0.85\, g \approx 8.34\, \text{m/s}^2$ on dry asphalt. ESC will cut engine torque or apply individual-wheel brake pressure if $a_{y,\text{ref}}$ exceeds about **0.3 – 0.4 g** of available grip margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Advanced Subsystems

| Subsystem | Primary Sensor | Primary Actuator | Control Variable | Control Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electronic Throttle Control (ETC) | Accelerator Pedal Position (APP) | DC motor + gear train | Throttle plate angle $\theta_t$ | **50 – 100 Hz** |
| Antilock Braking System (ABS) | 4× Wheel Speed Sensors | Hydraulic brake modulator | Slip ratio $\lambda$ | **20 – 50 Hz** |
| Traction Control System (TCS) | Wheel Speed + Yaw | Engine torque / brake | Slip ratio $\lambda$ (negative) | **20 – 50 Hz** |
| Electronic Stability Control (ESC) | Yaw rate + Lateral g + $\delta$ | Differential brake + torque | Yaw rate error $\Delta\dot{\psi}$ | **50 – 100 Hz** |
| Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) | 77 GHz radar + camera | Throttle / brake | Time-headway $\tau_h$ | **10 – 20 Hz** |
| Electric Power Steering (EPS) | Steering torque + angle | Rack-mounted BLDC motor | Assist torque $T_a$ | **200 – 500 Hz** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]:**
> A passenger car is travelling at $v = 25\, \text{m/s}$. The driver brakes hard on a dry asphalt road. The wheel speed sensor reports $\omega_w = 110\, \text{rad/s}$, and the effective tire radius is $r = 0.32\, \text{m}$. Compute the slip ratio and comment on whether ABS will intervene.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Compute the rotational contact-patch velocity:

$$
v_{\text{rot}} = \omega_w \cdot r = 110 \times 0.32 = 35.2\, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the regime. Since $v_{\text{rot}} > v$ would imply acceleration, and the driver is braking, this is unusual — let us instead consider the case where the wheel is decelerating faster than the car:

> **Re-check:** In braking, we expect $\omega_w r < v$. Let us take $\omega_w = 70\, \text{rad/s}$ for a more realistic scenario.

$$
v_{\text{rot}} = 70 \times 0.32 = 22.4\, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the slip ratio definition (braking phase, $v$ is the reference):

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{v - v_{\text{rot}}}{v} = \dfrac{25 - 22.4}{25} = \dfrac{2.6}{25} = 0.104
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. The slip ratio $\lambda = 0.104$ lies in the **optimal friction band (0.08 – 0.20)** where the tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ is near its maximum. Hence:

> *ABS will NOT release brake pressure at this instant; the slip is healthy. If $\lambda$ crosses ~ 0.25, ABS will modulate pressure to bring it back into the 0.10 – 0.15 band.*

This answer matches the KTU-recommended step pattern: **formula → substitution → numerical result → physical interpretation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Vehicle Electronics Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a modern vehicle's advanced electronic subsystems and their communication backbone.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Driver[DRIVER INPUT] --> Pedal[Accelerator Pedal APP Sensor]
    Driver --> Steer[Steering Wheel Angle Sensor]
    Driver --> Brake[Brake Pedal Sensor]

    Pedal --> ECUpwr[POWERTRAIN ECU]
    Steer --> EPS[EPS ECU]
    Brake --> ESCm[ESC ECU]

    WSS[Wheel Speed Sensors x4] --> ESCm
    Yaw[Yaw Rate Sensor] --> ESCm
    Latg[Lateral Accelerometer] --> ESCm
    Radar[77 GHz Radar] --> ADAS[ADAS Domain Controller]
    Cam[Front Camera] --> ADAS

    ADAS --> ECUpwr
    ADAS --> ESCm

    ECUpwr --> TBODY[Throttle Body Actuator]
    ECUpwr --> INJ[Fuel Injectors]
    EPS --> Rack[Steering Rack Motor]
    ESCm --> HCU[Hydraulic Control Unit]
    HCU --> WhlFL[Wheel Brake FL]
    HCU --> WhlFR[Wheel Brake FR]
    HCU --> WhlRL[Wheel Brake RL]
    HCU --> WhlRR[Wheel Brake RR]

    ECUpwr <==> BusA[CAN High Speed Bus 500 kbps]
    ESCm <==> BusA
    EPS <==> BusA
    ADAS <==> BusA

    BCM[Body Control Module] <==> BusB[CAN Low Speed Bus 125 kbps]
    Mirror[Side Mirrors and Seats] <==> BusC[LIN Bus 19.2 kbps]
    BCM --- BusC

    BusA --> Gate[GATEWAY ECU]
    BusB --> Gate
    Gate --> OBD[OBD II Diagnostic Port]
    Gate --> Eth[Automotive Ethernet 100 Mbps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** All ECUs (rectangular boxes) communicate over the **CAN bus** (the central double-arrows). The **gateway ECU** bridges the high-speed powertrain/chassis bus to the low-speed body bus and to the diagnostic **OBD-II port** used by service technicians.

---

## 4.2 ABS Control Loop — Closed-Loop Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Deceleration a_des] --> C[Slip Ratio Controller PI]
    W[Measured Wheel Speed omega_w] --> S[Slip Calculator]
    V[Estimated Vehicle Speed v_est] --> S
    S -->|lambda| C
    C -->|Brake Pressure Command| V1[Pressure Modulator Valve]
    V1 --> H[Brake Caliper Hydraulic Pressure]
    H --> F[Tire Road Friction Force]
    F --> W
    F -->|Updates| V
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The loop is *cascaded* — the **inner loop** is fast hydraulic pressure control, the **outer loop** is the slower slip-ratio control running in the ECU. This separation of time scales is what makes ABS robust against hydraulic lag.

---

## 4.3 ESC Decision Logic State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Monitor
    Monitor --> Understeer: Measured yaw less than reference
    Monitor --> Oversteer: Measured yaw greater than reference
    Monitor --> Nominal: Error within tolerance band
    Understeer --> Brake_Rear_Inside: Apply brake to rear inner wheel
    Oversteer --> Brake_Front_Outside: Apply brake to front outer wheel
    Brake_Rear_Inside --> Monitor: Yaw restored
    Brake_Front_Outside --> Monitor: Yaw restored
    Nominal --> Monitor: Continuous 50 Hz update
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked *"What action does ESC take during understeer?"*, answer **"It applies brake pressure to the rear-inner wheel, generating a yaw moment that rotates the vehicle toward the inside of the turn, restoring the driver's intended trajectory."**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define slip ratio. State its significance in ABS. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Slip ratio $\lambda$ is the normalized difference between the vehicle's translational velocity $v$ and the wheel-contact-patch velocity $\omega_w r$ at the wheel.
- **Significance (1 mark):** In ABS, $\lambda$ is the controlled variable; the controller drives $\lambda$ to the **0.10 – 0.20 band** where the tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ peaks, preventing wheel lock and preserving steering control.

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{v - \omega_w r}{\max(v,\, \omega_w r)}
$$

---

### Q2. What is drive-by-wire? Differentiate it from drive-by-cable. *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Drive-by-wire (2 marks):** A vehicle subsystem in which the traditional mechanical linkage (cable, rod, hydraulic line) between driver input and actuator is **replaced by an electronic sensor-controller-actuator chain**. Examples: electronic throttle control (ETC), steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire.
- **Differentiation (1 mark):** Drive-by-cable uses a *physical* Bowden cable to pull the throttle plate; drive-by-wire uses *no mechanical link* — only sensor voltage and a motor command.

| Feature | Drive-by-Cable | Drive-by-Wire |
|---|---|---|
| Linkage | Mechanical cable | Electrical signal |
| Failure mode | Mechanical wear | Sensor/actuator redundancy |
| Precision | Limited by cable stretch | High (closed-loop control) |
| Integration with ESC | Not possible | Seamless (CAN bus) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> *(a)* Explain the working principle of **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the reference yaw rate from the bicycle model. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A vehicle of wheelbase $L = 2.6\, \text{m}$ and understeer gradient $K_u = 0.0015\, \text{rad/(m/s}^2)$ is driven at $v = 22\, \text{m/s}$ with a steering wheel angle equivalent to $\delta = 0.04\, \text{rad}$. Calculate the reference yaw rate. Comment on whether the request is physically achievable. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Describe the operating principle. ESC continuously monitors:
- Steering wheel angle $\delta$
- Vehicle yaw rate $\dot{\psi}_{\text{meas}}$ (from yaw sensor)
- Lateral acceleration $a_y$
- Wheel speeds (4×)

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The ECU computes the reference yaw rate $\dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}}$ using the bicycle model and compares it with $\dot{\psi}_{\text{meas}}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** If $\Delta\dot{\psi} = \dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} - \dot{\psi}_{\text{meas}}$ exceeds a threshold:
- **Understeer** ($\dot{\psi}_{\text{meas}} < \dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}}$): brake the **rear-inner** wheel.
- **Oversteer** ($\dot{\psi}_{\text{meas}} > \dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}}$): brake the **front-outer** wheel.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Provide the block diagram (drawn in answer sheet — should match SECTION 4.3).

The reference yaw rate derivation (refer Section 3.2):

$$
\dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{v\, \delta}{L + K_u\, v^2}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $L = 2.6\, \text{m}$, $K_u = 0.0015\, \text{rad/(m/s}^2)$, $v = 22\, \text{m/s}$, $\delta = 0.04\, \text{rad}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute denominator term:

$$
L + K_u v^2 = 2.6 + 0.0015 \times 22^2 = 2.6 + 0.0015 \times 484 = 2.6 + 0.726 = 3.326\, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute numerator:

$$
v \cdot \delta = 22 \times 0.04 = 0.88\, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute reference yaw rate:

$$
\dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{0.88}{3.326} = 0.2646\, \text{rad/s} = 15.16°/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Physical interpretation. The reference yaw rate of $0.265\, \text{rad/s}$ corresponds to a steady-state lateral acceleration:

$$
a_y = v \cdot \dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = 22 \times 0.265 = 5.83\, \text{m/s}^2 \approx 0.6\,g
$$

This is well within the tire grip envelope on dry asphalt ($\mu \approx 0.85$ gives $\approx 8.34\, \text{m/s}^2$). **Hence the driver's request is physically achievable without ESC intervention.**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC)** system. Discuss why drive-by-wire is preferred over drive-by-cable. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* The APP sensor of an ETC system outputs $V_{\text{APP}} = 1.85\, \text{V}$ on a 5 V reference. The pedal map maps 0.5 V → 0° throttle and 4.5 V → 80° throttle. Determine the desired throttle plate angle $\theta_{t,\text{des}}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Block diagram (must be drawn — refer Section 4 architecture). Components: APP sensor, ECU with pedal map, TPS, PI controller, DC motor driver, throttle body.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Working:
- APP sensor generates two redundant analog voltages.
- ECU reads both, performs plausibility check.
- Desired angle obtained from pedal map: $\theta_{t,\text{des}} = f(V_{\text{APP}})$.
- TPS feeds back actual angle; PI controller computes motor voltage.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Why drive-by-wire is preferred:
- **Traction control integration:** ESC can request throttle reduction during wheel slip — impossible with a cable.
- **Cruise control compatibility:** ECU directly commands throttle without vacuum actuators.
- **Elimination of cable stretch, wear, and adjustment.**
- **Better emissions and fuel economy** via precise airflow control.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mention redundancy (two APP tracks, two TPS tracks, limp-home mode).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Identify the linear pedal map. The mapping is linear between the endpoints:

$$
\theta_{t,\text{des}} = \theta_{\min} + \dfrac{V_{\text{APP}} - V_{\min}}{V_{\max} - V_{\min}} \cdot (\theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min})
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the given values:

$$
\theta_{t,\text{des}} = 0 + \dfrac{1.85 - 0.5}{4.5 - 0.5} \times 80 = \dfrac{1.35}{4.0} \times 80
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute:

$$
\theta_{t,\text{des}} = 0.3375 \times 80 = 27.0°
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Interpretation. The driver has requested a 27° throttle opening, which on a typical 2.0 L petrol engine corresponds to approximately **35–40 % volumetric airflow** at idle-equivalent manifold depression, delivering moderate acceleration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Always check that $\dot{\psi}$ is converted to either $\text{rad/s}$ or $\text{°/s}$ consistently. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Missing physical interpretation:** A numerical answer without a one-line comment on its physical meaning (achievable / not achievable, optimal friction band, etc.) loses 1 mark per sub-question.
> 3. **Skipping the plausibility check:** In ETC problems, if the question gives two APP voltages, you must comment on whether they are consistent (within ± 5 %). Examiners reward this explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\max(v, \omega_w r)$ term in the slip ratio:** Using just $v$ in the denominator is acceptable in the braking case but will cost you a mark if the question is in traction phase.
> 5. **Block diagram quality:** A block diagram drawn in the answer booklet should be **labelled** (not just a generic rectangle). Include arrow directions and signal names ($\theta_{\text{des}}$, $V_{\text{motor}}$, etc.).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Advanced):**

- [ ] **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC)** uses a *PI controller* on the throttle plate angle, driven by the APP sensor through a pedal map. Look for the **0.5 V – 4.5 V** dual-track Hall sensor range.
- [ ] **Slip ratio** $\lambda$ is the controlled variable of ABS/TCS. Braking phase: $\lambda \in [0, 1]$. Optimal range: **0.10 – 0.20**.
- [ ] **Reference yaw rate** for ESC comes from the bicycle model: $\dot{\psi}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{v \delta}{L + K_u v^2}$. Compare with *measured* yaw rate to detect understeer / oversteer.
- [ ] **Understeer** → brake rear-inner wheel. **Oversteer** → brake front-outer wheel. (This single line is worth 2 marks.)
- [ ] **CAN bus** runs at **500 kbps** (high-speed) and **125 kbps** (low-speed). Frame format: 11-bit or 29-bit ID, 8-byte payload max in classic CAN.
- [ ] **FlexRay** = **10 Mbps**, time-triggered, used in X-by-wire. **LIN** = **19.2 kbps**, used in body electronics.
- [ ] **Automotive Ethernet** = **100 Mbps** (100BASE-T1) and emerging 1 Gbps (1000BASE-T1) for ADAS and infotainment.
- [ ] **ASIL-D** under ISO 26262 is the highest safety integrity level, demanding **< 10 ms** latency and dual-channel redundancy.
- [ ] **Lateral acceleration limit** of dry asphalt tires: $\approx 0.85\,g \approx 8.34\, \text{m/s}^2$. ESC keeps the vehicle at roughly **30–40 %** below this as a safety margin.
- [ ] **Wheel speed** $v_{\text{rot}} = \omega_w r$; use this in every ABS slip-ratio problem.
- [ ] Always close your ETC / ESC numerical answers with a **physical interpretation sentence** — KTU examiners reward this consistently with 1 mark per sub-question.
- [ ] **Drive-by-wire** advantages: traction/cruise integration, no cable stretch, precise emissions control, ASIL-D redundancy. Drawbacks: cybersecurity risk, latency-bound failure modes.
- [ ] **Limp-home mode** in ETC: a fixed throttle angle of about **8°** at idle if APP sensor fault is detected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *Formula → Substitution → Number → Interpretation.* Four steps. Every numerical. Every time. This is the single highest-yield habit for scoring full marks on Module 2 numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post Injection and Retarded Post Injection Layout of the Common Rail Fuel Injection System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Post Injection and Retarded Post Injection Layout of the Common Rail Fuel Injection System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Post Injection & Retarded Post Injection in Common Rail CRDI Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post Injection (PI)** is a *secondary, small-volume fuel injection event* introduced by the Engine Control Unit (ECU) **after** the Main Injection (MI) pulse and **before the exhaust valve opens**, within the same working stroke of a four-stroke Compression Ignition (CI) engine. In a **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** system, the ECU actuates the piezo/solenoid injector to deliver a metered quantity of fuel (typically **1 mm³ to 6 mm³ per stroke**) at rail pressures of **600 bar to 2000 bar** at a crank-angle position (CAD aTDC) that is *retarded* (later) with respect to the main injection.

**Retarded Post Injection (RPI)** is a *highly delayed* post injection, timed very close to the exhaust valve opening (typically **60° to 140° CAD aTDC**), whose principal engineering purpose is **thermal management of the exhaust aftertreatment system** — specifically, raising the exhaust gas temperature above the **particulate matter (PM) oxidation light-off temperature of ~ 600 °C** to actively regenerate the **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** and/or assist in **NOx reduction** via the **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** catalyst light-off (~ 200 °C – 250 °C).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Toaster Pre-Heat"**
> Imagine you have burnt a piece of toast and the crumbs are stuck inside the toaster. To clean it, you do not pour water; instead, you **briefly heat the toaster again** so the crumbs burn off into ash. The crumbs = soot trapped in the DPF. The brief re-heating = the **Retarded Post Injection** of a small fuel charge. The fuel does not push the piston (produce work) — it is *deliberately wasted as heat* into the exhaust, raising its temperature so the DPF can "self-clean" (regenerate). The standard **Post Injection** is like a milder version: it still helps the DPF, but its timing is closer to the main injection, and a small fraction of the fuel can still undergo partial combustion in the cylinder — often used to **raise in-cylinder temperature late in the expansion stroke**, helping reduce soot formation *before* it even leaves the cylinder.

### 1.3 Why Post Injection Exists — The Emissions Tri-Lemma

Modern diesel engines must simultaneously comply with:

| Regulation | Pollutant | Limit (Euro 6/BS-VI) | Role of Post Injection |
|------------|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| NOx | Nitrogen Oxides | 80 mg/km | Thermal management enables SCR light-off |
| PM | Particulate Matter | 4.5 mg/km | Raises exhaust T to oxidise soot in DPF |
| CO₂ | Carbon Dioxide | 95 g/km | Minimal — slight penalty accepted |
| NH₃ | Ammonia Slip (SCR) | 10 ppm | Excessive PI can cause NH₃ slip |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

- **Rail Pressure during PI**: **600 bar – 1600 bar** (system is already pressurised; no extra pump work needed).
- **PI Volume per Stroke**: **1 mm³ – 6 mm³** (compared to 50–80 mm³ for Main Injection).
- **Crank-Angle Position (SOI for RPI)**: **60° – 140° aTDC** (retarded deep into expansion stroke).
- **DPF Light-off / Regen Temperature**: **~ 600 °C** (soot oxidation onset); passive regen occurs ≥ 350 °C.
- **SCR Light-off Temperature**: **~ 200 °C – 250 °C** (AdBlue/DEF dosing requires this minimum).
- **Diesel LHV (Lower Heating Value)**: **≈ 42.5 MJ/kg** → used in exhaust enthalpy rise calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Injection Phases of a CRDI Combustion Event

A modern CRDI engine may execute **up to 9 separate injection events per combustion cycle**. For Module 2, the relevant ones are:

1. **Pilot Injection (SOI ≈ 15° – 25° bTDC)** — small charge to prep mixture, reduce combustion noise.
2. **Main Injection (SOI ≈ 5° – 12° bTDC)** — primary work-producing combustion.
3. **Post Injection (SOI ≈ 20° – 60° aTDC)** — close-coupled, partial combustion, soot reduction.
4. **Retarded Post Injection (SOI ≈ 60° – 140° aTDC)** — primarily *thermal energy* delivery, DPF regen.

## 2.2 Operating Logic — Step-by-Step Engine Control

- **Step 1: ECU computes demanded rail pressure** based on engine speed (N), load (BMEP), and emissions target.
- **Step 2: ECU schedules Pilot → Main → Post → Retarded Post** in a single 720° (4-stroke) cycle.
- **Step 3: The High-Pressure Pump (HP Pump)** maintains rail pressure at 600–2000 bar via the **pressure control valve (PCV)**.
- **Step 4: Injector solenoid/piezo driver** is energised for a precise *energising time* $t_{en}$ (in µs), which controls the **metered fuel volume** $V_{inj}$.
- **Step 5: For Retarded Post Injection**, ECU verifies:
  - DPF ΔP (differential pressure) > threshold → soot loaded.
  - Coolant temperature > 70 °C.
  - Vehicle speed / load stable.
  - Rail pressure > 600 bar.
  If all OK, RPI is triggered and the **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** in front of the DPF oxidises unburned HC/CO from the RPI charge, releasing **exothermic heat** that elevates the exhaust T.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols are aligned with KTU Automotive Engineering notation.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | $V_{inj} = q \cdot N_{pulses} \cdot t_{en}$ | Volume injected per stroke | mm³ | 1 – 80 mm³ |
| 2 | $m_{f,PI} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot V_{PI}$ | Mass of post-injected fuel | mg | 0.85 – 5.1 mg |
| 3 | $Q_{th,PI} = m_{f,PI} \cdot LHV$ | Thermal energy released by PI | J | 36 – 217 J |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{exh} = \dfrac{Q_{th,PI}}{\dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh}}$ | Exhaust temp rise from PI | K | 30 – 200 K |
| 5 | $P_{rail} = P_{cam} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Common rail pressure | bar | 600 – 2000 bar |
| 6 | $t_{SOI,PI} = \theta_{aTDC} \cdot \dfrac{60}{N \cdot 360}$ | Time-of-injection (PI) in s | s | 0.005 – 0.04 s |
| 7 | $\eta_{comb,PI} = 1 - \dfrac{m_{soot,out}}{m_{f,PI}}$ | Combustion efficiency of PI | – | 0.4 – 0.85 |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{soot,regen} = k_{ox} \cdot e^{\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T_{DPF}}}$ | DPF soot oxidation rate (Arrhenius) | g/s | depends on T_DPF |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{DPF} = K_1 \cdot m_{soot} + K_2 \cdot \dot{V}_{exh}$ | DPF back-pressure vs soot load | mbar | 0 – 200 mbar |
| 10 | $BSFC_{PI} = \dfrac{m_{f,total}}{W_{shaft}}$ | BSFC including RPI penalty | g/kWh | + 1 to 3 g/kWh |

> **Notation Guide:** $LHV$ = Lower Heating Value of diesel (**42.5 MJ/kg**), $c_{p,exh}$ = exhaust specific heat (**~ 1.10 kJ/kg·K**), $E_a$ = activation energy for soot oxidation (**~ 150 kJ/mol**), $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**), $K_1, K_2$ = DPF empirical constants.

## 2.4 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Why Pilot Injection?** Pre-ignites a small charge → raises in-cylinder T & P modestly → Main Injection burns faster, quieter.
- **Why Post Injection (close-coupled)?** Late-cycle combustion of fuel-rich products oxidises soot **inside the cylinder** before it reaches the DPF. Effect: **30–50% reduction in PM at tailpipe**.
- **Why Retarded Post Injection?** Fuel has *no time* to combust in-cylinder. It travels mostly unburned to the **DOC**, where HC/CO oxidation is **exothermic (ΔH ≈ –850 kJ/mol for CO → CO₂)**. This **sustained heat release** in the DOC is the *only practical on-board method* to raise exhaust T to the DPF regeneration window during low-load urban driving.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** (Bosch, Delphi, Denso, Continental) use 5–7 injection events per cycle in BS-VI / Euro 6 engines.
- **Off-road & Mining equipment**: RPI enables DPF regen in low-load conditions where exhaust T would otherwise be too low.
- **Heavy-duty trucks (Euro VI)**: Mandatory DPF + SCR → RPI is a baseline control feature, not optional.
- **Hybrid vehicles with downsized diesels**: Low-load operation is the *default*, making RPI indispensable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Exhaust Temperature Rise Due to Retarded Post Injection

We want to determine how much the exhaust gas temperature rises when a quantity of fuel $m_{f,RPI}$ is delivered as Retarded Post Injection, fully oxidised in the DOC, with the resulting enthalpy transferred to the exhaust stream.

### **Given:**

- Retarded Post Injection mass: $m_{f,RPI} = 2.5 \text{ mg/stroke} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/stroke}$
- Engine speed: $N = 2000 \text{ rpm}$ → $\dfrac{N}{2} = 1000 \text{ working strokes/sec}$ (4-stroke)
- Diesel LHV: $LHV = 42.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$
- DOC oxidation efficiency of HC/CO from RPI: $\eta_{DOC} = 0.85$
- Exhaust mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_{exh} = 0.085 \text{ kg/s}$
- Exhaust specific heat: $c_{p,exh} = 1.10 \times 10^{3} \text{ J/kg·K}$
- Number of cylinders: $n = 4$

### **Step 1 — Mass flow rate of the post-injected fuel per second:**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,RPI} = m_{f,RPI} \cdot \frac{N}{2} \cdot n
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,RPI} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1000 \cdot 4
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,RPI} = 0.010 \text{ kg/s}
$$

> **Logic:** Each cylinder fires once every 2 revolutions (4-stroke). At 2000 rpm there are 1000 firing events per cylinder per second; with 4 cylinders this scales to 4000 events/s, each delivering 2.5 mg of fuel.

### **Step 2 — Thermal power released by RPI after DOC oxidation:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{RPI} = \dot{m}_{f,RPI} \cdot LHV \cdot \eta_{DOC}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{RPI} = 0.010 \cdot 42.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.85
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{RPI} = 361{,}250 \text{ W} = 361.25 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Logic:** The Lower Heating Value represents the chemical energy per kg of diesel. Only 85% of the HC/CO is oxidised in the DOC (catalyst conversion efficiency), so we multiply by $\eta_{DOC}$.

### **Step 3 — Temperature rise of the exhaust stream:**

$$
\Delta T_{exh} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{RPI}}{\dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{exh} = \frac{361{,}250}{0.085 \cdot 1100}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{exh} = \frac{361{,}250}{93.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{exh} \approx 3863 \text{ K}}
$$

> **Logic (interpretation):** This raw value is *not physically achievable* in practice because (a) the cylinder wall absorbs energy, (b) only a fraction of fuel escapes unburned to the DOC, and (c) the cylinder gas is being expanded (cooling). In a real-world estimate, a practical correction factor $\eta_{util} \approx 0.10$ – 0.20 is applied:

$$
\Delta T_{exh,actual} = \Delta T_{exh} \cdot \eta_{util}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{exh,actual} = 3863 \cdot 0.15 \approx 580 \text{ K}
$$

This realistic temperature rise is sufficient to push exhaust T from a low-load baseline of ~ 200 °C to the **DPF regeneration window of ~ 600 °C**, confirming the engineering viability of RPI.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Post Injection vs. Retarded Post Injection

| Parameter | **Standard Post Injection (PI)** | **Retarded Post Injection (RPI)** |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Crank-angle timing (SOI)** | 20° – 60° aTDC | 60° – 140° aTDC |
| **Volume per stroke** | 2 – 6 mm³ | 1 – 4 mm³ |
| **In-cylinder combustion** | Partial (50 – 80%) | Negligible (5 – 15%) |
| **Primary function** | In-cylinder soot oxidation | Exhaust heating for DPF regen |
| **Effect on PM** | ↓ 30 – 50% | ↓ 5 – 15% (via DPF) |
| **Effect on NOx** | Slight ↑ | ↓ 10 – 20% (via SCR light-off) |
| **Effect on BSFC** | + 1 – 2% | + 2 – 5% |
| **Effect on HC emissions** | ↑ 50 – 100 ppm | ↑ 100 – 250 ppm |
| **Effect on CO emissions** | ↑ moderate | ↑ high (oxidised in DOC) |
| **Activation condition** | Always (injection map) | Only when DPF ΔP threshold crossed |
| **Catalyst impact** | Thermal aging of DOC | Major contributor to DOC exotherm |
| **NOx–PM trade-off** | Helps PM, hurts NOx | Helps PM (via regen), helps NOx (via SCR) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Verifying DPF Regeneration Feasibility

**Problem:** A 2.0 L CRDI engine at idle (800 rpm) carries 8 g of soot in its DPF. Calculate the time required to regenerate the DPF using Retarded Post Injection.

**Given:**

- Soot mass to be oxidised: $m_{soot} = 8 \text{ g} = 0.008 \text{ kg}$
- DPF temperature (steady): $T_{DPF} = 650 \text{ °C} = 923 \text{ K}$
- Activation energy: $E_a = 150{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$
- Pre-exponential factor: $k_0 = 5.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$

**Step 1 — Arrhenius soot oxidation rate constant:**

$$
k_{ox} = k_0 \cdot e^{\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T_{DPF}}}
$$

$$
k_{ox} = 5.0 \times 10^{9} \cdot e^{\frac{-150000}{8.314 \cdot 923}}
$$

$$
k_{ox} = 5.0 \times 10^{9} \cdot e^{-19.55}
$$

$$
k_{ox} = 5.0 \times 10^{9} \cdot 3.27 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
k_{ox} \approx 16.35 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

> **Logic:** The exponential term quantifies the *temperature sensitivity* of soot oxidation. Each 50 K rise nearly doubles $k_{ox}$.

**Step 2 — Time required (assuming first-order decay, $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = -k_{ox} \cdot m$):**

$$
m(t) = m_0 \cdot e^{-k_{ox} \cdot t} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t = \frac{1}{k_{ox}} \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)
$$

For 99% regeneration, $m_f = 0.01 \cdot m_0$:

$$
t_{regen} = \frac{1}{16.35} \cdot \ln(100)
$$

$$
t_{regen} = \frac{1}{16.35} \cdot 4.605
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{regen} \approx 0.282 \text{ s per gram, total} \approx 2.25 \text{ s for 8 g}}
$$

> **Caveat:** Real DPF regeneration is *transport-limited* (oxygen diffusion, soot location, ash masking), so actual regen time is **5 – 15 minutes** in OEM applications. The Arrhenius estimate is a *kinetic floor*, useful for understanding thermal dependence.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Mermaid Block Diagrams

## 4.1 Layout of a Common Rail Fuel Injection System with Post & Retarded Post Injection Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tank1[Fuel Tank] --> lp1[Feed Pump]
    lp1 --> hp1[High Pressure Pump]
    hp1 --> pcv1[Pressure Control Valve]
    pcv1 --> rail1[Common Rail Accumulator]
    rail1 --> rps1[Rail Pressure Sensor]
    rail1 --> inj1[Injector Cyl 1]
    rail1 --> inj2[Injector Cyl 2]
    rail1 --> inj3[Injector Cyl 3]
    rail1 --> inj4[Injector Cyl 4]
    inj1 --> cyl1[Cylinder 1]
    inj2 --> cyl2[Cylinder 2]
    inj3 --> cyl3[Cylinder 3]
    inj4 --> cyl4[Cylinder 4]
    cyl1 --> exh1[Exhaust Manifold]
    cyl2 --> exh1
    cyl3 --> exh1
    cyl4 --> exh1
    exh1 --> egt1[Exhaust Gas Temp Sensor]
    egt1 --> doc1[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC]
    doc1 --> dpf1[DPF Diesel Particulate Filter]
    dpf1 --> scr1[SCR Catalyst with DEF Injection]
    scr1 --> tail[Tailpipe]
    ecm1[Engine Control Unit ECU] -->|PWM Signal| hp1
    ecm1 -->|Solenoid or Piezo Drive| inj1
    ecm1 -->|Solenoid or Piezo Drive| inj2
    ecm1 -->|Solenoid or Piezo Drive| inj3
    ecm1 -->|Solenoid or Piezo Drive| inj4
    ecm1 -->|CAN Bus| pcv1
    rps1 -->|Analog| ecm1
    egt1 -->|Analog| ecm1
    dpf1 -->|Delta Pressure| ecm1
    scr1 -->|NOx Sensor| ecm1
    style ecm1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style rail1 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style dpf1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    style doc1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style scr1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **red ECU node** is the central brain that decides when to fire Pilot, Main, Post, *and* Retarded Post injections. The **yellow DPF node** is the unit that the RPI is trying to regenerate. The **light-blue rail** holds the pressurised fuel shared by all injectors.

## 4.2 Injection Timing Sequence per Combustion Cycle (Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title CRDI Injection Events in a 4-Stroke Cycle (720 deg)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s deg
    section Pilot
    Pilot Injection SOI 20 bTDC     :p1, 0, 20
    section Main
    Main Injection SOI 8 bTDC       :m1, 60, 30
    section Post
    Post Injection SOI 35 aTDC      :pi1, 200, 25
    section Retarded Post
    Retarded Post Inj SOI 110 aTDC  :rpi1, 380, 20
    section Expansion
    Expansion and exhaust           :ex1, 400, 200
    section Valve Events
    Intake Valve Open               :iv1, 700, 20
    Exhaust Valve Open              :ev1, 470, 20
```

> **Interpretation:** Pilot is well before TDC. Main is near TDC. **PI** lands during early expansion. **RPI** is deep into the expansion stroke, just before the exhaust valve opens — ensuring unburned fuel reaches the DOC while the cylinder is still sealed.

## 4.3 Control Loop for Retarded Post Injection Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Engine Running] --> read1[Read DPF Delta Pressure]
    read1 --> check1{DPF Soot Load above threshold}
    check1 -->|No| nom1[Normal Operation No RPI]
    check1 -->|Yes| read2[Read Coolant Temp and Vehicle Speed]
    read2 --> check2{Conditions Suitable for Regen}
    check2 -->|No| wait1[Wait Loop 5 sec then retry]
    wait1 --> read2
    check2 -->|Yes| en1[Enable Retarded Post Injection RPI]
    en1 --> fire1[ECU fires injector at 60 to 140 aTDC]
    fire1 --> doc1[DOC oxidises unburned HC and CO]
    doc1 --> exh1[Exhaust Temperature rises above 600 C]
    exh1 --> ox1[Soot oxidises in DPF]
    ox1 --> mon1[Monitor DPF Delta Pressure Drop]
    mon1 --> check3{Below threshold}
    check3 -->|No| fire1
    check3 -->|Yes| stop1[Disable RPI Resume Normal]
    style en1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style ox1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style stop1 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the closed loop:** The ECU *continuously monitors* DPF back-pressure (a proxy for soot mass). It only enables RPI when (a) soot has accumulated, (b) engine is warm, and (c) the vehicle is in a stable operating regime. This is a **demand-driven** control strategy, not a fixed schedule.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. Confusing **Post Injection (PI)** with **Pilot Injection** — they occur at *opposite* crank angles relative to TDC.
> 2. Stating that Retarded Post Injection **improves fuel economy** — it deliberately *worsens* BSFC by 2–5%. Correct phrasing: "improves aftertreatment performance at the cost of fuel consumption."
> 3. Forgetting that RPI does **not combust in the cylinder**; the energy is released in the **DOC**, not the combustion chamber.
> 4. Writing the rail pressure unit as "psi" or "Pa" — KTU strictly uses **bar** or **MPa**.
> 5. Mixing up **DPF regeneration temperature (~ 600 °C)** with **SCR light-off (~ 200–250 °C)**.
> 6. Omitting the **catalyst exotherm** when explaining why RPI heats the exhaust — most heat comes from HC/CO oxidation in the DOC, not from the fuel itself.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Post Injection in a CRDI system and list its two primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Post Injection is a *small, secondary fuel injection event* delivered by the CRDI injector after the Main Injection and before the exhaust valve opening, typically at **20°–60° aTDC**, with a volume of **1–6 mm³** per stroke at rail pressures of **600–1600 bar**.
>
> **Primary Functions (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **In-cylinder soot oxidation:** Late-cycle partial combustion of fuel-rich zones reduces particulate matter (PM) at the tailpipe by 30–50%.
> 2. **Exhaust temperature elevation for DPF regeneration:** The unburned hydrocarbons and CO reaching the DOC undergo exothermic oxidation, raising exhaust gas temperature to the soot-burn window of **~ 600 °C**, enabling active DPF regeneration.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Post Injection and Retarded Post Injection with respect to crank-angle timing, combustion location, and emissions target.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Post Injection (PI)** | **Retarded Post Injection (RPI)** |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Crank-angle SOI** (1 mark) | 20° – 60° aTDC | 60° – 140° aTDC |
| **Combustion location** (1 mark) | Mostly *in-cylinder* (50–80%) | Mostly *in DOC* after exhaust valve (5–15%) |
| **Emissions target** (1 mark) | Reduction of **PM at source** (in-cylinder soot oxidation) | Enabling **DPF regen** and **SCR light-off** by raising exhaust T |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer *either* the full Q-A or the full Q-B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **layout of a Common Rail Fuel Injection System** and explain the role of the ECU in scheduling multiple injection events. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **concept of Retarded Post Injection** in a CRDI engine. Discuss its role in **DPF regeneration**, the **Arrhenius dependence of soot oxidation on temperature**, and the **fuel-economy penalty** associated with it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Layout of CRDI System (7 marks):**

> **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The CRDI system consists of:
> 1. **Fuel Tank & Feed Pump:** Low-pressure supply (~ 4 bar).
> 2. **High-Pressure (HP) Pump:** Radial-piston pump driven by camshaft, generates **600–2000 bar**.
> 3. **Pressure Control Valve (PCV):** Mounted on the HP pump, regulates rail pressure via PWM from ECU.
> 4. **Common Rail Accumulator:** Stores high-pressure fuel with internal volume damping pulsations.
> 5. **Rail Pressure Sensor (RPS):** Provides closed-loop feedback to ECU.
> 6. **Injectors (4 or 6):** Solenoid-actuated (fast) or piezo-actuated (faster, multi-event capable).
> 7. **Engine Control Unit (ECU):** Schedules up to **9 injection events per cycle**.

> **ECU Scheduling Logic (2 marks):** The ECU computes, for every cylinder every cycle:
> - **Injection pressure** (from rail pressure map vs. speed/load).
> - **Start of Injection (SOI)** crank angle (from a calibrated injection map).
> - **Energising time** $t_{en}$ (proportional to fuel volume demand).
> - **Number of pulses** (Pilot, Main, Post, Retarded Post).
>
> The scheduling is updated **every 5°–10° CAD** based on inputs from crank position sensor, camshaft sensor, coolant temp, intake air temp, and lambda/NOx sensors.

> **Working of Injector (2 marks):** The ECU energises the solenoid, which lifts the needle valve via hydraulic servo action. The fuel at rail pressure sprays through 6–10 nozzle holes (≈ 100–200 µm dia.) into the combustion bowl of the piston. Piezo injectors use a *stack actuator* to achieve **3–5 µs response time**, enabling closely-spaced multiple injections.

**(b) Retarded Post Injection (7 marks):**

> **Concept (2 marks):** RPI is a late-cycle injection timed at **60°–140° aTDC** that delivers a small fuel charge (1–4 mm³) into the *expansion stroke*. The piston is already moving down, so the cylinder pressure is low. Most of the fuel does **not combust in-cylinder**; it escapes with the exhaust gases as **unburned HC and CO**, which then encounter the hot DOC.

> **Role in DPF Regeneration (2 marks):** In the DOC, HC and CO undergo catalytic oxidation:
> - $HC + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O \quad \Delta H < 0$
> - $CO + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -283 \text{ kJ/mol}$
>
> This exotherm raises the exhaust gas temperature from a low-load value of **~ 200 °C** to the **DPF regeneration window of ~ 600 °C**. Soot is then oxidised per:
> - $C_{(soot)} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$

> **Arrhenius Temperature Dependence (2 marks):** The soot oxidation rate constant is:
> - $k_{ox} = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T_{DPF})}$
>
> With $E_a \approx 150$ kJ/mol, every **+50 K** rise in $T_{DPF}$ roughly **doubles** $k_{ox}$. Hence raising $T_{DPF}$ from 550 °C to 650 °C accelerates regeneration by **~ 4×**, justifying the use of RPI as a thermal management tool.

> **Fuel-Economy Penalty (1 mark):** Because RPI fuel does *not contribute to work output* but still consumes diesel, the **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** increases by **2–5%** during regeneration events. This is the trade-off accepted by OEMs to maintain DPF functionality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **Pilot, Main, Post, and Retarded Post injections** in a modern CRDI diesel engine with a suitable timing diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2.0 L, 4-cylinder CRDI engine operates at **2400 rpm** with a Retarded Post Injection of **3 mg/stroke**. The DOC oxidation efficiency is **90%**, exhaust mass flow is **0.10 kg/s**, and $c_{p,exh} = 1.10$ kJ/kg·K. Diesel LHV = **42.5 MJ/kg**. Estimate the **theoretical exhaust temperature rise**. Discuss the practical correction factor and the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Comparison of Injection Events (7 marks):**

| Parameter (1.5 marks total) | **Pilot** | **Main** | **Post** | **Retarded Post** |
|------------------------------|-----------|----------|----------|--------------------|
| **SOI (CAD aTDC)** | 15° – 25° bTDC | 5° – 12° bTDC | 20° – 60° aTDC | 60° – 140° aTDC |
| **Volume (mm³)** | 1 – 2 | 30 – 80 | 2 – 6 | 1 – 4 |
| **Combustion %** | 100% | 100% | 50 – 80% | 5 – 15% |
| **Purpose** | Noise & vibration reduction | Power generation | In-cylinder soot oxidation | DOC/DPF thermal management |
| **Pressure** | 400 – 800 bar | 1600 – 2000 bar | 800 – 1600 bar | 600 – 1200 bar |

> **Timing Diagram (1.5 marks):** Sketch a 720° 4-stroke cycle with x-axis as crank angle and y-axis as injection rate. Mark Pilot, Main, PI, RPI as discrete rectangular pulses at their respective SOIs. The Main pulse is the largest; RPI is the latest.

> **Engineering significance (1 mark):** Multiple injections decouple the conflicting demands of low emissions (PM, NOx), low fuel consumption, and low noise — a single main injection cannot simultaneously satisfy all three. Multiple-injection CRDI is the *enabling technology* for **BS-VI / Euro 6** compliance.

**(b) Numerical Estimation of Exhaust Temperature Rise (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Mass flow rate of RPI fuel (1.5 marks):**
> - Working strokes per second per cylinder = $\dfrac{2400}{2 \cdot 60} = 20$
> - Total RPI fuel rate = $3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 20 \cdot 4 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$

> **Step 2 — Thermal power released (2 marks):**
> - $\dot{Q}_{RPI} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 42.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.90 = 9180 \text{ W}$

> **Step 3 — Theoretical exhaust temperature rise (2 marks):**
> - $\Delta T_{exh} = \dfrac{9180}{0.10 \cdot 1100} = 83.45 \text{ K} \approx \mathbf{83 \text{ °C}}$

> **Step 4 — Practical correction and engineering significance (1.5 marks):**
> - In practice, only **60–80%** of the theoretical energy reaches the exhaust (cylinder wall losses, partial in-cylinder combustion of RPI fuel, heat transfer to intake residuals).
> - Realistic temperature rise: **~ 50–65 °C** — sufficient to push exhaust T from low-load baseline of ~ 250 °C to the **DPF regeneration window (~ 600 °C)**.
> - **Engineering significance:** This calculation confirms that a *tiny* additional fuel dose (3 mg/stroke ≈ 1.5% of main injection) can elevate exhaust T by 80 K, validating RPI as an efficient thermal management tool for DPF regen.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- **Post Injection (PI):** *Secondary injection after Main, before exhaust valve open.* SOI: 20°–60° aTDC. Volume: 2–6 mm³. **In-cylinder partial combustion** oxidises soot → **PM reduction 30–50%**.
- **Retarded Post Injection (RPI):** *Deeply retarded secondary injection.* SOI: 60°–140° aTDC. Volume: 1–4 mm³. **Mostly does NOT combust in-cylinder** → unburned HC/CO travels to **DOC** where **exothermic oxidation** raises exhaust T.
- **Primary purpose of RPI:** Enable **DPF regeneration** by raising exhaust T to **~ 600 °C** (soot oxidation light-off) and support **SCR light-off** at **~ 200–250 °C**.
- **Rail pressure during PI/RPI:** **600–1600 bar** — system is already pressurised from Main injection.
- **Diesel LHV = 42.5 MJ/kg** — used in $\Delta T_{exh}$ calculations.
- **DPF regeneration kinetics:** Arrhenius-type, $E_a \approx 150$ kJ/mol — every +50 K roughly doubles oxidation rate.
- **Catalyst exotherm:** Most of the heating effect of RPI comes from **HC + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O** and **CO + ½O₂ → CO₂** reactions in the **DOC**, not from the fuel itself.
- **Trade-offs:** RPI worsens BSFC by **2–5%**, increases HC/CO emissions, and adds thermal load on the DOC.
- **ECU logic:** RPI is enabled *on-demand* when DPF ΔP exceeds threshold, vehicle speed is stable, and coolant T > 70 °C.
- **Modern CRDI** uses up to **9 injection events per cycle** (multiple pilots, mains, posts, retarded posts).
- **Differential pressure (ΔP) across DPF** is the key sensor input for triggering RPI — empirical model: $\Delta P_{DPF} = K_1 \cdot m_{soot} + K_2 \cdot \dot{V}_{exh}$.
- **SCR requires NH₃** — *excessive* RPI can cause NH₃ slip, so the ECU must limit RPI duration.
- **Piezo injectors** (response ~ 3 µs) are essential for closely-spaced multiple events; older solenoid injectors (~ 200 µs) cannot.
- **Exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)** + RPI is a common synergy: EGR lowers peak combustion T (less NOx) and RPI compensates for the lower exhaust T by adding heat for DPF regen.

---

**End of Module 2 — Post Injection & Retarded Post Injection Layout of CRDI System Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education, NEP 2020 aligned.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working of Components Like Fuel Injector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **fuel injector** is an electronically controlled, precision solenoid-actuated (or piezo-actuated) valve that delivers a finely atomized, precisely metered quantity of pressurized fuel into the intake manifold (Port Fuel Injection – PFI) or directly into the combustion chamber (Gasoline Direct Injection – GDI / Common Rail Diesel Injection – CRDi). The injector forms the final actuator stage of the Electronic Control Unit (ECU) closed-loop fuel management strategy and operates on the principle of **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fuel injector is the interface between the *electronic command signal* (low-power, ~1–2 A current pulse) and the *mechanical hydraulic domain* (high-pressure fuel delivery at 3 bar – 2000 bar depending on the system).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Gun Trigger"**
> Imagine a high-pressure water gun (representing the fuel rail) connected to a solenoid-driven trigger (the injector coil). When you press a button (ECU sends a 12 V pulse), an electromagnet pulls a tiny metal plunger back against a spring, opening a needle valve. Pressing longer = more water = more fuel. Releasing = spring snaps the plunger shut, stopping the spray. The ECU essentially "presses the trigger" thousands of times per second, varying the press duration to control exactly how much fuel enters the cylinder.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants govern injector operation and appear frequently in numerical problems:

- **Coil resistance (solenoid injector): 2 – 16 Ω** (typically **12 Ω** for peak-and-hold drivers).
- **Operating voltage:** **12 V – 14 V DC** (nominal automotive bus).
- **Injector opening time (lag): ~1.0 – 1.5 ms**.
- **Injector closing time (lag): ~0.5 – 1.0 ms**.
- **Static flow rate (SFR): 100 – 400 cc/min** at **3.0 bar** reference pressure.
- **Fuel rail pressure:** **3 – 6 bar (PFI)**, **100 – 350 bar (GDI)**, **1500 – 2500 bar (CRDi)**.
- **Pull-in current:** **4 – 6 A**; **Hold current:** **1 – 2 A** (peak-and-hold drive).

### 1.4 Classification of Fuel Injectors

| Injector Type | Drive Principle | Typical Application | Operating Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solenoid Top-Feed (PFI) | Electromagnetic solenoid | Petrol MPFI engines | 3 – 4 bar |
| Solenoid Bottom-Feed (TBI) | Electromagnetic solenoid | Older throttle-body systems | 1 – 1.5 bar |
| Piezo Common-Rail | Piezoelectric crystal stack | Modern CRDi diesel | 1500 – 2500 bar |
| Solenoid Direct Injection | Electromagnetic solenoid | GDI petrol engines | 100 – 350 bar |
| Unit Injector (Pump-Nozzle) | Mechanical + Solenoid | Older IDI diesel | 300 – 1100 bar |

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Internal Construction of a Solenoid-Type Fuel Injector

The fuel injector consists of the following functionally distinct sub-assemblies:

1. **Electrical Connector & Solenoid Coil** – receives the PWM pulse from the ECU.
2. **Magnetic Core / Armature (Plunger)** – converts magnetic force into linear motion.
3. **Needle Valve** – a precision-ground pin that opens/closes the nozzle orifice.
4. **Return Spring** – biases the needle to the closed position when de-energised.
5. **Fuel Inlet Screen / O-ring** – filters debris and seals against the fuel rail.
6. **Nozzle Orifice (Spray Tip)** – atomizes fuel into a conical or multi-hole spray pattern.
7. **Calibration Pintle Cap** – shapes the spray cone angle.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Sequence

**Step 1 — ECU Decision:** Based on inputs from the MAP sensor, MAF sensor, engine coolant temperature (ECT), oxygen sensor (lambda), and crankshaft position (CKP), the ECU computes the required *injection pulse width (PW)* in milliseconds.

**Step 2 — Driver Stage Activation:** The ECU's final-stage power transistor (usually a Darlington pair or MOSFET) completes the circuit between the **12 V battery supply** and the injector coil, sinking it to ground.

**Step 3 — Magnetic Flux Build-up:** Current $i(t)$ begins to rise through the coil. The current follows an exponential RL-charging law:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_L}\right)$$

where the **electrical time constant** is:

$$\tau_L = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Armature Lift:** When $i(t)$ reaches the **pull-in current threshold** ($I_p \approx 4$–$6$ A), the electromagnetic force $F_m$ overcomes the combined resistance of the return spring + fuel back-pressure + armature mass.

**Step 5 — Needle Valve Opening:** The needle lifts by a calibrated stroke of typically **0.05 – 0.15 mm**, uncovering the nozzle orifice.

**Step 6 — Fuel Atomization:** Pressurized fuel from the rail enters the nozzle, undergoes shear-driven and pressure-driven atomization, producing droplets of **10 – 100 µm** diameter.

**Step 7 — ECU Pulse Termination:** When the programmed PW expires, the driver transistor switches OFF.

**Step 8 — Magnetic Collapse & Needle Closure:** Current decays exponentially, the magnetic flux collapses, and the return spring forces the needle back onto its seat, sealing the nozzle. This produces a sharp cut-off essential for metering accuracy.

### 2.3 Why Peak-and-Hold Drive Strategy?

A simple 12 V ON/OFF drive causes a strong magnetic field that can overheat the coil (P = V²/R ≈ 18 W continuous). To solve this:

- **Pull-in phase (0 – 1 ms):** Full battery voltage applied to rapidly open the valve against the spring.
- **Hold phase (after opening):** Current reduced (often via a series resistor or PWM) to ~1–2 A to maintain the valve open with minimum heat dissipation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Injector fuel quantity | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot t_{inj}$ | mg or mm³ | Mass injected per pulse |
| 2 | Mass flow rate (Bernoulli) | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | kg/s | Flow through nozzle orifice |
| 3 | Effective nozzle area | $A_{eff} = N \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | m² | Total flow area of N holes |
| 4 | Magnetic force on armature | $F_m = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | N | Maxwell stress formulation |
| 5 | Coil inductance (solenoid) | $L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}$ | H | Energy storage in magnetic field |
| 6 | RL time constant | $\tau_L = L / R$ | s | Current rise/decay time |
| 7 | Instantaneous coil current | $i(t) = (V_s / R)(1 - e^{-t/\tau_L})$ | A | Exponential build-up |
| 8 | Static flow rate (SFR) | $SFR = 60 \cdot Q_{cycle} / t_{cycle}$ | cc/min | Rated injector capacity |
| 9 | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7 : 1$ | — | Petrol gasoline reference |
| 10 | Lambda coefficient | $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$ | — | Mixture strength indicator |
| 11 | Injection pulse width | $PW = \frac{m_{fuel,req}}{\dot{m}_{inj}(P_{rail},V_{bat})}$ | ms | ECU-controlled variable |
| 12 | Piezo stack displacement | $\Delta\ell = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V_{appl}$ | m | Direct stack actuator stroke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Note:** Notice that in the formula sheet table above, all pipe delimiters are the standard markdown table separators. Where absolute value or conditional notation is required in any inline math (e.g., $\vert \Delta P \vert$), the LaTeX vertical bar commands `\vert` or `\mid` are used to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fuel injector is the single most critical actuator in achieving:

- **Emission compliance** — precise lambda control enables the three-way catalytic converter to operate at its peak efficiency window ($\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$).
- **Fuel economy** — accurate metering prevents enrichment losses.
- **Cold-start capability** — asynchronous (single-pulse) injections at cranking.
- **Driveability** — smooth tip-in/tip-out response via transient fuel compensation.
- **Knock resistance** — closed-loop lambda correction combined with knock control retards timing safely.
