#### C. Fully Active Suspension System
- An **external actuator** (hydraulic or electro-mechanical) supplies force in addition to the spring.
- Can *both* dissipate and *add* energy to the suspension.
- Maintains the vehicle body nearly level under all conditions.
- **Cost:** High; **Effectiveness:** Highest. (Used in high-end systems like Mercedes ABC, Lexus LS, Citroën Hydropneumatic).

### 2.2 Major System Components

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners frequently ask for block diagrams and the role of each sensor. Memorize the input-output mapping below.

#### 2.2.1 Input Sensors

| Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheel Speed Sensor** | Rotational speed of each wheel | Detects wheel hop/imminent lock-up |
| **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** | Longitudinal vehicle velocity | Scales damping with speed |
| **Steering Angle Sensor** | Angle of steering wheel | Detects cornering intent |
| **Lateral Acceleration Sensor** | Sideways G-force | Detects cornering severity |
| **Longitudinal Acceleration Sensor** | Forward/backward G-force | Detects braking/acceleration pitch |
| **Brake Pressure Sensor** | Hydraulic pressure in brake line | Detects hard braking events |
| **Ride Height Sensor** | Distance between body & axle | Maintains level stance and adjusts for load |
| **Body Acceleration Sensor (Vertical)** | Vertical G-force at body | Detects road-induced vibration |

#### 2.2.2 Electronic Control Unit (ECU)
- Microprocessor-based module running a **closed-loop control algorithm**.
- Sample rate typically **≥ 200 Hz** to capture wheel-hop frequencies.
- Outputs **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** signals to actuator solenoid valves.

#### 2.2.3 Actuators
- **Solenoid-Controlled Hydraulic Valves:** Regulate oil flow in dampers.
- **MR Fluid Dampers:** Use Magneto-Rheological fluid whose viscosity changes in milliseconds under a magnetic field.
- **Air Suspension Compressors + Valves:** Adjust spring rate by changing air spring pressure.
- **Hydraulic Rams (Active Systems):** Provide upward/downward force at each wheel.

### 2.3 Control Strategy — Sky-Hook Damping

The **Sky-Hook Control** concept is the most important theoretical principle in semi-active suspension design. It is a favourite KTU question topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Idea:** Imagine the vehicle body is "hooked" to the sky by a virtual damper. This virtual damper only resists vertical motion *of the body* — the moment the body tries to move up or down, the damper applies a counter-force. The wheel, however, is free to follow the road surface.

The resulting ideal damping force is:

$$F_{damper, ideal} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_{b}$$

where $C_{sky}$ is the sky-hook damping coefficient and $\dot{z}_{b}$ is the *vertical velocity of the vehicle body*.

Since a real damper cannot push the wheel down (only resist compression), the practical implementation uses a **clipped sky-hook rule**:

$$F_{damper} = \begin{cases} -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_{b} & \text{if } (\dot{z}_{b} - \dot{z}_{w})(\dot{z}_{b}) > 0 \text{ and } F_{damper} \cdot \dot{z}_{rel} \geq 0 \\ F_{passive} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\dot{z}_{w}$ is the wheel vertical velocity and $\dot{z}_{rel} = \dot{z}_{w} - \dot{z}_{b}$ is the relative velocity across the damper.

> **Real-world utility:** Sky-hook control is implemented in production cars (e.g., Bilstein, Öhlins, Delphi MRC systems) and reduces body acceleration by **30 – 50 %** compared to passive damping over the same road profile.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Term | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{d} = c \cdot \dot{z}_{rel}$ | Viscous damping force | N | Damper force calculation |
| 2 | $c_{eq} = \sqrt{c \cdot k}$ | Critical damping coeff. | N·s/m | Transient response design |
| 3 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s | Body bounce mode |
| 4 | $\zeta = c / (2 \sqrt{km})$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | Comfort vs. handling |
| 5 | $F_{damper,sky} = -C_{sky}\dot{z}_b$ | Sky-hook target force | N | Semi-active control law |
| 6 | $\tau_{MR} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot H^2}{2}$ | MR fluid yield stress | Pa | MR damper design |
| 7 | $P_{air} \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | Ideal gas (air spring) | Pa·m³ | Ride height adjust |
| 8 | $a_{body} = \ddot{z}_b$ | Body vertical accel. | m/s² | Ride comfort metric |
| 9 | $f_{wheel} = \frac{v}{2\pi r}$ | Wheel hop frequency | Hz | Anti-hop control |
| 10 | $T_{resp} = \frac{1}{f_{sample}}$ | ECU control period | s | Real-time design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Board:** Always specify the SI unit when defining a symbol. Examiners deduct marks for missing units. For example, write "$c$ = damping coefficient in N·s/m", not just "$c$ = damping coefficient".

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quarter-Car Two-Degree-of-Freedom Model

The standard KTU analytical model for a suspension is the **quarter-car model**, which represents one corner of the vehicle (one wheel + one quarter of the body mass).

#### 3.1.1 System Definition

- Let $m_b$ = sprung mass (one quarter of vehicle body).
- Let $m_w$ = unsprung mass (wheel + tyre + brake assembly).
- Let $k_s$ = suspension spring stiffness (N/m).
- Let $c_s$ = suspension damping coefficient (N·s/m).
- Let $k_t$ = tyre stiffness (N/m).
- Let $z_b$ = vertical displacement of body (m).
- Let $z_w$ = vertical displacement of wheel centre (m).
- Let $z_r$ = road input displacement (m).

#### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram Equations

**For the Sprung Mass (Body):**
The body is acted upon by the suspension spring force (pulling up when compressed) and the damper force (opposing relative velocity).

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b = -k_s (z_b - z_w) - c_s (\dot{z}_b - \dot{z}_w)$$

**For the Unsprung Mass (Wheel):**
The wheel is acted upon by the spring (pulling down), the damper (opposing relative motion), and the tyre spring force (pushing wheel up when road goes up).

$$m_w \ddot{z}_w = k_s (z_b - z_w) + c_s (\dot{z}_b - \dot{z}_w) - k_t (z_w - z_r)$$

#### 3.1.3 Rearranging into State-Space Form

Bring all acceleration terms to the left-hand side:

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b + c_s \dot{z}_b - c_s \dot{z}_w + k_s z_b - k_s z_w = 0$$

$$m_w \ddot{z}_w - c_s \dot{z}_b + c_s \dot{z}_w + (k_s + k_t) z_w - k_s z_b = k_t z_r$$

#### 3.1.4 Natural Frequency Derivation (Body Bounce Mode)

For the body alone (ignoring unsprung mass for a quick estimate), the equation reduces to:

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b + c_s \dot{z}_b + k_s z_b = F_{road}$$

This is a standard second-order mass-spring-damper system. The **undamped natural frequency** is found by setting the damping term to zero and assuming a solution $z_b(t) = Z \cos(\omega t)$:

$$-m_b \omega^2 Z + k_s Z = 0$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k_s}{m_b}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_b}} \quad \text{rad/s}}$$

The corresponding frequency in Hertz is:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_b}} \quad \text{Hz}$$

#### 3.1.5 Critical Damping Derivation

The critical damping coefficient is the value of $c$ that prevents any oscillation. For the simplified single-DOF body system:

$$c_{critical} = 2 m_b \omega_n = 2\sqrt{k_s m_b}$$

The **damping ratio** (a dimensionless measure of damping effectiveness) is:

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_b}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For a comfortable ride, $\zeta$ is typically held in the range **0.2 – 0.4** (under-damped). For a sport-tuned vehicle, $\zeta$ approaches **0.7** (near critical). A value of $\zeta = 1.0$ gives critical damping (no overshoot, but harsh). Values above 1.0 are over-damped and feel "wooden".

#### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A vehicle has sprung mass per wheel $m_b = 400$ kg, suspension stiffness $k_s = 20{,}000$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c_s = 1{,}600$ N·s/m. Find (a) natural frequency, (b) damping ratio, and (c) state whether the system is under/critical/over-damped.

**Solution (a) — Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_b}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{400}} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{7.07}{2\pi} \approx 1.125 \text{ Hz}$$

**Solution (b) — Damping Ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_b}} = \frac{1600}{2\sqrt{20{,}000 \times 400}} = \frac{1600}{2 \times 2828.4} = \frac{1600}{5656.8} \approx 0.283$$

**Solution (c) — Classification:**

Since $\zeta = 0.283 < 1.0$, the system is **under-damped** — it will oscillate a few cycles before settling. This is desirable for ride comfort because the body returns to equilibrium smoothly.

#### 3.1.7 Analytical Matrix — Performance Trade-offs

| Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | Feel | Body Bounce | Wheel Hop | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **< 0.2** | Floaty, boat-like | Large | Small | Poor — never used |
| **0.2 – 0.4** | Comfortable | Moderate | Well-controlled | Luxury sedans |
| **0.4 – 0.7** | Firm, controlled | Small | Moderate | Sports sedans |
| **0.7 – 1.0** | Stiff, harsh | Very small | Can be large | Race cars (with active anti-roll bars) |
| **> 1.0** | Dead, wooden | None | Large | Trucks carrying heavy loads |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electronic Suspension System

The Mermaid diagram below maps the closed-loop signal flow from road excitation to damper actuation, following the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasis on *system-level understanding*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RoadInput["Road Surface Excitation z_r"]:::input
    WheelSensor["Wheel Speed Sensor & Vertical Accelerometer"]:::sensor
    BodySensor["Body Vertical Accelerometer"]:::sensor
    SteerSensor["Steering Angle Sensor"]:::sensor
    BrakeSensor["Brake Pressure Sensor"]:::sensor
    HeightSensor["Ride Height Sensor"]:::sensor
    SpeedSensor["Vehicle Speed Sensor"]:::sensor
    ECU["Electronic Control Unit (ECU)\nControl Algorithm: Sky-Hook"]:::controller
    PWMGen["PWM Signal Generator\nFrequency: 100-500 Hz"]:::controller
    DamperActuator["Variable Damping Actuator\nMR Fluid or Solenoid Valve"]:::actuator
    Spring["Air Spring / Coil Spring"]:::mech
    Damper["Hydraulic Damper"]:::mech
    Body["Vehicle Body (Sprung Mass)"]:::mech
    Driver["Driver Mode Select\nComfort / Normal / Sport"]:::input

    RoadInput --> WheelSensor
    RoadInput --> Spring
    RoadInput --> Damper
    Spring --> Body
    Damper --> Body
    Body --> BodySensor
    WheelSensor --> ECU
    BodySensor --> ECU
    SteerSensor --> ECU
    BrakeSensor --> ECU
    HeightSensor --> ECU
    SpeedSensor --> ECU
    Driver --> ECU
    ECU --> PWMGen
    PWMGen --> DamperActuator
    DamperActuator --> Damper
    DamperActuator --> Spring

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU exams:** Trace the signal path from sensors (blue) → ECU (pink) → PWM (pink) → Actuator (green) → Mechanical components (grey). Note the feedback loop: the body's response is *measured* by the body accelerometer and fed back to the ECU, making it a **closed-loop system**.

### 4.2 Sky-Hook Control Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["ECU Reads Sensors Every 5 ms"]:::ctrl
    ReadZb["Read Body Vertical Velocity z_dot_b"]:::sensor
    ReadZw["Read Wheel Vertical Velocity z_dot_w"]:::sensor
    ComputeRel["Compute Relative Velocity z_dot_rel = z_dot_w - z_dot_b"]:::math
    Condition{"z_dot_b times z_dot_rel less than 0?"}:::decide
    ForceMax["Set Damper to MAXIMUM Damping Force"]:::actuate
    ForceMin["Set Damper to MINIMUM Damping Force"]:::actuate
    Hold["Hold Current Damping Force"]:::actuate
    Apply["Send PWM Command to Solenoid Valve"]:::ctrl
    Loop["Wait 5 ms and Repeat"]:::ctrl

    Start --> ReadZb
    Start --> ReadZw
    ReadZb --> ComputeRel
    ReadZw --> ComputeRel
    ComputeRel --> Condition
    Condition -- Yes --> ForceMax
    Condition -- No --> ForceMin
    ForceMax --> Apply
    ForceMin --> Apply
    Apply --> Loop

    classDef ctrl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef math fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decide fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuate fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explanation of the decision logic:** When $\dot{z}_b \cdot \dot{z}_{rel} < 0$, the body and wheel are moving in *opposite directions relative to the strut compression*. This is the moment the damper should be **stiff** to absorb energy. Otherwise, the damper should be **soft** to allow the suspension to absorb the bump gently.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an electronic suspension system. List any four sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electronic suspension system** is a computer-controlled vehicle suspension that uses sensors, an ECU, and actuators to continuously vary damping force, spring rate, and ride height in response to real-time road and driving conditions, optimizing both ride comfort and handling. *(1 mark)*

Four sensors used: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*
1. **Vehicle speed sensor**
2. **Steering angle sensor**
3. **Brake pressure sensor**
4. **Body (vertical) acceleration sensor / Ride height sensor**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between active and semi-active suspension systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Semi-Active | Fully Active |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy input** | No external energy to add force; only dissipates | External hydraulic/electrical energy supplied |
| **Damping control** | Variable between min & max | Continuous, can apply force in either direction |
| **Components** | MR damper or solenoid valve damper | Hydraulic actuator + spring + damper |
| **Cost & complexity** | Moderate | High |
| **Energy storage** | Cannot store energy | Can add energy to the system |

*(Award 1 mark for each of: definition of semi-active, definition of active, and any two correct differentiating points.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Question A: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an electronic suspension system. Describe the function of the ECU and the role of any four sensors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the natural frequency and damping ratio of a quarter-car suspension model. *(7 marks)*

**OR**

**Question B: (a)** What is sky-hook control? Derive the sky-hook damping law and explain its physical significance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A semi-active suspension has sprung mass $m_b = 350$ kg, suspension stiffness $k_s = 17{,}500$ N/m, and a variable damping coefficient that can be set between $c_{min} = 800$ N·s/m and $c_{max} = 4{,}000$ N·s/m. Determine: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the damping ratio at maximum damping, and (iii) comment on ride comfort. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Working *(7 marks)*

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. The road surface creates a vertical disturbance $z_r(t)$ that acts on the wheel.
2. The wheel displacement $z_w(t)$ and body displacement $z_b(t)$ are sensed by accelerometers mounted on each.
3. Additional sensors provide vehicle speed, steering angle, brake pressure, and ride height data.
4. The **ECU** runs a real-time algorithm (often a sky-hook or ground-hook variant) that computes the *ideal* damping force required at each wheel.
5. The ECU sends a **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** signal to the actuator solenoid.
6. The actuator adjusts the damping force within **5 – 10 ms**, often before the body even feels the disturbance.

**Block Diagram (2 marks)** — refer to SECTION_4 Figure above; the student should sketch a similar flow with Sensors → ECU → Actuator → Damper → Body, with feedback from body to ECU.

**Role of ECU (1 mark):** The ECU is a 32-bit microcontroller running a deterministic real-time operating system. It samples sensors at ≥ 200 Hz, executes the control law using floating-point math, and outputs a PWM duty cycle proportional to the desired damping force.

**Role of any 4 sensors (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
- **Wheel speed sensor** → Detects wheel hop; triggers stiff damping to suppress resonance.
- **Steering angle sensor** → Predicts cornering; pre-empts body roll by stiffening the outer dampers.
- **Brake pressure sensor** → Detects emergency braking; pre-empts dive by stiffening front dampers.
- **Body vertical accelerometer** → Direct feedback of body motion for closed-loop sky-hook control.
- **Ride height sensor** → Detects load changes (passenger, cargo) and adjusts air spring pressure.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio *(7 marks)*

**Setup (2 marks):**

The quarter-car body is modelled as a single-DOF mass-spring-damper system:

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b + c_s \dot{z}_b + k_s z_b = F_{road}(t)$$

**Natural Frequency (2.5 marks):**

Assume a harmonic solution $z_b(t) = Z e^{j\omega t}$. Substituting:

$$-m_b \omega^2 Z + j c_s \omega Z + k_s Z = F_0$$

For the *undamped* case ($c_s = 0$), the system resonates when denominator magnitude is zero:

$$-m_b \omega^2 + k_s = 0$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_b}} \text{ rad/s} \quad ; \quad f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_b}} \text{ Hz}}$$

**Damping Ratio (2.5 marks):**

The damping ratio compares actual damping to critical damping. Critical damping occurs at:

$$c_{crit} = 2 m_b \omega_n = 2\sqrt{k_s m_b}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{c_s}{c_{crit}} = \frac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_b}}}$$

For a comfortable ride, **0.2 ≤ ζ ≤ 0.4**.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — Sky-Hook Control *(7 marks)*

**Definition (2 marks):**

The **sky-hook control** is a reference model for semi-active suspension where the vehicle body is conceptually attached to a *fixed inertial reference* (the "sky") by a virtual damper of coefficient $C_{sky}$. The virtual damper only reacts to body motion, not wheel motion, giving ideal ride isolation.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The target ideal force exerted on the body is:

$$F_{ideal} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_b$$

Since a real damper cannot pull the wheel down (it can only resist compression), the **clipped sky-hook law** is used:

$$F_{damper} = \begin{cases} -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_b, & \text{if } F_{ideal} \cdot (\dot{z}_w - \dot{z}_b) \geq 0 \text{ and } \dot{z}_b (\dot{z}_w - \dot{z}_b) > 0 \\ C_{min} (\dot{z}_w - \dot{z}_b), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This ensures the damper force always opposes relative strut motion, and the magnitude is capped at either $F_{ideal}$ or the maximum physically achievable force.

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**

- It achieves the **best possible ride comfort** by attenuating body oscillations at the natural bounce frequency (1 – 2 Hz).
- Body acceleration is reduced by **30 – 50 %** compared to passive damping.
- It requires only **damping modulation** — no external energy source — making it ideal for semi-active systems using MR fluid dampers.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $m_b = 350$ kg, $k_s = 17{,}500$ N/m, $c_{min} = 800$ N·s/m, $c_{max} = 4{,}000$ N·s/m.

**(i) Natural Frequency (2 marks):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_b}} = \sqrt{\frac{17{,}500}{350}} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{7.07}{2\pi} \approx 1.125 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Damping Ratio at Maximum Damping (3 marks):**

$$\zeta_{max} = \frac{c_{max}}{2\sqrt{k_s m_b}} = \frac{4{,}000}{2 \times \sqrt{17{,}500 \times 350}}$$

$$\zeta_{max} = \frac{4{,}000}{2 \times \sqrt{6{,}125{,}000}} = \frac{4{,}000}{2 \times 2{,}475} = \frac{4{,}000}{4{,}950}$$

$$\boxed{\zeta_{max} \approx 0.808}$$

**At minimum damping:**

$$\zeta_{min} = \frac{800}{4{,}950} \approx 0.162$$

**(iii) Comment on Ride Comfort (2 marks):**

At maximum damping ($\zeta \approx 0.81$), the system is **near critically-damped** — body bounce is heavily suppressed, giving sport-mode firm handling but a slightly harsh ride.

At minimum damping ($\zeta \approx 0.16$), the system is **lightly damped** — body oscillates freely, giving a soft, floaty comfort mode.

> The ECU continuously varies $\zeta$ between **0.16 and 0.81** in real time, providing the driver with the full spectrum between comfort and sport tuning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to include N·s/m for damping coefficient or rad/s for natural frequency. *Penalty: ½ mark each.*
> 2. **Confusing Active with Semi-Active:** Writing that a semi-active system *adds* energy. It does not. *Penalty: Full 2 marks lost on the differentiation question.*
> 3. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** In the 7-mark working question, students often describe text only and skip the diagram. *Penalty: At least 1.5 marks lost.*
> 4. **Mixing up $\omega_n$ and $f_n$:** Using rad/s where Hz is required, or vice versa. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 5. **Forgetting the Formula for $\zeta$:** The critical-damping denominator $2\sqrt{km}$ is often miswritten as $2\pi\sqrt{km}$ (mixing in the $2\pi$ conversion factor). *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Not stating the boundary of $\zeta$ for comfort:** Examiners reward students who explicitly state that $\zeta$ should lie in **0.2 – 0.4** for comfort.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electronic Suspension System**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** ECU-controlled system that varies damping, spring rate, and ride height in real time.
>
> ✅ **Three Types:** Adaptive (discrete modes), Semi-Active (continuous variable damping), Fully Active (force in both directions).
>
> ✅ **Eight Key Sensors:** Wheel speed, vehicle speed, steering angle, lateral G, longitudinal G, brake pressure, ride height, body vertical acceleration.
>
> ✅ **Sky-Hook Damping Law:** $F = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_b$ — the gold-standard reference for body isolation.
>
> ✅ **Quarter-Car Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_s / m_b}$ rad/s.
>
> ✅ **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = c_s / (2\sqrt{k_s m_b})$ — comfort range is **0.2 – 0.4**.
>
> ✅ **MR Fluid Damper:** Magneto-Rheological fluid changes viscosity in **< 5 ms** under a magnetic field; used in most modern semi-active systems (e.g., Delphi MagneRide, Cadillac, Ferrari).
>
> ✅ **Air Suspension:** Uses compressed air in bellows springs to vary effective spring rate and adjust ride height; pressure controlled by an electric compressor and solenoid valves.
>
> ✅ **ECU Sampling:** Minimum **200 Hz**; actuator response **< 10 ms**.
>
> ✅ **Real-world Examples:** Mercedes-Benz Active Body Control (ABC), BMW AdaptiveDrive, Audi Adaptive Air Suspension, Citroën Hydropneumatic, Ferrari SCM (Skyhook Control Module).
>
> ✅ **Key Trade-off:** Comfort ($\zeta \approx 0.3$) vs. Handling ($\zeta \approx 0.7$) — electronic suspension resolves this by making $\zeta$ a *function of time* rather than a fixed value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airbags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Airbags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Airbag** (also called Supplementary Restraint System / SRS) is a vehicle safety device consisting of a flexible fabric envelope (cushion) designed to inflate rapidly during a vehicle collision, providing a soft cushioning surface between the occupant and the vehicle interior to reduce the severity of injuries caused by impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEMRT831):** An airbag is a *passive safety restraint system* that deploys once during a crash event when deceleration exceeds a predefined threshold, supplementing (not replacing) the seatbelt system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking and accidentally collide with a wall — your hands instinctively come forward to soften the blow. An airbag does the exact same job, but at a speed **150 times faster** and with a much larger "hand" made of nylon fabric filled with nitrogen gas. It is the vehicle's built-in reflex action to protect you during an unexpected crash.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics**
> - Deployment Time: **20–30 milliseconds** (ms)
> - Deflation Time: **100 ms**
> - Inflation Pressure: **70 kPa** (approximately)
> - Operating Temperature Range: **−40 °C to +85 °C**
> - Gas used: Primarily **Nitrogen (N₂)**
> - Propellant Chemical: **Sodium Azide (NaN₃)**

### 1.3 Role in a Vehicle

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| Energy Absorption | Absorbs kinetic energy through controlled deflation |
| Force Distribution | Spreads impact force over larger body area |
| Head/Neck Protection | Prevents direct impact with steering/dashboard |
| Complement to Seatbelt | Works in conjunction with 3-point seatbelts |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember that the airbag is a **supplementary** system — it does *not* replace the seatbelt. KTU questions often test this conceptual distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle – Stepwise Logic

The deployment of an airbag follows a precise, time-critical sequence:

1. **Crash Initiation:** A vehicle strikes an obstacle, creating rapid deceleration (typically > **18–20 km/h** barrier equivalent speed).
2. **Sensor Activation:** Crash sensors (mechanical or electronic) detect the sudden negative acceleration.
3. **Signal Transmission:** Sensors send a voltage signal to the **SRS Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** within **< 1 ms**.
4. **ECU Decision:** The ECU analyzes the deceleration profile using a pre-programmed algorithm. If the rate of deceleration exceeds the threshold, a firing command is issued.
5. **Squib Ignition:** An electrical pulse heats a **squib** (small ignition charge).
6. **Propellant Combustion:** The squib ignites the solid propellant (Sodium Azide), producing nitrogen gas within **20 ms**.
7. **Bag Inflation:** Nitrogen gas rushes through a filter and inflates the folded nylon bag to full size in approximately **30 ms**.
8. **Occupant Contact:** At about **40–50 ms** post-crash, the occupant contacts the inflated bag.
9. **Deflation:** Vents in the bag release the gas in a controlled manner, absorbing kinetic energy.
10. **Settle:** After **100 ms**, the bag is deflated, and the occupant moves forward into the seatbelt system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Sodium Azide (NaN₃)?**
> It rapidly produces large volumes of **non-toxic, non-combustible nitrogen gas** at controlled temperatures, making it ideal for fast, predictable inflation.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are essential for solving numerical problems in KTU examinations:

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $F = m \cdot a$ | Force experienced by occupant during crash | $F$ in N, $m$ in kg, $a$ in m/s² |
| $a = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | Deceleration during impact | $\Delta v$ in m/s, $\Delta t$ in s |
| $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of vehicle at impact | $KE$ in J, $m$ in kg, $v$ in m/s |
| $2\text{NaN}_{3} \rightarrow 2\text{Na} + 3\text{N}_{2}$ | Gas-generating chemical reaction | Produces 3 moles of N₂ |
| $n = \dfrac{m}{\text{Molar mass}}$ | Moles of gas produced | $n$ in mol |
| $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$ | Volume of gas generated (Ideal Gas Law) | $V$ in m³, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| $P \cdot t = \text{const}$ | Impulse–Momentum (force × time) | $P$ in N·s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Conditions for Numerical Problems**
> - Crash threshold deceleration: typically **> 5 g** (≈ 49 m/s²)
> - Time to full inflation: **≤ 30 ms**
> - Bag volume (driver-side): **~60 litres**
> - Bag volume (passenger-side): **~120 litres**
> - Molar mass of NaN₃: **65 g/mol**

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Occupant Survival:** Reduces fatality risk in frontal crashes by approximately **30%** when used with seatbelts.
- **Whiplash Prevention:** Curtain airbags reduce head/neck injuries in side impacts.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory under **ECE R94**, **FMVSS 208**, and **AIS 95** (Indian standards adopted by automotive OEMs).
- **Engineering Applications:** Crash modeling, finite-element analysis (FEA) of cushion material, sensor threshold tuning.

> [!TIP]
> **Connection to KTU Module 3:** Airbag systems form a key subsystem under "Active and Passive Safety Systems" — directly linked with **ABS, EBD, ESC**, and **ADAS** topics covered in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deceleration Force on Occupant During Crash

**Problem Context:** A vehicle traveling at 60 km/h strikes a wall and comes to rest in 0.1 s. Calculate the force experienced by a 70 kg occupant *without* an airbag.

**Given Data:**
- Initial velocity, $u = 60$ km/h
- Final velocity, $v = 0$ m/s
- Time to stop (without airbag), $\Delta t_1 = 0.1$ s
- Time to stop (with airbag), $\Delta t_2 = 0.3$ s
- Mass of occupant, $m = 70$ kg

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= 60 \text{ km/h} \times \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{3600 \text{ s}} \\
u &= \frac{60000}{3600} \text{ m/s} \\
u &= 16.67 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate deceleration without airbag**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{v - u}{\Delta t_1} \\
a_1 &= \frac{0 - 16.67}{0.1} \\
a_1 &= -166.7 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration.

**Step 3 — Calculate force on occupant without airbag**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= m \cdot a_1 \\
F_1 &= 70 \times (-166.7) \\
F_1 &= -11669 \text{ N} \approx -11.67 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is approximately **17 times** the occupant's body weight — almost certainly fatal without restraint.

**Step 4 — Calculate force with airbag (extended stopping time)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_2 &= \frac{v - u}{\Delta t_2} \\
a_2 &= \frac{0 - 16.67}{0.3} \\
a_2 &= -55.56 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate reduced force with airbag**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= m \cdot a_2 \\
F_2 &= 70 \times (-55.56) \\
F_2 &= -3889 \text{ N} \approx -3.89 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Reduction in force (efficacy of airbag)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{F_1 - F_2}{F_1} \times 100\% \\
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{11669 - 3889}{11669} \times 100\% \\
\text{Reduction} &= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The airbag, by extending the stopping time from **0.1 s** to **0.3 s** (a 3× increase), reduces the impact force by approximately **66.7%**. This is the fundamental *physics principle* behind every airbag design.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volume of Nitrogen Gas Produced

**Problem Context:** An airbag inflator contains 200 g of Sodium Azide (NaN₃). Calculate the moles and approximate volume of N₂ generated at standard conditions.

**Step 1 — Write balanced chemical equation**

$$
2\text{NaN}_{3} \rightarrow 2\text{Na} + 3\text{N}_{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate moles of NaN₃**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{NaN}_3} &= \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} \\
n_{\text{NaN}_3} &= \frac{200 \text{ g}}{65 \text{ g/mol}} \\
n_{\text{NaN}_3} &= 3.077 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply stoichiometry**

From the balanced equation, **2 moles** of NaN₃ produce **3 moles** of N₂.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{N_2} &= n_{\text{NaN}_3} \times \frac{3 \text{ mol } N_2}{2 \text{ mol } \text{NaN}_3} \\
n_{N_2} &= 3.077 \times 1.5 \\
n_{N_2} &= 4.615 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply ideal gas law at STP**

Using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 273$ K, $P = 101325$ Pa:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{n_{N_2} \cdot R \cdot T}{P} \\
V &= \frac{4.615 \times 8.314 \times 273}{101325} \\
V &= \frac{10478.6}{101325} \\
V &= 0.1034 \text{ m}^3 \\
V &= 103.4 \text{ litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** ~103 litres of N₂ is sufficient to fully inflate a passenger-side airbag (~120 L capacity), confirming the design suitability of NaN₃ for a 200 g propellant charge.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Without Airbag | With Airbag | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stopping Time | 0.1 s | 0.3 s | **3× longer** |
| Deceleration | 166.7 m/s² | 55.56 m/s² | **66.7% lower** |
| Force on Occupant | 11.67 kN | 3.89 kN | **66.7% lower** |
| Survival Probability | Low | High | Significantly improved |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Airbag System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **signal flow and functional architecture** from crash detection to airbag deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRASH EVENT Occurs] --> B[CRASH SENSOR Mechanical or Electronic]
    B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING Amplification and Noise Filtering]
    C --> D[SRS ECU Microcontroller Based Decision Logic]
    D --> E[THRESHOLD CHECK Is deceleration greater than 5g]
    E -- NO --> F[NO DEPLOYMENT Continue Normal Operation]
    E -- YES --> G[FIRE COMMAND ECU Activates Squib Circuit]
    G --> H[SQUIB IGNITION Small Charge Heats and Ignites]
    H --> I[PROPELLANT COMBUSTION NaN3 Decomposes to N2 Gas]
    I --> J[FILTER AND COOLER Removes Solid Residues]
    J --> K[NYLON CUSHION INFLATION 20 to 30 milliseconds]
    K --> L[OCCUPANT CONTACT Bag Deflates Through Vents]
    L --> M[ENERGY ABSORPTION Controlled Deceleration 100 milliseconds]
    M --> N[SYSTEM RESET Fault Code Stored in ECU Memory]

    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Crash Sensor Architecture Diagram

The crash detection subsystem uses multiple sensors for redundancy and accuracy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_SIDE[FRONT IMPACT ZONE]
        S1[SATELLITE SENSOR Front Bumper]
        S2[SAFETY SENSOR Passenger Compartment]
        S3[MECHANICAL SENSOR Backup Trigger]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_UNIT[SAFETY ECU]
        P[PROCESSING UNIT Microcontroller]
        M[MEMORY Crash Algorithm and Calibrations]
        D[DIAGNOSTIC MODULE Self Test Every Ignition Cycle]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_SIDE[DEPLOYMENT MODULE]
        Q1[SQUIB DRIVER Driver Airbag]
        Q2[SQUIB DRIVER Passenger Airbag]
        Q3[SQUIB DRIVER Side Curtain]
        Q4[SQUIB DRIVER Knee Airbag]
    end

    S1 --> P
    S2 --> P
    S3 --> P
    P --> M
    M --> D
    P --> Q1
    P --> Q2
    P --> Q3
    P --> Q4

    style S1 fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#10ac84,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#ee5253,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#ee5253,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#ee5253,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q4 fill:#ee5253,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Airbag Inflation Sequence (Timing Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[T0 Equals 0 ms Crash Initiated] --> T10[T10 ms Sensors Detect Deceleration]
    T10 --> T15[T15 ms ECU Issues Fire Command]
    T15 --> T25[T25 ms Squib Ignites Propellant]
    T25 --> T35[T35 ms Bag Fully Inflated]
    T35 --> T50[T50 ms Occupant Contacts Bag]
    T50 --> T100[T100 ms Bag Fully Deflated]
    T100 --> T200[T200 ms System Stores Crash Data]

    style T0 fill:#ee5253,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T10 fill:#feca57,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T15 fill:#feca57,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T25 fill:#10ac84,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T35 fill:#10ac84,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T50 fill:#54a0ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T100 fill:#5f27cd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T200 fill:#576574,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Explain the working principle of an airbag system with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An airbag system is a **passive safety device** that protects vehicle occupants during a crash. Its working involves the following steps:

1. **Crash Detection:** Crash sensors (mechanical ball-type or electronic accelerometer-based) detect sudden deceleration exceeding the threshold (typically > **5 g**).
2. **ECU Processing:** The **SRS Electronic Control Unit** processes the sensor signals using a pre-programmed algorithm.
3. **Squib Activation:** The ECU sends a current pulse to the **squib**, which ignites the solid propellant (Sodium Azide, NaN₃).
4. **Gas Generation:** NaN₃ decomposes rapidly to produce nitrogen gas (N₂), inflating the folded nylon bag within **20–30 ms**.
5. **Cushioning:** The inflated bag provides a soft cushion that absorbs kinetic energy and reduces impact force on the occupant.

**(Diagram: 1 mark; Explanation: 2 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List the different types of airbags used in modern vehicles and state the location of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Airbag Type | Location / Coverage Zone |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Driver Front Airbag** | Steering wheel center |
| 2 | **Passenger Front Airbag** | Dashboard (front passenger side) |
| 3 | **Side Airbag (Torso)** | Seat side bolster |
| 4 | **Curtain Airbag** | Roof rail — covers head/window area |
| 5 | **Knee Airbag** | Below steering column / glove box |
| 6 | **Rear Seat Side Airbag** | Rear seat side bolster |
| 7 | **Center Airbag** | Between front seats (prevents occupant clash) |

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a pyrotechnic airbag inflator**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **types and working of crash sensors** used in airbag systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Pyrotechnic Airbag Inflator — Construction and Working**

**Construction:**
A pyrotechnic inflator consists of the following components:

1. **Housing:** Steel cylindrical body, hermetically sealed.
2. **Igniter / Squib:** A small resistance-wire bridge coated with pyrotechnic material (zirconium–potassium perchlorate).
3. **Solid Propellant Pellets:** Sodium Azide (NaN₃) stacked in cylindrical pellets.
4. **Filter Assembly:** Stainless steel mesh + ceramic fiber filter to remove solid Na/Na₂O residues and cool the gas.
5. **Output Orifices:** Multiple vents to channel gas into the cushion.

**Working:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: ECU sends firing current (typically 1–2 A) through squib.} \\
&\text{Step 2: Squib ignites → ignites propellant pellets.} \\
&\text{Step 3: NaN}_3 \text{ decomposes exothermically:} \\
&\qquad 2\text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{Na} + 3\text{N}_2 \\
&\text{Step 4: Hot Na reacts with secondary additives (e.g., Fe}_2\text{O}_3\text{) to form silicates.} \\
&\text{Step 5: Filtered, cooled N}_2 \text{ inflates the cushion in 20–30 ms.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Diagram: 2 marks; Component description: 3 marks; Working steps: 2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Types and Working of Crash Sensors**

**(i) Mechanical (Ball-in-Tube) Sensor — 3 marks**

A small steel ball is held by a permanent magnet at one end of a smooth tube. During sudden deceleration, inertial force overcomes the magnetic force and the ball rolls forward, striking a pair of contacts, completing the firing circuit. These are simple, cheap, and used as backup sensors.

**(ii) Electronic Accelerometer Sensor — 3 marks**

A micromachined MEMS accelerometer detects deceleration along the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The output voltage is proportional to deceleration. The ECU continuously samples this signal and compares it against a stored deceleration-time threshold curve. Modern vehicles use **multiple electronic sensors** (satellite sensors at the front + a safety sensor in the ECU) for accurate discrimination of crash severity.

**(iii) Rollover Sensor — 1 mark**

Detects vehicle roll angle using a gyroscope; used to deploy curtain airbags and seatbelt pretensioners during rollover events.

| Sensor Type | Application | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Ball-in-tube | Backup / low-speed trigger | Magnetic vs. inertial force |
| MEMS Accelerometer | Primary trigger | Capacitive change with deceleration |
| Gyroscope | Rollover detection | Angular rate sensing |

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **chemistry of gas generation in airbag inflators** with relevant balanced equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of the SRS Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** with its input, processing, and output functions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Chemistry of Gas Generation**

Modern airbag inflators use a multi-component propellant mixture. The primary reaction involves **Sodium Azide (NaN₃)**, but secondary reactions use oxidizers to convert hazardous Na byproducts into harmless silicates.

**Primary Reaction (Azide Decomposition):**

$$
2\text{NaN}_3(s) \rightarrow 2\text{Na}(l) + 3\text{N}_2(g)
$$

This reaction releases approximately **+43 kcal/mol** of energy and produces **3 moles of N₂ per 2 moles of NaN₃**.

**Secondary Reaction (Conversion of Sodium):**

$$
10\text{Na} + 2\text{KNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{O} + 5\text{Na}_2\text{O} + \text{N}_2
$$

The sodium produced by primary decomposition reacts with potassium nitrate (KNO₃) to form non-toxic oxides.

**Tertiary Reaction (Silicate Formation with Filter):**

$$
\text{Na}_2\text{O} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3
$$

The sodium oxide reacts with silicon dioxide (SiO₂) in the ceramic filter to form harmless **sodium silicate (glass)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend:** Many manufacturers are shifting to **non-azide propellants** (e.g., guanidine nitrate, 5-aminotetrazole) due to environmental concerns over Sodium Azide toxicity.

**(Equations: 4 marks; Explanation of byproducts: 3 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**SRS ECU — Role and Functions**

The **SRS Electronic Control Unit** is the brain of the airbag system. It performs the following:

**(i) Inputs — 2 marks**
- Crash sensor signals (front satellite + safety sensor).
- Seat occupancy sensor (for passenger airbag enable/disable).
- Seatbelt buckle switch (for pretensioner activation).
- Vehicle speed signal (from CAN bus).
- Battery and ignition status.

**(ii) Processing — 3 marks**
- Continuous **self-diagnosis** runs every ignition cycle (checks squib continuity, sensor health, capacitor backup).
- **Crash algorithm** compares real-time deceleration with stored "crash signature" threshold curves.
- Maintains **backup capacitor** (charged to ~25 V) to fire airbags even if main battery is disconnected during crash.
- Stores **fault codes (DTCs)** readable via OBD-II scanner.

**(iii) Outputs — 2 marks**
- Driver airbag squib.
- Passenger airbag squib.
- Side / curtain airbag squibs.
- Seatbelt pretensioner.
- Warning lamp on instrument cluster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to mention that the SRS ECU has a **backup capacitor** — this is a high-frequency KTU question.
> 2. ❌ Stating that airbags *replace* seatbelts — they are **supplementary**, not replacements.
> 3. ❌ Missing the **self-diagnostic feature** of the ECU — losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. ❌ Writing unbalanced chemical equations for NaN₃ decomposition.
> 5. ❌ Confusing mechanical (ball-type) sensors with electronic (MEMS) sensors in the explanation.
> 6. ❌ Not mentioning the **secondary reactions** for Na conversion — examiners specifically look for environmental handling of byproducts.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Airbag = Supplementary Restraint System (SRS)** — works *with*, never *instead of*, seatbelts.
- ✅ **Inflation time is 20–30 ms** — the entire crash-to-deploy sequence must occur in < 100 ms.
- ✅ **Primary gas-generating chemical:** Sodium Azide (NaN₃); product gas is Nitrogen (N₂).
- ✅ **Core reaction:** $2\text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{Na} + 3\text{N}_2$
- ✅ **Backup capacitor in ECU** ensures deployment even if battery is severed during crash.
- ✅ **Two key sensor types:** Mechanical (ball-in-tube) and Electronic (MEMS accelerometer).
- ✅ **Deceleration threshold:** typically **> 5 g** for airbag firing.
- ✅ **Crash physics principle:** Increasing stopping time by 3× reduces force by ~67%.
- ✅ **Modern vehicles use multiple airbags:** Front, Side, Curtain, Knee, Center — typically **6–10 airbags** in premium cars.
- ✅ **Standards compliance:** ECE R94 (frontal), ECE R95 (side), FMVSS 208, AIS 95 (India).
- ✅ **Pyrotechnic inflators** are single-use; **stored gas / hybrid inflators** exist for advanced systems.
- ✅ **Always mention secondary reactions** for Na conversion — environmental safety is a key scoring area.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Vehicles and Hybrid Vehicles: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electric Vehicles and Hybrid Vehicles: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND HYBRID VEHICLES: INTRODUCTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Vehicle (EV):** An automotive vehicle in which the propulsion (traction) force is derived entirely or partially from electric energy stored in an onboard energy storage system, converted into mechanical work through one or more electric machines (traction motors), without the direct combustion of fossil fuels inside the powertrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):** A vehicle that employs **two or more distinct energy sources and corresponding powertrains**—typically an internal combustion engine (ICE) combined with an electric drive system—to deliver traction. The two power units are intelligently coordinated by an energy management controller to optimize fuel economy, emissions, and performance.

### 1.2 The Broader Electromobility Spectrum (KTU Syllabus-Aligned Classification)

The KTU 2024 OEMRT831 syllabus categorizes xEVs (electrified vehicles) into the following standard nomenclature:

| Acronym | Full Form | Primary Energy Source | Grid Plug-in Capable? | ICE Present? |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery (grid charged) | Yes | No |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Fuel + Battery (regen) | No | Yes |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid EV | Fuel + Battery (grid + fuel) | Yes | Yes |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen Fuel Cell | No (H₂ station) | No |
| **MHEV** | Mild Hybrid EV | Fuel + 48V battery | No | Yes |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you want to push a heavy cart from point A to point B. In a **conventional ICE vehicle**, you carry a small fuel tank (like a tiny concentrated energy pill) and burn it continuously to push the cart — most of the chemical energy is lost as heat and sound. In an **EV**, you carry a giant rechargeable battery (like a large water bucket) full of electrical energy that can be poured into an electric motor on demand — very little energy is wasted. In a **HEV**, you carry BOTH: the small fuel pill AND a smaller water bucket. An intelligent controller decides when to use the pill, when to use the bucket, and when to refill the bucket using the pill (regenerative braking) — getting the best of both worlds.

### 1.4 Why Autotronics Engineers Must Study This

The transition from mechanical/hydraulic vehicle control to **electronic, sensor-driven, and software-defined vehicle control** is the core subject of Autotronics. EVs and HEVs are essentially **rolling electronic systems** featuring:
- High-voltage **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**
- **Motor Control Units (MCU)** with PWM inverters
- **Vehicle Control Units (VCU)** running real-time energy management algorithms
- **CAN/LIN-based** automotive networks
- Regenerative braking controllers and DC-DC converters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - **Energy density of gasoline ≈ 12,000 Wh/kg** vs. Li-ion battery ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg**
> - **Electric motor efficiency:** 85–95%
> - **ICE thermal efficiency:** 25–40%
> - **Regenerative braking recovery:** up to 70% of kinetic energy in urban cycles
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Vehicle Propulsion

A vehicle moving at velocity $v$ on a flat road must overcome four resistive forces. The **tractive force equation** is the foundational equation of all vehicle dynamics (EV, HEV, and ICE):

$$F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}$$

Where each term is defined below in the KTU cheat sheet.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Energy Flow in an EV

1. **Driver intent capture:** Accelerator pedal position sensor (often a Hall-effect or potentiometric sensor) sends a voltage signal (typically 0–5 V) to the VCU.
2. **Torque arbitration:** The VCU computes the requested torque based on pedal position, vehicle speed, and battery State of Charge (SOC).
3. **Motor command:** The MCU (Motor Control Unit) generates a three-phase PWM signal using **Space Vector Modulation (SVM)** or **Field Oriented Control (FOC)** to drive the inverter.
4. **Power conversion:** The inverter switches IGBTs/MOSFETs at high frequency (typically 5–20 kHz) to convert DC bus voltage into variable-frequency three-phase AC.
5. **Mechanical output:** The traction motor (PMSM, Induction, or SRM) converts electrical energy to mechanical torque on the wheels via a single-speed or multi-speed reducer.
6. **Regenerative mode:** During braking, the motor acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy back to electrical energy stored in the battery.

### 2.3 Hybrid Vehicle Operational Modes

A parallel HEV can operate in any of the following **five standard modes** — a KTU high-yield topic:

| Mode | ICE | Motor | Battery | Application |
|------|-----|-------|---------|-------------|
| **1. Pure EV Mode** | OFF | ON | Discharging | Low speed, city driving |
| **2. Pure ICE Mode** | ON | OFF | Idle | Steady highway cruise |
| **3. Hybrid Traction** | ON | ON | Discharging | High torque demand (hill climb) |
| **4. Regenerative Braking** | OFF | ON (as generator) | Charging | Braking/deceleration |
| **5. Battery Charging (ICE-driven)** | ON | ON (as generator) | Charging | SOC low, vehicle stationary or moving |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **complete formula cheat sheet** required to solve any Part A or Part B question on EV/HEV fundamentals. All units are SI unless stated. Pipes in absolute values have been replaced with `\vert` per KTU formatting rules.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Tractive Force** | $F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}$ | Sum of all resistive forces | N (Newtons) |
| 2 | **Rolling Resistance** | $F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$ | $m$=mass, $g$=9.81, $C_{rr}$=roll coeff., $\theta$=slope | N |
| 3 | **Aerodynamic Drag** | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | $\rho$=air density (1.225), $C_d$=drag coeff., $A$=frontal area, $v$=velocity | N |
| 4 | **Grade Resistance** | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Resists motion on inclines | N |
| 5 | **Acceleration Force** | $F_{accel} = m \cdot a$ | $a$=linear acceleration | N |
| 6 | **Power at Wheels** | $P_{wheels} = F_{trac} \cdot v$ | Tractile power demand | W |
| 7 | **Battery Energy Capacity** | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{Ah}$ | $V_{nom}$=nominal V, $Q_{Ah}$=capacity | Wh |
| 8 | **Vehicle Range (EV)** | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{sys}}{E_{100km}}$ | $\eta_{sys}$=drivetrain efficiency, $E_{100km}$=energy per 100 km | km |
| 9 | **State of Charge (SOC)** | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of remaining capacity | % |
| 10 | **State of Health (SOH)** | $SOH = \dfrac{Q_{max\_aged}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Battery degradation metric | % |
| 11 | **Depth of Discharge** | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ | Inverse of SOC | — |
| 12 | **C-Rate (Discharge)** | $C = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{Ah}}$ | Indicates discharge speed | dimensionless |
| 13 | **Kinetic Energy of Vehicle** | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$ | Recoverable during regen braking | J |
| 14 | **Regen Recovery Power** | $P_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{brake} \cdot v$ | $\eta_{regen}$=regen efficiency (0.5–0.7) | W |
| 15 | **Motor Output Power** | $P_{motor} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | $\tau$=torque, $\omega$=angular velocity | W |
| 16 | **ICE Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{shaft}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Carnot limit ≈ 0.4–0.5, real ≈ 0.25–0.40 | dimensionless |
| 17 | **Overall HEV Efficiency** | $\eta_{HEV} = \dfrac{W_{useful}}{Q_{fuel} + E_{bat\_in}}$ | Combined fuel + electrical energy in | dimensionless |
| 18 | **Equivalent Fuel Economy (PHEV)** | $E_{fuel} = \dfrac{E_{elec}}{\eta_{chg} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | Converts electric km to gasoline L/100km | L/100km |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BMS calibration** (rows 9–12): Critical for warranty management; Tesla, BYD, and NMC battery packs use these formulas in firmware.
- **Range anxiety mitigation:** Row 8 is the standard formula used by on-board range estimators.
- **Emission compliance (BS-VI, Euro 7):** Rows 16–18 determine fleet-level CO₂ compliance for OEMs.
- **Charging infrastructure design:** Row 7 determines grid load and station sizing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Required to Drive an EV Over Distance D

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the **total tractive energy** required to move a vehicle of mass $m$ at constant velocity $v$ over a flat road for a distance $D$, accounting for rolling and aerodynamic losses.

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass $m$ (kg)
- Constant cruise velocity $v$ (m/s)
- Distance $D$ (m)
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr}$
- Drag coefficient $C_d$, frontal area $A$ (m²)
- Air density $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³** (sea level, standard)
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{sys}$

**Step 1:** Identify the resistive forces on a flat road (no grade, no acceleration):

$$F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the standard expressions for $F_{roll}$ and $F_{aero}$:

$$F_{trac} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

**Step 3:** The mechanical energy delivered to the wheels equals force × distance. Since velocity is constant, kinetic energy does not change, so all work done is against resistive forces:

$$E_{wheels} = F_{trac} \cdot D$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $F_{trac}$ from Step 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{wheels} &= \left( m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \right) \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Because the drivetrain has efficiency $\eta_{sys} < 1$, the **battery must supply more energy** than the wheels receive. Invert the efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{bat} &= \dfrac{E_{wheels}}{\eta_{sys}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the **range of the EV** with a battery of nominal energy $E_{bat}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{sys}}{m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the $v^2$ term in the denominator — **aerodynamic drag dominates at high speed**, which is why EVs (with no gearbox) are more efficient in city driving and lose efficiency on highways. This is the exact opposite of ICE vehicles, which are most efficient at highway speeds.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: BEV Range Calculation

**Problem:** A BEV has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.2$ m²
- $v = 27.78$ m/s (= 100 km/h)
- Battery pack = 60 kWh, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.85$
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Find the theoretical range in km.

**Step 1:** Calculate rolling resistance force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \\
&= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \\
&= 176.58 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate aerodynamic drag force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (27.78)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 771.73 \\
&= 291.18 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total tractive force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{trac} &= F_{roll} + F_{aero} \\
&= 176.58 + 291.18 \\
&= 467.76 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Energy consumed per km (in Joules, then convert to Wh):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{per\_km} &= \dfrac{F_{trac} \cdot 1000}{\eta_{sys}} \\
&= \dfrac{467.76 \cdot 1000}{0.85} \\
&= 550,306 \text{ J/km} \\
&= \dfrac{550,306}{3600} \text{ Wh/km} \\
&\approx 152.86 \text{ Wh/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{60,000 \text{ Wh}}{152.86 \text{ Wh/km}} \\
&\approx 392.5 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Verdict:** A well-answered numerical question scores full **14 marks** only if (a) units are shown at every step, (b) the $\eta_{sys}$ is correctly placed in the denominator for $E_{bat}$ calculation, and (c) the final answer is rounded to one decimal place. Skipping unit conversions is the #1 mark-loss pitfall.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Step 1:** A vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ has kinetic energy:

$$KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

**Step 2:** During braking, the electric motor acts as a generator. Not all kinetic energy can be recovered due to losses in the motor, inverter, and battery charging acceptance:

$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

**Step 3:** Typical regen efficiency $\eta_{regen} \in [0.3, 0.7]$ depending on:
- Battery SOC (high SOC reduces regen acceptance)
- Brake pedal position (blended vs. full regen)
- Motor speed (low-speed regen is inefficient)

**Step 4:** The **braking force** delivered by regen is:

$$F_{regen} = \dfrac{P_{motor\_max}}{v} = \dfrac{\tau_{max} \cdot \omega_{max}}{v}$$

This sets the maximum deceleration achievable through regen alone — typically **0.1g to 0.3g** in passenger EVs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) Powertrain Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **full functional flow** of energy and signals in a BEV — this is a guaranteed high-yield diagram for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grid Charger AC Level 1 2 3] --> B[Onboard Charger OBC]
    B --> C[High Voltage Battery Pack 400V 800V]
    C --> D[Battery Management System BMS]
    C --> E[DC DC Converter]
    E --> F[12V Auxiliary Battery]
    C --> G[Inverter VCU controlled]
    G --> H[3 Phase PMSM Traction Motor]
    H --> I[Single Speed Reducer]
    I --> J[Drive Wheels]
    K[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] --> L[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    M[Brake Pedal Sensor] --> L
    N[Wheel Speed Sensors] --> L
    L --> G
    L --> D
    H --> G
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
    style L fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Series-Parallel Hybrid Powertrain (e.g., Toyota THS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine]
    B --> C[Planetary Gearset Power Split Device]
    D[High Voltage Battery] --> E[Power Control Unit Inverter]
    E --> C
    C --> F[MG1 Generator Motor]
    C --> G[MG2 Traction Motor]
    F --> E
    G --> H[Final Drive]
    H --> I[Wheels]
    J[Hybrid Control Unit HCU] --> B
    J --> E
    style B fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.3 Classification Flowchart of xEV Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[xEV Electrified Vehicle] --> B{External Charging Capable?}
    B -->|Yes| C{ICE Present?}
    B -->|No| D[HEV HEV MHEV]
    C -->|Yes| E[PHEV Plug in Hybrid]
    C -->|No| F[BEV Battery EV]
    A --> G[Special]
    G --> H[FCEV Fuel Cell EV]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: BEV vs. HEV vs. PHEV vs. FCEV

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|-----------|-----|-----|------|------|
| Primary Energy | Grid Electricity | Petrol/Diesel | Both | Hydrogen |
| Range | 200–600 km | 800–1000 km | 600–800 km | 400–700 km |
| Refuel/Recharge Time | 30 min–8 h | 3 min (fuel) | 3 min + 2–4 h | 5–10 min (H₂) |
| Tailpipe Emissions | **0 g CO₂/km** | 80–120 g CO₂/km | 30–60 g CO₂/km | **0 g CO₂/km** |
| Battery Size | 30–100 kWh | 1–2 kWh | 8–18 kWh | 1–2 kWh |
| Key Component | Large battery | Small battery + ICE | Mid battery + ICE | Fuel cell stack + H₂ tank |
| Example | Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Toyota Prius Prime, BMW X5 PHEV | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]
**Define the term "Hybrid Electric Vehicle". List any four advantages of HEVs over conventional ICE vehicles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) is a vehicle that uses **two or more energy sources**, typically a combination of an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and an electric drive system (battery + electric motor), for propulsion. The two power units are coordinated by a Hybrid Control Unit (HCU) to deliver optimum performance, fuel economy, and emissions.

**Four advantages of HEVs:**
1. **Improved fuel economy** (typically 20–35% better than conventional ICE vehicles) due to engine operation near its optimal efficiency region and use of regenerative braking.
2. **Lower exhaust emissions** — reduced CO₂, NOx, and particulate matter, helping meet BS-VI/Euro 7 norms.
3. **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy otherwise lost as heat, improving overall energy efficiency.
4. **No range anxiety** compared to pure BEVs, since the ICE serves as a range extender.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is State of Charge (SOC) of a battery? Why is SOC monitoring critical in EVs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
State of Charge (SOC) is defined as the **ratio of the remaining capacity of a battery to its rated (full-charge) capacity**, expressed as a percentage.

**Mathematically:**

$$SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

**Importance of SOC monitoring in EVs:**
1. **Range estimation:** The on-board range predictor uses real-time SOC to display remaining kilometres to the driver.
2. **Battery protection:** SOC must be kept within a safe window (typically 20–80%) to prevent over-charge (causing thermal runaway) and over-discharge (causing permanent cell damage).
3. **Energy management strategy:** The VCU and BMS use SOC as a primary input to decide torque split between ICE and motor in HEVs, and to enable/disable regen braking in BEVs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **major components and working of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** powertrain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **theoretical range of a BEV** cruising at constant velocity on a flat road, and calculate the range for a vehicle with mass = 1600 kg, $C_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area = 2.3 m², $v = 20$ m/s, battery = 75 kWh, and drivetrain efficiency = 0.88. Use $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A BEV powertrain consists of the following major subsystems, shown in the block diagram in Section 4.1:

1. **Energy Storage System — High-Voltage Battery Pack:** Typically a Lithium-ion pack with nominal voltage between 400V (modern) and 800V (next-gen, e.g., Hyundai E-GMP). Energy capacity 30–100 kWh.

2. **Battery Management System (BMS):** Monitors individual cell voltages, temperatures, and SOC. Performs cell balancing (passive or active), thermal management, and communicates SOC/SOH to the VCU via CAN bus.

3. **On-Board Charger (OBC) and Charging Port:** Converts grid AC (Level 1: 120V, Level 2: 240V) to DC for battery charging. Supports fast DC charging via CCS2/CHAdeMO connector bypassing the OBC.

4. **DC-DC Converter:** Steps down the high-voltage DC bus (400V) to 12V DC for the auxiliary battery and low-voltage electronics (lights, infotainment, ECUs).

5. **Inverter (Power Electronics):** Converts DC bus voltage to variable-frequency three-phase AC for the traction motor. Uses IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs switched at 5–20 kHz using PWM techniques (SVM, FOC).

6. **Traction Motor:** Usually a **3-phase Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** or AC Induction Motor. Power range 100–400 kW. Provides peak torque from 0 RPM (single-speed transmission friendly).

7. **Single-Speed Reducer + Differential:** Mechanical gearbox with fixed ratio (typically 8–10:1) that multiplies motor torque to wheel level.

8. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):** The brain of the BEV. Implements torque arbitration, pedal map, regenerative braking blending, and fault diagnostics.

**Working Principle:** When the driver presses the accelerator, the pedal sensor sends a 0–5V signal to the VCU. The VCU computes requested torque, commands the inverter to drive the motor, the motor produces torque through the reducer to the wheels, and the BMS continuously monitors battery safety. During braking, the VCU commands the inverter to switch the motor into generator mode, recovering kinetic energy back into the battery.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.1, the range of a BEV at constant velocity on a flat road is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{sys}}{m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

**Step 1:** Rolling resistance force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= 1600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.015 = 235.44 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Aerodynamic drag force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{aero} &= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (20)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.3 \cdot 400 \\
&= 169.05 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total tractive force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{trac} &= 235.44 + 169.05 = 404.49 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Energy required per km (Wheels side):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km\_wheels} &= 404.49 \cdot 1000 = 404,490 \text{ J/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Battery energy required per km:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km\_bat} &= \dfrac{404,490}{0.88} = 459,648 \text{ J/km} \\
&= \dfrac{459,648}{3600} = 127.68 \text{ Wh/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{75,000 \text{ Wh}}{127.68 \text{ Wh/km}} \approx 587.5 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer: $R \approx 587.5$ km**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU ESE Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles** with suitable examples. Compare their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, describe the **series, parallel, and series-parallel hybrid powertrain architectures**. Which is the most fuel-efficient and why? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

As classified in Section 1.2 and the flowchart in Section 4.3, xEVs are classified into the following five categories:

1. **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):** 100% electric, e.g., Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3.
   - *Merits:* Zero tailpipe emissions, low running cost, low maintenance.
   - *Demerits:* Limited range, long charging time, requires charging infrastructure.

2. **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):** Cannot be plugged in. Battery charged only by ICE and regen. e.g., Toyota Camry Hybrid.
   - *Merits:* No range anxiety, no external charging needed, better fuel economy.
   - *Demerits:* Still uses fossil fuel, no pure EV mode for long, emissions not zero.

3. **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV):** Has both a charging port and ICE. e.g., Toyota Prius Prime, BMW X5 xDrive45e.
   - *Merits:* 30–80 km pure electric range covers daily commute; ICE acts as range extender.
   - *Demerits:* Heavier (carries both battery and ICE), complex powertrain, expensive.

4. **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV):** Uses hydrogen fuel cell. e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.
   - *Merits:* Zero emissions, fast refuelling, long range.
   - *Demerits:* Hydrogen infrastructure scarce, expensive fuel cell stack.

5. **Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle (MHEV):** Uses 48V system. e.g., Maruti Suzuki Ciaz Smart Hybrid.
   - *Merits:* Lowest cost hybridization, modest fuel economy gain.
   - *Demerits:* Cannot drive in pure EV mode.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Series Hybrid Architecture:** The ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels. The ICE drives a generator, which charges the battery, which in turn drives the electric motor connected to the wheels. Think of it as an **electric vehicle with an onboard range extender**.

**Parallel Hybrid Architecture:** Both the ICE and the electric motor are **mechanically coupled to the wheels** (typically via a clutch). Either or both can deliver tractive force. Simpler mechanically, but requires a complex torque-coupling device.

**Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid:** Uses a **planetary gearset** (e.g., Toyota Hybrid System) to combine the ICE, MG1, and MG2 in a continuously variable power split. Allows operation in all five modes of Section 2.3 seamlessly.

**Most fuel-efficient:** The **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) architecture** is the most fuel-efficient because:
1. The ICE can always operate near its **peak efficiency point** (Bezier curve of best BSFC) regardless of vehicle speed, by electronically varying the power split.
2. It can combine the best features of series (electric drive flexibility) and parallel (mechanical direct drive efficiency).
3. Real-world example: Toyota Prius achieves **~25 km/L** consistently — among the best fuel economy figures for any non-plug-in vehicle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **In Numerical Problems:** Students frequently forget to convert the velocity from km/h to m/s before plugging into the aerodynamic drag formula. Always convert: $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} \times \dfrac{5}{18}$. Loss: up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **In Block Diagrams:** Drawing a block diagram WITHOUT labeling the direction of energy/signal flow loses at least **1 mark** in Part B. Always annotate arrows.
> 3. **In Definitions:** Writing "EV is a vehicle that runs on battery" is incomplete. The **board model answer** requires mention of the electric machine (motor) and the absence of ICE combustion. Loss: up to **1 mark**.
> 4. **SOC vs. DOD Confusion:** SOC and DOD are NOT the same — DOD = 1 − SOC. Examiners specifically test this. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **EV = traction from electric energy only; HEV = combination of ICE + electric drive.**
- [ ] **BEV has no ICE; HEV has ICE but no plug; PHEV has both; FCEV uses hydrogen.**
- [ ] **Tractive force equation** is the foundational equation: $F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}$.
- [ ] **Aerodynamic drag dominates at high speed** ($v^2$ dependence) — that's why EVs are more efficient in city driving.
- [ ] **ICE thermal efficiency ≈ 25–40%**; **electric motor efficiency ≈ 85–95%** — a key KTU factual question.
- [ ] **SOC is the % of remaining battery capacity**; SOC range in operation is typically **20–80%** for longevity.
- [ ] **C-rate = discharge current / rated capacity** — a 1C discharge of a 100 Ah battery = 100 A.
- [ ] **Regenerative braking recovery** can be up to **70%** of kinetic energy in urban stop-go cycles.
- [ ] **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) hybrid** is the most fuel-efficient architecture due to ICE operation at peak BSFC point.
- [ ] **PMSM is the most common traction motor** in modern EVs due to high power density and efficiency.
- [ ] **Lithium-ion energy density ≈ 150–250 Wh/kg** vs. gasoline ≈ 12,000 Wh/kg (50× energy density gap is the root of EV range anxiety).
- [ ] **Three key automotive networks:** CAN, LIN, FlexRay/Ethernet — used by BMS, VCU, MCU to communicate.
- [ ] **Two main inverter PWM techniques:** SPWM and SVPWM (Space Vector PWM is preferred for lower harmonic distortion).
- [ ] **Two motor control strategies:** V/f (scalar) control and FOC (vector) control — FOC is industry standard for traction.
- [ ] **Tata Nexon EV = BEV example; Toyota Camry = HEV; Toyota Prius Prime = PHEV; Toyota Mirai = FCEV.**
- [ ] **For range calculation: convert velocity to m/s, calculate $F_{trac}$, then divide battery energy by Wh/km.**
- [ ] **In HEV block diagrams, always show the HCU/VCU at the top controlling both ICE and electric drive paths.**
- [ ] **Remember the 5 operating modes of a hybrid: Pure EV, Pure ICE, Hybrid Traction, Regen, Engine-driven Charging.**
- [ ] **On-board charger (OBC) handles AC charging; DC fast charging bypasses the OBC and feeds the battery directly.**
- [ ] **DC-DC converter steps down 400V bus to 12V for auxiliaries — it operates continuously, not just at start-up.**
- [ ] **Active cell balancing > Passive cell balancing** in terms of efficiency, but more expensive — KTU often tests this distinction.

> [!WARNING]
> **FINAL EXAM-DAY ADVICE:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, Module 3 (EV/HEV Introduction) questions typically test (1) component identification via block diagrams — practice drawing from memory, (2) range/speed/energy numericals — memorize the tractive force equation, and (3) classification tables — know BEV/HEV/PHEV/FCEV/MHEV distinctions cold. Allocate 7–8 minutes per Part-A (3-mark) and 18–20 minutes per Part-B (14-mark) question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Vehicle Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electric Vehicle Development # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Electric Vehicle (EV) Development** is the integrated multidisciplinary engineering process of conceptualizing, designing, prototyping, testing, validating, and industrializing a road vehicle whose primary propulsion energy is derived from electricity stored in electrochemical energy sources (batteries) or generated on-board via fuel cells, with zero tailpipe emissions during operation. According to KTU's OEMRT831 (Autotronics) framework, EV development is treated as a **systems engineering problem** spanning five inter-locked domains: 1. **Energy Storage Sub-System (ESS)** — Battery packs, BMS, thermal management. 2. **Electric Propulsion Sub-System (EPS)** — Motor + inverter + transmission. 3. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — Torque mapping, energy management, regenerative braking logic. 4. **Charging & Power Electronics Sub-System** — On-board chargers (OBC), DC-DC converters. 5. **Auxiliary & Body Electronics** — 12 V/48 V networks, HVAC, lighting, ADAS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically focuses on the *development methodology, performance modeling, component selection trade-offs, and standards* involved in converting an automotive concept into a production-ready EV. It is **not** a basic electrical machines course — emphasis is on **vehicle-level integration**. ## 1.2 The Four Canonical EV Architectures | Acronym | Full Form | Energy Source | Grid Plug-in | Tailpipe CO₂ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only | Yes | **0 g/km** | | **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE | No | ~80 g/km | | **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid EV | Battery (large) + ICE | Yes | ~30–50 g/km | | **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen | No (H₂ refuel) | **0 g/km** (well-to-wheel non-zero) | > [!NOTE] > A **Mild-Hybrid (MHEV)** uses a 48 V belt-driven starter-generator (BSG) and *cannot* propel the vehicle electrically alone — it merely assists the ICE. It is **not** classified as an EV under KTU's autotronics scope. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The EV as a Giant Smartphone on Wheels" Imagine an iPhone — three internal blocks decide its user experience: - **The Battery (State of Charge %)** → The lithium-ion pack of the EV. - **The Processor (Efficiency of Apps)** → The electric motor + inverter efficiency. - **The Operating System (iOS)** → The VCU software managing everything. If any one of these three is poor — bulky battery but inefficient motor, or smart OS but weak cells — the user experience suffers. **EV development is the art of balancing these three blocks against cost, weight, range, and customer expectation.** The same iPhone analogy explains why Tesla (great software + great cells) succeeded where early 2010s EVs (great cells but no software integration) failed. ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Every KTU Examiner Expects > [!IMPORTANT] > Standardize these **bold constants/metrics** — examiners award marks for using exact terminology: - **Energy density (Wh/kg)** — gravimetric; Li-ion ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg** (NMC); LFP ≈ **90–160 Wh/kg**. - **Power density (W/kg)** — peak discharge capability. - **WLTP range (km)** — Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicle Test Procedure; **standard benchmark** for KTU numericals. - **0–100 km/h time (s)** and **top speed (km/h)**. - **DC fast-charging rate** — measured in **C-rate**: a **1C** rate fully charges in 1 hour;
