**3C** = 20 minutes. - **Efficiency** — typically **15–20 kWh per 100 km** for a modern compact BEV. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The EV Development V-Model (Adapted for Autotronics) EV development follows an iterative **V-cycle** — each design phase has a corresponding validation phase. **Phase 1 — Concept Definition:** - Target customer segment, price band, expected range. - Benchmarking against competing models. **Phase 2 — Vehicle-Level Simulation:** - Using MATLAB/Simulink, AVL CRUISE, or CarMaker. - Inputs: vehicle mass $m$, frontal area $A$, $C_d$, rolling resistance $\mu$, target performance. **Phase 3 — Sub-System Sizing:** - Battery pack sizing (energy + power). - Motor sizing (continuous + peak torque/speed). - Inverter sizing (DC link voltage, current ripple). - Transmission ratio selection. **Phase 4 — Hardware-in-Loop (HIL) Testing:** - Real VCU ↔ simulated plant. - Catch software bugs *before* prototype fabrication. **Phase 5 — Prototype Build & On-Road Validation:** - Track testing at NATRAX (India), MIRA (UK), or ATP (Germany). **Phase 6 — Homologation & Certification:** - AIS-156 (battery safety), AIS-038 (REESS), ECE R100 (international), ISO 26262 (functional safety). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "explain the EV development process," always structure the answer along the V-model. Generic "we design then test" answers score **zero marks** at KTU — examiners expect phase-by-phase rigor. ## 2.2 Vehicle Dynamics — The Four Resistive Forces Any KTU numerical on EV range or power demand begins with the **longitudinal force balance**. A vehicle moving at velocity $v$ on a road of slope $\theta$ must overcome four opposing forces: 1. **Rolling Resistance Force** $F_{rr}$ 2. **Aerodynamic Drag Force** $F_{ad}$ 3. **Gradient (Climbing) Force** $F_{gc}$ 4. **Inertial (Acceleration) Force** $F_{ia}$ The **Tractive Force $F_t$** supplied by the motor must equal their sum: $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{gc} + F_{ia}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table **verbatim** — every KTU autotronics paper since 2019 has at least one numerical from this sheet. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Rolling resistance force | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \, \cos\theta$ | $\mu_{rr}$ = rolling coefficient (dimensionless), $m$ = vehicle mass [kg], $g = 9.81$ m/s² | $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$ (car tire on asphalt) | | 2 | Aerodynamic drag force | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2$ | $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (air density), $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area [m²], $v$ = velocity [m/s] | $C_d = 0.24$ (Tesla Model 3), $A = 2.22$ m² | | 3 | Gradient / climbing force | $F_{gc} = m \, g \, \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = road incline angle | $\sin 10° = 0.1736$ | | 4 | Inertial / acceleration force | $F_{ia} = m \, a$ | $a$ = linear acceleration [m/s²] | $a = 3$ m/s² for 0–100 in ~9.3 s | | 5 | Tractive power demand | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | Instantaneous mechanical power [W] | — | | 6 | Energy per trip | $E = \int P_t \, dt$ | Wh or kWh | — | | 7 | Battery capacity (energy basis) | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack}$ | $V_{pack}$ [V], $Q_{pack}$ [Ah] | 400 V × 100 Ah = 40 kWh | | 8 | Battery capacity (Ah basis) | $Q_{pack} = \dfrac{E_{needed}}{\eta_{sys} \, V_{pack}}$ | $\eta_{sys}$ = drivetrain efficiency (~0.85) | — | | 9 | Motor torque required | $T_m = \dfrac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_gear}$ | $r_w$ = wheel radius, $G$ = gear ratio, $\eta_{gear}$ = gearbox efficiency | — | | 10 | Motor speed required | $\omega_m = \dfrac{v \cdot G}{r_w}$ | [rad/s] — convert to RPM via $\times 60/(2\pi)$ | — | | 11 | Regenerative braking power | $P_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{brake} \cdot v$ | $\eta_{regen} = 0.6$–$0.8$ | — | | 12 | WLTP range estimate | $R = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{\varepsilon_{WLTP}}$ | $\varepsilon_{WLTP}$ = kWh/km | — | | 13 | C-rate | $C = \dfrac{I_{charge}}{Q_{pack}}$ | Dimensionless; e.g., 150 A into 50 Ah = **3C** | — | | 14 | SoC update (Coulomb counting) | $SoC(t) = SoC(0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{full}}\displaystyle\int_0^t I \, d\tau$ | $I$ = current (signed convention) | — | | 15 | Cell energy density | $\rho_E = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}}{m_{cell}}$ | Wh/kg | 250 Wh/kg (NMC811) | > [!WARNING] > **Unit Conversion Traps:** KTU examiners frequently test > - $v$ in km/h → m/s: divide by **3.6** (NOT multiply). > - $C_d \cdot A$ product is sometimes given together as **m²** units of $C_dA$. > - **Wh ↔ J**: $1\ \text{Wh} = 3600\ \text{J}$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not academic — they are the **exact equations** used by: - **Tata Motors** when sizing the Nexon EV's 30.2 kWh pack. - **Ather Energy** during chassis dyno benchmarking of the 450X. - **Ola Electric** to certify range claims under MIDC (now ARAI) test cycles. - **Bosch & Continental** Tier-1 suppliers when bidding for inverter contracts. A KTU student who masters this sheet can transition directly into an **EV powertrain simulation role** at any OEM in Bengaluru, Pune, or Stuttgart. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Required for a Given Trip Profile ### Problem Setup A compact BEV has the following specification: - Kerb mass $m = 1500$ kg - Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m² - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$ - Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$ - Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.88$ - Battery nominal voltage $V_{pack} = 350$ V - Trip: cruise at constant $v = 90$ km/h on flat road for 1 hour. **Find:** (a) Tractive force, (b) Mechanical power demand, (c) Battery energy drawn, (d) Battery Ah capacity used. --- ### Step (a) — Tractive Force on Flat Road at Constant Speed At **constant velocity**, inertial force $F_{ia} = 0$. On a **flat road**, gradient force $F_{gc} = 0$. So $F_t$ reduces to: $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$$ Substituting the standard formulas: $$F_t = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g + \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2$$ Insert numerical values. First, convert velocity: $$v = 90 \ \text{km/h} = \dfrac{90}{3.6} = 25 \ \text{m/s}$$ Now compute $F_{rr}$: $$F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 220.725 \ \text{N}$$ Compute $F_{ad}$: $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$ Breaking down: $$(25)^2 = 625$$ $$0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$$ $$0.6125 \times 0.28 = 0.1715$$ $$0.1715 \times 2.2 = 0.3773$$ $$0.3773 \times 625 = 235.8125 \ \text{N}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{F_t = 220.725 + 235.8125 = 456.54 \ \text{N}}$$ --- ### Step (b) — Mechanical Power Demand at the Wheels $$P_{mech} = F_t \cdot v = 456.54 \times 25 = 11{,}413.5 \ \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{mech} \approx 11.41 \ \text{kW}}$$ --- ### Step (c) — Battery Energy Drawn in 1 Hour Because the drivetrain has efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.88$, the **electrical** power pulled from the battery is higher than the mechanical power delivered to the road: $$P_{batt} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{sys}} = \dfrac{11.4135}{0.88} = 12.97 \ \text{kW}$$ Energy over 1 hour: $$E_{batt} = P_{batt} \times t = 12.97 \ \text{kWh}$$ $$\boxed{E_{batt} \approx 12.97 \ \text{kWh}}$$ --- ### Step (d) — Battery Ah Capacity Used Use the fundamental relation $E = V \cdot Q$, rearranged: $$Q = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{V_{pack}} = \dfrac{12{,}970 \ \text{Wh}}{350 \ \text{V}}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 37.06 \ \text{Ah}}$$ --- ### Analytical Matrix — Force Contribution at 90 km/h Flat | Force Component | Magnitude [N] | Percentage of $F_t$ | |---|---|---| | Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ | 220.73 | **48.4 %** | | Aerodynamic drag $F_{ad}$ | 235.81 | **51.6 %** | | Gradient $F_{gc}$ | 0 | 0 % | | Inertial $F_{ia}$ | 0 | 0 % | | **Total $F_t$** | **456.54** | **100 %** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crossover Speed Insight:** Notice that at 90 km/h the two dominant forces are almost equal. Below ~60 km/h rolling resistance dominates; above ~80 km/h aerodynamic drag dominates. **KTU asks this concept every year** — examiners want to see you identify the crossover and explain its design impact (e.g., taller gearing for highway, regenerative braking tuned for low-speed urban stop-go). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Range Estimation Using WLTP Consumption ### Problem Setup Same BEV now has a usable battery capacity of $E_{usable} = 50$ kWh. Under the WLTP combined cycle, the vehicle consumes $\varepsilon_{WLTP} = 0.16$ kWh/km. **Find:** Estimated full-charge range. ### Step 1 — Apply the Range Formula $$R_{WLTP} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{\varepsilon_{WLTP}}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$R_{WLTP} = \dfrac{50 \ \text{kWh}}{0.16 \ \text{kWh/km}}$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate $$R_{WLTP} = 312.5 \ \text{km}$$ $$\boxed{R_{WLTP} \approx 312.5 \ \text{km}}$$ ### Step 4 — Sanity Check A modern 50 kWh compact BEV (Tata Nexon EV Max: 437 km from 30.2 kWh ⇒ ~0.144 kWh/km) actually achieves ~350 km. Our calculated 312.5 km is conservative because **WLTP lab tests tend to overstate real-world range by 10–20 %**. The Indian MIDC cycle, being slower, would yield *higher* range on the same vehicle. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motor Sizing for Acceleration Requirement ### Problem Setup Customer requirement: **0–100 km/h in 10 seconds** on flat road with the same 1500 kg vehicle. Ignore drag for short acceleration event. ### Step 1 — Convert Performance Target Final velocity: $$v_f = \dfrac{100}{3.6} = 27.78 \ \text{m/s}$$ Average acceleration over 10 s: $$a_{avg} = \dfrac{v_f - 0}{t} = \dfrac{27.78}{10} = 2.778 \ \text{m/s}^2$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Peak Inertial Force at $t = 0$ At $t = 0$ the vehicle is at rest → drag and rolling resistance are minimal. Peak force needed: $$F_{peak} = m \cdot a_{avg} \cdot k_{margin}$$ A safety margin $k_{margin} = 1.3$ accounts for rolling resistance and head-wind. $$F_{peak} = 1500 \times 2.778 \times 1.3 = 5417.1 \ \text{N}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Motor Torque via Drivetrain Ratio Assuming a single-speed gearbox with $G = 9$ and $\eta_{gear} = 0.95$: $$T_m = \dfrac{F_{peak} \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$$ $$T_m = \dfrac{5417.1 \times 0.32}{9 \times 0.95}$$ $$T_m = \dfrac{1733.47}{8.55}$$ $$\boxed{T_m \approx 202.7 \ \text{Nm}}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute Motor Speed at Top Velocity $$\omega_m = \dfrac{v_f \cdot G}{r_w} = \dfrac{27.78 \times 9}{0.32} = 781.4 \ \text{rad/s}$$ Convert to RPM: $$N_m = \dfrac{781.4 \times 60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{46{,}884}{6.2832}$$ $$\boxed{N_m \approx 7462 \ \text{RPM}}$$ ### Step 5 — Interpret the Result A motor rated at **~200 Nm continuous, ~7500 RPM peak** with a **single-speed 9:1 gearbox** can deliver the demanded performance. This is consistent with a **PMSM** in the **150–160 kW peak** class, exactly matching the Tata Nexon EV's PMSM spec. --- ## 3.4 Validation — Energy & Power Sanity Check Energy delivered to road during 0–100 km/h event: $$E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_f^2 = 0.5 \times 1500 \times (27.78)^2$$ $$E_{kinetic} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 771.6 = 578{,}722 \ \text{J} \approx 160.8 \ \text{Wh}$$ Battery energy actually drawn (after losses): $$E_{drawn} = \dfrac{160.8}{0.88} \approx 182.7 \ \text{Wh}$$ This is **negligible** vs. the 50 kWh pack ⇒ the 0–100 event drains < **0.4 %** of battery. **Cruise at 90 km/h dominates range**, not acceleration bursts — a key insight for KTU answers. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 EV Development V-Model Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Concept Definition
Target Range Price Segment] nodeB[Vehicle Level Simulation
MATLAB Simulink AVL Cruise] nodeC[Sub System Sizing
Battery Motor Inverter Gearbox] nodeD[Hardware in Loop HIL
Bench Validation of VCU Software] nodeE[Prototype Build
Chassis Dyno and Track Testing] nodeF[Homologation
AIS 156 ECE R100 ISO 26262] nodeG[Production SOP
Start of Production] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeV1[Requirements Specification] nodeV2[Model Validation] nodeV3[Sub System Validation] nodeV4[Software Validation] nodeV5[Vehicle Integration Validation] nodeV6[Compliance Audit] nodeV1 -.validates.-> nodeA nodeV2 -.validates.-> nodeB nodeV3 -.validates.-> nodeC nodeV4 -.validates.-> nodeD nodeV5 -.validates.-> nodeE nodeV6 -.validates.-> nodeF classDef phase fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff; classDef valid fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff; class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG phase; class nodeV1,nodeV2,nodeV3,nodeV4,nodeV5,nodeV6 valid; ``` ## 4.2 EV Powertrain Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeBATT[Battery Pack
350 to 800 V DC] nodeINV[Motor Inverter
SiC or IGBT] nodeMTR[PMSM or Induction Motor
100 to 250 kW] nodeGBX[Single Speed Reducer
Gear Ratio 9 to 11] nodeWHL[Drive Wheels
Radius 0.32 m] nodeVCU[Vehicle Control Unit VCU
Torque Map Energy Management] nodeOBC[On Board Charger OBC
6.6 to 22 kW AC] nodeDCDC[HV to LV DC DC
400 V to 12 V] nodeBMS[Battery Management System BMS
Cell Balancing Thermal] nodeTELE[Telematics Unit
OTA Updates Diagnostics] nodeBATT --> nodeINV nodeINV --> nodeMTR nodeMTR --> nodeGBX nodeGBX --> nodeWHL nodeVCU --> nodeINV nodeVCU --> nodeBMS nodeVCU --> nodeDCDC nodeOBC --> nodeBATT nodeDCDC --> nodeBATT nodeBMS --> nodeTELE nodeVCU --> nodeTELE classDef power fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff; classDef ctrl fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff; classDef aux fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff; class nodeBATT,nodeINV,nodeMTR,nodeGBX,nodeWHL power; class nodeVCU,nodeBMS,nodeTELE ctrl; class nodeOBC,nodeDCDC aux; ``` ## 4.3 Component Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeQ1{Application Segment?} nodeA1[Urban Commuter 50 kW] nodeA2[Mid Sedan 150 kW] nodeA3[Performance SUV 300 kW] nodeQ2{Motor Topology?} nodeB1[BLDC Low Cost] nodeB2[PMSM High Efficiency] nodeB3[Induction Robust] nodeB4[SRM Fail Safe] nodeQ3{Battery Chemistry?} nodeC1[LFP Long Life Safe] nodeC2[NMC High Energy] nodeC3[NCA Tesla Style] nodeQ1 -->|city| nodeA1 nodeQ1 -->|highway| nodeA2 nodeQ1 -->|flagship| nodeA3 nodeA1 --> nodeB1 nodeA2 --> nodeB2 nodeA3 --> nodeB2 nodeA3 --> nodeB3 nodeB1 --> nodeC1 nodeB2 --> nodeC2 nodeB3 --> nodeC2 nodeB4 --> nodeC1 classDef seg fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000; classDef mot fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff; classDef chem fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff; class nodeA1,nodeA2,nodeA3 seg; class nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeB3,nodeB4 mot; class nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3 chem; ``` ## 4.4 Energy Flow During Regenerative Braking ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeK1[Kinetic Energy of Vehicle] nodeK2[Wheels act as Generators] nodeK3[Inverter in Rectifier Mode] nodeK4[DC Link Charging] nodeK5[Battery Pack SoC Increases] nodeK6[VCU Decides Regen Torque Limit] nodeK1 --> nodeK2 --> nodeK3 --> nodeK4 --> nodeK5 nodeK6 -.governs.-> nodeK3 classDef energy fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff; class nodeK1,nodeK2,nodeK3,nodeK4,nodeK5,nodeK6 energy; ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the EV block diagram," examiners expect **at minimum** the Battery → Inverter → Motor → Gearbox → Wheels chain plus a **separate control signal arrow** from VCU to Inverter. Diagrams without the control loop are considered incomplete and lose 2–3 marks. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV with respect to energy source, propulsion mode, and grid dependency.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV | |---|---|---|---|---| | Energy source | Battery only | Battery + ICE | Battery (large) + ICE | Hydrogen fuel cell | | Propulsion | Electric motor only | Both (mostly ICE) | Both (electric-first) | Electric motor only | | Grid charging | Yes (mandatory) | No | Yes (mandatory) | No (H₂ refuel) | | Range extender | None | ICE | ICE (when SoC low) | None | | Example | Tata Nexon EV | Toyota Camry Hybrid | MG ZS EV + REx | Toyota Mirai | > Each correctly differentiated row ≈ **0.5 mark**. Naming an example per type = remaining marks. → **Full 3 marks**. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **List any three Indian EV standards and briefly state their scope.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **AIS-156 (Rev 2, 2020):** Specifies safety requirements for **REESS (Rechargeable Electrical Energy Storage System)** — battery packs — including thermal propagation, water immersion, mechanical shock, and overcharge protection. *(1 mark)* 2. **AIS-038 (Rev 2):** Construction and functional safety requirements specifically for **electric powertrain vehicles**, covering high-voltage wiring, isolation monitoring, and crash safety. *(1 mark)* 3. **AIS-156 + Amendment 3:** Mandates a **minimum 5-year warranty** on EV batteries and a **battery passport** concept introduced in 2024 to enable traceability for second-life applications. *(1 mark)* > Acceptable alternatives: **IEC 61851** (conductive charging), **ISO 26262** (functional safety), **ECE R100** (UN-level EV safety). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question B — Choice A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]` **A city BEV is being designed with the following target specifications:** - Kerb mass = 1200 kg - Frontal area = 2.1 m² - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$ - Rolling resistance coefficient = 0.013 - Wheel radius = 0.31 m - Gear ratio (single speed) = 8.5 - Drivetrain efficiency = 0.86 - Battery nominal voltage = 360 V - Top speed target = 120 km/h #### (a) **Compute the tractive force required at top speed on a flat road. Also compute the peak motor torque needed.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI:** $$v = \dfrac{120}{3.6} = 33.33 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance force:** $$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g = 0.013 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 153.04 \ \text{N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute aerodynamic drag force:** $$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.1 \times (33.33)^2$$ Breaking down: - $(33.33)^2 = 1110.89$ - $0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$ - $0.6125 \times 0.30 = 0.18375$ - $0.18375 \times 2.1 = 0.3859$ - $0.3859 \times 1110.89 = 428.7 \ \text{N}$ **Step 4 — Total tractive force (constant speed, flat road ⇒ $F_{ia}=F_{gc}=0$):** $$\boxed{F_t = 153.04 + 428.7 = 581.74 \ \text{N}}$$ **Step 5 — Motor torque at top speed:** Using $T_m = \dfrac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$ (with $\eta_{gear} \approx 0.95$ implicit): $$T_m = \dfrac{581.74 \times 0.31}{8.5 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{180.34}{8.075}$$ $$\boxed{T_m \approx 22.33 \ \text{Nm}}$$ **Marks distribution:** - Velocity conversion: 1 - Each force correctly computed: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 - Total tractive force: 1 - Torque conversion: 1 --- #### (b) **The vehicle must achieve 0–60 km/h in 6 seconds on flat road. Estimate the motor peak power requirement and the battery Ah consumption during this acceleration event.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Final velocity:** $$v_f = \dfrac{60}{3.6} = 16.67 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Average acceleration:** $$a = \dfrac{16.67}{6} = 2.778 \ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Step 3 — Peak tractive force at launch (neglect drag at v ≈ 0):** $$F_{peak} \approx m \cdot a = 1200 \times 2.778 = 3333.6 \ \text{N}$$ Adding rolling resistance for safety: $$F_{peak} = 3333.6 + 153.04 = 3486.6 \ \text{N}$$ **Step 4 — Peak motor torque:** $$T_{m,peak} = \dfrac{3486.6 \times 0.31}{8.5 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{1080.85}{8.075}$$ $$\boxed{T_{m,peak} \approx 133.85 \ \text{Nm}}$$ **Step 5 — Motor speed at end of event:** $$\omega_m = \dfrac{v_f \cdot G}{r_w} = \dfrac{16.67 \times 8.5}{0.31} = 457.04 \ \text{rad/s}$$ Convert: $N_m = 457.04 \times 60 / (2\pi) \approx 4364$ RPM. **Step 6 — Peak mechanical power:** $$P_{peak} = T_{m,peak} \cdot \omega_m = 133.85 \times 457.04 \approx 61{,}176 \ \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{peak} \approx 61.18 \ \text{kW}}$$ **Step 7 — Energy in acceleration event (kinetic energy gained):** $$E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_f^2 = 0.5 \times 1200 \times (16.67)^2 = 0.5 \times 1200 \times 277.89$$ $$E_{kin} = 166{,}734 \ \text{J} = 46.31 \ \text{Wh}$$ **Step 8 — Battery energy drawn (with losses):** $$E_{batt} = \dfrac{46.31}{0.86} = 53.85 \ \text{Wh}$$ **Step 9 — Battery Ah drawn:** $$Q = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{V_{pack}} = \dfrac{53.85}{360}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.15 \ \text{Ah}}$$ **Marks distribution:** - Acceleration & force: 2 - Motor torque & speed: 2 - Peak power: 1 - Energy & Ah computation: 2 --- ### Question B — Choice B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` **A delivery fleet operator wants to convert 50 three-wheelers (auto-rickshaws) from ICE to BEV. As an EV development engineer, propose:** #### (a) **The complete powertrain architecture for the BEV variant. Justify each component selection.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Proposed Architecture:** 1. **Battery Pack — LFP, 7 kWh usable, 96 V nominal, 73 Ah.** - *Justification:* LFP chemistry preferred over NMC because delivery vehicles do 80–100 km/day with frequent fast-charge cycles — LFP's **>3000 cycle life** vs NMC's ~1500 is critical for fleet TCO. 96 V architecture allows **lower cable currents** (reducing I²R losses), safer for high-humidity Indian conditions. *(2 marks)* 2. **Motor — BLDC, 5 kW peak, 3 kW continuous, liquid-cooled.** - *Justification:* BLDC is **cheaper** than PMSM and the round-trip efficiency (~88 %) is acceptable for low-speed stop-go duty cycles typical of last-mile delivery. Air-cooling is risky in tropical Indian summers; liquid cooling extends motor life. *(2 marks)* 3. **Motor Controller — 96 V to 96 V IGBT inverter with sinusoidal FOC.** - *Justification:* Field-Oriented Control (FOC) gives **smooth torque at low speeds**, important for passenger comfort. IGBT rated for 200 A peak handles inrush. *(1 mark)* 4. **Transmission — Single-stage belt drive, ratio 4.5:1.** - *Justification:* Auto-rickshaws never exceed 45 km/h, so a **simple fixed-ratio belt drive** is sufficient and **50 % cheaper** than a multi-speed gearbox. *(1 mark)* 5. **On-Board Charger — 1.5 kW AC, Type-2 connector with IEC 60309 industrial plug.** - *Justification:* Fleet operator can deploy **standard 230 V, 16 A sockets** at depots; 1.5 kW means a full charge takes ~5 hours overnight, fitting depot schedule. *(1 mark)* --- #### (b) **Estimate the daily energy consumption, range per charge, and annual operating cost saving versus the existing CNG variant.** *(7 marks)* **Given data:** - Auto-rickshaw kerb mass = 450 kg (with battery replacing CNG tank) - Payload = 200 kg - Total mass $m = 650$ kg - Frontal area $A = 1.6$ m², $C_d = 0.45$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.018$ - Average trip speed $v = 25$ km/h = 6.94 m/s - Operating hours per day = 8 h - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ - Battery pack energy $E_{usable} = 7$ kWh **Step 1 — Compute resistive forces:** $$F_{rr} = 0.018 \times 650 \times 9.81 = 114.78 \ \text{N}$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 1.6 \times (6.94)^2$$ $(6.94)^2 = 48.16$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 1.6 \times 48.16 = 21.24 \ \text{N}$$ $$F_t = 114.78 + 21.24 = 136.02 \ \text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Tractive power:** $$P_t = F_t \cdot v = 136.02 \times 6.94 = 944 \ \text{W} \approx 0.944 \ \text{kW}$$ **Step 3 — Battery power draw:** $$P_{batt} = \dfrac{0.944}{0.85} = 1.11 \ \text{kW}$$ **Step 4 — Daily energy consumption (8 h):** $$\boxed{E_{day} = 1.11 \times 8 = 8.88 \ \text{kWh/day}}$$ **Step 5 — Range per charge:** At $v = 25$ km/h, energy per km $= 1.11 / 25 = 0.0444$ kWh/km. $$R = \dfrac{7}{0.0444} = 157.6 \ \text{km} \ \text{per charge}$$ Assuming **two partial charges per day** (mid-day top-up): $$\boxed{R_{day} \approx 150 \ \text{km}}$$ This comfortably meets the **80–100 km/day** fleet requirement. **Step 6 — Annual cost comparison:** Assume: - BEV electricity cost: ₹8/kWh → ₹8 × 8.88 × 365 = **₹25,930/vehicle/year** - CNG cost: ₹85/kg, mileage 35 km/kg → 150 km/day ÷ 35 = 4.29 kg/day × 365 × 85 = **₹1,33,143/vehicle/year** **Annual saving per vehicle:** $$\boxed{\text{Saving} = 1{,}33{,}143 - 25{,}930 = ₹1{,}07{,}213}$$ **For 50 vehicles: ₹53.6 lakh/year fleet-wide saving** — payback period on a ₹4 lakh conversion kit ≈ **3.7 years**. **Marks distribution:** - Resistive forces: 2 - Power & energy per day: 2 - Range estimate: 1 - Cost saving calculation: 2 --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-loss patterns reported by KTU examiners (2019–2024 paper analysis):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** — losing 1 mark per occurrence. Always write the conversion line. > 2. **Mixing up $C_d$ and $A$ in $F_{ad}$ formula** — students write $F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$ but plug $A = 2.2$ for both frontal area and area coefficient — read the question twice. > 3. **Ignoring drivetrain efficiency** — energy/power questions are graded on whether you divided by $\eta$. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for this step. > 4. **Writing "C-rate" instead of a numerical value** when the question asks "what is the charging rate?" — always compute $I = C \times Q$. > 5. **In diagrams, drawing the motor controller inside the motor block** — keep inverter separate; they are physically distinct components with separate cooling. > 6. **Using Python/C-style pseudocode** in EV numerical answers is **strictly prohibited** by KTU 2024 scheme for non-CS papers — always show step-by-step algebra in the `aligned` block format as demonstrated in Section 3. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Electric Vehicle Development:** - ☐ **V-Model phases:** Concept → Simulation → Sizing → HIL → Prototype → Homologation → SOP. - ☐ **EV categories:** BEV (battery only), HEV (no plug), PHEV (large battery + ICE + plug), FCEV (hydrogen). - ☐ **Four tractive forces:** Rolling, Aerodynamic, Gradient, Inertial — **memorize the four formulas and unit sets.** - ☐ **Critical constants:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $1$ kWh $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ J. - ☐ **Crossover speed concept:** ~80 km/h is where $F_{ad}$ overtakes $F_{rr}$ — influences gearbox and regen tuning strategy. - ☐ **Battery chemistry trade-off:** **LFP** = long life + safety; **NMC** = high energy density; **NCA** = highest energy but thermal-runaway risk. - ☐ **Motor topology trade-off:** **PMSM** = efficiency king (most modern EVs); **Induction** = robust (Tesla legacy); **BLDC** = cheap (2W/3W); **SRM** = fail-safe (Reliance/Reva). - ☐ **C-rate definition:** $C = I / Q$. **3C** means 20-minute full charge. - ☐ **WLTP vs MIDC vs EPA:** WLTP ~ 15 % optimistic vs real-world; MIDC (Indian) is more lenient; EPA is most conservative. - ☐ **Standards to name-check:** AIS-156, AIS-038, IEC 61851, ISO 26262, ECE R100. - ☐ **VCU role:** Torque-vectoring, energy management, regenerative brake blending, thermal coordination. - ☐ **Regenerative braking efficiency:** 60–80 % recapture possible in urban duty cycles. - ☐ **Always show unit conversion** (km/h → m/s, Wh → kWh, km → m) — these are the "free marks" KTU awards. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark Part-B question on EV development, allocate answer space as **20 % diagram + 30 % component selection rationale + 50 % numerical computation**. Examiners are instructed to scan for these three distinct blocks before awarding marks.

topics.title: System Layout
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Layout

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Layout** in an automobile refers to the structured, spatially organized and logically interconnected arrangement of all electronic, electrical, electromechanical, and communication sub-systems that together perform the functional duties of the modern vehicle. It defines **where** each component (ECUs, sensors, actuators, wiring harness, power sources, buses) is physically placed, **how** they are electrically connected, and **how data and power flow** between them in real time.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, the System Layout is studied under five functional domains:

| Domain | Full Form | KTU Term Used |
|---|---|---|
| **PT** | Powertrain | Engine + Transmission + Driveline Electronics |
| **CH** | Chassis | Braking, Steering, Suspension Electronics |
| **BD** | Body | Lighting, Wiper, Door, HVAC Electronics |
| **IN** | Infotainment | Audio, Video, Navigation, Telematics |
| **AD** | ADAS / Safety | Airbag, ABS, ESC, Cruise, Camera, Radar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The System Layout is *not* just a wiring diagram. It is a **three-dimensional architectural blueprint** comprising (a) physical placement, (b) electrical power distribution, and (c) data-bus communication topology. Examiners expect answers covering all three layers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a modern car as a **hospital**:

- The **ECUs (Electronic Control Units)** are the specialist doctors.
- The **CAN / LIN bus** is the hospital's internal telephone/PBX network through which doctors share patient reports instantly.
- The **Sensors** are the diagnostic machines (X-ray, MRI, blood test) collecting data.
- The **Actuators** are the nurses administering treatment based on the doctor's orders.
- The **Battery + Alternator** is the central power generator of the hospital.
- The **Wiring Harness** is the building's plumbing + electrical cabling running through walls, floors, and ceilings.
- The **Central Gateway ECU** is the hospital reception desk routing every external call to the correct department.

Just as a hospital cannot function with doctors placed randomly and no communication between them, a modern car cannot function without a well-defined System Layout. **The vehicle is essentially a "rolling network of computers."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant / Standard:** A typical mid-segment 2024 passenger car contains **70 to 150 ECUs**, **2 to 5 km** of wiring harness, and operates on the standardized **12 V DC** electrical system (heavy commercial vehicles use **24 V DC**). The dominant in-vehicle network standard is **CAN 2.0B** running at **500 kbps** for powertrain and **100 kbps** for body electronics.

## 1.3 Why System Layout Matters in Autotronics

Without a properly engineered System Layout, the vehicle will suffer from:

1. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** — random sensor noise and corrupted CAN frames.
2. **Voltage drop** — distant ECUs receiving under-voltage.
3. **Latency in safety-critical signals** — e.g., airbag deployment delay.
4. **Serviceability nightmares** — technicians unable to trace faults.
5. **Weight and fuel-economy penalty** — unnecessarily long, heavy harnesses.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of an Automotive System Layout

A KTU 2024 answer must explicitly divide the layout into these three layers:

### Layer 1 — Physical / Spatial Layer
This is the **geometric placement** of components inside the vehicle body.

- **Front Compartment (Engine Bay):** ECM/PCM (Engine Control Module), TCM (Transmission Control Module), ABS/ESC Module, Battery, Alternator, Starter Motor, Front Sensors (Crash, Radar, Camera, Ambient Temp).
- **Passenger Compartment (Dashboard & Center Console):** Instrument Cluster, BCM (Body Control Module), HVAC Control Module, Infotainment Head Unit, Gateway ECU, Airbag ECU (SRS), OBD-II Port, Center Display.
- **Floor / Tunnel:** Fuel Pump Module, Oxygen Sensors, Wiring Harness Trunk Line.
- **Rear Compartment (Trunk / Under-Rear Floor):** Rear BCM, Battery (in EVs), TPMS Receiver, Parking Assist Module, Subwoofer Amplifier, Rear Camera Module.
- **Underbody (Chassis):** Wheel Speed Sensors, Suspension Height Sensors, EPS Motor, Brake Caliper Actuators.

### Layer 2 — Power Distribution Layer
Defines how **+12 V (or +24 V)** reaches every load.

```
Battery (+12 V) 
   → Main Fuse Box (Engine Bay) 
       → Subsystem Fuse Boxes (Cabin, Trunk) 
           → Relays / Solid-State Smart Fuses 
               → ECU Power Supply Pins 
                   → Sensor / Actuator Power Rails
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Voltages to Remember:**
> - **Battery nominal:** **12.6 V** (at rest), **13.8 – 14.4 V** (running, alternator charging).
> - **Sensor supply:** **5 V** (regulated by ECU) or **12 V** (direct).
> - **Actuator supply:** **12 V** (motors, solenoids) or **high-voltage bus** (HEV/EV: **200 – 800 V DC**).

### Layer 3 — Data Communication / Network Layer
Defines how ECUs exchange information.

| Bus Protocol | Speed | Typical Application | Topology |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAN (Controller Area Network)** | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | Powertrain, Chassis | Linear bus, terminated at 120 Ω |
| **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** | 19.2 kbps | Mirrors, Windows, Seat, Wiper | Single master, multiple slaves |
| **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | X-by-Wire (Steering, Brake) | Star or linear, deterministic |
| **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport)** | 25 – 150 Mbps | Infotainment (Audio/Video) | Ring topology |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | ADAS, Camera, V2X | Point-to-point, full duplex |
| **LVDS** | >1 Gbps | Display panels | Point-to-point differential |

## 2.2 The Five Functional Domains (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Powertrain (PT) Domain
- **Master ECU:** ECM / PCM.
- **Sub-ECUs:** TCM, Fuel Injector Driver, Ignition Coil Driver, Throttle Body Controller, VGT (Variable Geometry Turbo) Controller, Glow Plug Controller (Diesel), BMS (Battery Management System in HEV/EV).
- **Key Sensors:** Crank Position (CKP), Cam Position (CMP), MAP, MAF, TPS, O2/lambda, Knock, Coolant Temp, Fuel Rail Pressure, Vehicle Speed.
- **Key Actuators:** Injectors, Ignition Coils, Throttle Valve, EGR Valve, VGT Vanes, Variable Valve Timing (VVT) Solenoids, Electric Water Pump, Electric Coolant Valve.

### 2.2.2 Chassis (CH) Domain
- **Master ECU:** ABS / ESC Module.
- **Sub-ECUs:** EPS Module, Active Suspension Module, AWD Coupling Controller, Electric Parking Brake.
- **Key Sensors:** 4 × Wheel Speed Sensors, Steering Angle Sensor, Yaw Rate Sensor, Lateral/Longitudinal Accelerometer, Brake Pedal Position, Master Cylinder Pressure.
- **Key Actuators:** ABS Hydraulic Pump, Wheel Brake Modulators, EPS Motor, Damper Solenoids, Transfer Clutch.

### 2.2.3 Body (BD) Domain
- **Master ECU:** BCM (Body Control Module).
- **Sub-ECUs:** Lighting Control Module, HVAC Control Module, Seat Control Module, Door Module, Tailgate Module.
- **Key Sensors:** Ambient Light, Rain, Door Ajar, Trunk Ajar, Seat Occupancy, Sunload, In-Car Temperature, Key Fob Receiver, Cabin Humidity.
- **Key Actuators:** Headlamp, Tail Lamp, Wiper Motor, Blower, Compressor, Door Lock Motor, Window Lifter, Mirror Adjust Motor, Seat Motor, Horn.

### 2.2.4 Infotainment (IN) Domain
- **Master ECU:** Head Unit (HU) / IVI (In-Vehicle Infotainment).
- **Sub-ECUs:** Amplifier, Tuner, Navigation Module, Telematics Control Unit (TCU), T-Box.
- **Key Sensors:** Microphone, GPS Antenna, Camera (Rear), Touch Overlay, Steering Wheel Switch Matrix.
- **Key Actuators:** Speakers, Display, Vibration Motor (Haptics), External Antenna.

### 2.2.5 ADAS / Safety (AD) Domain
- **Master ECU:** ADAS Domain Controller / Fusion ECU.
- **Sub-ECUs:** Front Camera, Front Radar, Corner Radars (×4), Ultrasonic Park Assist, Surround View ECU, SRS Airbag ECU.
- **Key Sensors:** Forward-Facing Camera, 77 GHz Radar, Lidar (premium), Ultrasonic, Driver Monitoring Camera, Steering Torque Sensor.
- **Key Actuators:** Seatbelt Pretensioner, Airbag Squibs, Audible Warning Buzzer, Haptic Steering Wheel, Brake Booster.

## 2.3 The Central Gateway ECU — The Heart of the System Layout

The **Gateway ECU** is the central router that interconnects the **CAN powertrain bus, CAN chassis bus, CAN body bus, CAN infotainment bus, and the OBD-II diagnostic port.** It performs:

1. **Message routing** between buses of different speeds.
2. **Protocol translation** (CAN ↔ LIN ↔ Ethernet).
3. **Network management** (sleep / wake-up coordination).
4. **Security gateway** (firewall between powertrain and OBD-II to prevent unauthorized tuning).
5. **Diagnostic multiplexing** — single OBD-II port reaches all ECUs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nominal battery voltage | $V_{bat}$ | **12 V DC** (cars), **24 V DC** (HCV) | Power layer |
| 2 | Alternator charging voltage | $V_{alt}$ | 13.8 – 14.4 V | Charging system |
| 3 | CAN bus bit-rate (powertrain) | $f_{CAN-PT}$ | **500 kbps** | Network layer |
| 4 | CAN bus bit-rate (body) | $f_{CAN-BD}$ | 100 kbps | Network layer |
| 5 | CAN bus termination resistance | $R_{term}$ | **120 Ω** at each end | Network layer |
| 6 | LIN bus speed | $f_{LIN}$ | 19.2 kbps | Sub-bus |
| 7 | FlexRay speed | $f_{FR}$ | 10 Mbps | X-by-Wire |
| 8 | Automotive Ethernet | $f_{ETH}$ | 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps | ADAS |
| 9 | OBD-II connector pins used | — | Pins 6 (CAN-H), 14 (CAN-L) | Diagnostics |
| 10 | Typical ECU count | $N_{ECU}$ | 70 – 150 | Architecture |
| 11 | Harness length | $L_{h}$ | 2 – 5 km | Weight / layout |
| 12 | Cranking voltage (engine start) | $V_{cr}$ | 9.6 V minimum (spec) | Power layer |
| 13 | Sensor supply (regulated) | $V_{ref}$ | **5.0 V ± 2 %** | Sensor layer |
| 14 | Maximum quiescent current (parked) | $I_{q}$ | ≤ 80 mA | Battery drain |
| 15 | EV / HEV high-voltage bus | $V_{HV}$ | 200 – 800 V DC | EV layout |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The System Layout is the **master document** used by:

- **Automotive OEMs** (Tata Motors, Maruti, Toyota) — to assemble the vehicle on the production line.
- **Harness suppliers** (Yazaki, Sumitomo, Leoni) — to cut and route the wiring.
- **Service technicians** — to trace faults using the layout diagram.
- **Software / functional-safety engineers** — to design signal routing, define ASIL levels (ISO 26262), and perform FMEA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Trend (Post-2020):** With the rise of zonal architectures (Tesla, Volvo), the System Layout is moving from a "domain-based" architecture to a **zonal architecture** where a single zonal ECU handles power, data, and diagnostics for a physical region of the car (front-left, front-right, rear-left, rear-right, central). This is a high-yield point for KTU 2024 — examiners love asking "compare domain vs zonal architecture."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-through & System Design Logic

Because "System Layout" is a systems-architecture topic, the analytical walk-through below demonstrates **how a layout is engineered from scratch** — a classic 14-mark KTU design-style question.

## 3.1 Problem Setup (Typical KTU Question)

> *"Design the System Layout of a mid-size petrol sedan equipped with ABS, EPS, automatic HVAC, infotainment, and 4 airbags. Sketch the physical placement, the power distribution tree, and the CAN network topology. Identify the Gateway ECU location."*

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Engineering Solution

### Step 1 — Functional Decomposition
List all required vehicle features.

$$
F_{vehicle} = \{F_{PT},\ F_{CH},\ F_{BD},\ F_{IN},\ F_{AD}\}
$$

Where:
- $F_{PT}$ = Engine control, automatic transmission control.
- $F_{CH}$ = ABS + ESC, Electric Power Steering.
- $F_{BD}$ = Auto HVAC, central locking, power windows, lighting.
- $F_{IN}$ = Touchscreen head unit, 6 speakers, BT, USB, navigation.
- $F_{AD}$ = 2 front airbags + 2 side airbags + seatbelt pretensioners.

### Step 2 — ECU Allocation
Each function (or cluster of functions) is assigned to a dedicated ECU.

$$
F_{PT} \rightarrow \text{PCM} + \text{TCM}
$$

$$
F_{CH} \rightarrow \text{ABS/ESC Module} + \text{EPS Module}
$$

$$
F_{BD} \rightarrow \text{BCM} + \text{HVAC Module}
$$

$$
F_{IN} \rightarrow \text{HU} + \text{AMP}
$$

$$
F_{AD} \rightarrow \text{SRS ECU} + \text{Crash Sensors (×2 front + 2 side)}
$$

### Step 3 — Physical Placement
Using ergonomics, thermal, and EMI considerations:

- **PCM + TCM →** Engine bay, near firewall, heat-shielded.
- **ABS/ESC →** Engine bay, near brake master cylinder.
- **EPS →** Mounted on steering column (column-type EPS).
- **BCM + Gateway →** Behind dashboard, driver-side kick panel (central, accessible).
- **HVAC →** Behind dashboard, passenger side.
- **SRS ECU →** Center tunnel, under console (senses central impact).
- **HU + AMP →** Center stack of dashboard.
- **Battery + Main Fuse Box →** Engine bay, right side.
- **Crash Sensors (front) →** Behind front bumper / radiator support.
- **Side Crash Sensors →** B-pillars.
- **Wheel Speed Sensors →** At each wheel hub.

### Step 4 — Power Distribution Tree Design

$$
\text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Main Fuse Box} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{PCM/TCM} \\
\text{ABS/ESC} \\
\text{EPS} \\
\text{BCM} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{HVAC} \\
\text{Lighting} \\
\text{Locks} \\
\text{Wipers}
\end{cases} \\
\text{HU + AMP} \\
\text{SRS ECU}
\end{cases}
$$

Each branch is protected by an **appropriately rated fuse** (e.g., PCM: **40 A**, BCM: **60 A**, HU: **15 A**, SRS: **10 A**).

### Step 5 — CAN Network Topology Design

We use **three CAN buses** plus LIN sub-buses:

$$
\text{PT-CAN (500 kbps)} = \{\text{PCM},\ \text{TCM},\ \text{ABS/ESC},\ \text{EPS}\}
$$

$$
\text{CH-CAN (500 kbps)} = \{\text{ABS/ESC},\ \text{EPS},\ \text{SRS ECU}\}
$$

$$
\text{BD-CAN (100 kbps)} = \{\text{BCM},\ \text{HVAC},\ \text{HU (diagnostic only)}\}
$$

LIN sub-networks off the BCM:

$$
\text{BCM} \rightarrow \text{LIN Master} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Driver Door Module (window, mirror, lock)} \\
\text{Passenger Door Module} \\
\text{Seat Module} \\
\text{Rain/Light Sensor} \\
\text{Wiper Motor Controller}
\end{cases}
$$

The **Gateway ECU** connects PT-CAN, CH-CAN, and BD-CAN, and exposes the OBD-II port to all three.

### Step 6 — Verification (Mathematical sanity check)

- **Total ECUs:** 1 (PCM) + 1 (TCM) + 1 (ABS) + 1 (EPS) + 1 (BCM) + 1 (HVAC) + 1 (HU) + 1 (AMP) + 1 (SRS) = **9 ECUs** (excluding LIN slaves).
- **CAN message load (worst case):** Each ECU averages 10 messages/s at average 8-byte payload.  
  $f_{load} = 9 \times 10 \times 8 \times 8 = 5760$ **bits/s** on each bus → negligible compared to 500 000 bps capacity (≈ **1.15 %** bus utilization, well within the **30 %** safe-load guideline).
- **LIN bus load (BCM as master):** 5 slave frames × 20 ms = 25 % utilization → safe.
- **Power budget check:** Sum of all branch fuse ratings = 40 + 40 + 30 + 60 + 10 + 15 + 10 = **205 A** continuous. Main fuse link: **250 A**, which gives a **~22 %** head-room — acceptable for engine-off inrush of HVAC and EPS simultaneously.

### Step 7 — Final Architecture Statement

The vehicle has a **3-CAN + multiple-LIN domain architecture** with a **central Gateway ECU** behind the dashboard. Safety-critical buses (PT-CAN, CH-CAN, SRS) are physically and logically isolated from the body and infotainment buses to comply with **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** requirements for airbag and braking systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The Gateway ECU's location is the *physical midpoint* between all three CAN buses to minimize harness length. The OBD-II port is hard-wired to the Gateway and is also connected to the **CAN-H (pin 6)** and **CAN-L (pin 14)** lines with **two 120 Ω termination resistors** in parallel (effective **60 Ω** between CAN-H and CAN-L when the OBD-II connector is plugged in — this is the diagnostic test value used by service tools).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams implement the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** for the System Layout as required by the KTU 2024 protocol.

## 4.1 High-Level Vehicle Electronic System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWERTRAIN[Powertrain Domain]
        PCM[PCM - Engine Control]
        TCM[TCM - Transmission Control]
        SENS_PT[PT Sensors - CKP CMP MAP MAF TPS O2]
        ACT_PT[PT Actuators - Injector Ignition Throttle VVT]
    end

    subgraph CHASSIS[Chassis Domain]
        ABS[ABS and ESC Module]
        EPS[EPS Module]
        SENS_CH[Chassis Sensors - Wheel Speed Yaw Steering Angle]
        ACT_CH[Chassis Actuators - ABS Pump EPS Motor]
    end

    subgraph BODY[Body Domain]
        BCM[BCM - Body Control Module]
        HVAC[HVAC Module]
        SENS_BD[Body Sensors - Rain Light Door Ajar]
        ACT_BD[Body Actuators - Wiper Lamp Lock Window]
    end

    subgraph INFO[Infotainment Domain]
        HU[Head Unit IVI]
        AMP[Audio Amplifier]
    end

    subgraph ADAS[Safety ADAS Domain]
        SRS[SRS Airbag ECU]
        RADAR[Front Radar Module]
        CAM[Front Camera Module]
    end

    GATEWAY[Central Gateway ECU]
    BATTERY[Battery 12V]
    ALTERNATOR[Alternator 14V]
    OBD[OBD-II Diagnostic Port]

    BATTERY --> ALTERNATOR
    ALTERNATOR --> POWERTRAIN
    ALTERNATOR --> CHASSIS
    ALTERNATOR --> BODY
    ALTERNATOR --> INFO
    ALTERNATOR --> ADAS

    PCM <-->|CAN 500 kbps| GATEWAY
    TCM <-->|CAN 500 kbps| GATEWAY
    ABS <-->|CAN 500 kbps| GATEWAY
    EPS <-->|CAN 500 kbps| GATEWAY
    BCM <-->|CAN 100 kbps| GATEWAY
    HVAC <-->|CAN 100 kbps| GATEWAY
    HU <-->|CAN 100 kbps| GATEWAY
    SRS <-->|CAN 500 kbps| GATEWAY
    RADAR <-->|ETH 100 Mbps| GATEWAY
    CAM  <-->|ETH 100 Mbps| GATEWAY

    GATEWAY <-->|CAN-H CAN-L| OBD

    SENS_PT --> PCM
    PCM --> ACT_PT
    SENS_CH --> ABS
    ABS --> ACT_CH
    SENS_BD --> BCM
    BCM --> ACT_BD
```
