KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a fuel cell. How is it different from a primary battery?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of a continuously supplied fuel (e.g., H₂) and oxidant (e.g., O₂) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products, without combustion. **Difference from a primary battery:** | Aspect | Primary Battery | Fuel Cell | |--------|-----------------|-----------| | Reactants | Sealed inside | Supplied externally | | Refillable | No (single-use) | Yes (continuous) | | Output | Declines as reactants are used | Constant as long as fuel is supplied | | Energy storage | Yes (electrochemical) | No (energy converter) | *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the comparison table, 1 mark for any one-line concluding statement.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What are the three regions of loss in a fuel-cell polarization curve? Name the dominant loss in each region.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three regions of overpotential loss in a polarization curve are: 1. **Activation loss region** (low current density) — caused by sluggish electrode kinetics; dominant at the start of the curve. 2. **Ohmic loss region** (mid current density) — caused by ionic resistance of the electrolyte and electronic resistance of electrodes; linear drop. 3. **Concentration loss region** (high current density) — caused by mass-transport limitations of reactants reaching the catalyst; sharp drop at the tail. *(1 mark per region.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module-Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). State the half-cell and overall reactions. (7 marks) **(b)** A PEMFC stack has 400 cells, each operating at 0.65 V under load. The stack delivers 100 A of current. Calculate: (i) the stack power output, (ii) the mass of H₂ consumed per hour, (iii) the overall efficiency if the LHV of H₂ is 120 MJ kg⁻¹. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks 1. **Definition (1 mark):** PEMFC = Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell; uses a solid polymer electrolyte (e.g., Nafion) as the ion conductor; mobile ion is **H⁺**; operating temperature 60–100 °C. 2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** (See the block diagram in Section 4.1 — H₂ tank → humidifier → anode → membrane → cathode → air → water/heat exhaust; electrons forced through external load.) 3. **Half-cell reactions (2 marks):** - Anode: $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ 4. **Overall reaction (1 mark):** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$ 5. **Role of platinum catalyst (1 mark):** Lowers activation energy for H–H bond dissociation; essential because PEMFC operates at low temperature where reaction kinetics are slow. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** $N = 400$ cells, $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.65$ V, $I = 100$ A, LHV = 120 MJ kg⁻¹. **(i) Stack Power Output (2 marks):** $$V_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell}} = 400 \times 0.65 = 260\;\text{V}$$ $$P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I = 260 \times 100 = 26\,000\;\text{W} = 26\;\text{kW}$$ **(ii) H₂ Consumed per Hour (3 marks):** $$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 3600}{2 \times 96\,485}$$ Numerator: $2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 3600 = 725.76 \times 10^{-3} = 0.72576$ Denominator: $2 \times 96\,485 = 192\,970$ $$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{0.72576}{192\,970} = 3.762 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{kg s}^{-1}$$ Convert to per hour: $$m_{\text{H}_2} = 3.762 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 0.01354\;\text{kg h}^{-1} = 13.54\;\text{g h}^{-1}$$ **(iii) Overall Efficiency (2 marks):** $$E_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{LHV} = 0.01354 \times 120\,000 = 1624.8\;\text{kJ h}^{-1}$$ $$E_{\text{in per second}} = 1624.8 / 3600 = 0.4513\;\text{kW} = 451.3\;\text{W}$$ $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{E_{\text{in rate}}} = \dfrac{26\,000}{451.3} \approx 57.6$$ Wait — verify by another method: $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{\text{cell}}}{M \cdot \text{LHV}} = \dfrac{2 \times 96\,485 \times 0.65}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 \times 10^{6}}$$ Numerator: $125\,430.5$ Denominator: $241\,920$ $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{125\,430.5}{241\,920} = 0.5185 = 51.85\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{overall}} \approx 52\%}$$ > (The first method gave 57.6 % due to a units slip — the second method is the **correct and recommended** approach. Examiners check unit consistency!) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Compare PEMFC, AFC, PAFC, MCFC, and SOFC in terms of electrolyte, mobile ion, operating temperature, and application. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the Nernst equation for the H₂/O₂ fuel cell. A PEMFC operates at 70 °C with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.18$ atm, $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.31$ atm. The standard cell potential at 70 °C is 1.20 V. Calculate the open-circuit voltage. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Type | Electrolyte | Mobile Ion | Operating T | Application | |------|-------------|------------|-------------|-------------| | PEMFC | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | H⁺ | 60–100 °C | Cars (Toyota Mirai) | | AFC | KOH (alkaline) | OH⁻ | 60–90 °C | Spacecraft (Apollo) | | PAFC | Liquid H₃PO₄ | H⁺ | 150–220 °C | Buses, stationary | | MCFC | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | CO₃²⁻ | 600–700 °C | Power plants, marine | | SOFC | Yttria-stabilized ZrO₂ | O²⁻ | 800–1000 °C | Trucks, APU | **Conclusion (2 marks):** PEMFC is preferred for automotive use because of its low operating temperature (fast start-up), high power density, and solid (non-leaking) electrolyte. High-temperature types (MCFC, SOFC) offer higher efficiency and fuel flexibility but are too heavy and slow to start for cars. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Derivation (3 marks):** (See Section 3.1. The Nernst equation derivation yields $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{P_{\text{H}_2} P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ for n = 2.) **Numerical Evaluation (4 marks):** Given: $T = 70 + 273 = 343$ K, $E^\circ = 1.20$ V, $n = 2$, $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹. **Step 1 — pre-factor:** $$\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 343}{2 \times 96\,485} = \dfrac{2851.7}{192\,970} = 0.01478\;\text{V}$$ **Step 2 — argument of ln:** $$\dfrac{1.5 \times \sqrt{0.18}}{0.31} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.4243}{0.31} = \dfrac{0.6364}{0.31} = 2.053$$ **Step 3 — natural log:** $$\ln(2.053) = 0.7191$$ **Step 4 — final EMF:** $$E = 1.20 + 0.01478 \times 0.7191 = 1.20 + 0.01063 = 1.2106\;\text{V}$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 1.21\;\text{V}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU Fuel-Cell problems:** > > 1. **Unit Slip in Faraday's Law:** Using M in *kg mol⁻¹* but writing the answer in *g*, or vice versa. Always state the unit and the molar mass in the same system (SI: kg mol⁻¹ for kg; CGS: g mol⁻¹ for g). > 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** When the question says "HHV = 285.8 kJ mol⁻¹" but asks for efficiency "based on LHV", the answer *changes*. Read the question stem twice. > 3. **Forgetting the "½" in O₂ coefficient:** The overall reaction uses **½ O₂**, not O₂. This changes the standard free energy per mole of O₂. Stick to **per mole of H₂** as the reference. > 4. **Sign of the Nernst term:** The standard form is $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ with Q in the form *reactants/products* for the KTU convention. Mixing up the sign is the #1 cause of negative-voltage answers. > 5. **Mistaking Fuel Cell for a Heat Engine:** Never apply the Carnot formula to a fuel cell. It is an electrochemical converter, not a heat engine — that is *why* it is more efficient than an IC engine. > 6. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin:** KTU graders allot a 0.5-mark deduction for using °C in the Nernst equation. > 7. **Stack vs Cell confusion:** A stack of *N* cells gives *N* times the voltage but the *same current* as a single cell. Power = N · V_cell · I. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Cell (Module 3)** > > ✅ **Definition:** Electrochemical converter — fuel + oxidant in, electricity + water + heat out. **Open system, no combustion.** > > ✅ **Overall reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$; $E^\circ = 1.229$ V; theoretical efficiency = **83 % (HHV basis)**, **98 % (LHV basis)**. > > ✅ **PEMFC is the automotive favourite** — low T, fast start-up, solid electrolyte, high power density. > > ✅ **Mobile ion in PEMFC = H⁺** (in SOFC = O²⁻; in MCFC = CO₃²⁻; in AFC = OH⁻). > > ✅ **Three losses in polarization curve:** activation (low j), ohmic (linear), concentration (high j). > > ✅ **Faraday's law:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — used to compute H₂ consumption. > > ✅ **Nernst equation (KTU form):** $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{P_{\text{H}_2} P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$. > > ✅ **Constants to memorise:** $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, LHV H₂ = 120 MJ kg⁻¹, HHV H₂ = 142 MJ kg⁻¹. > > ✅ **FCEV real-world data:** Mirai = 128 kW stack, 5 kg H₂ @ 700 bar, ≈ 650 km range, refuel in 5 min. > > ✅ **Hybrid architecture:** FC stack + small Li-ion buffer battery + VCU + inverter + PMSM traction motor. > > ✅ **Hydrogen storage:** Compressed gas at **350 bar (Type III)** or **700 bar (Type IV, carbon-fibre wrapped)**. > > ✅ **Advantages over IC engine:** Zero tailpipe emissions, higher efficiency, no Carnot limit, silent operation. > > ✅ **Disadvantages:** Hydrogen production (mostly from natural gas reforming), storage difficulty, catalyst cost (Pt), infrastructure scarcity. > > ✅ **Comparison with BEV:** FCEV = fast refuel, longer range, lower battery mass. BEV = simpler, better infrastructure, no H₂ handling. > > ✅ **Polarization curve sketch** — be ready to draw and label the three regions in any viva or 3-mark question. > > ✅ **Key overpotential formula:** $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$.

topics.title: Electric Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electric Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is an automotive vehicle whose propulsion system is entirely or partially driven by one or more electric motors, using electrical energy stored in an onboard energy storage system (typically a rechargeable battery, fuel cell, or supercapacitor) or generated through an onboard generator (as in the case of hybrid configurations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Per the OEMRT831 Autotronics syllabus, an *Electric Vehicle* is classified as a vehicle that uses one or more electric machines (motors/generators) for traction drive, combined with an energy storage system, power electronic converter, and a vehicle control unit, replacing or supplementing the conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) powertrain.

The electric powertrain comprises the following primary subsystems:

1. **Energy Storage System (ESS)** — Battery pack, Battery Management System (BMS), and thermal management.
2. **Power Electronics Converter** — DC-DC converter, DC-AC inverter, and onboard charger.
3. **Electric Traction Motor** — PMSM, Induction Motor, BLDC, or SRM.
4. **Transmission / Reduction Gearbox** — Single-speed or multi-speed reducer.
5. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — Master controller coordinating motor, battery, and regenerative braking.
6. **Auxiliary Systems** — HVAC, EPS, lighting, infotainment, and vacuum/electric brake boosters.

## 1.2 Classification of Electric Vehicles

| Vehicle Type | Abbreviation | Energy Source | Electric Drive? | Grid Charging? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Battery Electric Vehicle | BEV | Battery only | Yes (sole drive) | Yes |
| Hybrid Electric Vehicle | HEV | Battery + ICE + Fuel | Yes (assists ICE) | No |
| Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | PHEV | Battery + ICE + Fuel | Yes (assists ICE) | Yes |
| Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | FCEV | Hydrogen Fuel Cell | Yes (sole drive) | No (H₂ refuel) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** HEVs are further subdivided based on powertrain topology into *Series Hybrid*, *Parallel Hybrid*, and *Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid* configurations. KTU questions frequently test the identification of topologies and their relative advantages.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an EV like a **giant remote-controlled toy car scaled up to full size**:
- The **battery pack** is the rechargeable toy cells (but much larger and smarter).
- The **electric motor** is the small DC motor in the toy (but industrial-grade, water/oil-cooled).
- The **throttle pedal** is simply a *variable resistor* (or hall-effect sensor) — pressing it sends a signal to the VCU, which then commands the inverter to feed more current to the motor. There is no physical cable connecting pedal to wheels.
- **Regenerative braking** is like when you push a shopping cart backwards — the wheels spin a generator, slowing the cart while charging it.

Compared to an ICE vehicle, an EV has **~20× fewer moving parts** (≈ 20 vs. ≈ 2000), no oil changes, no exhaust system, no multi-speed transmission complexity, and instant peak torque from zero RPM.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Acceleration of gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Air density at sea level: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**
- Standard coefficient of aerodynamic drag for a sedan: **C_d ≈ 0.22 to 0.30**
- Typical lithium-ion cell nominal voltage: **V_cell ≈ 3.6 to 3.7 V**
- Energy density of gasoline: **≈ 12,000 Wh/kg** (heat-of-combustion basis)
- Practical energy density of Li-ion cells: **150 to 270 Wh/kg** (cell level)
- Standard onboard charger levels:
  - **Level 1 (AC): 120 V / 12–16 A ≈ 1.4 to 1.9 kW**
  - **Level 2 (AC): 240 V / 32 A ≈ 7.7 to 19.2 kW**
  - **Level 3 (DC Fast): 50 to 350 kW**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Vehicle Dynamics — The Four Resistive Forces

To design any EV powertrain, one must balance the **Tractive Force (F_t)** generated by the motor against the **Total Resistive Force (F_R)** acting against motion. The resistive force consists of four components:

### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance Force (F_rr)

Caused by tire deformation at the contact patch. It opposes motion and depends on vehicle mass and road surface.

> **Formula:**
> $$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$
> where $\mu_{rr}$ is the dimensionless coefficient of rolling resistance (≈ 0.008 to 0.015 for radial tires).

### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force (F_ad)

Caused by air pushing against the front fascia and turbulent flow around the body.

> **Formula:**
> $$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$
> where $A$ is the frontal cross-sectional area (m²) and $v$ is the vehicle velocity (m/s).

### 2.1.3 Gradient (Hill-Climbing) Force (F_g)

Due to gravity acting on a slope.

> **Formula:**
> $$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$
> where $\theta$ is the road inclination angle.

### 2.1.4 Acceleration Force (F_a)

Newton's second law applied to vehicle mass (including rotational inertia correction).

> **Formula:**
> $$F_a = m \cdot (1 + k_m) \cdot a$$
> where $k_m$ is the mass factor (≈ 0.05 to 0.15) accounting for rotational inertia of rotating components, and $a$ is linear acceleration (m/s²).

### 2.1.5 Total Resistive Force & Tractive Equation

The total opposing force and the equilibrium for steady-state cruising (constant velocity) are:

$$F_R = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta$$

$$F_t = F_R + m(1+k_m)\,a$$

> **Tractive Power (P_t)** required at the wheels:

$$P_t = F_t \cdot v = \big[\mu_{rr}\, m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m(1+k_m)\,a\big]\cdot v$$

## 2.2 Battery Pack Sizing — Energy & Power Requirements

### 2.2.1 Battery Energy Capacity (E_batt)

> **Formula:**
> $$E_{batt} = V_{pack} \cdot I_{pack} \cdot t = Q_{pack} \cdot V_{pack} \;\; (\text{in Wh})$$

For a pack with $N_s$ cells in series and $N_p$ in parallel:

$$E_{batt} = N_s \cdot V_{cell} \cdot N_p \cdot C_{cell} \;\; (\text{Wh})$$

where $C_{cell}$ is the per-cell capacity in Ah.

### 2.2.2 Required Battery Energy for a Target Range (R)

$$E_{req} = \dfrac{(F_{rr} + F_{ad}) \cdot R}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$$

> where $\eta_{drivetrain}$ ≈ 0.85 to 0.92 (motor + inverter + gearbox combined efficiency).

### 2.2.3 State of Charge (SOC)

> **Formula:**
> $$SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_0^t I_{batt}(\tau)\, d\tau$$
> where $Q_{rated}$ is the rated battery capacity in Ah.

## 2.3 Electric Motor Equations

### 2.3.1 Motor Output Power

$$P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor}$$

> where $T_{motor}$ is torque (Nm) and $\omega_{motor}$ is angular speed (rad/s).

### 2.3.2 Wheel-Side Torque (After Gearbox)

$$T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot i_{gb}$$

> where $i_{gb}$ is the gearbox reduction ratio and $\eta_{gb}$ is gearbox efficiency (≈ 0.95 to 0.98 for single-speed).

### 2.3.3 Vehicle Speed from Motor Speed

$$v = \dfrac{\omega_{motor} \cdot r_{wheel}}{i_{gb}}$$

> where $r_{wheel}$ is the effective wheel radius.

## 2.4 Regenerative Braking Power Recovery

When the motor acts as a generator, kinetic energy is partially recovered:

$$P_{regen} = T_{gen} \cdot \omega_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gen}$$

$$E_{recovered} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P_{regen}\, dt = \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\, (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$$

> where $\eta_{regen}$ ≈ 0.6 to 0.8 depending on battery SOC and motor controller limits.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta$ | N | $\mu_{rr}$ ≈ 0.008 – 0.015 |
| 2 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | N | $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³ |
| 3 | Gradient force | $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta$ in radians |
| 4 | Acceleration force | $F_a = m(1+k_m)\,a$ | N | $k_m$ ≈ 0.05 – 0.15 |
| 5 | Tractive power | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | W | Power at wheels |
| 6 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot Q$ | Wh | $V$ in V, $Q$ in Ah |
| 7 | Range energy | $E_{req} = (F_R \cdot R)/\eta$ | Wh | $\eta$ = drivetrain efficiency |
| 8 | Motor power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 9 | Wheel torque | $T_w = T_m \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot i_{gb}$ | Nm | Includes reduction |
| 10 | Vehicle speed | $v = \omega_m r_w / i_{gb}$ | m/s | $r_w$ = wheel radius |
| 11 | Regen recovery | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)\eta_{regen}$ | J | 0.6 – 0.8 efficiency |
| 12 | SOC | $SOC = SOC_0 - \tfrac{1}{Q}\int I\, dt$ | fraction | 0 to 1 (or %) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to compute tractive force or battery energy, always start by listing the four resistive components. Forgetting the inertial mass factor $k_m$ in Part (a) of an EV problem is one of the most common mark-losing errors in KTU valuation scripts.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tractive force equation is the **backbone of EV powertrain sizing**. Engineers at Tata Motors, BYD, and Tesla use the same equations to:
- Select motor peak power (kW) for 0–100 km/h targets.
- Determine battery pack capacity (kWh) for target range (km).
- Estimate HVAC auxiliary load impact on range (≈ 10–30% loss in winter).
- Optimize regenerative-braking mapping for typical Indian stop-and-go traffic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I: Tractive Force Equation from Newton's Second Law

**Problem Statement:** A four-wheeled EV of mass $m$ moves with velocity $v$ along a road inclined at angle $\theta$. Prove that the total tractive force required is:

$$F_t = \mu_{rr}\, m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m(1+k_m)\,a$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free-Body Force Decomposition:**
Consider the EV as a rigid body of mass $m$ moving up an incline at angle $\theta$. Forces acting opposite to motion are:
- Rolling resistance at all four tires.
- Aerodynamic drag on the vehicle body.
- Gravitational component along the incline.
- Inertial resistance to acceleration.

**Step 2 — Resolve Gravity Along and Normal to Road:**
Decompose the weight vector $mg$ into components parallel and perpendicular to the road surface:

$$W_{\parallel} = m g \sin\theta \quad \text{(acts opposite to forward motion on upslope)}$$

$$W_{\perp} = m g \cos\theta \quad \text{(acts normal to road surface)}$$

**Step 3 — Rolling Resistance:**
Rolling resistance force is proportional to the normal load on the tires:

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot N = \mu_{rr} \cdot m g \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Aerodynamic Drag:**
From fluid dynamics, drag is proportional to dynamic pressure and frontal area:

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration Force (Inertial Term):**
Newton's second law gives the net force required for acceleration as:

$$F_{net} = m_{eff} \cdot a$$

The effective mass includes rotational inertia of wheels, gears, and motor rotor:

$$m_{eff} = m(1 + k_m)$$

Hence:

$$F_a = m(1 + k_m) \cdot a$$

**Step 6 — Apply Equilibrium (Sum of Forces = Tractive Force):**
For the vehicle to accelerate at rate $a$ up the incline:

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_a$$

Substituting all four terms:

$$F_t = \mu_{rr}\, m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m(1+k_m)\,a$$

Hence proved. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation II: Required Battery Capacity for a Target Range

**Given Data:**
- Vehicle mass $m$ = 1500 kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr}$ = 0.012
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d$ = 0.28
- Frontal area $A$ = 2.2 m²
- Target cruising speed $v$ = 90 km/h = 25 m/s (on level road, $\theta = 0$)
- Target range $R$ = 300 km
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta$ = 0.90
- Air density $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

### Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Convert Range to Meters:**
$$R = 300 \,\text{km} = 300{,}000 \,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rolling Resistance:**
$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(0) = 0.012 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1$$
$$F_{rr} = 176.58 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag:**
$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (25)^2$$
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 625$$
$$F_{ad} = 235.79 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Sum Resistive Force (level road, no acceleration phase energy considered separately):**
$$F_R = F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 176.58 + 235.79 = 412.37 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Mechanical Energy at Wheels:**
$$E_{mech} = F_R \cdot R = 412.37 \cdot 300{,}000$$
$$E_{mech} = 1.237 \times 10^8 \,\text{J}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Watt-Hours:**
$$E_{mech} = \dfrac{1.237 \times 10^8}{3600} = 34{,}361 \,\text{Wh} = 34.36 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Account for Drivetrain Losses (battery must supply more energy):**
$$E_{batt} = \dfrac{E_{mech}}{\eta} = \dfrac{34.36}{0.90}$$
$$E_{batt} = 38.18 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 8 — Apply Usable SOC Window (typical 80% to protect battery life):**
$$E_{usable} = 0.80 \cdot E_{batt} = 30.54 \,\text{kWh} \quad (\text{range with 80\% SOC window})$$

**Step 9 — Interpret:**
A 38.2 kWh pack would deliver 300 km in ideal conditions; practical rated range including HVAC, auxiliaries, and city cycles would be ≈ 250–280 km.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **exact structure** expected by KTU examiners. Show every algebraic step and unit. Skipping the drivetrain efficiency correction loses 2 marks easily.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Motor-to-Wheel Torque Transmission

**Given:**
- Motor rated torque $T_m$ = 210 Nm
- Motor rated speed $N_m$ = 10,000 rpm
- Single-speed gearbox ratio $i_{gb}$ = 9.3
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb}$ = 0.97
- Wheel radius $r_w$ = 0.32 m

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert Motor Speed to Angular Velocity:**
$$\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N_m}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 10{,}000}{60}$$
$$\omega_m = 1047.2 \,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Motor Output Power:**
$$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 210 \cdot 1047.2$$
$$P_m = 219{,}912 \,\text{W} \approx 220 \,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Wheel Torque:**
$$T_w = T_m \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot i_{gb} = 210 \cdot 0.97 \cdot 9.3$$
$$T_w = 1894.4 \,\text{Nm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Vehicle Speed:**
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{i_{gb}} = \dfrac{1047.2 \cdot 0.32}{9.3}$$
$$v_{max} = 36.03 \,\text{m/s} \approx 129.7 \,\text{km/h}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Tractive Force at Wheels:**
$$F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r_w} = \dfrac{1894.4}{0.32} = 5919.9 \,\text{N}$$

This force can accelerate a 1500 kg vehicle at:

$$a = \dfrac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_g}{m(1+k_m)}$$

(evaluated at any chosen speed of interest).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[GRID AC SUPPLY] --> A2[ONBOARD CHARGER]
    B1[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY PACK] --> B2[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM]
    A2 --> B1
    B2 --> B3[DC BUS HIGH VOLTAGE]
    B3 --> C1[DC DC CONVERTER]
    C1 --> C2[12V AUXILIARY BATTERY]
    C2 --> C3[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT]
    C3 --> D1[MOTOR CONTROLLER INVERTER]
    D1 --> D2[ELECTRIC TRACTION MOTOR]
    D2 --> E1[SINGLE SPEED GEARBOX]
    E1 --> E2[DIFFERENTIAL]
    E2 --> F1[WHEELS]
    F1 -.->|KICKBACK KINETIC ENERGY| D2
    D2 -.->|REGEN POWER| B3
    C3 <-->|CAN BUS SIGNALS| G1[BRAKE PEDAL SENSOR]
    C3 <-->|CAN BUS SIGNALS| G2[ACCELERATOR SENSOR]
    C3 <-->|CAN BUS SIGNALS| G3[STEERING SENSOR]
```

## 4.2 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWERTRAIN_SHEV
        ICE[INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE] --> GEN[THREE PHASE GENERATOR]
        GEN --> RECT[AC DC RECTIFIER]
        RECT --> DCB[DC LINK BUS]
        BATT[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY] --> DCB
        DCB --> INV[DC AC INVERTER]
        INV --> MOT[ELECTRIC TRACTION MOTOR]
        MOT --> GBX[GEARBOX]
        GBX --> DIFF[DIFFERENTIAL]
        DIFF --> WH[WHEELS]
    end
```

## 4.3 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWERTRAIN_PHEV
        ICE[INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE] --> CLUTCH[CLUTCH OR COUPLER]
        CLUTCH --> SUM[SPEED SUM GEAR SET]
        MOT[ELECTRIC TRACTION MOTOR] --> SUM
        BATT[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY] --> INV[INVERTER]
        INV --> MOT
        SUM --> GBX[TRANSMISSION]
        GBX --> DIFF[DIFFERENTIAL]
        DIFF --> WH[WHEELS]
    end
```

## 4.4 Battery Management System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BMS_ARCH
        MB[MASTER BMS UNIT] --> S1[CHARGE CONTROL MODULE]
        MB --> S2[DISCHARGE CONTROL MODULE]
        MB --> S3[THERMAL MANAGEMENT MODULE]
        MB --> S4[CELL BALANCING MODULE]
        MB --> S5[STATE OF CHARGE ESTIMATOR]
        MB --> S6[STATE OF HEALTH MONITOR]
        S5 --> S7[CELL 1 TO CELL N VOLTAGE SENSORS]
        S6 --> S8[TEMPERATURE SENSORS PACK WIDE]
        S7 --> MB
        S8 --> MB
        MB --> VCU[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT]
    end
```

## 4.5 Regenerative Braking Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BRAKE PEDAL PRESSED] --> B{BRAKE DEMAND LOGIC}
    B -->|LIGHT TO MODERATE| C[REGENERATIVE MODE]
    B -->|HEAVY EMERGENCY| D[FRICTION BRAKE MODE]
    B -->|BLENDED| E[BLENDED REGEN PLUS FRICTION]
    C --> F[MOTOR OPERATES AS GENERATOR]
    F --> G[POWER ELECTRONICS CONVERTS TO DC]
    G --> H[DC CHARGED INTO HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY]
    E --> I[PARTIAL FRICTION PARTIAL REGEN]
    I --> H
    D --> J[CONVENTIONAL HYDRAULIC BRAKES APPLIED]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Notes:**
> - The first diagram shows the **central nervous system** of a BEV: energy flows from Grid → Charger → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels, with regenerative feedback flowing in reverse.
> - Series HEVs always have the ICE mechanically decoupled from wheels; only the motor drives the wheels.
> - Parallel HEVs allow either ICE or motor (or both) to drive wheels via a torque/speed summing device.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Electric Vehicle. List the main components of an EV powertrain.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

An Electric Vehicle (EV) is a vehicle that uses one or more electric motors for traction, drawing energy from an onboard rechargeable energy storage system (typically a battery) and/or a fuel cell, with optional grid recharging capability.

**Main components of an EV powertrain:**
1. Energy Storage System (Battery pack + BMS)
2. Power Electronics (DC-DC converter, Traction Inverter, Onboard Charger)
3. Electric Traction Motor (PMSM / IM / BLDC / SRM)
4. Transmission / Single-speed Reducer
5. Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)
6. Charging Port and Auxiliary Systems

### Question 2: Differentiate between Series Hybrid and Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicles.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Series Hybrid (SHEV) | Parallel Hybrid (PHEV) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link between ICE and wheels | None (ICE only drives generator) | Direct (ICE + Motor both can drive wheels) |
| ICE size | Larger, runs at optimal point | Smaller, variable operating point |
| Motor size | Larger (sole traction source) | Smaller (assist function) |
| Mechanical losses | Higher (multiple energy conversions) | Lower (direct mechanical path) |
| Complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Best suited for | Urban stop-and-go | Highway + combined driving |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A: EV Powertrain Sizing and Tractive Force Calculation
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the tractive force equation for an electric vehicle moving with acceleration $a$ on an inclined road of slope angle $\theta$, considering rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and rotational inertia. Define each term clearly.

**Model Solution:**

Refer to Section 3.1 for the complete derivation. The examiner expects:
- Free-body diagram (mention forces in words: gravity, normal, drag, rolling, inertia).
- Decomposition of weight into $mg\sin\theta$ and $mg\cos\theta$.
- Expression of rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$
- Expression of aerodynamic drag: $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$
- Inclusion of rotational inertia factor: $F_a = m(1+k_m)a$
- Final equation: $F_t = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m(1+k_m)a$

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** An EV has the following specifications:
- Mass m = 1800 kg, $k_m$ = 0.10
- $\mu_{rr}$ = 0.013, $C_d$ = 0.30, A = 2.3 m²
- $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²
- Cruising speed v = 60 km/h = 16.67 m/s
- Slope angle $\theta$ = 5°

Calculate: (i) Rolling resistance, (ii) Aerodynamic drag, (iii) Gradient force, (iv) Tractive force at cruising speed (no acceleration).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit Conversions:**
$$v = 60 \,\text{km/h} = 60 \cdot \frac{1000}{3600} = 16.67 \,\text{m/s}$$
$$\cos 5° = 0.9962, \quad \sin 5° = 0.0872$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance:**
$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta = 0.013 \cdot 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9962$$
$$F_{rr} = 228.69 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Aerodynamic Drag:**
$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (16.67)^2$$
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.3 \cdot 277.89$$
$$F_{ad} = 117.42 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Gradient Force:**
$$F_g = m g \sin\theta = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0872$$
$$F_g = 1539.6 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Tractive Force at Steady Cruise (no acceleration):**
$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g = 228.69 + 117.42 + 1539.6$$
$$F_t = 1885.71 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Required Motor Power:**
$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{drivetrain}} = \dfrac{1885.71 \cdot 16.67}{0.90}$$
$$P_{motor} = 34{,}929 \,\text{W} \approx 34.93 \,\text{kW}$$

---

### QUESTION B: Battery Pack Sizing and Range Estimation
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the functions of a Battery Management System (BMS) in an Electric Vehicle. List at least six key BMS functions and explain the importance of cell balancing.

**Model Solution:**

A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an electronic control unit that monitors and manages the rechargeable battery pack to ensure safety, longevity, and optimal performance.

**Key BMS Functions:**
1. **Cell Voltage Monitoring** — Continuously samples each cell voltage to detect over-voltage or under-voltage conditions.
2. **Pack Current Sensing** — Uses Hall-effect or shunt resistors to measure charge/discharge currents.
3. **Temperature Monitoring** — Multiple NTC thermistors across the pack detect thermal runaway precursors.
4. **State of Charge (SOC) Estimation** — Coulomb counting + Kalman filter algorithms estimate remaining capacity.
5. **State of Health (SOH) Estimation** — Tracks degradation over the battery's life cycle.
6. **Cell Balancing** — Equalizes charge across all cells to prevent capacity mismatch.
7. **Thermal Management** — Controls cooling fans, liquid coolant pumps, or PTC heaters.
8. **Communication (CAN / LIN)** — Reports to VCU for power-limit arbitration.
9. **Safety Protection** — Triggers contactors open during fault events.

**Importance of Cell Balancing:**
In a series-connected pack, the weakest cell (lowest capacity) limits the entire pack's usable energy. Cell balancing ensures all cells reach the same SOC at full charge (top balancing) or same minimum SOC at discharge end (bottom balancing), thereby maximizing usable capacity and preventing over-charge/over-discharge damage.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** An EV requires a target range of 250 km at a constant cruising speed of 80 km/h on a level road. Vehicle specifications: m = 1600 kg, $\mu_{rr}$ = 0.011, $C_d$ = 0.27, A = 2.1 m², $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², $\eta_{drivetrain}$ = 0.88. Calculate the required battery pack energy capacity in kWh.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Units:**
$$v = 80 \,\text{km/h} = 22.22 \,\text{m/s}, \quad R = 250 \,\text{km} = 250{,}000 \,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rolling Resistance:**
$$F_{rr} = 0.011 \cdot 1600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(0) = 172.66 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag:**
$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.27 \cdot 2.1 \cdot (22.22)^2$$
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.27 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 493.83$$
$$F_{ad} = 170.97 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Sum Resistive Force:**
$$F_R = 172.66 + 170.97 = 343.63 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Energy at Wheels:**
$$E_{mech} = F_R \cdot R = 343.63 \cdot 250{,}000 = 8.591 \times 10^7 \,\text{J}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh:**
$$E_{mech} = \dfrac{8.591 \times 10^7}{3600 \cdot 1000} = 23.86 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Account for Drivetrain Efficiency:**
$$E_{batt} = \dfrac{23.86}{0.88} = 27.12 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 8 — Apply 80% Usable SOC Window:**
$$E_{pack} = \dfrac{27.12}{0.80} = 33.9 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Interpretation:** A 33.9 kWh battery pack is required for the target range.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors in EV Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the mass factor $(1+k_m)$:** When asked about acceleration force, students write $F_a = ma$ instead of $m(1+k_m)a$. Cost: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing units:** Writing speed in km/h directly into the drag formula $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$. Always convert to m/s first. Cost: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring drivetrain efficiency $\eta$:** Battery energy must be divided by $\eta$, not multiplied. Cost: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Series vs Parallel HEV:** In Series HEV, ICE never mechanically drives wheels. In Parallel, it can. Examiners test this distinction. Cost: **3 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping unit in final answer:** Always write units (N, kW, kWh). Cost: **0.5 mark per question**.
> 6. **Missing the gradient term:** On inclines, $mg\sin\theta$ dominates and frequently exceeds rolling + drag combined. Cost: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electric Vehicle (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** EV = vehicle propelled by electric motor(s) using onboard energy storage.
- **Classification:** BEV, HEV (Series / Parallel / Series-Parallel), PHEV, FCEV.
- **Series HEV:** ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels. ICE never directly drives wheels.
- **Parallel HEV:** ICE and Motor both can mechanically drive wheels via torque/speed summing device.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split):** Combines advantages of both; uses planetary gear (Toyota THS).
- **Tractive Force Equation:** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_a$ — always start here.
- **Rolling Resistance:** Depends on normal load: $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$.
- **Aerodynamic Drag:** Quadratic in velocity: $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$.
- **Gradient Force:** $F_g = mg\sin\theta$ — dominant on hills.
- **Acceleration Force:** Includes rotational inertia: $F_a = m(1+k_m)a$.
- **Battery Energy:** $E = V \times Q$ in Wh or kWh.
- **Pack Sizing:** Must account for $\eta_{drivetrain}$ and usable SOC window (80%).
- **Motor Power:** $P = T\omega$, wheel torque = motor torque $\times i_{gb} \times \eta_{gb}$.
- **Regenerative Braking:** Recovers kinetic energy as electrical energy to battery.
- **BMS Functions:** Voltage, current, temperature, SOC, SOH, balancing, thermal management, safety.
- **Cell Balancing:** Essential to maximize usable pack capacity and prevent cell damage.
- **Standard Constants:** g = 9.81 m/s², $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³.
- **Charging Levels:** Level 1 (≈ 1.4–1.9 kW), Level 2 (≈ 7.7–19.2 kW), Level 3 (50–350 kW DCFC).
- **Lithium-ion cell voltage:** ≈ 3.6–3.7 V nominal.
- **Energy Density:** Gasoline ≈ 12,000 Wh/kg vs. Li-ion ≈ 150–270 Wh/kg.
- **Always include units** in final numerical answers.
- **Always convert km/h to m/s** before using velocity in drag formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Vehicle: Series Hybrid Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Vehicle: Series Hybrid Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

A **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** is a powertrain architecture in which the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** is **mechanically decoupled** from the driving wheels. The ICE drives a **generator** (or generator/motor unit) to produce electrical energy, which is then used to charge a high-voltage **traction battery** and/or to feed an **electric traction motor** that is mechanically coupled to the wheels through a single fixed-ratio transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — OEMRT831 Module 3:**
> In a series hybrid, the engine has **NO mechanical path** to the road. ALL propulsion at the wheels is delivered electrically. This is the most "EV-like" of all hybrid topologies.

Mathematically, the power flow at any instant is governed by the fundamental series power balance:

$$P_{wheels} = P_{engine} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} - P_{aux} \pm P_{batt}$$

where $\eta_{gen}$, $\eta_{inv}$, and $\eta_{motor}$ are the efficiencies of the generator, power inverter, and traction motor respectively, $P_{aux}$ represents accessory loads, and $P_{batt}$ is the battery charge (negative) or discharge (positive) power.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diesel-Electric Locomotive" Principle

Imagine a **train locomotive** that you might have seen at a railway station. The huge diesel engine under the hood does **not turn the wheels directly**. Instead, the engine spins a generator that produces electricity, which then powers electric motors mounted on the axles. The train moves purely by electric traction, even though the prime mover is a diesel engine.

A **Series Hybrid Car** works on exactly the same principle — just scaled down:

| Locomotive Component | Series Hybrid Vehicle Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Diesel Engine | Gasoline / Diesel ICE |
| Main Generator | Generator (often integrated with motor as M/G) |
| Overhead Wires / Third Rail | High-Voltage Traction Battery (e.g., 200–400 V) |
| Traction Motors on Axles | Single (or dual) PMSM / Induction Traction Motor |
| Throttle | Accelerator Pedal → Electronic Control Unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of the ICE in a series hybrid as a **portable power plant** that happens to be carried along inside the car. Its job is to keep the "extension cord" (battery) topped up — not to push the car directly.

## 1.3 Why This Architecture Exists — Engineering Motivation

The ICE has a **narrow window of high efficiency** (typically a **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** band of 220–260 g/kWh for a modern gasoline engine). In a conventional car, the engine must operate across vastly different load/speed points as the driver accelerates, climbs hills, or idles — often at poor efficiency. A series hybrid **decouples engine speed from wheel speed**, allowing the engine to run at its **optimal operating point** (e.g., along the Best Fuel Curve) almost all the time. This is the central engineering justification for the SHEV architecture.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics to Remember

- **All-Electric Range (AER):** typically **30–80 km** for plug-in series hybrids.
- **Battery Voltage:** typically **200 V to 400 V** (modern systems trending to **800 V**).
- **Engine Power-to-Vehicle Mass Ratio:** engine sized for average, not peak, demand.
- **Two Energy Conversion Stages:** Chemical → Mechanical → Electrical → Mechanical (3 conversions), hence efficiency is critically dependent on each stage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Level Breakdown

A series hybrid powertrain consists of **five primary power-train components** connected by **two power electronics stages**:

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — runs on a constant optimal speed/load point; often a small-displacement Atkinson-cycle engine to maximize thermal efficiency.
2. **Generator (G)** — a three-phase AC machine; converts ICE mechanical output into electrical energy.
3. **Power Inverter / Rectifier** — converts AC from the generator into DC for the battery bus, and DC into AC for the traction motor.
4. **Traction Battery (B)** — high-voltage Li-ion pack with Battery Management System (BMS).
5. **Traction Motor (M)** — typically a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) or AC Induction Motor; drives the wheels through a single fixed-ratio reduction gearbox.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In modern series hybrids (e.g., Chevrolet Volt, Nissan e-POWER), the generator and traction motor are **physically separate machines**. In some Range-Extended EVs (REEV), the "engine-generator" set is called the **APU (Auxiliary Power Unit)**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component Decision

| Component | Why It Is There | Efficiency Range |
|---|---|---|
| ICE | Energy source with high energy density (kWh/kg) | **30% – 40%** |
| Generator | Decouples ICE from wheels, enables variable generation | **90% – 95%** |
| Inverter/Converter | DC-AC & AC-DC conversion with PWM control | **93% – 97%** |
| Traction Battery | Buffers transient demand, captures regen braking | **90% – 95%** round-trip |
| Traction Motor | Final conversion to mechanical torque at wheels | **90% – 95%** |

## 2.3 Operating Modes of a Series Hybrid

A series hybrid can operate in any of the following **five modes**, selected by the Hybrid Control Unit (HCU) / Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):

### Mode 1 — Pure Electric (EV Mode, Engine OFF)
- ICE is shut down.
- Battery alone powers the traction motor.
- Typical up to speeds of **~50–80 km/h** depending on battery SoC and load.

### Mode 2 — Engine-Only Series Mode (Battery SoC Low)
- ICE drives generator → electricity → traction motor.
- Battery acts as a passive buffer (charge-sustaining).
- ICE runs at its **optimal BSFC point**.

### Mode 3 — Combined Mode (Engine + Battery)
- ICE provides average power; battery provides peak power.
- This is the **most common** mode during hard acceleration.

### Mode 4 — Battery Charging Mode (Engine > Load)
- Excess ICE power charges the battery (e.g., during steady cruising downhill on flat road).

### Mode 5 — Regenerative Braking
- Traction motor acts as a generator; ICE is decoupled or off.
- Kinetic energy is converted back to electrical energy stored in the battery.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wheels} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{wheels}$ | Mechanical power at wheels | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $P_{ICE} = P_{wheels} / (\eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor})$ | ICE power required for a given road load | W |
| 3 | $P_{batt} = P_{gen} - P_{motor} - P_{aux}$ | Battery power balance (positive = discharge) | W |
| 4 | $\Delta SoC = \dfrac{\int P_{batt} \, dt}{E_{batt}} \cdot 100$ | State of Charge change | % |
| 5 | $V_{bus} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | Battery terminal voltage (simple model) | V |
| 6 | $T_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{\omega_{motor}}$ | Motor torque from power balance | N·m |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $F_{trac} = m g \mu_{rr} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m a$ | Road load equation (tractive force) | N |
| 9 | $P_{road} = F_{trac} \cdot v$ | Power required to overcome road load at speed $v$ | W |
| 10 | $\omega_{motor} = G \cdot \omega_{wheels}$ | Motor speed related to wheel speed (G = gear ratio) | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** The vertical line symbol $\vert$ in the LaTeX columns is the **mathematical "such that" / absolute value** symbol written as `\vert`. Never use the raw keyboard `|` inside a markdown table cell — it will break the table parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Series hybrid technology is the **dominant architecture** in:

- **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEV)** such as the Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1 & 2), BMW i3 REx, Fisker Karma.
- **Range-Extended Electric Vehicles (REEV)** such as the Nissan e-POWER system.
- **Heavy commercial vehicles** — series hybrid is favored for city buses (e.g., Proterra, BYD e-Bus with range extender) because of the stop-and-go duty cycle and the ability to size the engine for average, not peak, power.
- **Diesel-electric military vehicles** (e.g., the M1 Abrams tank is technically a series hybrid in concept).
- **Railway locomotives** (the original application, before automotive).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Flow Through a Series Hybrid Drivetrain

**Problem Setup:** A series hybrid car of mass **$m = 1500 \text{ kg}$** climbs a **5%** grade at a constant speed of **$v = 60 \text{ km/h}$**. The combined drivetrain efficiency from engine to wheels is $\eta_{ICE-mech} = 0.35$ (ICE), $\eta_{gen} = 0.93$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$. Compute the engine power required, the fuel consumption rate, and the traction motor current draw.

**Given:**
- $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- Grade $= 5\% \Rightarrow \sin\theta \approx 0.05$ (small-angle approx.)
- $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$ (rolling resistance coefficient)
- $C_d = 0.29$, $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $V_{bus} = 360 \text{ V}$ (DC bus)
- Lower heating value of gasoline $\text{LHV} = 42.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$, density $\rho_f = 0.745 \text{ kg/L}$

**Step 1 — Compute Tractive Force Required:**

The road load equation consists of three terms: rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and grade climbing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{trac} &= F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} \\
F_{roll} &= m \cdot g \cdot \mu_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta \\
&\approx 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 1.000 \quad (\cos\theta \approx 1 \text{ for small }\theta) \\
F_{roll} &= 176.58 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.29 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (16.67)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.29 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 277.89 \\
F_{aero} &= 108.65 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{grade} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \\
&= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 \\
F_{grade} &= 735.75 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{trac} &= 176.58 + 108.65 + 735.75 = 1020.98 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Power at the Wheels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheels} &= F_{trac} \cdot v \\
&= 1020.98 \cdot 16.67 \\
P_{wheels} &= 17{,}019.7 \text{ W} \approx 17.02 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Power at Each Conversion Stage (working backward from wheels to ICE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor,out} &= P_{wheels} = 17.02 \text{ kW} \\
P_{motor,in} &= P_{motor,out} / \eta_{motor} = 17.02 / 0.92 = 18.50 \text{ kW} \\
P_{inv,out} &= P_{motor,in} = 18.50 \text{ kW} \\
P_{inv,in} &= P_{inv,out} / \eta_{inv} = 18.50 / 0.95 = 19.47 \text{ kW} \\
P_{gen,out} &= P_{inv,in} = 19.47 \text{ kW} \\
P_{gen,in} &= P_{gen,out} / \eta_{gen} = 19.47 / 0.93 = 20.94 \text{ kW} \\
P_{ICE,out} &= P_{gen,in} = 20.94 \text{ kW} \\
P_{ICE,in} &= P_{ICE,out} / \eta_{ICE} = 20.94 / 0.35 = 59.82 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Fuel Consumption Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= P_{ICE,in} / \text{LHV} \\
&= 59{,}820 / 42{,}500{,}000 \\
\dot{m}_f &= 1.408 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \\[4pt]
\dot{V}_f &= \dot{m}_f / \rho_f = 1.408 \times 10^{-3} / 0.745 \\
\dot{V}_f &= 1.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/s} = 6.80 \text{ L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute DC Bus Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{bus} &= P_{inv,in} / V_{bus} \\
&= 19{,}470 / 360 \\
I_{bus} &= 54.08 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** Overall drivetrain efficiency $= 17.02 / 59.82 = 0.284 = 28.4\%$. This is realistic for a series hybrid under load (compared to a conventional car which might be ~18–22% at this point). Series hybrids are more efficient *on average* but pay a "two-conversion" tax — this trade-off is a frequent KTU question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery State of Charge (SoC) Drift Over a Drive Cycle

**Problem Setup:** A series hybrid travels a **city-bus drive cycle** of total duration $T = 1800 \text{ s}$ (30 min). The net battery power over the cycle (charge = negative, discharge = positive) varies. Compute the SoC change given a battery capacity of $E_{batt} = 12 \text{ kWh}$.

For simplicity, approximate the battery power profile in three segments:

| Segment | Duration (s) | $P_{batt}$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Acceleration (EV mode) | 0 to 600 | $+15$ (discharge) |
| 2 — Steady cruise (engine charging) | 600 to 1200 | $-5$ (charge) |
| 3 — Deceleration (regen) | 1200 to 1800 | $-8$ (charge) |

**Step 1 — Compute Energy In/Out for Each Segment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= P_{batt,1} \cdot t_1 = 15 \cdot 600 = 9{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
E_2 &= P_{batt,2} \cdot t_2 = (-5) \cdot 600 = -3{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
E_3 &= P_{batt,3} \cdot t_3 = (-8) \cdot 600 = -4{,}800 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Energy Change:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{batt} &= E_1 + E_2 + E_3 \\
&= 9{,}000 - 3{,}000 - 4{,}800 \\
\Delta E_{batt} &= 1{,}200 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{batt} &= 1{,}200 / 3600 = 0.333 \text{ kWh (net discharge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SoC Change:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SoC &= \dfrac{\Delta E_{batt}}{E_{batt}} \cdot 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.333}{12} \cdot 100 \\
\Delta SoC &= 2.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Comment:** Note the **sign convention** is critical. Always declare whether positive $P_{batt}$ means discharge or charge. KTU valuations will deduct 1 mark for ambiguous sign usage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

The following matrix captures the **design trade-offs** of a series hybrid versus other architectures — a frequent KTU Part B (14 mark) question pattern.

| Parameter | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid | Series-Parallel (PSHEV) | Battery EV (BEV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link ICE–Wheels | **None** | Yes (via clutch/gear) | Yes (via PSD*) | None |
| Engine always at best BSFC? | **Yes** | No | Mostly | N/A (no ICE) |
| Number of energy conversions | 3 (mech→elec→mech) | 1 (mech→mech) | 1 or 3 (mode dep.) | 1 (elec→mech) |
| Drivetrain efficiency | Moderate (~25–32%) | **High (~32–38%)** | High | Very High (~85–90%) |
| Transmission complexity | **Very Low** (1 gear) | High (multi-gear) | Very High | Very Low (1 gear) |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate | **High** | High (battery) |
| Best use case | City buses, PHEV | Highway sedans | SUVs, luxury cars | Short urban trips |
| Example | Chevy Volt, BMW i3 REx | Honda Insight (1st gen) | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape | Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV |

*PSD = Power Split Device (planetary gearset)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Optimal Engine Operating Point (Charge-Sustaining Mode)

**Problem Setup:** A series hybrid must cruise at a constant $P_{road} = 12 \text{ kW}$. If the engine is operated at its **best BSFC point** delivering $P_{ICE,opt} = 18 \text{ kW}$ (mechanical), find the battery power.

**Step 1 — Power Available at Generator Output:**

$$
P_{gen,out} = P_{ICE,opt} \cdot \eta_{gen} = 18 \cdot 0.93 = 16.74 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Power Reaching Inverter Input:**

$$
P_{inv,in} = P_{gen,out} = 16.74 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Power Reaching Motor Input:**

$$
P_{motor,in} = P_{inv,in} = 16.74 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power at Wheels:**

$$
P_{wheels} = P_{motor,in} \cdot \eta_{motor} = 16.74 \cdot 0.92 = 15.40 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Surplus Power to Battery:**

$$
P_{batt,charge} = P_{wheels} - P_{road} = 15.40 - 12.00 = 3.40 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The engine is **over-sized for the instantaneous load** so that it can stay on its best-BSFC curve. Excess energy is stored in the battery. This is the essence of the **"charge-sustaining" control strategy** that distinguishes hybrid from pure EV operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series Hybrid Vehicle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete power-flow architecture of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle, including all power electronics, the bidirectional battery, and the five operating modes. The **engine has no mechanical arrow to the wheels** — this is the defining feature of a series hybrid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ICE Internal Combustion Engine] -->|Mechanical shaft| B[GEN Three-phase Generator]
    B -->|Three-phase AC| C[Rectifier AC to DC]
    C -->|DC Bus 360V| D[DC Link Capacitor]
    D -->|DC Bus| E[INV Traction Inverter DC to AC]
    D <-->|Bidirectional DC| F[HV Battery Li-ion pack with BMS]
    E -->|Three-phase AC PWM| G[MOT Traction Motor PMSM or IM]
    G -->|Single fixed ratio gearbox| H[WHEELS Drive Wheels]
    H -.->|Regen energy on braking| G
    I[VCU Vehicle Control Unit] -->|CAN Bus| A
    I -->|CAN Bus| B
    I -->|CAN Bus| E
    I -->|CAN Bus| F
    I -->|CAN Bus| G
    J[Accelerator Pedal] -->|Sensor signal| I
    K[Brake Pedal] -->|Sensor signal| I
    L[12V Aux Battery] -->|Powers| I
    M[DC DC Converter] -->|Steps 360V down to 12V| L
    F -->|Feeds 360V| M
```

## 4.2 Power Flow Modes — State Machine Diagram

This state machine illustrates the **five operating modes** of a series hybrid and the conditions that trigger mode transitions. The states use safe alphanumeric identifiers prefixed with `mode`.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> mode1EV
    mode1EV --> mode3Combined : SoC low and high torque demand
    mode1EV --> mode4Charge : SoC low and low torque demand
    mode3Combined --> mode2Series : SoC low and steady demand
    mode2Series --> mode3Combined : Sudden acceleration
    mode2Series --> mode4Charge : SoC critically low
    mode2Series --> mode1EV : SoC high
    mode4Charge --> mode2Series : SoC reaches target
    mode3Combined --> mode1EV : SoC high and demand low
    mode3Combined --> mode5Regen : Brake pedal pressed
    mode2Series --> mode5Regen : Brake pedal pressed
    mode1EV --> mode5Regen : Brake pedal pressed
    mode5Regen --> mode1EV : Brake released and SoC less than max
    mode5Regen --> mode2Series : Brake released and SoC greater than max
    mode1EV --> [*] : Vehicle OFF
    mode2Series --> [*] : Vehicle OFF
```

## 4.3 Energy Conversion Sequence (Visual Cascade)

The defining feature of a series hybrid is that energy passes through **THREE conversions** before reaching the wheels. The following Mermaid diagram makes this explicit:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chem[Chemical energy in fuel] -->|Combustion| mech1[Mechanical energy at ICE crankshaft]
    mech1 -->|Rotation| elec1[Electrical energy at generator terminals]
    elec1 -->|DC bus| elec2[Electrical energy at traction motor terminals]
    elec2 -->|Electromagnetic torque| mech2[Mechanical energy at wheels]
    mech2 -->|Vehicle motion| kin[Kinetic energy of vehicle]
    kin -.->|Braking| regen[Electrical energy regenerated to battery]
    regen -.-> elec2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This triple-conversion chain is **why** series hybrids lose some efficiency at high steady speeds (where single-conversion parallel hybrids win) but **gain** efficiency in stop-and-go city traffic (where the engine can shut off and regen captures braking energy).

## 4.4 Component-Level Power Flow with Energy Management Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph drivetrain[Powertrain]
        ICE[ICE Engine]
        GEN[Generator]
        BATT[HV Battery]
        INV[Motor Inverter]
        MOT[Traction Motor]
        WHL[Drive Wheels]
    end
    subgraph control[Control Layer]
        VCU[VCU Hybrid Control Unit]
        BMS[BMS Battery Management]
        ECU[Engine ECU]
        MCU[Motor Control Unit]
    end
    ICE -- Shaft --> GEN
    GEN -- 3 phase AC --> INV
    BATT -- DC bus --> INV
    INV -- 3 phase AC --> MOT
    MOT -- Single ratio gearbox --> WHL
    VCU -- Throttle command --> ECU
    VCU -- Torque request --> MCU
    VCU -- SoC setpoint --> BMS
    BMS -- SoC feedback --> VCU
    ECU -- Engine status --> VCU
    MCU -- Motor status --> VCU
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**"Differentiate between a series hybrid and a parallel hybrid vehicle with respect to engine-wheel coupling and drivetrain efficiency."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| Engine–Wheel Coupling | **No mechanical coupling** (engine decoupled from wheels) | **Direct mechanical coupling** via clutch/gears |
| Energy Conversions | 3 (mech → elec → mech) | 1 (mech → mech) |
| Drivetrain Efficiency | **Lower (~25–32%)** due to conversion losses | **Higher (~32–38%)** |
| Engine Operating Point | **Always at best BSFC** (independent of speed) | Varies with road speed (sub-optimal at low loads) |
| Best Application | **Stop-and-go city** (buses, taxis) | **Highway cruising** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "series hybrid is more efficient" without qualifying **"in which duty cycle?"** Always state the context (city vs highway) to earn full marks.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"List the major components of a series hybrid powertrain and state the function of the generator."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major components of a series hybrid powertrain are:
1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — fuel-to-mechanical energy conversion.
2. **Generator (G)** — converts mechanical energy from ICE into **three-phase AC electrical energy**.
3. **Power Inverter/Rectifier** — converts AC to DC and DC to AC.
4. **Traction Battery** — stores electrical energy and buffers load.
5. **Traction Motor** — converts electrical energy into mechanical torque to drive the wheels.
6. **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU/VCU)** — coordinates engine start/stop, generator excitation, and motor torque demand.

**Function of Generator:** The generator is mechanically coupled to the ICE and acts as the **electrical power source** for the drivetrain. It enables the ICE to run at its **optimal BSFC point** regardless of vehicle speed, by converting the ICE's mechanical output into regulated DC bus power (after rectification) that charges the battery and/or feeds the traction inverter.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and operating principle of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**. Discuss all **five operating modes** with their power flow paths. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle is one in which the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) has no mechanical connection to the drive wheels**. The ICE drives a generator that produces electrical energy, which is used to charge a traction battery and/or to power an electric traction motor coupled to the wheels through a single fixed-ratio transmission.

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The block diagram consists of the following components connected in series:
ICE → Generator → Rectifier → DC Bus → (Bidirectional link to) HV Battery → Traction Inverter → Traction Motor → Single-Ratio Gearbox → Wheels.
The Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) supervises all components via CAN bus, receiving inputs from the accelerator pedal, brake pedal, and battery SoC sensor.

**Five Operating Modes (4 marks — 1 mark each concise description):**

1. **Pure EV Mode:** Engine OFF. Battery alone supplies traction motor. Used at low speeds and during light load with adequate SoC. Power flow: $BATT \to INV \to MOT \to WHEELS$.

2. **Engine-Only Series Mode (Charge Sustaining):** Engine drives generator, all generated electricity powers motor, battery acts as passive buffer. Power flow: $ICE \to GEN \to INV \to MOT \to WHEELS$, with minor branch $BATT \leftrightarrow DC$ bus.

3. **Combined Mode (Engine + Battery):** Engine provides average power; battery delivers peak power for acceleration. Power flow: $ICE \to GEN \to INV \to MOT$ (in parallel with $BATT \to INV$).

4. **Battery Charging Mode:** Engine power exceeds traction demand; surplus charges battery. Power flow: $ICE \to GEN \to INV \to BATT$ (in parallel with $INV \to MOT \to WHEELS$).

5. **Regenerative Braking:** Traction motor acts as a generator; ICE is off or decoupled. Power flow: $WHEELS \to MOT \to INV \to BATT$ (battery charging).

**Conclusion:** The series hybrid is best suited for **city duty cycles** with frequent stops, where regen and engine shut-off maximize fuel economy.

---

**Q. (b)** A series hybrid vehicle of mass **$m = 1800 \text{ kg}$** travels at a constant speed of **$v = 90 \text{ km/h}$** on a flat road. Given: $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, drivetrain efficiency chain: $\eta_{ICE} = 0.36$, $\eta_{gen} = 0.94$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.93$. Compute: **(i)** tractive force, **(ii)** power at wheels, **(iii)** engine fuel power input, and **(iv)** fuel consumption in L/100 km. Assume $\text{LHV} = 43 \text{ MJ/kg}$ and $\rho_{fuel} = 0.75 \text{ kg/L}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $v = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$, flat road ($\sin\theta = 0$).

**(i) Tractive Force (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{trac} &= m g \mu_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \\
&= 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.010 + 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (25)^2 \\
&= 176.58 + 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.3 \cdot 625 \\
&= 176.58 + 258.75 \\
F_{trac} &= 435.33 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power at Wheels (1 mark):**

$$
P_{wheels} = F_{trac} \cdot v = 435.33 \cdot 25 = 10{,}883.25 \text{ W} \approx 10.88 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Engine Fuel Power Input (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ICE,fuel} &= P_{wheels} / (\eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}) \\
&= 10{,}883.25 / (0.36 \cdot 0.94 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.93) \\
&= 10{,}883.25 / 0.2987 \\
P_{ICE,fuel} &= 36{,}433 \text{ W} \approx 36.43 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Fuel Consumption in L/100 km (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= P_{ICE,fuel} / \text{LHV} = 36{,}433 / 43{,}000{,}000 = 8.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{V}_f &= \dot{m}_f / \rho_{fuel} = 8.47 \times 10^{-4} / 0.75 = 1.13 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/s} \\
\dot{V}_f \text{ in L/h} &= 1.13 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600 = 4.066 \text{ L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Per 100 km at 90 km/h, time taken = $100/90$ h = $1.111$ h.

$$
\text{Fuel} = 4.066 \cdot 1.111 = 4.52 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistake: students forget to convert **90 km/h to 25 m/s** when computing $F_{trac} \cdot v$. This alone loses **1 mark**. Always carry units in your calculation to self-audit.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the **energy management strategies** used in a series hybrid vehicle. Compare **thermostat (on-off) control** with **power-follower control** and state which is more fuel-efficient and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The energy management strategy (EMS) is the algorithm in the VCU that decides how to split power between the engine and the battery. In a series hybrid, the EMS has full authority to set engine on/off and engine power level, since the engine is decoupled from the wheels.

**Thermostat (On-Off) Control (3 marks):**
- The engine is **switched ON** when the battery SoC drops below a **lower threshold** (e.g., 30%) and runs at a **single fixed operating point** delivering a **constant power** until SoC reaches an **upper threshold** (e.g., 70%).
- The engine is then switched OFF and the vehicle runs in pure EV mode until SoC falls again.
- **Hysteresis band** prevents frequent engine cycling.
- **Advantages:** Very simple to implement; engine always at best BSFC.
- **Disadvantages:** Sudden torque transients when engine switches on/off; battery experiences large SoC swings; poor driveability.

**Power-Follower Control (2 marks):**
- The engine is **always ON** (or follows a minimum SoC threshold) and its output is continuously varied to **track the average road load power** plus a small charging offset to maintain target SoC.
- A **low-pass filter** on the driver's torque request extracts the average demand; transients are absorbed by the battery.
- **Advantages:** Smoother drive; smaller battery SoC swings; better transient response.
- **Disadvantages:** More complex; engine may deviate from best BSFC during rapid load changes.

**Comparison and Conclusion (1 mark):**
The **thermostat strategy is theoretically more fuel-efficient** in steady-state because the engine never deviates from its best BSFC point. However, in real-world transient operation, the **power-follower strategy is preferred** for driveability and battery life. Most production SHEVs (e.g., Chevy Volt) use an **adaptive blend** of both — called **"Adaptive Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (A-ECMS)"** or **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)**-based optimal control.

---

**Q. (b)** A series hybrid has the following component efficiencies when the engine is on: $\eta_{ICE} = 0.38$, $\eta_{gen} = 0.92$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.94$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.91$. A **60 kW** engine running at its **best BSFC point** is used. The vehicle is cruising on a highway requiring a constant **$P_{road} = 25 \text{ kW}$** at the wheels. The battery is currently at **SoC = 65%** and the VCU wants to **charge-sustain at 60%**. Determine: **(i)** power available at the wheels from the engine, **(ii)** surplus power to the battery, and **(iii)** the time required to charge-sustain back to 60% if the SoC drops to 55% with the same surplus. Battery capacity $E_{batt} = 8 \text{ kWh}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Power Available at Wheels from Engine (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheels,engine} &= P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \\
&= 60 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.94 \cdot 0.91 \\
&= 60 \cdot 0.7871 \\
P_{wheels,engine} &= 47.22 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Surplus Power to Battery (2 marks):**

$$
P_{batt,charge} = P_{wheels,engine} - P_{road} = 47.22 - 25 = 22.22 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Time to Charge from 55% to 60% SoC (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SoC &= 60\% - 55\% = 5\% \\
\Delta E_{batt} &= \Delta SoC \cdot E_{batt} / 100 \\
&= 0.05 \cdot 8 = 0.4 \text{ kWh} \\
\Delta E_{batt} &= 0.4 \cdot 3600 = 1440 \text{ kJ} = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ J} \\[4pt]
t &= \Delta E_{batt} / P_{batt,charge} = 1{,}440{,}000 / 22{,}220 = 64.8 \text{ s} \approx 1.08 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students make the **error of multiplying efficiencies instead of dividing** while going from wheels back to engine. Always remember: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta \Rightarrow P_{in} = P_{out} / \eta$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Series Hybrid Vehicle (SHEV)**

- [x] **SHEV = ICE + Generator + Battery + Motor + Inverter** in a **strictly series** power flow chain.
- [x] **No mechanical link** exists between ICE and wheels — the ICE is essentially a **portable power plant** on board.
- [x] **Three energy conversions**: Chemical → Mechanical → Electrical → Mechanical (3 stages, hence $\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 \eta_2 \eta_3$).
- [x] **Five operating modes**: Pure EV, Engine-Only Series, Combined (Engine + Battery), Battery Charging, Regenerative Braking.
- [x] **Engine runs at constant best BSFC point** (~220–260 g/kWh for gasoline) regardless of vehicle speed — this is the **core design advantage**.
- [x] **Total drivetrain efficiency** of SHEV is typically **25–32%** under load, lower than parallel hybrid (~32–38%) due to conversion losses.
- [x] **Best application:** Stop-and-go city driving (buses, taxis, delivery vans) and plug-in PHEVs.
- [x] **Power flow equation:** $P_{ICE,fuel} = P_{wheels} / (\eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor})$.
- [x] **Battery power balance:** $P_{batt} = P_{gen} - P_{motor,elec,in} - P_{aux}$ (sign convention matters!).
- [x] **Energy management strategies:** Thermostat (on-off) vs. Power-Follower — most modern SHEVs use an adaptive blend.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Chevrolet Volt, BMW i3 REx, Nissan e-POWER, Proterra buses.
- [x] **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Differentiate series vs parallel hybrid" (always state duty cycle) and "Compute engine fuel power for given road load" (always work from wheels to engine using division by efficiency).
- [x] **Sign convention reminder:** Positive $P_{batt}$ = discharge; negative = charge. Always declare at the start of the solution.
- [x] **Key constants to remember:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\text{LHV}_{gasoline} \approx 42$–$44 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $\rho_{gasoline} \approx 0.74$–$0.75 \text{ kg/L}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Hybrid Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parallel Hybrid Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
A **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** is a hybrid powertrain architecture in which the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Motor (EM)** are mechanically coupled to the driveline in such a way that **both power sources can deliver tractive effort to the wheels either independently or simultaneously**. The two energy converters share a common mechanical path (typically via a clutch, gearbox, or planetary gearset), so their torques add up algebraically at the transmission input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A parallel hybrid is defined by the *parallel* mechanical arrangement of the ICE and EM, in contrast to a *series* hybrid where the EM alone drives the wheels and the ICE acts purely as a range extender/auxiliary power unit.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Horses Cart
Imagine a cart being pulled by **two horses harnessed side-by-side** to the same axle:
- Horse A = the **ICE** (powerful, but slow to start and inefficient at low load).
- Horse B = the **Electric Motor** (instant torque, silent, but limited by battery stamina).
- The **driver (Hybrid Control Unit)** decides which horse(s) to engage based on the road condition.

For a steep climb ➜ both horses pull together (Hybrid Mode).
For slow city traffic ➜ only Horse B pulls (EV Mode).
For highway cruising ➜ only Horse A pulls (ICE Mode).
While going downhill ➜ Horse B is re-energised by the cart's motion (Regenerative Braking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier:** The word "parallel" refers to the **mechanical** topology, not the electrical one. The ICE and EM sit in *parallel* branches of the mechanical power path.

### Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Parallel Hybrids

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Battery Pack Voltage | $V_b$ | **144 V – 400 V** |
| State of Charge | $SOC$ | **30 % – 80 %** (operational window) |
| ICE Specific Power | — | **0.5 – 1.0 kW/kg** |
| Motor Peak Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | **90 % – 95 %** |
| ICE Peak Efficiency | $\eta_{ICE}$ | **35 % – 42 %** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture — The Mechanical Coupling

In a parallel hybrid, the ICE and EM are typically coupled through **one of three** arrangements:

1. **Pre-transmission coupling (most common)** — both machines connect to the input shaft of the gearbox via a clutch.
2. **Post-transmission coupling** — torque combiner after the gearbox.
3. **Through a planetary gearset** (used in systems like Honda IMA / Toyota HSD where the "split" is partial).

### 2.2 Operating Modes of a Parallel Hybrid

A KTU examiner expects the student to list and explain **all six operating modes**:

| Mode | ICE | Motor | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. ICE-Only Drive** | ON, propelling | OFF | Highway cruising; high-speed steady load. |
| **2. EV-Only Drive** | OFF | ON, propelling | Low-speed urban driving; zero tail-pipe emission. |
| **3. Hybrid (Combined) Drive** | ON, propelling | ON, propelling | Hard acceleration / hill climb — torques add. |
| **4. Battery Charging Mode** | ON, propelling | ON as **Generator** | ICE produces extra power to charge battery. |
| **5. Regenerative Braking** | OFF | ON as **Generator** | Vehicle kinetic energy → electrical energy. |
| **6. Idle Stop-Start** | OFF | OFF | Vehicle stationary; ICE shut down to save fuel. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Parallel"?** In mode 3, the ICE torque and Motor torque *add* on the same shaft. In a Series hybrid, the ICE torque **never reaches the wheels** directly — this is the textbook distinguishing feature.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of plain `\` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{wheels} = i_g \, (T_{ICE} + T_{motor})$ | Wheel torque from combined sources | Nm |
| 2 | $P_{wheels} = T_{wheels} \cdot \omega_{wheels}$ | Mechanical power at wheels | W |
| 3 | $P_{ICE} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV$ | ICE power from fuel | W |
| 4 | $P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor}$ | Electric motor mechanical power | W |
| 5 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{E_{bat}} \int_{0}^{t} (P_{gen} - P_{motor}) \, d\tau$ | Battery state-of-charge evolution | — |
| 6 | $E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Vehicle kinetic energy (basis for regen) | J |
| 7 | $P_{regen,max} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \, v^{2} / \Delta t$ | Theoretical regenerative power limit | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel} + P_{elec}}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | — |

Where:
- $i_g$ = gearbox ratio
- $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $LHV$ = lower heating value of fuel (≈ **43 MJ/kg** for petrol)
- $E_{bat}$ = battery energy capacity (J)
- $\eta_{ICE}$ = brake specific fuel consumption efficiency
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $v$ = vehicle velocity (m/s)

### 2.4 Why Parallel Hybrids Are Used — Engineering Justification
- **No double energy conversion** (unlike series hybrids where mechanical → electrical → mechanical). Hence **higher efficiency on highways**.
- ICE can operate near its **sweet spot** with motor assist during transients.
- Mechanical path is shorter → **lower mass, lower cost** vs. series hybrids with two large motors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Torque Combination at the Transmission Input

Let:
- $T_{ICE}$ = torque produced by the ICE at its output shaft
- $T_{motor}$ = torque produced by the electric motor
- Both shafts connected through a **clutch / coupler** to a common input shaft feeding the gearbox.

**Step 1 — Mechanical Coupling Equation**

Because both torques act on the *same rotational node* (the input shaft), by rotational equilibrium:

$$T_{in} = T_{ICE} + T_{motor}$$

where $T_{in}$ is the torque entering the gearbox input shaft.

**Step 2 — Transmission Multiplication**

The gearbox multiplies torque by the gear ratio $i_g$. After the differential with ratio $i_0$, the torque at the wheels becomes:

$$T_{wheels} = (T_{ICE} + T_{motor}) \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

**Step 3 — Power at the Wheels**

Using rotational power $P = T\omega$, and recognising that all rotating elements share the same angular velocity at the input shaft, $\omega_{in}$:

$$\boxed{P_{wheels} = (T_{ICE} + T_{motor}) \cdot \omega_{in} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{trans}}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Conservation Interpretation**

This shows the **additive nature of torques**, which is the *defining* mechanical signature of the parallel architecture. In contrast, a series hybrid would give:

$$T_{wheels} = T_{generator} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

because the ICE only drives a generator and **never directly contributes torque to the wheels**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Step 1 — Vehicle kinetic energy at speed $v$**

$$E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$
