where $n = 10$ bits, $V_{ref,ADC} = +5.0\ \text{V}$.

$$N_{ADC} = \dfrac{3.0}{5.0} \cdot 1023 = 0.6 \cdot 1023 = 613.8 \approx 614$$

So the digital representation is **614 / 1023** of full scale.

---

### Step 4 — Microcontroller Algorithm (Mapping to PWM)

The microcontroller maps the digital reading to a PWM duty cycle using a proportional rule:

$$D = \dfrac{N_{ADC}}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{614}{1023} \approx 0.600$$

Hence **60 % duty cycle**.

If the PWM switching period is $T_{PWM} = 1\ \text{ms}$ (i.e., $f_{PWM} = 1\ \text{kHz}$):

$$t_{on} = D \cdot T_{PWM} = 0.600 \cdot 1\ \text{ms} = 600\ \mu s$$

$$t_{off} = T_{PWM} - t_{on} = 1000\ \mu s - 600\ \mu s = 400\ \mu s$$

---

### Step 5 — H-Bridge Driver Output to Actuator

The H-bridge driver produces an average motor voltage:

$$V_{motor,avg} = D \cdot V_{batt}$$

With a loaded $V_{batt} = +13.8\ \text{V}$ (engine running):

$$V_{motor,avg} = 0.600 \cdot 13.8 = +8.28\ \text{V}$$

Driver power dissipation (assuming $R_{DS(on)} = 50\ \text{m}\Omega$ and $I_{motor} = 2\ \text{A}$ per switch):

$$P_{driver} = I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} = (2)^2 \cdot 0.05 = 0.20\ \text{W}$$

---

### Step 6 — System Response Time (Real-Time Constraint)

The total loop time must be within the ECU scheduler budget (typically 1–10 ms). Summing the delays:

$$t_{loop} = t_{sensor} + t_{filter} + t_{ADC} + t_{compute} + t_{PWM} + t_{driver}$$

A realistic budget is:

$$t_{loop} \approx 0.5 + 0.1 + 0.005 + 0.05 + 0.001 + 0.05 = 0.706\ \text{ms}$$

This is well within the **< 1 ms** budget for engine-speed control — satisfying real-time automotive constraints.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Function-to-Component Mapping

| Functional Block | Typical Hardware Components | KTU-Favored Example |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | TPS, MAP, MAF, O₂ sensor, knock sensor | NTC thermistor for coolant temp |
| Signal Conditioning | Op-amp (LM358), RC filter, Zener clamp | Unity-gain buffer + RC LPF |
| Processing | 32-bit automotive MCU | NXP S32K144 / Infineon TC275 |
| Driver | MOSFET H-bridge, opto-isolator, Darlington | BTS7960 (43 A H-bridge IC) |
| Power Supply | Buck/boost converter, TVS diode, LDO | LM2596 buck + LM7805 LDO |
| Communication | CAN transceiver, LIN slave, bus isolator | TJA1050 CAN transceiver |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark answers**, draw the block diagram first (≈ 6 marks), then explain **each block** with at least one component example and one governing equation (≈ 8 marks). This guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Automotive ECU

Below is a **Mermaid functional architecture flow** of the Basic Structure of an automotive electronic control system. This diagram is **board-ready** for KTU answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery 12V or 24V] --> B[Power Supply Stage\nReverse Polarity Protection\nBuck Regulator and LDO]
    B --> C1[+5V Rail]
    B --> C2[+3.3V Rail]
    B --> C3[+1.8V Rail]

    S1[Sensor 1\nMAP Pressure] --> SC[Signal Conditioning\nAmplifier Filter Clamp]
    S2[Sensor 2\nMAF Airflow] --> SC
    S3[Sensor 3\nNTC Coolant Temp] --> SC
    S4[Sensor 4\nO2 Lambda] --> SC

    SC --> MCU[Automotive Microcontroller\n32 bit MCU with ADC PWM Timer]
    MCU --> DRV[Driver Stage\nH Bridge MOSFET Darlington]
    DRV --> ACT1[Actuator 1\nFuel Injector]
    DRV --> ACT2[Actuator 2\nThrottle Motor]
    DRV --> ACT3[Actuator 3\nSolenoid Valve]

    MCU --> COMM[Communication Stage\nCAN LIN FlexRay]
    COMM --> ECU2[Other Vehicle ECUs]
    COMM --> DIAG[OBD II Diagnostic Port]

    WDT[Watchdog Timer] --> MCU
    BOR[Brown Out Reset] --> MCU
```

## 4.2 Layered Software Stack (AUTOSAR-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer\nASW Software Components]
    L2[Runtime Environment RTE\nMessage Passing Interface]
    L3[Basic Software BSW\nServices OS Memory Com]
    L4[Microcontroller Abstraction Layer MCAL\nADC PWM Driver Port]
    L5[Microcontroller Hardware\n32 bit Automotive MCU]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Feedback Flow (Control-Engineering View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Input\nDriver Demand] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    FBK[Feedback Signal\nfrom Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller\nMCU with PID Algorithm]
    CTRL --> PLANT[Plant\nEngine or Motor or Brake]
    PLANT --> OUT[Controlled Output\nSpeed or Torque or Pressure]
    PLANT --> SNS[Sensor]
    SNS --> FBK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A`, `S1`, `MCU`, `WDT`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs.
> - Inside double-quoted labels, no markdown bold (`**`), italics, or HTML tags are present — only raw uppercase alphanumeric text and line breaks.

## 4.4 Physical Realization (Functional-to-Physical Map)

| Logical Block | Physical Realization in a Real Vehicle |
|---|---|
| Sensor | Bosch MAP sensor mounted on intake manifold |
| Signal Conditioning | PCB layer inside ECU housing |
| MCU | Sealed under EMC shield on main PCB |
| Driver | Power PCB with TO-220 MOSFETs and heat sink |
| Power Supply | Separate SMPS module near battery |
| Communication | Twisted-pair CAN bus running through chassis harness |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six functional blocks of a basic automotive electronic control unit (ECU).**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Sensor / Input Stage** – converts physical quantities to electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning Stage** – amplification, filtering, level shifting.
3. **Microcontroller / Processing Stage** – executes control algorithms.
4. **Actuator Driver Stage** – H-bridge / MOSFET drivers.
5. **Power Supply Stage** – regulators, reverse-polarity protection, TVS clamps.
6. **Communication Stage** – CAN, LIN, FlexRay transceivers.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an Electronic Control Unit. State its primary function in a modern automobile.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** An Electronic Control Unit (ECU) is a microcontroller-based embedded system that processes real-time sensor data and drives actuators to control a specific vehicle subsystem.
- **Primary Function:** To execute closed-loop control algorithms ensuring optimal engine performance, emissions compliance, fuel economy, safety, and driver comfort — typically within a **< 10 ms** response time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**a)** Draw the basic block diagram of an automotive ECU and explain the function of **each block** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **basic structure of a microcontroller-based engine management system**. List the major sensors and actuators used. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram of an Automotive ECU (7 marks)

**Block diagram** (drawn in answer sheet — refer to **Section 4.1** above for the reference architecture).

**Explanation of each block:**

1. **Power Supply Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - Takes **+12 V / +24 V** from the vehicle battery.
   - Provides regulated **+5 V, +3.3 V, +1.8 V** rails to digital and analog circuitry.
   - Includes reverse-battery (P-MOSFET), load-dump (TVS), and brown-out protection.
   - Linear regulators (LM7805) for low-current rails; switching regulators (LM2596) for high-current rails (> 85 % efficiency).

2. **Sensor / Input Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - MAP, MAF, O₂ (lambda), crankshaft position (CKP), camshaft position (CMP), knock, coolant temperature (NTC), throttle position (TPS).
   - Both active and passive sensors.

3. **Signal Conditioning Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - Op-amp amplifiers (LM358), RC low-pass filters (e.g., $f_c = 159\ \text{Hz}$ for $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega, C = 100\ \text{nF}$), Zener clamps for ESD/surge protection.

4. **Microcontroller Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - 32-bit automotive MCU (e.g., Infineon TC275, NXP S32K144).
   - Runs the embedded control algorithm at **< 1 ms** loop time.
   - Performs A/D conversion, PWM generation, timer-based scheduling.

5. **Actuator Driver Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - H-bridge ICs (BTS7960) for motors, Darlington pairs (TIP122) for relays/solenoids, optocouplers for isolation.
   - Power dissipation: $P = I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.

6. **Actuator Output Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - Fuel injectors, throttle motor, EGR valve, idle-air control valve, ignition coils, glow plugs (diesel).

7. **Communication Block** *(1 mark)*  
   - CAN (TJA1050), LIN (TJA1027), FlexRay, OBD-II diagnostic port.
   - Bus speeds: CAN = 250 kbps–1 Mbps; LIN = 19.2 kbps.

---

#### Part (b) — Basic Structure of a Microcontroller-Based Engine Management System (7 marks)

**Neat sketch** — refer to the layered architecture in **Section 4.2**.

**Major Sensors** *(2 marks)*:
- Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor
- Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor
- Oxygen / Lambda (O₂) sensor
- Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)
- Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)
- Knock sensor
- Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)
- Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)

**Major Actuators** *(2 marks)*:
- Fuel injectors (solenoid / piezoelectric)
- Ignition coils
- Throttle body / electronic throttle motor
- Idle Air Control (IAC) valve
- Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) valve
- Variable Valve Timing (VVT) solenoid
- Evaporative Emission (EVAP) purge valve

**Working Principle** *(2 marks)*:
- Sensors continuously monitor engine state.
- MCU executes **PID control algorithm** to maintain the air–fuel ratio at the **stoichiometric value λ = 1**.
- PWM-driven actuators respond in real time.
- Closed-loop correction ensures **minimal exhaust emissions** (Euro 6 / BS-VI compliant).
- Diagnostic data is broadcast on **CAN bus** and made available via **OBD-II** port.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** Explain the **basic structure** of a typical automotive electronic system with a block diagram. Discuss the role of **signal conditioning** and **actuator driver** stages in detail. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the **basic architecture of an automotive powertrain control module (PCM)**. Compare the CAN, LIN, and FlexRay communication buses used in modern vehicles. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Basic Structure with Signal Conditioning and Driver Stages (7 marks)

**Block diagram** — same as Section 4.1.

**Role of Signal Conditioning Stage** *(3.5 marks)*:
- Converts raw, noisy sensor outputs into clean, microcontroller-compatible voltage levels (0–5 V or 0–3.3 V).
- Performs:
  - **Amplification:** Increases weak signals from sensors like thermocouples.
  - **Filtering:** RC low-pass filters remove EMI/EMC noise (typical cutoff $f_c \approx 100\ \text{Hz}$).
  - **Level shifting:** Maps 0–12 V signals to 0–5 V ADC range.
  - **Protection:** Zener diodes and TVS clamps protect ADC inputs from voltage transients up to **+60 V**.

**Role of Actuator Driver Stage** *(3.5 marks)*:
- Microcontroller output pins deliver only **~20 mA**; actuators require **2–40 A**.
- Driver ICs (e.g., BTS7960, TLE8209) provide:
  - **Current amplification** via MOSFET H-bridge.
  - **Galvanic isolation** via opto-couplers (preventing ground loops).
  - **Flyback / freewheeling protection** across inductive loads.
- PWM modulation at the driver controls average power: $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{max}$.
- Failure modes (open load, short circuit) are detected via diagnostic feedback to the MCU.

---

#### Part (b) — Powertrain Control Module Architecture & Bus Comparison (7 marks)

**PCM Basic Architecture** *(3.5 marks)*:
- A Powertrain Control Module (PCM) integrates **engine ECU** + **transmission TCU** into a single housing.
- Multi-core MCU architecture (e.g., TriCore TC3xx) for parallel processing.
- Layered software: AUTOSAR-compliant — Application → RTE → BSW → MCAL.
- Inputs: CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, O₂, vehicle speed, gear position.
- Outputs: Fuel injectors, ignition coils, transmission shift solenoids, torque management.

**Bus Comparison Table** *(3.5 marks)*:

| Feature | CAN | LIN | FlexRay |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | 250 kbps – 1 Mbps | Up to 19.2 kbps | Up to 10 Mbps |
| **Topology** | Multi-master, bus | Single-master, bus | Multi-master, star/bus |
| **Wiring** | Twisted pair, 2 wires | 1 wire | 4 wires (2 twisted pairs) |
| **Cost** | Medium | Very low | High |
| **Determinism** | Non-deterministic | Non-deterministic | Deterministic (TDMA) |
| **Typical Use** | Powertrain, chassis | Window, mirror, seat | X-by-wire, ADAS |
| **Standard** | ISO 11898 | ISO 17987 | ISO 17458 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Power Supply block** in the ECU diagram — *−2 marks* (it is mandatory).
> 2. **Drawing sensors and actuators inside the MCU block** instead of as separate blocks — *−1 mark*.
> 3. **Writing "CAN bus" without mentioning speed (kbps/Mbps)** — examiners deduct *0.5–1 mark*.
> 4. **Not stating the nominal battery voltage** (12 V / 24 V) in power-supply answers — *−0.5 mark*.
> 5. **Confusing LIN with CAN** — they are very different; LIN is a *low-cost single-master* slave bus.
> 6. **Skipping the OBD-II diagnostic port** in communication-block answers — examiners expect it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Basic Structure":**

- 🔹 The **Basic Structure of an automotive electronic system** is built on **5 universal blocks**: Sensor → Signal Conditioning → Microcontroller → Actuator Driver → Communication, **plus** a dedicated **Power Supply** stage.
- 🔹 A modern vehicle contains **~70–100 ECUs**, communicating over **CAN, LIN, FlexRay, and Automotive Ethernet**.
- 🔹 **Nominal battery voltage:** +12 V (cars), +24 V (heavy vehicles). **Load-dump transient:** up to **+60 V**.
- 🔹 **Signal conditioning** = Amplify + Filter + Level-shift + Protect.
- 🔹 **Actuator drivers** = H-bridge (motor), Darlington (relay), opto-isolator (isolation), freewheeling diode (protection).
- 🔹 **ADC resolution** in most automotive MCUs: **10-bit or 12-bit**, $V_{ref}$ = +5 V or +3.3 V.
- 🔹 **PWM duty cycle:** $D = t_{on} / T_{PWM}$, controlling average actuator power.
- 🔹 **Buck converter:** $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ (ideal, continuous conduction mode).
- 🔹 **Microcontroller watchdog timer** is mandatory for functional safety (ISO 26262).
- 🔹 **OBD-II** is the standardized diagnostic port mandated since **1996 (US)** and adopted globally; uses **CAN** as the underlying physical layer in most modern vehicles.
- 🔹 **AUTOSAR** software layers mirror the hardware structure: **ASW → RTE → BSW → MCAL → Hardware**.
- 🔹 For 14-mark answers, **always draw the block diagram first**, then explain block-by-block with **at least one component example and one equation** per block.

---

# Module 4 — Vision Based Autonomous Road Vehicles

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *Vision-Based Autonomous Road Vehicle* is a self-driving ground vehicle that primarily relies on **computer vision algorithms** running over monocular, stereo, or multi-camera inputs to perceive its environment, localize itself, detect lane geometry, recognize traffic signs, classify obstacles (vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists), and ultimately plan & execute a safe trajectory without human intervention.

According to the **SAE J3016 standard (Levels 0–5)** adopted in the KTU Autotronics syllabus, vision-based autonomy spans across **Driver Assistance (Level 1–2)** features such as **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)**, **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)**, **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)**, **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** and **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)**, all the way up to **Full Driving Automation (Level 5)** where cameras form the primary perception modality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Line:**
> "Vision is the *primary perceptual organ* of an autonomous vehicle — analogous to human eyes, but operating at **60–120 FPS**, with infrared capability, **360° coverage**, and **sub-centimetre pixel resolution**."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine teaching a 5-year-old to cross a road. You point at the road and say: *"Look — cars come from there, the white line on the ground tells you where to stop, and the red man on the signal tells you not to cross."*

A Vision-Based Autonomous Vehicle is doing **exactly the same thing**, but with:

- **Eyes → Cameras** (mono, stereo, RGB, thermal)
- **Brain → AI/Deep Learning Processor (GPU/NPU)**
- **Memory → HD Maps + SLAM-built local map**
- **Decision-making → Path Planner + Control Algorithm**
- **Hands & Feet → Drive-by-wire steering, throttle, brake actuators**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module-4 specifically emphasises the *image acquisition → preprocessing → feature extraction → semantic interpretation → actuation* pipeline, which mirrors the classic *Sense–Plan–Act* robotics paradigm.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Memorise for KTU)

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Frame rate | **30–60 FPS** (ADAS), up to **120 FPS** (high-speed) | Real-time perception |
| Camera resolution | **1–8 Megapixels** | Object detection range |
| Detection range | **up to 250 m** for cars, **60 m** for pedestrians | Stopping distance |
| Latency (end-to-end) | **< 100 ms** | Safety-critical bound |
| Field of View (FOV) | **50°–200°** | Wide-angle for blind spots |
| Operating wavelength | **Visible (400–700 nm)** + Near-IR (850 nm) | Day/Night capability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip:** Examiners love asking *"Why do autonomous vehicles use a *fusion* of cameras with LiDAR/Radar instead of cameras alone?"* — Answer: cameras fail in adverse weather (fog, glare, heavy rain) where **Radar** penetrates and **LiDAR** provides accurate 3-D depth.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vision Perception Pipeline (Sense → Understand → Act)

The functioning of a vision-based autonomous road vehicle can be broken down into **five sequential functional blocks**:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Synchronised capture from front, rear, surround-view, and stereo cameras. Auto-exposure, auto-white-balance, HDR (High Dynamic Range) imaging is used to handle tunnels, direct sunlight, and night scenes.
2. **Image Pre-processing** — Denoising (Gaussian / Bilateral filtering), undistortion (removing lens radial & tangential distortion), rectification (aligning stereo pairs), and ROI (Region of Interest) cropping for computational efficiency.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Classical CV (Hough Transform, Canny edges, Sobel gradients, Optical Flow) **AND/OR** Deep-Learning features (CNN feature maps from ResNet, EfficientNet backbones).
4. **Semantic Interpretation** — Multi-task perception: **Lane Detection**, **Object Detection (cars, pedestrians)**, **Semantic Segmentation** (drivable area, sidewalk, vegetation), **Traffic Sign & Light Classification**, **Free-Space Estimation**, **Depth Estimation**.
5. **Decision & Actuation** — Path planning (polynomial / Frenet-frame trajectories), Model Predictive Control (MPC), drive-by-wire actuation of steering, throttle, and brake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Always draw this **Sense–Perceive–Plan–Act** pipeline when answering long questions.

## 2.2 Camera Model — The Mathematical Eye

### 2.2.1 Pinhole Camera Geometry

A pinhole camera projects a 3-D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ onto a 2-D image plane point $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)$ via **perspective projection**.

The complete projection is governed by:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \, [R \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:

- $s$ is a scalar (homogeneous scale factor).
- $K$ is the **Intrinsic Matrix** (camera-internal parameters).
- $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the **Extrinsic Matrix** (rotation + translation from world to camera frame).

The Intrinsic Matrix $K$ is given by:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $f_x = f \cdot m_x$ — focal length in pixel units along x.
- $f_y = f \cdot m_y$ — focal length in pixel units along y.
- $c_x, c_y$ — principal point (image centre).
- $\gamma$ — skew coefficient (≈0 for modern CMOS sensors).

### 2.2.2 Lens Distortion Correction

Real lenses introduce **radial** and **tangential** distortion, corrected by:

$$x_{\text{corrected}} = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2p_1 xy + p_2(r^2 + 2x^2)$$
$$y_{\text{corrected}} = y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1(r^2 + 2y^2) + 2p_2 xy$$

Where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ and $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$ are distortion coefficients obtained via **camera calibration** (Zhang's checkerboard method).

## 2.3 Lane Detection — Theory

Lane markings are detected using a **four-step classical pipeline**:

1. **Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM):** Removes the perspective effect, transforming the front-view image into a **bird's-eye view** of the road plane.
2. **Edge Detection:** Canny / Sobel operator applied on the IPM image.
3. **Line Fitting:** Hough Transform or RANSAC-based polynomial fit.
4. **Temporal Filtering:** Kalman filter or particle filter to stabilise lane curvature across frames.

Modern implementations replace steps 1–3 with **end-to-end CNN models** like **SCNN (Spatial CNN)**, **LaneNet**, or **Ultra-Fast-Lane-Detection (UFLD)**.

## 2.4 Object Detection — Deep Learning Era

Two architectural families dominate KTU-relevant syllabus content:

| Architecture | Type | Speed | Accuracy | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **YOLO (You Only Look Once)** | Single-stage | Real-time (60+ FPS) | High | Real-time vehicle/pedestrian detection |
| **Faster R-CNN** | Two-stage | Slower (~7 FPS) | Very High | High-precision offline mapping |
| **SSD (Single Shot Detector)** | Single-stage | Real-time | Medium | Embedded ECU platforms |
| **CenterNet / FCOS** | Anchor-free | Real-time | High | Modern ADAS stacks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why YOLO is preferred in cars:** It treats detection as a **single regression problem** — predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities in one forward pass, making it ideal for **real-time** ECU inference.

## 2.5 Stereo Vision — Depth from Two Eyes

Stereo vision mimics **human binocular disparity** to compute per-pixel depth. The depth $Z$ of a world point is recovered from disparity $d$:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

Where:
- $f$ = focal length (pixels)
- $B$ = baseline distance between the two cameras (m)
- $d = x_{\text{left}} - x_{\text{right}}$ = disparity (pixels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse relationship:** As disparity decreases (farther objects), depth increases hyperbolically. This is the same principle used by the human brain to estimate distance.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pinhole projection | $x = f \cdot X/Z$ | pixels / m | Image formation |
| Camera matrix | $s\,\mathbf{x} = K[R\vert\mathbf{t}]\,\mathbf{X}$ | — | Full 3-D→2-D mapping |
| Depth from stereo | $Z = f \cdot B / d$ | metres | Distance estimation |
| Optical Flow constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | — | Motion estimation |
| Disparity–Depth relation | $d = f \cdot B / Z$ | pixels | Stereo correspondence |
| Pinhole intrinsics | $f_x = f \cdot m_x$ | pixels | Camera calibration |
| Camera FoV | $\theta = 2\arctan(w / 2f)$ | radians / deg | Lens selection |
| Frame period | $T = 1/\text{FPS}$ | seconds | Real-time budget |
| Lane curvature (parabola) | $y = a x^2 + b x + c$ | — | Lane fitting |
| Centripetal acc. (turning) | $a_c = v^2 / R$ | m/s² | Path planning |
| Stopping distance | $d_s = v t_r + v^2 / (2\mu g)$ | metres | AEB algorithm |
| Homography (IPM) | $\mathbf{x}' = H \cdot \mathbf{x}$ | — | Bird's-eye view |
| SSD vs YOLO speed | $\sim 7$ FPS vs $\sim 60$ FPS | — | Detector trade-off |
| Pixel size | $\Delta = \text{sensor width} / \text{image width}$ | µm | Resolution check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Modern self-driving stacks (Waymo, Tesla, Mobileye) use **sensor fusion** of Camera + LiDAR + Radar + Ultrasonic + GPS-IMU — because no single sensor is reliable in **all** weather/lighting conditions. KTU examiners love this fusion question.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Vision-based autonomy is the *backbone* of:
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** and **Lane Centring Assist** in modern cars.
- **Robotaxis** (Waymo, Cruise, Baidu Apollo Go).
- **Autonomous trucking** (TuSimple, Embark, Kodiak).
- **Last-mile delivery robots** (Nuro, Starship).
- **Agricultural & mining autonomous vehicles** (John Deere, Komatsu).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pinhole Projection Equation

**Statement:** Show that a 3-D world point $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in the camera frame projects to an image point $(x, y)$ given by $x = f X_c / Z_c$ and $y = f Y_c / Z_c$.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometric model.**
Consider the optical centre $O$ at the origin, the image plane placed at distance $f$ (focal length) along the $Z_c$ axis. A point $\mathbf{P} = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in front of the camera emits (or reflects) a light ray that travels through $O$ and strikes the image plane at $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, f)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of similar triangles.**
The ray from $\mathbf{P}$ to $O$ forms a triangle with the $Z_c$ axis. The image plane triangle is geometrically similar (the pinhole creates an inverted, scaled copy).

$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X_c}{Z_c} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the image coordinates.**

$$x = \frac{f X_c}{Z_c} \quad \text{and} \quad y = \frac{f Y_c}{Z_c}$$

This is the **fundamental pinhole equation**, the bedrock of all camera-based perception. In homogeneous form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Depth from Stereo Disparity

**Statement:** Derive $Z = f \cdot B / d$ for a stereo camera pair.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the rectified stereo pair.**
After rectification, both cameras have parallel optical axes separated by baseline $B$. The optical axes lie along the $Z$ axis. A world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to image-plane coordinate $x_L$ in the left camera and $x_R$ in the right camera.

**Step 2 — Apply the pinhole equation to both cameras.**
Using the derivation from §3.1:

$$x_L = \frac{f X}{Z} \quad \text{and} \quad x_R = \frac{f (X - B)}{Z}$$

Note: The right camera is shifted by $B$ along the $X$ axis relative to the left.

**Step 3 — Define disparity.**
Disparity is the horizontal pixel shift between the two projections:

$$d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f X}{Z} - \frac{f (X - B)}{Z} = \frac{f B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for depth.**
Inverting the disparity equation yields the canonical stereo-depth relation:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- A nearby object has **large disparity** → small $Z$.
- A distant object has **small disparity** → large $Z$.
- Sub-pixel disparity estimation precision directly limits depth accuracy.

**Quantitative example:** If $f = 1000$ pixels, $B = 0.12$ m, and $d = 10$ pixels, then:

$$Z = \frac{1000 \times 0.12}{10} = 12 \text{ metres}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Optical Flow Constraint Equation (Lucas–Kanade)

**Statement:** Derive the brightness-constancy constraint that underlies visual odometry.

**Step 1 — Brightness constancy assumption.**
A moving pixel at image location $(x, y)$ at time $t$ moves to $(x + dx, y + dy)$ at time $t + dt$, and **its intensity remains constant**:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$$

**Step 2 — First-order Taylor expansion of the RHS.**

$$I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt) \approx I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Subtract $I(x, y, t)$ from both sides.**

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} dt = 0$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $dt$ and define velocity components.**

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} = 0$$

Letting $u = dx/dt$ and $v = dy/dt$, and using notation $I_x = \partial I / \partial x$, $I_y = \partial I / \partial y$, $I_t = \partial I / \partial t$:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This single equation in two unknowns is **the aperture problem** — solved over a local patch by Lucas–Kanade using a least-squares fit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs Computational Cost

| Algorithm | Task | Approach | Computational Cost | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hough Transform | Lane/Line detection | Vote in parameter space | $O(N \cdot \theta)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| Canny Edge | Pre-processing | Gradient + non-max suppression | $O(N)$ | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| RANSAC Lane Fit | Robust curve fitting | Random sample consensus | $O(k \cdot n)$ | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| YOLO v5/v8 | Object detection | Single-shot CNN | $\sim 140$ GFLOPs | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| Faster R-CNN | Object detection | Region proposal CNN | $\sim 250$ GFLOPs | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| U-Net | Semantic segmentation | Encoder–decoder CNN | $\sim 50$ GFLOPs | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| Lucas–Kanade | Optical flow | Local least-squares | $O(N)$ | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| SIFT / ORB | Feature matching | Scale-space keypoints | $O(N \log N)$ | ⭐ Low |
| Kalman Filter | Tracking | Recursive Bayesian | $O(n^3)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| Hungarian (IoU) | Multi-object tracking | Bipartite matching | $O(n^3)$ | ⭐ Low |

## 4.1 End-to-End Vision-Based Autonomous Vehicle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensA[Front Mono Camera] --> pre
    sensB[Stereo Camera Pair] --> pre
    sensC[Surround View Camera] --> pre
    sensD[Rear Camera] --> pre
    sensE[LiDAR Sensor] --> fusion
    sensF[Radar Sensor] --> fusion

    laneDetect --> perc[Perception Output]

    segNet --> perc
    signRec --> perc

    fusion[Sensor Fusion: Camera + LiDAR + Radar + IMU] --> local[Localization: SLAM + HD Map Matching]

    perc --> plan[Path Planner: Behavior + Trajectory]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cam[Camera Frame Input] --> ipm[Inverse Perspective Mapping]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

### Q1. Define a Vision-Based Autonomous Vehicle. Mention the SAE J3016 levels relevant to camera-based driving.
*(3 marks — CO4, Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**

A vision-based autonomous road vehicle is a self-driving vehicle that uses **cameras as its primary perception sensor** along with image-processing and AI algorithms to perceive surroundings, identify lanes, obstacles, traffic signs, and make driving decisions.

The **SAE J3016** standard defines **six automation levels** (0–5). Camera-based features operate across all levels:

- **Level 0:** No automation — cameras only for warning (e.g., LDW).
- **Level 1:** Driver assistance — AEB, ACC (camera + radar).
- **Level 2:** Partial automation — Lane Centring + ACC together.
- **Level 3:** Conditional automation — Traffic Jam Pilot.
- **Level 4:** High automation — Geo-fenced robotaxi.
- **Level 5:** Full automation — no human required.

---

### Q2. What is the pinhole camera model? Write its projection equation.
*(3 marks — CO4, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

The pinhole camera model describes how a 3-D world point is projected onto a 2-D image plane through an infinitely small aperture. Light from a scene point $\mathbf{P}$ passes through the optical centre $O$ and strikes the image plane at $\mathbf{p}$.

The fundamental pinhole projection equations are:

$$x = \frac{f X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y = \frac{f Y_c}{Z_c}$$

In homogeneous form, using intrinsic matrix $K$ and extrinsic $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \, [R \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This model is the **foundation** of all computer vision in autonomous vehicles, including lane detection, object detection, and SLAM.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

### Question A — Lane Detection & Image Processing (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the classical computer-vision pipeline for lane detection in an autonomous vehicle. Mention the role of Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM).** *(7 marks — CO4, Bloom Level: Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

The classical lane detection pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Front-facing camera captures frames at 30–60 FPS.
2. **Pre-processing** — Undistortion (removes lens distortion), grayscale conversion, Gaussian smoothing to remove sensor noise.
3. **Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)** — This is the *most critical* step. It transforms the front-view camera image into a **bird's-eye view** by applying a homography matrix $H$ that maps road-plane pixels to a top-down view. After IPM, lane markings appear as **near-parallel lines**, simplifying detection. The homography is computed assuming a flat road plane and known camera extrinsics.
4. **Edge Detection** — Canny edge detector extracts intensity gradients corresponding to lane markings.
5. **ROI Masking** — Only the lower half of the image (road region) is processed to reduce computation.
6. **Line Fitting** — Hough Transform accumulates votes in $(\rho, \theta)$ space to identify straight lines; RANSAC fits robust polynomials for curved lanes.
7. **Temporal Filtering** — A Kalman Filter smooths lane parameters across frames, providing stable output even when individual frames have noise or occlusions.

**Role of IPM (Key Point):** IPM removes perspective foreshortening so that lane markings, which in the original image converge to a *vanishing point*, become **parallel** in the bird's-eye view. This converts the problem from a difficult *curve-fitting in perspective* to an easier *line-fitting in top-down view*, dramatically improving accuracy and reducing computational cost.

**Block diagram:**

```
Camera Frame → Undistort → Grayscale → IPM (Bird's-Eye) → Canny Edge
→ ROI Mask → Hough Transform → RANSAC Polynomial Fit → Kalman Filter → Lane Output
```

---

**(b) Derive the equation for depth estimation in a stereo vision system. A stereo camera pair has focal length 800 pixels, baseline 0.2 m, and detects a pedestrian at 25 pixels disparity. Calculate the distance to the pedestrian.** *(7 marks — CO5, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse)*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

After rectification, both cameras have parallel optical axes separated by baseline $B$. A world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to image coordinates $x_L$ in the left camera and $x_R$ in the right camera. Applying the pinhole projection:

$$\begin{aligned} x_L &= \frac{f X}{Z} \\[4pt] x_R &= \frac{f (X - B)}{Z} \end{aligned}$$

Disparity $d$ is defined as the horizontal pixel difference:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= x_L - x_R \\[4pt] &= \frac{f X}{Z} - \frac{f(X - B)}{Z} \\[4pt] &= \frac{f B}{Z} \end{aligned}$$

Inverting to obtain depth:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $f = 800$ pixels
- $B = 0.2$ m
- $d = 25$ pixels

Substituting into the depth formula:

$$\begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d} \\[4pt] &= \frac{800 \times 0.2}{25} \\[4pt] &= \frac{160}{25} \\[4pt] &= 6.4 \text{ metres} \end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The pedestrian is at a distance of **6.4 metres** from the camera.

**Engineering Insight:** A disparity estimation error of even 1 pixel at this range would yield a depth error of:

$$\Delta Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d^2} \cdot \Delta d = \frac{160}{625} \times 1 \approx 0.256 \text{ m}$$

This is why stereo matching algorithms strive for **sub-pixel disparity accuracy**, especially for far-field objects where disparity is small.

---

### Question B — Deep Learning for Object Detection & Sensor Fusion (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the YOLO (You Only Look Once) object detection architecture. Why is it preferred over R-CNN for real-time autonomous driving applications?** *(7 marks — CO4, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse)*

**Model Answer:**

**YOLO Architecture Overview:**

YOLO ("You Only Look Once") reframes object detection as a **single regression problem** rather than a two-stage classification + localization problem. The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $13 \times 13$, $26 \times 26$, $52 \times 52$ in YOLOv5/v8). Each grid cell predicts:

- $B$ bounding boxes $(x, y, w, h)$ with confidence scores.
- $C$ class probabilities.

The YOLO pipeline has three key blocks:

1. **Backbone (e.g., CSP-Darknet):** Extracts hierarchical features via convolutional layers.
2. **Neck (FPN + PAN):** Aggregates multi-scale features using a Feature Pyramid Network + Path Aggregation Network, enabling detection of small (far) and large (near) objects simultaneously.
3. **Detection Head:** Three heads at different grid resolutions predict bounding boxes, objectness score, and class probabilities.

All predictions are produced in a **single forward pass**, followed by Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) to remove duplicate detections.

**Why YOLO is Preferred for Autonomous Driving:**

| Aspect | YOLO | Faster R-CNN |
|---|---|---|
| Stages | Single stage | Two stages (RPN + Classifier) |
| Speed | 60–150 FPS | 5–10 FPS |
| Latency | Low (real-time) | High (offline mapping) |
| Hardware | Embedded GPU/NPU sufficient | Requires powerful GPU |
| Accuracy | Very Good | Slightly higher (mAP) |
| Suitability | On-vehicle inference | Server-side mapping |

**Engineering Reason:** Autonomous driving demands **< 100 ms end-to-end latency** to react to obstacles. YOLO's single-pass inference makes this feasible on vehicle-grade **NVIDIA Orin, Qualcomm Ride, or Mobileye EyeQ** chips.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of sensor fusion in autonomous vehicles. Why is camera-alone perception insufficient, and how does multi-sensor fusion address this limitation?** *(7 marks — CO5, Bloom Level: Evaluate)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Sensor Fusion:**

Sensor fusion is the **real-time integration of data from multiple heterogeneous sensors** — cameras, LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic, GPS, and IMU — to produce a more accurate, robust, and complete perception of the vehicle's environment than any single sensor can provide alone.

**Why Camera-Alone Perception is Insufficient:**

| Scenario | Camera Performance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy fog / rain | Poor | Light scattering reduces contrast |
| Direct sun glare | Glares the sensor | Loss of dynamic range |
| Night without IR | Poor | Insufficient illumination |
| Depth estimation | Indirect (via stereo/mono-depth ML) | Less accurate than LiDAR |
| Velocity of distant cars | Indirect (via optical flow) | Radar measures directly |
| Black-painted vehicles | Low contrast | Camera struggles to detect |

**How Multi-Sensor Fusion Solves This:**

The fusion combines **complementary strengths**:

- **Camera** — High resolution, recognises colour, signs, lane markings, traffic lights.
- **LiDAR** — Accurate 3-D geometry, works in darkness, gives precise distance.
- **Radar** — Works in fog/rain, gives **direct radial velocity** via Doppler effect.
- **IMU + GPS** — Provides ego-pose (where the car is and how it's moving).
- **Ultrasonic** — Short-range (< 5 m) parking manoeuvres.

**Fusion Algorithms (KTU-relevant):**

- **Kalman Filter** — Linear-Gaussian state estimation.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** — Non-linear extensions for vision + IMU.
- **Particle Filter** — Multi-modal belief tracking.
- **Deep Fusion** — End-to-end neural networks (e.g., PointPainting, TransFusion).

**Real-world example:** Tesla Autopilot uses **8 cameras + 12 ultrasonic + 1 radar** (now vision-only); Waymo uses **cameras + LiDAR + radar** for redundancy; Mobileye True Redundancy fuses **cameras + radar-only** independently.

**Conclusion:** Sensor fusion is the *enabling technology* that makes Level 3+ autonomous driving safe enough for public roads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *focal length* in mm vs pixels** — Always state units. Conversion: $f_{\text{px}} = f_{\text{mm}} \cdot \frac{\text{image width}}{\text{sensor width}}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the intrinsic matrix** $K$ — many students write only $x = fX/Z$ without $c_x, c_y$ principal point — full marks lost.
> 3. **Stereo depth derivation** — must explicitly write the **two pinhole equations** before subtracting; examiners give method marks for this step.
> 4. **SAE Levels** — Students often confuse Level 3 with Level 4. Level 3 still **requires a human driver ready to take over**; Level 4 is **geo-fenced no-human-needed**.
> 5. **YOLO vs R-CNN** — Don't just say "YOLO is faster"; quantify with **FPS numbers** and state the **hardware implication** (embedded vs server).
> 6. **Sensor fusion** — always draw a **block diagram** with labelled data flow; marks are reserved for it.
> 7. **Forgetting unit conversion** — disparity in pixels, focal length in pixels, baseline in metres — keep units consistent to avoid unit-penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick Recall — Read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- **SAE Levels (0–5):** No automation → Driver assist → Partial → Conditional → High → Full.
- **Pinhole equation:** $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$ — the foundation of *every* vision algorithm.
- **Intrinsic Matrix $K$** contains $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$.
- **Extrinsic Matrix** $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ converts world coordinates to camera coordinates.
- **Stereo depth:** $Z = f B / d$ — disparity inversely proportional to depth.
- **Optical Flow equation:** $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ — basis of visual odometry.
- **IPM (Inverse Perspective Mapping)** removes perspective foreshortening — converts front view to bird's-eye view.
- **YOLO** = single-stage, real-time (~60+ FPS), best for embedded automotive inference.
- **Faster R-CNN** = two-stage, high accuracy, slow → server-side use only.
- **Canny Edge** → Hough Transform → RANSAC → Kalman = classical lane detection chain.
- **Distortion coefficients** $k_1, k_2, k_3$ (radial) and $p_1, p_2$ (tangential) — corrected via Zhang's calibration.
- **Sensor fusion** combines Camera + LiDAR + Radar + IMU + GPS → overcomes individual sensor weaknesses.
- **Latency budget** for ADAS: **< 100 ms** end-to-end perception-to-actuation.
- **Frame rate** for ADAS: **30–60 FPS** minimum, **120 FPS** for highway-speed vehicles.
- **Stopping distance** $d_s = v t_r + v^2/(2\mu g)$ — used in AEB algorithms.
- **AEB** = Automatic Emergency Braking (camera + radar).
- **LKA** = Lane Keeping Assist (camera-based steering correction).
- **LDW** = Lane Departure Warning.
- **TSR** = Traffic Sign Recognition (CNN classifier).
- **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (visual + inertial).
- **HD Maps** provide prior knowledge of lane geometry, improving localization.
- **CNN backbones** used in automotive: ResNet-50, EfficientNet, CSP-Darknet.
- **Field of View (FOV):** Front camera 50°, surround-view 180°+, rear 120°.
- **Pinhole limitation:** No depth from single image — need stereo, motion, or learned monocular depth.
- **Aperture problem:** Optical flow is ambiguous without spatial context — Lucas-Kanade patches solve it.
- **Radial distortion** dominates in wide-angle/fisheye surround-view cameras.
- **Camera calibration** uses a checkerboard target (Zhang's method, ≥10 images).
- **YOLO loss** = localisation loss + confidence loss + classification loss.
- **mAP (mean Average Precision)** is the standard accuracy metric for object detection.
- **Tesla Vision, Waymo Driver, Mobileye SuperVision, Apollo** are real-world vision-based autonomy platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mnemonic — "SAFETY":**
> **S**ensors (Camera + LiDAR + Radar), **A**lgorithms (YOLO + CNN), **F**usion (Kalman + EKF), **E**stimation (Depth + Pose), **T**racking (SORT/DeepSORT), **Y**ield-safe actuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture for Dynamic Vision System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture for Dynamic Vision System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Dynamic Vision System (DVS)** in the automotive context is an embedded, real-time computer vision architecture designed to perceive, interpret, and react to the dynamically changing visual environment surrounding a vehicle. Unlike static machine vision (e.g., industrial inspection on a conveyor belt), a Dynamic Vision System must operate under *uncontrolled illumination*, *non-rigid motion of both the camera and the scene*, *strict real-time latency budgets (typically 50–100 ms end-to-end)*, and *automotive-grade reliability constraints (ISO 26262 ASIL-B/D)*.

In the KTU 2024 Autotronics syllabus, the **Architecture for Dynamic Vision System** refers to the complete hardware-software co-design blueprint that integrates:

- **Image acquisition** (mono/stereo/multi-camera sensor arrays)
- **High-bandwidth data transport** (LVDS, FPD-Link III, MIPI CSI-2, Automotive Ethernet 1000BASE-T1)
- **Image Signal Processing (ISP)** pipelines
- **Compute acceleration fabrics** (DSP, GPU, FPGA, dedicated ASIC/NPU)
- **Computer vision & Deep Learning inference engines**
- **Vehicle network integration** (CAN, CAN-FD, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Dynamic" here specifically emphasizes that **both the ego-vehicle and the scene are in continuous motion**. This forces architectural choices around latency, frame rate (≥ **30 fps**, often 60 fps), temporal consistency, and motion-compensation — features that are *not* mandatory in static machine vision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Eye-Brain Driving System**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car. Your **two eyes** are the *cameras*. The **optic nerve** is the *high-speed data link*. The **LGN (lateral geniculate nucleus)** acts as the *Image Signal Processor* — adjusting contrast, brightness, and motion sensitivity. The **primary visual cortex (V1)** is the *feature extractor* (edges, corners). The **higher cortical areas (V4, IT)** are the *object recognition stage* (pedestrian, vehicle, sign). Finally, your **motor cortex** is the *actuator command unit* — steering, braking.
>
> A Dynamic Vision System in an autonomous car replicates this entire pipeline — but with silicon, deterministic clocks, and ISO 26262 functional safety guarantees instead of neurons and subjective judgment.

### 1.3 Why "Dynamic" Demands a Special Architecture

| Property | Static Vision (Industrial) | Dynamic Vision (Automotive) |
|---|---|---|
| Illumination | Controlled (ring lights, enclosures) | **Uncontrolled** (day/night/glare/tunnel) |
| Camera motion | Stationary | **Continuously vibrating (1–25 g shocks)** |
| Scene motion | Rigid / fixed object | **Non-rigid, multi-object, high-speed** |
| Latency budget | Seconds acceptable | **< 100 ms (often < 50 ms for AEB)** |
| Operating temp | 0–50 °C | **−40 °C to +85 °C (AEC-Q100)** |
| Functional Safety | Not mandatory | **ISO 26262 ASIL-B to ASIL-D** |

These constraints drive every block of the architecture we will study below.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Decomposition of the Architecture

A Dynamic Vision System can be rigorously decomposed into **seven logical layers**:

1. **Optical Layer** — Lens, aperture, IR-cut filter, bandpass filter.
2. **Sensor Layer** — CMOS image sensor (e.g., OV10640, IMX390) with rolling/global shutter.
3. **Pre-processing Layer (ISP)** — Demosaicing, gamma correction, lens distortion correction, HDR fusion, noise reduction.
4. **Data Transport Layer** — Serializer-deserializer (SerDes) pair, MIPI CSI-2 / FPD-Link III / GMSL.
5. **Compute & Inference Layer** — CPU + DSP + GPU + NPU (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Qualcomm Ride, Mobileye EyeQ, TI TDA4x).
6. **Algorithm / Application Layer** — Lane detection, obstacle detection, semantic segmentation, SLAM, traffic sign recognition.
7. **Actuation / Network Output Layer** — Object list → CAN/CAN-FD/Ethernet → ECU → brake/steer actuators.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Architectural Choice

- **Why CMOS over CCD?** CMOS enables on-chip ADCs, lower power (≈ **300 mW** vs **2–5 W** for CCD), and integrated HDR — critical for automotive thermal budgets.
- **Why Global Shutter?** Rolling shutter causes **"jello effect"** during high-speed ego-motion, distorting object geometry. Global shutter captures the entire frame at the same instant.
- **Why ISP before inference?** Neural networks are not robust to raw Bayer artifacts; ISP normalizes inputs.
- **Why dedicated NPU?** General-purpose CPUs cannot meet **> 30 TOPS** demand at < **30 W** power envelope.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield formulas** examiners expect you to recognize in Part A and apply in Part B derivations.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = \dfrac{v \cdot N_f}{N_t}$ | Frame rate (fps) | $N_f$ = frames, $N_t$ = time |
| 2 | $B_{bw} = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot b$ | Required bandwidth | $W,H$ = pixel dims, $b$ = bits/pixel |
| 3 | $t_{e2e} = t_{cap} + t_{tx} + t_{proc} + t_{act}$ | End-to-end latency | Must satisfy ASIL latency budget |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{x_L - x_R}$ | Stereo depth (disparity) | $B$ = baseline, $f$ = focal length |
| 5 | $v_{rel} = \dfrac{\Delta x \cdot f}{\Delta t \cdot Z}$ | Optical-flow velocity | $Z$ = depth |
| 6 | $\text{TOPS} = 2 \cdot N_{MAC} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Compute throughput | $N_{MAC}$ = MAC units |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Sensor signal-to-noise | > **40 dB** typical |
| 8 | $E_v = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_{rel})^2$ | Closing kinetic energy (AEB) | Joules |
| 9 | $P_{diss} = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power per gate | CMOS scaling law |
| 10 | $\text{DRI} = \dfrac{f \cdot H_{px}}{2 \tan(\theta/2)}$ | Detection-Range-Image (Euclidean) | $H_{px}$ = target pixel height |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (10) is the **DRI (Detect-Recognize-Identify)** metric mandated by Euro-NCAP and used by OEMs to size camera resolution and focal length for ADAS functions like AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Modern luxury vehicles contain **8–12 cameras** (front, surround-view, rear, mirror-replacement, in-cabin DMS). Each camera operates with a Dynamic Vision System architecture. The same architectural principles govern:
- Tesla HW4 (144 TOPS, 8 cameras, 5 MP each)
- Mobileye EyeQ6 (128 TOPS, multi-camera + REM crowdsourcing)
- NVIDIA DRIVE Orin (254 TOPS, multi-camera + radar fusion)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency Budget

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:** AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) is a **ASIL-D** function under ISO 26262. The KTU examiner often asks students to *prove* that the proposed architecture meets the safety latency budget.

**Given:**
- Frame rate $f = 30$ fps
- Sensor resolution $1920 \times 1080$ pixels
- Bit depth $b = 12$ bits/pixel (RAW)
- MIPI CSI-2 link = 4 lanes @ **2.5 Gbps** each
- ISP processing time $t_{ISP} = 8$ ms
- CNN inference time $t_{CNN} = 25$ ms (on dedicated NPU)
- Tracking & fusion $t_{fus} = 3$ ms
- Actuation response $t_{act} = 15$ ms

**Step 1: Compute capture latency** (one frame time)

$$
t_{cap} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{30} \approx 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute transport latency** over MIPI CSI-2

The required raw bandwidth is:

$$
B_{bw} = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot b = 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 12
$$

$$
B_{bw} = 746{,}496{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \approx 746.5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Transport time per frame over a 4-lane MIPI CSI-2 link (10 Gbps aggregate, ≈ 80% efficient):

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{746.5 \text{ Mbps}}{10{,}000 \text{ Mbps} \cdot 0.8} \times 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_{tx} \approx 3.11 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Sum all pipeline latencies**

$$
t_{e2e} = t_{cap} + t_{tx} + t_{ISP} + t_{CNN} + t_{fus} + t_{act}
$$

$$
t_{e2e} = 33.33 + 3.11 + 8 + 25 + 3 + 15
$$

$$
t_{e2e} = 87.44 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: ASIL-D verification**

Euro-NCAP AEB requires $t_{e2e} \leq 100$ ms. Since $87.44 < 100$, the architecture **passes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners reward showing all *six* sub-latencies explicitly. Skipping $t_{cap}$ alone can cost **2 marks**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stereo Depth (Disparity) Computation

For a forward-looking stereo camera rig mounted on a vehicle:

$$
d = \frac{f \cdot B}{x_L - x_R} = \frac{f \cdot B}{\Delta x}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Focal length $f = 1200$ pixels
- Baseline $B = 250$ mm
- Pixel disparity $\Delta x = 8$ pixels

$$
d = \frac{1200 \times 250}{8} = \frac{300{,}000}{8} = 37{,}500 \text{ mm} = 37.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Sub-pixel refinement** improves accuracy to ≈ **0.25 m** at 40 m range — sufficient for AEB.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compute Throughput (TOPS) Sizing

Given a CNN (e.g., YOLOv5-s) with $7.5 \times 10^9$ MACs per frame and $f = 30$ fps:

$$
\text{TOPS} = 2 \cdot \text{MACs} \cdot f
$$

$$
\text{TOPS} = 2 \times 7.5 \times 10^9 \times 30 = 450 \times 10^9 = 450 \text{ GOPS}
$$

$$
= 0.45 \text{ TOPS}
$$

With overhead factor $k = 3$ (memory, post-processing, NMS):

$$
\text{TOPS}_{req} = 0.45 \times 3 = 1.35 \text{ TOPS}
$$

Hence a low-end NPU like the TI TDA4VM (8 TOPS) is sufficient — explaining why mid-tier ADAS uses sub-10 TOPS parts.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Architecture

| Decision Axis | Option A | Option B | Selection Criterion (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shutter Type | Rolling | **Global** | High ego-speed → Global |
| Compute Fabric | CPU only | **CPU + NPU** | Inference latency → NPU |
| Topology | Distributed ECUs | **Centralized DCU** | OTA updates + SW consolidation |
| Sensor Type | Mono | **Stereo / Mono+Rad** | Depth requirement → Stereo |
| Data Link | Parallel CMOS | **FPD-Link III / GMSL2** | Cable length & EMI → SerDes |
| Memory | SRAM | **LPDDR4/5** | Bandwidth for NN weights |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Dynamic Vision System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Lens and Optics] --> A2[CMOS Image Sensor]
    A2 --> A3[Analog Front End and ADC]
    A3 --> A4[Image Signal Processor]
    A4 --> A5[MIPI CSI-2 Serializer]
    A5 --> A6[Deserializer on ECU]
    A6 --> A7[CPU Host and Safety MCU]
    A7 --> A8[DSP for Pre Processing]
    A8 --> A9[NPU for CNN Inference]
    A9 --> A10[Object Tracking and Fusion]
    A10 --> A11[Decision and Path Planner]
    A11 --> A12[CAN FD and Automotive Ethernet]
    A12 --> A13[Brake and Steer Actuators]
```

### 4.2 ISP Pipeline Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Raw Bayer Input] --> B2[Black Level Clamp]
    B2 --> B3[Lens Shading Correction]
    B3 --> B4[Bad Pixel Correction]
    B4 --> B5[Demosaicing]
    B5 --> B6[Color Correction Matrix]
    B6 --> B7[Gamma and Tone Mapping]
    B7 --> B8[HDR Fusion]
    B8 --> B9[Noise Reduction 2D and 3D]
    B9 --> B10[Output YUV or RGB to NN]
```

### 4.3 Stereo Vision Depth Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Left Camera] --> C2[Rectification Left]
    C3[Right Camera] --> C4[Rectification Right]
    C2 --> C5[SGBM or Deep Stereo Match]
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6[Disparity Map]
    C6 --> C7[Depth Map via Triangulation]
    C7 --> C8[Obstacle Localisation]
```

### 4.4 Distributed vs Centralized Architecture Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph D1[Distributed Legacy ECU Architecture]
        D2[Front Cam ECU] --> D5[CAN Bus]
        D3[Rear Cam ECU] --> D5
        D4[Surround Cam ECU] --> D5
        D5 --> D6[Central Gateway ECU]
    end

    subgraph E1[Centralized Domain Controller Architecture]
        E2[Front Cam] --> E3[SerDes Aggregator]
        E4[Rear Cam] --> E3
        E5[Surround Cam] --> E3
        E6[In Cabin Cam] --> E3
        E3 --> E7[Central ADAS DCU with SoC]
        E7 --> E8[Automotive Ethernet T1]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Centralized Architecture Wins (Modern Trend):** Tesla HW4, NVIDIA DRIVE, and Mobileye EyeQ6 all migrate to **centralized DCUs** because they consolidate compute (enabling larger NNs), reduce harness weight, and simplify OTA — a recurring KTU 2024 short-answer topic.

### 4.5 Sensor-Fusion Integration Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Camera NN Detections] --> F3[Track Level Fusion]
    F2[Radar Object Lists] --> F3
    F3 --> F4[Kalman or IMM Filter]
    F4 --> F5[Occupancy Grid]
    F5 --> F6[Behavioural Planner]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define a *Dynamic Vision System* in the automotive context. List **two** architectural challenges unique to dynamic (as opposed to static) machine vision.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Dynamic Vision System (DVS) is a real-time embedded computer vision architecture that perceives and interprets a *moving* visual scene from a *moving* ego-vehicle under automotive-grade constraints (ISO 26262, AEC-Q100).

Two unique challenges:
1. **High-vibration, high-shock environment (1–25 g)** demanding global-shutter sensors and rigid mounting.
2. **Strict end-to-end latency budget (< 100 ms for ASIL-D functions like AEB)** requiring parallelized ISP + NPU pipelines.

*(Third point for additional credit: uncontrolled illumination necessitating HDR-ISP fusion.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between **rolling shutter** and **global shutter** CMOS sensors. Why is global shutter preferred in Dynamic Vision Systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Rolling Shutter | Global Shutter |
|---|---|---|
| Exposure | Row-by-row sequentially | All pixels simultaneously |
| Distortion at high speed | Yes (jello/skew) | No |
| Cost / Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Use in DVS | Rare (only low-speed cabin) | **Mandatory (exterior ADAS)** |

**Reason:** During ego-vehicle motion or fast scene motion, rolling shutter produces geometric skew that breaks CNN feature extraction. Global shutter preserves Euclidean geometry, ensuring the DVS sees a *true* snapshot.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **complete functional block architecture of an automotive Dynamic Vision System** from lens to actuator. Label each block with its primary function and typical latency contribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forward-facing mono camera is mounted on a vehicle. Compute the **end-to-end latency** for an AEB scenario, given:
- Frame rate = **30 fps**
- Resolution = **1280 × 960**, 10-bit RAW
- MIPI CSI-2 = 2 lanes @ **1.5 Gbps** each
- ISP = **6 ms**
- CNN inference = **18 ms**
- Fusion + tracking = **4 ms**
- Actuation = **12 ms**

Verify whether the system satisfies the **Euro-NCAP AEB 100 ms** budget. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Lens + IR-cut filter (0 ms)** – focuses scene, blocks IR.
2. **CMOS Image Sensor (1–3 ms exposure)** – photo-electric conversion.
3. **AFE + ADC (1 ms)** – analog amplification, 10/12-bit digitization.
4. **ISP (6 ms)** – demosaic, gamma, HDR, denoise.
5. **SerDes (FPD-Link III / MIPI) (3–5 ms)** – serializes data over coax/STP cable.
6. **Deserializer + ISP output (1 ms)** – reconstructs parallel pixels.
7. **CPU + DSP pre-processing (2 ms)** – normalization, ROI crop.
8. **NPU CNN inference (18 ms)** – object detection, lane, sign.
9. **Tracker + sensor fusion (4 ms)** – Kalman filter, radar-camera fusion.
10. **Decision / Path planner (3 ms)** – AEB/ACC logic.
11. **CAN-FD output (1 ms)** – sends target deceleration.
12. **Brake actuator (12 ms)** – hydraulic/regen brake apply.

Total = ~60–70 ms typical.

**(b) Latency Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Capture latency**
$$
t_{cap} = \frac{1}{30} = 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Required raw bandwidth**
$$
B_{bw} = 1280 \times 960 \times 30 \times 10 = 368{,}640{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 368.64 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3: MIPI transport latency** (2 lanes × 1.5 Gbps = 3 Gbps, 80% efficiency = 2.4 Gbps)

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{368.64}{2400} \times 33.33 \approx 5.12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Sum all latencies**
$$
t_{e2e} = 33.33 + 5.12 + 6 + 18 + 4 + 12 = 78.45 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Verification**
$$
78.45 \text{ ms} < 100 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow \textbf{Architecture is ASIL-D compliant.}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $B_{bw}$ without multiplying by the **frame rate** $f$. Others forget the **MIPI efficiency factor (≈ 80%)**. Both errors cost **2 marks each**. Always state: *"Assuming 80% protocol efficiency."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of the Image Signal Processor (ISP)** in a Dynamic Vision System. List **five** ISP stages and describe each in one sentence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stereo camera rig has focal length $f = 1500$ pixels and baseline $B = 200$ mm. For a pedestrian detected at pixel disparity $\Delta x = 12$ pixels:
- (i) Compute the depth $d$.
- (ii) If the relative velocity (computed via optical flow) is $v_{rel} = 5$ m/s, compute the **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**.
- (iii) Compute the **closing kinetic energy** for a pedestrian mass $m = 75$ kg. Comment on whether AEB must trigger. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of ISP & Five Stages (7 marks):**

The ISP transforms the *raw Bayer* sensor output into a clean, normalized RGB/YUV stream that downstream CNNs can reliably consume. Without ISP, neural networks suffer severe accuracy degradation.

**Five ISP stages:**
1. **Black-level clamping** – subtracts sensor pedestal offset.
2. **Lens shading correction** – compensates for vignetting.
3. **Demosaicing** – interpolates Bayer pattern into full RGB.
4. **Color Correction Matrix (CCM)** – transforms sensor-RGB to sRGB.
5. **HDR fusion + 2D/3D noise reduction** – merges multi-exposure frames and suppresses noise.

**(b) Stereo + TTC + Kinetic Energy (7 marks):**

**(i) Depth**
$$
d = \frac{f \cdot B}{\Delta x} = \frac{1500 \times 200}{12} = \frac{300{,}000}{12} = 25{,}000 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) TTC**
$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{d}{v_{rel}} = \frac{25}{5} = 5 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Kinetic Energy**
$$
E_v = \frac{1}{2} m v_{rel}^2 = 0.5 \times 75 \times 5^2 = 937.5 \text{ J}
$$

**Comment:** Although TTC = 5 s appears safe, the pedestrian mass-energy is **937.5 J**, exceeding the **ISO/UNECE pedestrian-impact survivability threshold (~1000 J)**. **AEB must trigger** as the vehicle approaches, and ideally also include active hood deployment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **omit units** in the final answer (write "25" instead of "25 m"). Marks are reserved explicitly for unit correctness — losing them is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in Autotronics numericals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Architecture for Dynamic Vision System**

- [x] **DVS** = real-time, automotive-grade, ISO 26262-compliant computer vision pipeline.
- [x] **7 architectural layers:** Optical → Sensor → ISP → Transport → Compute → Algorithm → Actuation.
- [x] **Global shutter** is mandatory for exterior cameras due to ego-motion.
- [x] **HDR ISP fusion** is required to handle tunnel entries/exits and night glare.
- [x] **MIPI CSI-2 / FPD-Link III / GMSL2** are the standard automotive SerDes links.
- [x] **End-to-end latency budget** for AEB (ASIL-D) = **≤ 100 ms**.
- [x] **Compute sizing rule:** TOPS_required = 2 × MACs × fps × overhead_factor (k ≈ 3).
- [x] **Stereo depth:** $d = f \cdot B / \Delta x$ — sub-pixel refinement is critical.
- [x] **Optical-flow TTC:** $\text{TTC} = d / v_{rel}$.
- [x] **Centralized DCU topology** (Tesla HW4, NVIDIA Orin) is replacing distributed ECUs.
- [x] **Sensor fusion** with radar is required for redundancy (camera fails in fog/night).
- [x] **DRI metric** governs camera/lens sizing for NCAP compliance.
- [x] Always state **assumptions** (e.g., 80% protocol efficiency, 8-bit output post-ISP) in derivations.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Architecture for Dynamic Vision System**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **OEMRT831 – Autotronics (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, the term **"Features"** refers to the engineered functional capabilities, performance characteristics, and operational attributes integrated into modern automotive electronic subsystems. These features collectively enable **Active Safety**, **Passive Safety**, **Driver Assistance**, **Vehicle Dynamics Control**, and **Passenger Comfort** through real-time electronic intervention.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus alignment, Module 4 typically encompasses:

- **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)**
- **Traction Control System (TCS)**
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**
- **Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)**
- **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)**
- **Drive-by-Wire / Steer-by-Wire / Brake-by-Wire**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Automotive "Features" in this module are *not* mere cosmetic additions — they are **engineered Electronic Control Unit (ECU) driven functionalities** governed by sensors, actuators, and deterministic control algorithms operating within the **Controller Area Network (CAN bus)** protocol.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a wet road. Suddenly, you brake hard — the wheels lock, the bike skids, and you fall. Now imagine if the bicycle had a tiny, super-fast brain that could:

1. Detect that a wheel is about to lock.
2. Release and re-apply brake pressure **25 times per second**.
3. Decide which wheel needs braking based on steering angle.
4. Warn the rider before skidding even begins.

**That tiny brain is the modern automotive ECU**, and the abilities listed above are its **"Features."** Each feature is essentially a software-defined behavior running inside a microcontroller that monitors **physical world inputs (sensors)** and reacts through **physical world outputs (actuators)** — often faster than a human driver ever could.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Parallel:** A modern mid-segment car like the Toyota Hyryder or Hyundai Creta contains **80–100 ECUs** and executes over **100 million lines of code** — each line potentially enabling a specific "feature."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following physical constants and standard metrics are fundamental to the design of automotive electronic features:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|------------|---------|------------------------|-------|
| Wheel Speed Sensor Frequency | $f_{wss}$ | 0 – 2500 | Hz |
| Yaw Rate Sensor Range | $\dot{\psi}$ | 0 – 100 | °/s |
| Steering Angle Sensor Range | $\delta$ | ± 720 | ° |
| Vehicle Dynamic Control Loop Time | $t_{loop}$ | **5 – 20** | ms |
| CAN Bus Data Rate | $R_{CAN}$ | **125 kbps – 1 Mbps** | bit/s |
| LIN Bus Data Rate | $R_{LIN}$ | 19.2 | kbps |
| FlexRay Bandwidth | $R_{FlexRay}$ | 10 | Mbps |
| ABS Cycle Frequency | $f_{ABS}$ | **15 – 25** | Hz |
| Hydraulic Brake Pressure | $P_{brake}$ | 0 – 200 | bar |
| TPMS Transmission Frequency | $f_{TPMS}$ | 433.92 (Europe) / 315 (USA) | MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always quote **loop times (≤ 20 ms)** and **CAN baud rates (500 kbps standard for powertrain)** with units. Marks are deducted for unit omission.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Automotive Features

Automotive electronic features are systematically classified into five engineering domains:

1. **Active Safety Features** — Prevent accidents *before* they occur.
2. **Passive Safety Features** — Minimize injury *during* an accident.
3. **Driver Assistance Features** — Reduce driver workload.
4. **Vehicle Dynamics Features** — Improve handling and stability.
5. **Comfort & Convenience Features** — Enhance user experience.

### 2.2 Detailed Feature Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) — Features

The ABS is the foundational active safety feature. Its engineered capabilities include:

- **Wheel Speed Monitoring (WSM):** Each wheel has an inductive/Hall-effect WSS producing a frequency proportional to wheel angular velocity.
- **Lock-up Detection:** ECU computes slip ratio:
$$\lambda = \frac{v_{vehicle} - v_{wheel}}{v_{vehicle}}$$
where $\lambda \to 1$ indicates impending lock.
- **Hydraulic Modulation:** Three-phase cycle:
  - **Pressure Build** (apply brake)
  - **Pressure Hold** (maintain)
  - **Pressure Release** (dump)
- **EBD Integration (Electronic Brake-force Distribution):** Dynamically apportions brake force between front and rear axles.
- **Off-road / Loose-surface Mode:** Special software calibration for gravel/snow.

#### 2.2.2 Traction Control System (TCS) — Features

TCS prevents drive-wheel spin during acceleration. Its features:

- Slip control via engine torque reduction + individual wheel braking.
- Threshold slip ratio typically maintained at **λ ≈ 0.1 – 0.25**.
- Communicates with **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** via CAN.
- Variable intervention thresholds based on road surface (detected via wheel-speed deltas).

#### 2.2.3 Electronic Stability Control (ESC) — Features

ESC is the **highest-tier active safety feature**, integrating ABS + TCS + additional sensors.

Key engineered features:
- **Yaw Rate Control:** Compares actual yaw rate $\dot{\psi}_{actual}$ vs desired yaw rate $\dot{\psi}_{desired}$ computed from steering angle.
- **Side-slip Angle Minimization:** Keeps vehicle within the driver's intended trajectory.
- **Selective Wheel Braking:** Applies brake to *individual* wheels to generate a corrective yaw moment.
- **Engine Torque Intervention:** Reduces engine torque when oversteer detected.

The governing control equation is:

$$\tau_{corrective} = I_z \cdot (\dot{\psi}_{desired} - \dot{\psi}_{actual})$$

where $I_z$ is the yaw moment of inertia of the vehicle.

#### 2.2.4 Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) — Features

ACC extends conventional cruise control with:
- **Radar / Lidar-based headway sensing** (typically 77 GHz mmWave radar).
- **Time-gap selection:** Adjustable following time between 1.0 s and 2.5 s.
- **Stop & Go capability:** In traffic, the vehicle can come to a complete halt and resume.
- **Speed range:** 0 – 200 km/h depending on system class.
- **Forward Collision Warning (FCW) integration.**

#### 2.2.5 Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS) — Features

TPMS provides real-time tire pressure information:
- **Direct TPMS:** In-tire pressure sensor transmits pressure + temperature + accelerometer data at **433.92 MHz**.
- **Indirect TPMS:** Uses ABS wheel-speed sensors to infer pressure loss via rolling-radius differences.
- **Deflation warning:** Triggers dashboard warning when pressure drops **> 25 % below nominal**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Feature / System | Governing Formula / Equation | Variables | Units |
|---|------------------|-------------------------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Wheel Slip Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_v - v_w}{v_v}$ | $v_v$ = vehicle speed, $v_w$ = wheel speed | dimensionless |
| 2 | Wheel Deceleration Threshold | $a_w = \dfrac{d\omega_w}{dt} \cdot r_{wheel}$ | $\omega_w$ = wheel angular speed, $r$ = tire radius | m/s² |
| 3 | Desired Yaw Rate (Bicycle Model) | $\dot{\psi}_{des} = \dfrac{v \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K_s v^2\right)}$ | $v$ = velocity, $\delta$ = steer angle, $L$ = wheelbase, $K_s$ = stability factor | rad/s |
| 4 | Corrective Yaw Moment | $M_z = I_z (\dot{\psi}_{des} - \dot{\psi}_{act})$ | $I_z$ = yaw inertia | N·m |
| 5 | Safe Following Distance (ACC) | $d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot t_g + d_{min}$ | $t_g$ = time gap, $d_{min}$ = minimum standstill gap | m |
| 6 | Vehicle Stopping Distance | $d_s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $g$ = 9.81 | m |
| 7 | TPMS Pressure Warning | $P_{warn} = 0.75 \cdot P_{nom}$ | $P_{nom}$ = nominal cold pressure | bar / kPa |
| 8 | Brake Hydraulic Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F_{brake}}{A_{piston}}$ | $F$ = clamp load, $A$ = piston area | Pa |
| 9 | CAN Bus Propagation Delay | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{L_{bus}}{v_{signal}}$ | $L$ = bus length, $v_{signal}$ ≈ 2×10⁸ m/s | s |
| 10 | Slip Angle (Vehicle Dynamics) | $\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$ | $\delta$ = steer angle, $\theta_v$ = vehicle heading | rad |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These features are not academic — they are **regulated, life-saving technologies**:

- **ESC** is mandatory in the **EU (since 2014)**, **USA (since 2012)**, and **India (AIS-152 / Bharat NCAP protocols)**.
- **ABS** is mandatory for two-wheelers above 125 cc in India.
- **TPMS** became mandatory in the EU for new passenger cars from **November 2014**.
- **ADAS features** are central to achieving **5-star GNCAP / Euro NCAP** safety ratings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Bosch, Continental, ZF, and Denso collectively hold over **75 % of the global market share** for ESC/ECU modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wheel Slip Ratio for ABS Feature

**Objective:** Establish the mathematical definition of wheel slip and show its use in ABS lock-up detection.

**Step 1 — Define the reference (vehicle) velocity.**
The vehicle's longitudinal velocity is measured from the non-driven (free-rolling) wheels' average speed or computed via a longitudinal accelerometer / GPS-inertial fusion:

$$v_v = \frac{\omega_{FL} + \omega_{FR}}{2} \cdot r_{wheel}$$

This is valid when both front wheels are free-rolling (no brake applied). During ABS intervention, $v_v$ is estimated from a **reference speed calculator** within the ECU.

**Step 2 — Define the individual wheel velocity.**
Each wheel's tangential velocity at the tire-road contact patch is:

$$v_w = \omega_w \cdot r_{wheel}$$

where $\omega_w$ is the wheel angular velocity (rad/s) reported by the WSS.

**Step 3 — Compute slip ratio.**
By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{v_v - v_w}{v_v} \\
        &= 1 - \frac{v_w}{v_v} \\
        &= 1 - \frac{\omega_w \cdot r_{wheel}}{v_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret physical states.**

| Slip Ratio $\lambda$ | Physical State | ECU Action |
|-----------------------|----------------|------------|
| $\lambda = 0$ | Pure rolling, no slip | Hold pressure |
| $0 < \lambda < 0.15$ | Stable braking region | Pressure build |
| $\lambda > 0.25$ | Approaching lock-up | Pressure hold |
| $\lambda \to 1$ | Full lock-up | Pressure release |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The threshold for ABS intervention is typically **$\lambda \geq 0.15$–$0.20$**. Students often lose marks by writing $\lambda > 0.5$ — that is incorrect.

**Step 5 — Relate slip ratio to friction coefficient.**
The tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ is a function of slip:

$$\mu(\lambda) = \mu_{peak} \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot \lambda_{peak}}{\lambda^2 + \lambda_{peak}^2}$$

This is the empirical **Pacejka Magic Formula** simplified form. Maximum braking occurs at $\lambda = \lambda_{peak} \approx 0.08$–$0.15$ on dry asphalt.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Desired Yaw Rate for ESC Feature

**Objective:** Derive the reference yaw rate using the linear bicycle model.

**Step 1 — State the bicycle model assumptions.**
- Small steering angles ($\delta < 5°$).
- Constant vehicle speed $v$.
- Symmetric left/right tire properties.

**Step 2 — Write lateral force equilibrium.**
For the bicycle model:

$$m v (\dot{\psi} + \dot{\beta}) = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}$$

For small $\delta$: $\cos\delta \approx 1$, so:

$$m v (\dot{\psi} + \dot{\beta}) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

**Step 3 — Apply linear tire cornering stiffness.**
Lateral tire forces in linear region:

$$F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f, \quad F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r$$

where $C_{\alpha f}$ and $C_{\alpha r}$ are front and rear cornering stiffness (N/rad).

**Step 4 — Substitute slip angle definitions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_f &= \delta - \theta_v - \frac{a \cdot \dot{\psi}}{v} \\
\alpha_r &= -\theta_v + \frac{b \cdot \dot{\psi}}{v}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $a$ = distance from CG to front axle, $b$ = distance from CG to rear axle.

**Step 5 — Solve steady-state for $\dot{\psi}$.**
In steady state, $\dot{\beta} = 0$ and after algebraic simplification:

$$
\dot{\psi}_{des} = \frac{v \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K_s v^2\right)}
$$

where the **stability factor** is:

$$K_s = \frac{m (b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f})}{2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}$$

**Step 6 — ECU implementation.**
The ESC ECU compares $\dot{\psi}_{des}$ to measured $\dot{\psi}_{act}$ from the yaw-rate gyroscope and computes the corrective moment.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Safe Following Distance for ACC Feature

**Step 1 — Define time gap.**
The driver selects time gap $t_g$ (typically 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, or 2.5 s).

**Step 2 — Compute dynamic following gap.**
For ego vehicle velocity $v_{ego}$:

$$d_{gap}(t) = v_{ego}(t) \cdot t_g$$

**Step 3 — Add minimum standstill distance.**
To prevent nose-to-nose contact when both vehicles stop:

$$d_{safe} = v_{ego}(t) \cdot t_g + d_{min}$$

**Step 4 — Compute relative velocity and required deceleration.**
If target vehicle is at distance $D$ and ego velocity $v_{ego}$:

$$a_{req} = \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2(D - d_{safe})}$$

If $a_{req} > a_{max,comfort}$ (≈ 3 m/s²), FCW is triggered.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feature Comparison

| Feature | Primary Sensor | Actuator | Loop Time | Communication Protocol |
|---------|----------------|----------|-----------|------------------------|
| ABS | Wheel Speed Sensor (×4) | Hydraulic Modulator | **5 – 10 ms** | CAN 500 kbps |
| TCS | WSS + Yaw Sensor | Engine ECU + Brake | **10 – 20 ms** | CAN 500 kbps |
