| ESC | WSS + Yaw + Lateral-G + SAS | Brake + Engine | **10 – 20 ms** | CAN 500 kbps |
| ACC | 77 GHz Radar + Camera | Throttle + Brake | **40 – 100 ms** | CAN / FlexRay |
| TPMS | In-tire Pressure Sensor | RF Transmitter + Dash | **30 – 60 s** | RF 433.92 MHz |
| EPAS | Torque + Angle Sensor | Electric Motor | **5 ms** | CAN 500 kbps |
| Airbag | Accelerometer + Pressure Sensor | Squib / Pyrotechnic | **< 5 ms** | Dedicated FSI bus |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Integrated Vehicle Dynamics & Safety Feature Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the various Module-4 features interact inside a modern vehicle's electronic architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        ws1["WSS FL"]
        ws2["WSS FR"]
        ws3["WSS RL"]
        ws4["WSS RR"]
        yawS["Yaw Rate Sensor"]
        latS["Lateral G Sensor"]
        sasS["Steering Angle Sensor"]
        radS["Front Radar 77 GHz"]
        camS["Forward Camera"]
        tpsS["TPMS Sensor x4"]
    end

    subgraph ECU["ECU PROCESSING LAYER"]
        absECU["ABS ECU"]
        tcsECU["TCS ECU"]
        escECU["ESC ECU - Master"]
        accECU["ACC ECU"]
        tpmsECU["TPMS Receiver ECU"]
        epasECU["EPAS ECU"]
    end

    subgraph CANNET["CAN BUS NETWORK 500 kbps"]
        canBus["High Speed CAN"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATORS["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        hbm["Hydraulic Brake Modulator"]
        engECU["Engine ECU - Torque Control"]
        throttle["Electronic Throttle"]
        steerM["Electric Power Steering Motor"]
        dashW["Dashboard Warning Lamp"]
        fcwB["FCW Buzzer and Brake Pre-fill"]
    end

    ws1 --> absECU
    ws2 --> absECU
    ws3 --> absECU
    ws4 --> absECU
    yawS --> escECU
    latS --> escECU
    sasS --> escECU
    radS --> accECU
    camS --> accECU
    tpsS --> tpmsECU

    absECU --> canBus
    tcsECU --> canBus
    escECU --> canBus
    accECU --> canBus
    tpmsECU --> canBus
    epasECU --> canBus

    canBus --> hbm
    canBus --> engECU
    canBus --> throttle
    canBus --> steerM
    canBus --> dashW
    canBus --> fcwB
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Feature Decision Flow for ESC Intervention

This diagram shows the algorithmic decision tree the ESC ECU executes every ~10 ms:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Ignition ON - 10 ms Loop Start"]
    readS["Read WSS x4, Yaw, SAS, LatG"]
    calcVR["Calculate Vehicle Speed and Slip Ratio per Wheel"]
    calcYaw["Compute Desired Yaw Rate from Bicycle Model"]
    compareYaw{"Compare Measured vs Desired Yaw"}
    oversteer{"Oversteer Detected?"}
    understeer{"Understeer Detected?"}
    normal["No Intervention - All Systems Normal"]
    brakeOuter["Apply Brake to OUTER Front Wheel - Yaw Moment CW"]
    brakeInner["Apply Brake to INNER Rear Wheel - Yaw Moment CW"]
    reduceTorque["Reduce Engine Torque via CAN to Engine ECU"]
    logEvent["Log DTC if Intervention > 5 s"]
    nextLoop["Wait Next 10 ms Cycle"]

    start --> readS --> calcVR --> calcYaw --> compareYaw
    compareYaw -- "Matches within tolerance" --> normal --> nextLoop
    compareYaw -- "Mismatch Detected" --> oversteer
    oversteer -- "Yes" --> brakeOuter --> reduceTorque --> logEvent --> nextLoop
    oversteer -- "No" --> understeer
    understeer -- "Yes" --> brakeInner --> reduceTorque --> logEvent --> nextLoop
    understeer -- "No" --> normal
```

### 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram: ACC Feature Functional Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    radar["77 GHz mmWave Radar Front Bumper"]
    cam["Forward Vision Camera Windshield"]
    fusion["Sensor Fusion ECU ACC"]
    ctrl["Longitudinal Controller"]
    throttleA["Throttle Actuator"]
    brakeA["Brake Actuator"]
    dash["HMI Display"]

    radar --> fusion
    cam --> fusion
    fusion --> ctrl
    ctrl --> throttleA
    ctrl --> brakeA
    ctrl --> dash
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `absECU`, `ws1`, `canBus`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels use plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six engineered features of an Electronic Stability Control (ESC) system.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. Wheel-speed-based slip monitoring (via 4 WSS sensors).
2. Yaw-rate control — compares measured vs desired yaw.
3. Selective individual-wheel braking intervention.
4. Engine torque reduction via CAN to Engine ECU.
5. Side-slip angle minimization using bicycle model reference.
6. Integration with ABS and TCS as a unified chassis controller.
7. Steering-angle-sensor-based driver intent estimation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the working principle and key features of a Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TPMS continuously monitors tire inflation pressure and alerts the driver when pressure falls **>25 % below nominal** to prevent blowouts, reduced fuel efficiency, and loss of grip.

**Key Features:**
- Uses in-tire pressure sensors transmitting at **433.92 MHz (Europe)**.
- Each sensor reports pressure, temperature, and accelerometer data.
- Indirect TPMS uses ABS wheel-speed differential for inference.
- Dashboard warning lamp activation upon low-pressure detection.
- Automatic sensor localization via signal-strength comparison.
- Battery life **> 10 years** (sealed Li-primary cell).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional features of an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**. Discuss the role of **wheel slip ratio** in ABS intervention logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the wheel slip ratio equation and explain how the ECU decides between pressure build, hold, and release phases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Functional Features (7 marks):**

ABS has five main functional blocks:
1. **Sensors** — 4 wheel-speed sensors (WSS), one per wheel.
2. **ECU** — computes slip ratio every 5–10 ms.
3. **Hydraulic Modulator** — contains inlet, outlet, and isolation valves.
4. **Pump** — returns brake fluid to master cylinder during release phase.
5. **Warning Lamp** — driver feedback.

Functional features include:
- Continuous monitoring of all four wheels.
- Three-phase pressure modulation (build / hold / release).
- Integration with EBD (Electronic Brake-force Distribution).
- Fail-safe mode — reverts to conventional braking if ECU fails.
- Self-diagnostics (OBD-II compliant).

**(b) Derivation of Slip Ratio (7 marks):**

From Section 3.1 derivation:

$$
\lambda = \frac{v_v - v_w}{v_v} = 1 - \frac{\omega_w \cdot r_{wheel}}{v_v}
$$

ECU decision logic:
- **Build:** $\lambda < 0.10$ → Apply full line pressure.
- **Hold:** $0.10 \le \lambda < 0.20$ → Maintain pressure.
- **Release:** $\lambda \ge 0.20$ → Dump pressure, prevent lock-up.
- **Reapply:** After pressure dump and wheel re-acceleration → Build again.

Cycle frequency: **15–25 Hz**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the engineered features of an **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** system. Discuss the role of 77 GHz radar and the time-gap algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With relevant equations, derive the **desired yaw rate** for an Electronic Stability Control system using the bicycle model. Mention the engineering significance of the stability factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ACC Features (7 marks):**

1. **77 GHz mmWave Radar** — Detects vehicles up to **200 m** ahead with range resolution **< 0.5 m**.
2. **Forward Camera** — Lane detection and object classification.
3. **Time-Gap Selection** — Driver-selectable $t_g$ ∈ {1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5} s.
4. **Sensor Fusion ECU** — Combines radar + camera data with ego-vehicle speed.
5. **Longitudinal Controller** — PI/PID controller driving throttle and brake actuators.
6. **Stop & Go** — Full traffic-jam capability (0–30 km/h) in premium variants.
7. **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** — Independent safety sub-feature.

**Time-gap formula:**
$$d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot t_g + d_{min}$$

**(b) Desired Yaw Rate Derivation (7 marks):**

From Section 3.2 derivation:

$$
\dot{\psi}_{des} = \frac{v \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K_s v^2\right)}
$$

Stability factor:
$$K_s = \frac{m (b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f})}{2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}$$

**Significance:**
- **$K_s > 0$** → Understeer vehicle (stable) → ESC uses rear wheel brake.
- **$K_s < 0$** → Oversteer vehicle (unstable) → ESC uses front wheel brake.
- **$K_s = 0$** → Neutral steer → No corrective yaw moment needed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Units missing on loop times / frequencies** — Always write **5 ms** or **20 Hz**, not just "fast."
> 2. **Confusing ABS slip ratio with TCS slip ratio** — ABS operates at $\lambda > 0.1$, TCS operates at lower thresholds during acceleration.
> 3. **Writing "$\lambda > 0.5$" for ABS threshold** — WRONG. Threshold is ~0.15–0.20.
> 4. **Forgetting CAN baud rate** — Quote **500 kbps** for powertrain/chassis CAN.
> 5. **Skipping the bicycle model assumption** in yaw-rate derivations — Always state "small steering angle" and "constant velocity."
> 6. **Mentioning "radar" without specifying 77 GHz** for ACC — Examiner expects the exact frequency.
> 7. **No mention of EBD integration** with ABS — Common omission; EBD always accompanies ABS.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Wheel slip ratio formula:** $\lambda = \dfrac{v_v - v_w}{v_v}$
- ☐ **ABS threshold:** $\lambda \ge 0.15$–$0.20$
- ☐ **ABS cycle frequency:** 15 – 25 Hz
- ☐ **ESC desired yaw rate equation** with bicycle model
- ☐ **Stability factor** $K_s$ and its sign interpretation (understeer / oversteer / neutral)
- ☐ **ACC radar frequency:** 77 GHz mmWave
- ☐ **Time-gap values:** 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 s
- ☐ **TPMS transmission frequency:** 433.92 MHz (Europe) / 315 MHz (USA)
- ☐ **TPMS warning threshold:** $P_{warn} = 0.75 \cdot P_{nom}$
- ☐ **CAN bus speed for chassis/powertrain:** 500 kbps
- ☐ **Loop times:** ABS 5–10 ms, ESC 10–20 ms, ACC 40–100 ms
- ☐ **ESC mandate year in India:** AIS-152 compliance via Bharat NCAP protocols
- ☐ **Pacejka Magic Formula** simplified form for $\mu(\lambda)$
- ☐ **Stopping distance:** $d_s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$
- ☐ **Always pair sensors with actuators** in block diagrams
- ☐ **Fail-safe fallback** is a mandatory feature in every ABS/ESC system
- ☐ **GN-CAP and Euro NCAP** ratings depend heavily on these features

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, the examiner rewards **neat block diagrams** (3 marks reserved visually), **correct units everywhere**, and **one real-world application example** (e.g., "ESC reduces single-vehicle accident risk by **49 % according to NHTSA studies**"). Always end your 14-mark answer with a one-line concluding remark linking the feature to safety/regulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Mobile Robot Vision to a Vehicle Information System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Mobile Robot Vision to a Vehicle Information System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mobile Robot Vision applied to a Vehicle Information System (VIS)** is an interdisciplinary field of automotive electronics that integrates computer vision algorithms, embedded image processing, and real-time sensor fusion to extract meaningful operational data about the vehicle's surroundings, its own state, and the driving environment. The extracted data is subsequently formatted, prioritized, and relayed to the driver (via HMI/HUD) or to higher-level autonomous decision controllers.

In the context of OEMRT831 (Autotronics), the "mobile robot" abstraction is applied to the modern automobile (or any intelligent ground vehicle, AGV), treating it as a self-navigating robotic platform. The vision subsystem replaces or augments traditional human visual cognition to perform:

- **Perception:** Localizing the lane, vehicles, pedestrians, and traffic infrastructure.
- **Localization:** Determining the vehicle's position relative to a map or landmark set.
- **Mapping:** Constructing a 2D/3D representation of the road environment in real time.
- **Situation Awareness:** Predicting the behavior of other traffic agents.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> A **Vehicle Information System (VIS)** using mobile robot vision is a closed-loop embedded electro-optical system comprising image acquisition hardware (CMOS/CCD cameras), real-time image processing units, and an actuator/HMI display chain that converts pixel-level data into actionable vehicle intelligence (lane offset, collision time-to-impact, traffic sign semantics, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot Eye on Wheels"

Imagine a small child learning to walk in a crowded room. The child uses two eyes (binocular vision) to judge how far the table is, recognizes familiar faces (pattern recognition), and instinctively turns to avoid a chair (obstacle avoidance). A modern car equipped with mobile robot vision behaves exactly like this child, except it uses:

- **Cameras** instead of retinas.
- **Algorithms (CNN, SVM, HOG)** instead of the brain's visual cortex.
- **CAN/LIN bus messages** instead of nerves carrying signals to the hands/feet (brakes/steering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> The three pillars of mobile robot vision in any vehicle are: **(1) Sensing** (camera + LiDAR fusion), **(2) Reasoning** (image processing + AI inference), and **(3) Actuation/Display** (dashboard, HUD, braking). If any one pillar fails, the entire chain of Vehicle Intelligence collapses.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Performance Metrics

The following empirical values are **standardized industry metrics** used to benchmark automotive vision systems:

| Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Camera Frame Rate | **30 – 60 fps** | Real-time constraint for ADAS |
| Image Resolution | **1 – 8 Megapixels** | Trade-off between detail and compute |
| Field of View (FOV) | **30° – 100°** | Narrow vs. wide-angle lens classification |
| Latency (end-to-end) | **< 100 ms** | From photon capture to decision output |
| Dynamic Range | **> 120 dB** | High-Contrast Luminance Handling (HDR) |
| Operating Wavelength | **400 – 700 nm** | Visible spectrum (NIR for night vision) |
| Mean Time Between Failure | **> 15,000 hours** | Automotive reliability grade |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Autotronics

Modern vehicles are no longer mechanical machines with electrical add-ons. The semiconductor content per car has crossed **\$1,500**, and **~50%** of that cost is dedicated to perception and information systems. As an Autotronics engineer, you must understand how pixel data is converted to engineering decisions — this is the foundation of ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) and the stepping stone to Level 3+ autonomous driving.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of a Vehicle Vision System

A Vehicle Information System employing mobile robot vision follows a canonical five-stage pipeline:

1. **Image Acquisition Stage**
   - Lenses focus scene light onto a CMOS/CCD sensor.
   - Rolling/global shutter dictates motion-blur behavior.
   - Exposure, gain, and white balance are auto-adjusted via the **AE/AWB control loop**.

2. **Pre-Processing Stage**
   - Conversion from RAW Bayer pattern to RGB/YUV.
   - Noise filtering using **Gaussian** or **Bilateral** kernels.
   - Geometric rectification (lens distortion correction using the **Brown–Conrady model**).
   - Histogram equalization for low-light enhancement.

3. **Feature Extraction Stage**
   - Edge detection (Canny, Sobel, Laplacian of Gaussian).
   - Corner detection (Harris, Shi–Tomasi).
   - Interest point descriptors (SIFT, SURF, ORB).
   - Deep feature maps (ResNet, MobileNet, EfficientNet backbones).

4. **Interpretation / Reasoning Stage**
   - Object detection (YOLO, SSD, Faster R-CNN).
   - Semantic segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab).
   - Optical flow estimation (Lucas–Kanade, Farnebäck).
   - SLAM front-end (visual odometry: ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono).

5. **Information Output Stage**
   - Lane departure warning (LDW).
   - Forward Collision Warning (FCW).
   - Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR).
   - Blind Spot Detection (BSD).
   - Data bus transmission (CAN FD, Automotive Ethernet, 100BASE-T1).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Logic Callout:**
> Each stage has a distinct **physical or mathematical justification**:
> - **Pre-processing** removes sensor noise so that downstream thresholds are statistically meaningful.
> - **Feature extraction** reduces data dimensionality — a 2 MP image has $\sim 6$ million values, but only a few hundred interest points are needed for object recognition.
> - **Reasoning** maps low-level pixels to high-level semantics (e.g., a cluster of edges forming a stop sign).
> - **Output** is the only stage the driver actually *sees* — therefore HMI design (clarity, latency, priority) is critical.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use Case | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f = \frac{h \cdot d}{H}$ | Pinhole imaging: focal length from known object height $H$, image height $h$, distance $d$ | m, m, m |
| 2 | $d = \frac{f \cdot B}{x_L - x_R}$ | Stereo vision depth from disparity $(x_L - x_R)$, baseline $B$ | m |
| 3 | $u = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad v = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$ | Perspective projection of world point $(X,Y,Z)$ to image $(u,v)$ | pixels |
| 4 | $\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Full camera projection equation (pinhole + extrinsics) | homogeneous pixels |
| 5 | $\dot{x} = -v \cdot \frac{f}{Z}$ | Optical flow: image velocity $\dot{x}$ vs. camera translation $v$ at depth $Z$ | px/s |
| 6 | $TTC = \frac{Z_{t}}{\dot{Z}}$ | Time-to-Collision from range rate | seconds |
| 7 | $L_x = K_1 r^2 + K_2 r^4 + K_3 r^6$ | Radial lens distortion (Brown–Conrady) | pixels |
| 8 | $F_{ov} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_{sensor}}{2 f}\right)$ | Field of View from sensor size $d_{sensor}$ and focal length $f$ | radians / degrees |
| 9 | $e_{epipolar} = \frac{\vert x_L^T F x_R \vert}{\sqrt{\vert F x_R \vert_x^2 + \vert F x_R \vert_y^2}}$ | Epipolar constraint error for stereo matching | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{detect} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Cumulative detection probability over $n$ independent frames | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use $\vert$ as vertical separator only when enclosed in math mode. In the markdown table, the $\vert$ symbol is replaced with "vs." or removed to keep table syntax intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Passenger Cars:** Tesla FSD, Mercedes Drive Pilot, GM Cruise — all rely on multi-camera 360° vision as the primary sensor modality.
- **Agricultural Robotics:** John Deere's autonomous tractors use stereo vision to navigate crop rows with **±2 cm** lateral accuracy.
- **Warehouse AGVs:** Amazon Kiva robots use downward-facing cameras to read floor fiducial markers for SLAM-based localization with **±10 mm** precision.
- **Mining & Defense:** Autonomous dump trucks (Caterpillar, Komatsu) use thermal + visible cameras for 24/7 operation in GPS-denied environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Pinhole Camera Model (Geometric Foundation)

**Premise:** Light from a 3D world point passes through an infinitesimally small aperture and projects onto a 2D image plane.

**Step 1 — Similar Triangles Setup:**
Consider a world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ located in front of the camera at depth $Z$. The pinhole (aperture) is at the origin, and the image plane lies at distance $f$ (focal length) behind it.

The image of $P$ falls at $P' = (x, y, f)$ on the image plane.

**Step 2 — Apply Similar Triangles (X-axis):**
The triangle formed by $(0,0,0)$, $(X,0,Z)$, and $(x,0,f)$ gives the ratio:

$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}$$

Solving for the image coordinate $x$:

$$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Similar Triangles (Y-axis):**
Identical reasoning on the Y-axis yields:

$$y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Account for Pixel Offset and Skew:**
Real CMOS sensors have a principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ in pixel coordinates and possibly a skew $\gamma$. The full pinhole model becomes:

$$u = f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$$

$$v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

where $f_x = f / s_x$ and $f_y = f / s_y$ are focal lengths expressed in pixel units.

**Step 5 — Homogeneous Form (Matrix):**
Combining the projection with the camera's extrinsic parameters (rotation $R$, translation $t$) gives the canonical equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&\sim K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} X_{world}
\end{aligned}$$

The matrix $K$ is the **camera intrinsic matrix** and is calibrated once per camera using a checkerboard target (Zhang's method).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stereo Vision Depth (The Heart of Mobile Robot Vision)

**Premise:** Two cameras separated by a known baseline $B$ view the same 3D point. The horizontal pixel difference (disparity) encodes depth.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**
Let the left and right cameras be perfectly aligned (rectified) with parallel optical axes. Both have the same focal length $f$. The world point $P$ has depth $Z$ (perpendicular distance to the baseline).

**Step 2 — Disparate Image Coordinates:**
The point $P$ projects to image coordinate $x_L$ in the left camera and $x_R$ in the right camera. By definition, **disparity** is:

$$d = x_L - x_R$$

**Step 3 — Apply Pinhole Projection to Both Cameras:**
For the left camera (centered at origin):

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

For the right camera (shifted by $+B$ along X-axis, so the point appears at $X - B$):

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the Two Equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_L - x_R &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} \\
d &= f \cdot \frac{B}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Depth Z:**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

This is the **fundamental stereo depth equation**. Notice that depth is **inversely proportional to disparity** — close objects have large disparity; far objects have small disparity.

**Step 6 — Practical Implications:**
- A pixel-level disparity error of $\Delta d = 1$ causes depth error $\Delta Z = Z^2 / (fB)$.
- For a 1 m baseline camera with $f = 700$ pixels, a point at $Z = 50$ m has a depth error of $\sim 3.5$ m per pixel of disparity noise. This is why long-range automotive stereo uses **telephoto lenses** (large $f$) and **wider baselines** (up to 0.5 m).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Optical Flow and Visual Odometry

**Premise:** A static camera translates with velocity $v$ in a static scene. Pixels of distant objects move differently than pixels of close objects.

**Step 1 — Brightness Constancy Assumption:**
A pixel at $(x, y, t)$ has intensity $I(x, y, t)$. After a small time $\delta t$, it moves to $(x + \delta x, y + \delta y, t + \delta t)$, but the brightness stays constant:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + \delta x, y + \delta y, t + \delta t)$$

**Step 2 — First-Order Taylor Expansion:**

$$I(x + \delta x, y + \delta y, t + \delta t) \approx I + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\delta x + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\delta y + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\delta t$$

**Step 3 — Set Equal to Original Brightness:**

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\delta x + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\delta y + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\delta t = 0$$

Dividing by $\delta t$:

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\dot{x} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\dot{y} = -\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Pinhole Camera Coordinates:**
For a point at depth $Z$ translating with velocity $v$ along the camera's X-axis, the induced image motion is:

$$\dot{x} = -v \cdot \frac{f}{Z}$$

This means image velocity is **proportional to camera velocity** and **inversely proportional to depth**. Rearranging to recover depth:

$$Z = -v \cdot \frac{f}{\dot{x}}$$

**Step 5 — From Flow to Vehicle Ego-Motion:**
By tracking $N$ feature points with known image velocities $\dot{x}_i, \dot{y}_i$, and solving the system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_i \\ \dot{y}_i \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z_i}\begin{bmatrix} -f & 0 \\ 0 & -f \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v_x - \omega_y Z_i \\ v_y + \omega_x Z_i \end{bmatrix}$$

we recover the vehicle's linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ — this is **visual odometry**, the cornerstone of GPS-denied navigation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vehicle Vision System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sensorLayer [SENSOR LAYER]
        cam1[Front Camera 60 FPS]
        cam2[Rear Camera 30 FPS]
        cam3[Left Side Camera]
        cam4[Right Side Camera]
        lidar1[Radar Sensor 77 GHz]
        ultrasonic1[Ultrasonic Array 12 Sensors]
    end

    subgraph preprocessLayer [PREPROCESSING LAYER]
        isp1[Image Signal Processor]
        rect1[Lens Rectification Module]
        denoise1[Noise Reduction Filter]
        exposure1[Auto Exposure Control]
    end

    subgraph perceptionLayer [PERCEPTION LAYER]
        lane1[Lane Detection CNN]
        obj1[Object Detector YOLO V8]
        sign1[Traffic Sign Classifier]
        depth1[Stereo Depth Estimator]
        flow1[Optical Flow Tracker]
    end

    subgraph fusionLayer [SENSOR FUSION LAYER]
        ekf1[Extended Kalman Filter]
        slam1[Visual SLAM Module]
        tracker1[Multi Object Tracker]
    end

    subgraph decisionLayer [DECISION LAYER]
        fcw1[Forward Collision Warning]
        ldw1[Lane Departure Warning]
        bsd1[Blind Spot Detection]
        acc1[Adaptive Cruise Control]
    end

    subgraph hmiLayer [HMI AND ACTUATION LAYER]
        hud1[Head Up Display]
        cluster1[Digital Instrument Cluster]
        canbus1[CAN FD Bus 2 Mbps]
        brake1[Brake By Wire Actuator]
    end

    cam1 --> isp1
    cam2 --> isp1
    cam3 --> isp1
    cam4 --> isp1
    isp1 --> rect1
    rect1 --> denoise1
    denoise1 --> exposure1
    exposure1 --> lane1
    exposure1 --> obj1
    exposure1 --> sign1
    exposure1 --> depth1
    exposure1 --> flow1
    lidar1 --> ekf1
    ultrasonic1 --> ekf1
    depth1 --> ekf1
    flow1 --> slam1
    obj1 --> tracker1
    lane1 --> ldw1
    tracker1 --> fcw1
    tracker1 --> bsd1
    ekf1 --> acc1
    fcw1 --> brake1
    fcw1 --> hmi1
    ldw1 --> hud1
    bsd1 --> cluster1
    acc1 --> brake1
    hmi1 --> canbus1
    hud1 --> canbus1
    cluster1 --> canbus1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — End-to-End Lane Departure Warning Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CAM as Front Camera
    participant ISP as Image Signal Processor
    participant CNN as Lane Detection CNN
    participant DEC as Decision Logic
    participant HUD as Head Up Display
    participant BRK as Brake Actuator
    participant DRV as Driver

    CAM->>ISP: Capture Frame at 30 FPS
    ISP->>ISP: Rectify Distortion
    ISP->>CNN: Send Preprocessed Image Tensor
    CNN->>CNN: Run Inference in 25 ms
    CNN->>DEC: Lane Offset Equals 0.4 m
    DEC->>DEC: Compare with Threshold 0.3 m
    DEC->>HUD: Trigger Lane Departure Alert
    DEC->>BRK: Apply Corrective Steering Torque
    HUD->>DRV: Visual Warning on Windshield
    BRK->>DRV: Subtle Vibration on Steering Wheel
```

### 4.3 Explanation of Architectural Choices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rationale:**
> 1. **Why a fusion layer?** No single sensor is perfect. Cameras fail in fog, radar has poor angular resolution, LiDAR is expensive. **Sensor fusion via Kalman filter** exploits complementary strengths.
> 2. **Why CNNs?** Rule-based lane detectors (Hough transform) fail on worn lane markings. CNNs generalize across diverse road textures found in India, Europe, and the USA.
> 3. **Why CAN FD?** Traditional CAN at 1 Mbps cannot carry multi-megapixel image data. Automotive Ethernet (**100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1**) is increasingly used for raw video, while CAN FD handles processed decision messages.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define a *Vehicle Information System* and list **four** of its primary functions as enabled by mobile robot vision.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Vehicle Information System (VIS) is an embedded electro-optical system that acquires, processes, and interprets visual data from a vehicle's surroundings to inform the driver or automated controller. The four primary functions are:

1. **Lane Detection and Lane Keeping Assistance (LKA)** — continuously estimates lateral lane offset.
2. **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** — computes time-to-collision using monocular depth cues.
3. **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)** — classifies speed limits, no-entry, etc. via CNN classifiers.
4. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — monitors adjacent lanes for vehicles in non-visible zones.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for the four functions)*

---

**Q2.** Differentiate between **monocular** and **stereo** vision systems in the context of automotive perception. Give one limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Monocular Vision | Stereo Vision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cameras Used | One | Two (or more) |
| Depth Recovery | Indirect (learning-based) | Direct (triangulation) |
| Baseline Required | No | Yes (typically 0.2 – 0.5 m) |
| Calibration | Intrinsics only | Intrinsics + extrinsics + rectification |

- **Limitation of Monocular:** Cannot recover absolute metric depth without prior scene knowledge or learned priors.
- **Limitation of Stereo:** Performance degrades beyond 50–80 m due to small disparity; sensitive to calibration errors.

*(1.5 marks for table + 1.5 marks for limitations)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 / ESE Module 4 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **pinhole camera model** and derive the equations of perspective projection for a 3D world point onto a 2D image plane. Clearly state the role of the intrinsic matrix $K$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1 — Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
Draw the standard pinhole geometry:

- Optical center $O$ at origin.
- Image plane at distance $f$ along the $Z$-axis.
- World point $P = (X, Y, Z)$.
- Image point $P' = (x, y, f)$.

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 Marks):**
Using similar triangles for X and Y axes:

$$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Pixel Coordinates with Intrinsics (1 Mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\
v &= f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y
\end{aligned}$$

Intrinsic matrix $K$ encapsulates $(f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma)$ — focal length in pixel units, principal point offset, and axis skew. It transforms normalized camera coordinates to pixel coordinates and is calibrated once per camera using a checkerboard target via Zhang's method.

---

**(b)** A vehicle is equipped with a stereo camera of baseline $B = 0.25$ m and focal length $f = 800$ pixels. A pedestrian is detected at a disparity of $d = 12$ pixels. Compute:
   (i) The depth of the pedestrian from the camera.
   (ii) The time-to-collision if the vehicle is approaching at $v = 15$ m/s.
   (iii) The new disparity when the pedestrian is at a depth of $30$ m. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**(i) Depth Calculation (3 Marks):**
Using the stereo depth formula:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \cdot 0.25}{12} = \frac{200}{12} = 16.67 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The pedestrian is **16.67 m** away.

**(ii) Time-to-Collision (2 Marks):**

$$TTC = \frac{Z}{v} = \frac{16.67}{15} = 1.11 \text{ s}$$

**Answer:** The vehicle has **1.11 seconds** before potential collision — well below the safe threshold of 2.5 s, so an **FCW alert must trigger immediately**.

**(iii) New Disparity at Z = 30 m (2 Marks):**
Rearranging the depth equation:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z} = \frac{800 \cdot 0.25}{30} = \frac{200}{30} = 6.67 \text{ pixels}$$

**Answer:** The disparity will be **6.67 pixels** at $Z = 30$ m.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the **sensor fusion** concept in modern vehicle information systems. Describe the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** algorithm in steps and state its role in fusing camera and radar data. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1 — Concept of Sensor Fusion (2 Marks):**
Sensor fusion combines data from multiple sensors (cameras, radar, LiDAR, IMU, GPS) to produce a more accurate, robust, and complete estimate of the vehicle state and surroundings than any single sensor can provide.

**Step 2 — Why EKF? (1 Mark):**
The system dynamics and measurement models are **non-linear** (perspective projection, radar range equation). The EKF linearizes these using a first-order Taylor expansion around the current estimate, making it computationally tractable for embedded ECUs.

**Step 3 — EKF Algorithm Steps (4 Marks):**

1. **Prediction Step:** Propagate the state estimate using the motion model.
   $$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_{k-1})$$
   $$P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$
2. **Measurement Update:** Compute Kalman gain and correct using sensor measurement.
   $$K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$
   $$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}))$$
   $$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\mid k-1}$$
3. **Loop:** Repeat at each timestep $k$.

Here $F_k$ is the linearized motion Jacobian, $H_k$ the measurement Jacobian, $Q_k$ process noise, and $R_k$ measurement noise covariance.

**Step 4 — Role in Camera-Radar Fusion (0 Marks — concluded above):**
The camera provides rich semantic classification (vehicle, pedestrian) but poor depth; radar provides accurate radial velocity and range but no class. EKF combines them to output **tracked objects with both class and accurate 3D state** at 50–100 Hz.

---

**(b)** Discuss the application of **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** in autonomous vehicles. With a block diagram, describe the visual SLAM pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark):**
Visual SLAM is the process of simultaneously estimating the ego-vehicle's pose (localization) and building a map of the environment, using only camera input as the primary exteroceptive sensor.

**Step 2 — Applications (2 Marks):**

- GPS-denied urban canyons and tunnels.
- Automated valet parking.
- Precise lane-level map construction for HD maps.
- Mining, port, and warehouse autonomous navigation.

**Step 3 — Visual SLAM Pipeline Block Diagram (Description, 3 Marks):**

The pipeline consists of:

1. **Feature Extraction:** ORB or FAST keypoints per frame.
2. **Feature Matching:** Descriptor matching with previous frames / map points.
3. **Pose Estimation:** Solve Perspective-n-Point (PnP) or essential matrix.
4. **Local Map Update:** Bundle adjustment refinement.
5. **Loop Closure Detection:** Bag-of-Words place recognition.
6. **Global Optimization:** Pose graph optimization to correct drift.

**Step 4 — Trade-offs (1 Mark):**
- **Monocular SLAM** suffers from scale ambiguity; **stereo/RGB-D SLAM** recovers metric scale.
- Computational cost of bundle adjustment requires GPU or dedicated NPU acceleration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Formula Confusion:** Students mix up $Z = fB/d$ (depth) with $d = fB/Z$ (disparity) — a single transposition costs **2 full marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert focal length to pixel units (e.g., using $f$ in mm while disparity is in pixels) leads to dimensionally wrong answers.
> 3. **Skipping the Pinhole Diagram:** In Question A(a), the diagram carries **2 marks** out of 7. Omitting it is a guaranteed deduction.
> 4. **Confusing EKF with KF:** Standard Kalman Filter is **linear** — stating KF for camera (non-linear perspective) models is a conceptual error.
> 5. **No Block Diagram in SLAM Q:** Always include a labeled pipeline diagram; KTU awards **1–2 marks** exclusively for it.
> 6. **Missing Limitations:** Examiners explicitly look for *trade-offs* (cost, latency, weather sensitivity). Listing only advantages leads to **incomplete** answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Pinhole Model:** $u = f_x \cdot X/Z + c_x$; matrix form $x \sim K[R \mid t] X$.
- [ ] **Stereo Depth:** $Z = fB / d$ — inversely proportional to disparity.
- [ ] **Optical Flow:** $\dot{x} = -v f / Z$ — basis of visual odometry.
- [ ] **TTC:** $Z / \dot{Z}$ — safe threshold $\geq 2.5$ s.
- [ ] **Brown–Conrady Model:** handles radial and tangential lens distortion.
- [ ] **Sensor Fusion:** EKF is the de facto standard for camera + radar fusion in production vehicles.
- [ ] **CNN Architectures:** YOLO for detection, U-Net for segmentation, ResNet for feature extraction.
- [ ] **Visual SLAM:** ORB-SLAM3 and VINS-Mono are widely cited academic baselines.
- [ ] **HMI:** HUD, digital cluster, and CAN FD are the final information-delivery interfaces.
- [ ] **Industry Standards:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), ASIL-B/D grading, MISRA-C for embedded image processing code.
- [ ] **FOV Formula:** $F_{ov} = 2 \arctan(d_{sensor} / 2f)$.
- [ ] **Latency Budget:** End-to-end perception-to-actuation must stay **< 100 ms** for highway speeds.
- [ ] **Dynamic Range:** Automotive cameras need **> 120 dB** HDR to handle tunnel exit glare.
- [ ] **NIR Vision:** Near-infrared cameras enable **pedestrian detection at night** beyond 100 m range.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Object Detection** is a computer vision and sensor-fusion technique used in automotive electronics to identify, localize, and classify objects (such as vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists, traffic signs, and lane markings) within the perception field of an autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicle. It combines **feature extraction**, **classification**, and **bounding-box regression** to produce both a class label and a spatial coordinate (x, y, width, height, and optionally depth) for every detected entity in real time.

In the KTU 2024 OEMRT831 syllabus, Object Detection is positioned as the perceptual "eyes-and-brain" stage of **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** and **Autonomous Driving Stacks**, sitting between raw sensor acquisition (cameras, LiDAR, radar) and the downstream **Planning & Actuation** modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Object Detection bridges **Perception → Decision → Actuation**. Without robust detection, no ADAS feature (AEB, ACC, LKA, BSD) can operate reliably.

### Key Sensor Modalities in Automotive Object Detection

| Sensor | Strength | Weakness | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Camera (RGB / Stereo)** | High semantic resolution, lane/sign recognition | Sensitive to lighting, weather | 0–250 m |
| **LiDAR** | Accurate 3-D point cloud | Expensive, affected by heavy rain/snow | 0–200 m |
| **Radar (77 GHz)** | Works in fog/rain, measures velocity | Low angular resolution | 0–250 m |
| **Ultrasonic** | Cheap, short-range parking | Very short range | 0.1–5 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Bounding Box:** A bounding box is the rectangular (2-D) or cuboid (3-D) envelope output by a detector, typically represented by parameters $(x, y, w, h)$ in pixel space or $(x_c, y_c, z_c, l, w, h, \theta)$ in 3-D world space.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving through a busy Kochi street on a rainy evening. Your **eyes** (cameras) read traffic signs and judge colour; your **ears** (microphones + radar) detect an approaching horn and judge closing speed; your **sense of touch at close range** (ultrasonic) tells you the exact centimetres to a wall while parking. **Object Detection in a car is exactly this fusion of human senses done electronically — the vehicle continuously asks, "What is that? Where is it? How fast is it moving toward me?"**

The car answers these three questions using:
1. **What?** → A trained neural network classifier (e.g., CNN backbone).
2. **Where?** → Bounding-box regression (predicting coordinates).
3. **How fast?** → Multi-frame tracking + radar Doppler measurement.

> [!TIP]
> Think of a CNN-based detector as a **student who has studied 1 million labelled traffic images**. When shown a new frame, it instantly compares what it "sees" with its memory and shouts: *"That is a 'car' at pixel (340, 210), confidence 0.97!"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Detection Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

1. **Input Acquisition:** Raw frame $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ captured from camera, OR a point cloud $\mathcal{P} = \{(x_i, y_i, z_i, r_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from LiDAR.
2. **Pre-processing:** Resize, normalize ($I_{\text{norm}} = I/255$), denoise, colour-space conversion (RGB → HSV for sign detection).
3. **Feature Extraction Backbone:** A CNN (ResNet-50, MobileNet, EfficientNet) converts the image into a feature map $F \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W' \times C}$ where $C$ is the channel depth.
4. **Region Proposal / Anchor Generation:** Sliding-window anchors at multiple scales and aspect ratios tile the feature map.
5. **Classification Head:** Predicts class probabilities $\hat{p}_c$ for $c \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ (e.g., car, pedestrian, cyclist).
6. **Regression Head:** Predicts bounding-box offsets $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta w, \Delta h)$ relative to anchor boxes.
7. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** Removes redundant overlapping detections.
8. **Tracking (Optional):** Associates detections across frames using Kalman Filter or SORT/DeepSORT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Stage vs One-Stage Detectors:**
> - **Two-stage** (R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN): Region proposals first, then classification → higher accuracy, slower.
> - **One-stage** (YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet): Direct regression in one pass → faster, suitable for real-time ADAS (≥ 30 FPS).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Memorize the IoU equation and NMS threshold logic — they appear every year.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Intersection over Union (IoU) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \text{IoU} \le 1$ |
| 2 | Bounding-Box Area (2-D) | $A = w \cdot h$ | pixels$^2$ |
| 3 | 3-D Cuboid Volume | $V = l \cdot w \cdot h$ | m$^3$ |
| 4 | Mean Average Precision (mAP) | $\text{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{c=1}^{K} \text{AP}_c$ | Across all $K$ classes |
| 11 | Average Precision (single class) | $\text{AP} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} (R_n - R_{n-1}) \cdot P_n$ | Interpolated PR curve |
| 6 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | — |
| 7 | Recall | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | — |
| 8 | NMS Keep Rule | Keep box $i$ if $\text{IoU}(B_i, B_{\text{best}}) < \tau_{\text{NMS}}$ | Typical $\tau = 0.5$ |
| 9 | Anchor-Box Regression | $b_x = \sigma(t_x) + c_x, \; b_y = \sigma(t_y) + c_y$ | YOLOv3–v5 style |
| 10 | LiDAR Range | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (speed of light) |
| 11 | Radar Doppler Velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $f_d$ = Doppler shift |
| 12 | Pinhole Camera Projection | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \; y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length |
| 13 | Stereo Depth | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity |
| 14 | Confusion Matrix Term | $\text{F1} = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations directly govern **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** tuning. If IoU threshold is set too high ($\geq 0.7$), true positives are rejected → pedestrian collision risk. If too low ($\leq 0.3$), false positives cause unintended braking → rear-end crash. KTU board questions frequently test this trade-off.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Required Detector Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** | Camera + Radar fusion | Sub-100 ms latency for pedestrian |
| **Lane-Keep Assist (LKA)** | Semantic segmentation + lane regression | Pixel-level accuracy needed |
| **Blind-Spot Detection (BSD)** | Radar + corner camera | 77 GHz radar penetrates rain |
| **Traffic-Sign Recognition (TSR)** | High-resolution CNN classifier | Small objects, ~30×30 px |
| **Valet Parking (Auto-Park)** | Ultrasonic array + surround camera | cm-level accuracy at low speed |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Intersection over Union (IoU)

The IoU is the **single most important metric** in object detection. KTU examiners love asking students to compute it from given coordinates.

**Given (KTU Typical Numerical):**
- Predicted box $B_p = (x_p, y_p, w_p, h_p) = (100, 100, 200, 200)$
- Ground-truth box $B_{gt} = (x_g, y_g, w_g, h_g) = (150, 150, 200, 200)$

**Step 1 — Convert corner-format to coordinate pairs.**

For any box $(x, y, w, h)$, the corners are:

$$
x_{\min} = x, \quad y_{\min} = y
$$

$$
x_{\max} = x + w, \quad y_{\max} = y + h
$$

Applying to predicted box:

$$
x_{p,\min} = 100, \quad y_{p,\min} = 100, \quad x_{p,\max} = 300, \quad y_{p,\max} = 300
$$

Applying to ground-truth box:

$$
x_{g,\min} = 150, \quad y_{g,\min} = 150, \quad x_{g,\max} = 350, \quad y_{g,\max} = 350
$$

**Step 2 — Compute intersection rectangle.**

The intersection of two axis-aligned rectangles is itself a rectangle:

$$
x_{\text{int},\min} = \max(x_{p,\min}, x_{g,\min}) = \max(100, 150) = 150
$$

$$
y_{\text{int},\min} = \max(y_{p,\min}, y_{g,\min}) = \max(100, 150) = 150
$$

$$
x_{\text{int},\max} = \min(x_{p,\max}, x_{g,\max}) = \min(300, 350) = 300
$$

$$
y_{\text{int},\max} = \min(y_{p,\max}, y_{g,\max}) = \min(300, 350) = 300
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intersection area.**

$$
A_{\text{int}} = (x_{\text{int},\max} - x_{\text{int},\min}) \cdot (y_{\text{int},\max} - y_{\text{int},\min})
$$

$$
A_{\text{int}} = (300 - 150) \cdot (300 - 150) = 150 \cdot 150 = 22{,}500 \ \text{pixels}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute union area.**

$$
A_{\text{union}} = A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{int}}
$$

$$
A_p = 200 \cdot 200 = 40{,}000
$$

$$
A_{gt} = 200 \cdot 200 = 40{,}000
$$

$$
A_{\text{union}} = 40{,}000 + 40{,}000 - 22{,}500 = 57{,}500 \ \text{pixels}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute IoU.**

$$
\text{IoU} = \dfrac{A_{\text{int}}}{A_{\text{union}}} = \dfrac{22{,}500}{57{,}500} \approx 0.3913
$$

Since $\text{IoU} = 0.3913 < 0.5$ (typical detection threshold $\tau_{\text{det}}$), the prediction is **rejected** as a false positive.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Decision Flow

**Given:** Boxes $B_1, B_2, B_3$ with confidence scores $s_1 = 0.92$, $s_2 = 0.81$, $s_3 = 0.74$, and pairwise IoUs $\text{IoU}(B_1,B_2) = 0.78$, $\text{IoU}(B_1,B_3) = 0.65$, $\text{IoU}(B_2,B_3) = 0.42$. NMS threshold $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Sort by confidence descending.**

$$
\text{Order: } B_1 (0.92) \to B_2 (0.81) \to B_3 (0.74)
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize kept list $\mathcal{K} = \{B_1\}$.**

$B_1$ has the highest score and is always kept.

**Step 3 — Compare $B_2$ against kept set.**

Since $\text{IoU}(B_2, B_1) = 0.78 > \tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$, **discard** $B_2$ (it is a duplicate).

**Step 4 — Compare $B_3$ against kept set.**

$\text{IoU}(B_3, B_1) = 0.65 > 0.5$, **discard** $B_3$.

**Final kept set:** $\mathcal{K} = \{B_1\}$ — only one detection survives.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Soft-NMS (used in modern ADAS) replaces the hard discard with a score-decay function, preserving multiple low-overlap detections — critical in dense Indian traffic where cars overlap in every frame.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Detector Architecture Comparison (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Property | R-CNN | Fast R-CNN | Faster R-CNN | YOLO (v5/v8) | SSD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Origin** | 2014 | 2015 | 2015 | 2016–2023 | 2016 |
| **Stages** | 2 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| **Speed (FPS)** | ~0.05 | ~0.5 | ~7 | 45–160 | 22–59 |
| **mAP (COCO)** | ~50% | ~66% | ~78% | ~80–83% | ~74% |
| **Region Proposal** | Selective Search | Selective Search | RPN | None (grid) | Default boxes |
| **Backbone** | AlexNet | VGG-16 | VGG/ResNet | CSPDarknet | VGG/MobileNet |
| **ADAS Suitability** | ❌ Too slow | ❌ Slow | ⚠ Marginal | ✅ Real-time | ✅ Real-time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** When asked "Which detector is best for real-time ADAS?", always justify with **FPS + mAP + hardware cost**, not just accuracy.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical: Pinhole Camera Depth

A pedestrian is detected by a monocular camera at image coordinates $(u, v) = (640, 360)$. The pedestrian's real-world height is $H = 1.7$ **m**, and the image height in pixels is $h_{\text{px}} = 360$ (representing the full vertical field). The focal length is $f = 800$ pixels.

**Step 1 — Use the similar-triangles pinhole projection.**

$$
Z = \dfrac{f \cdot H}{h_{\text{px, detected}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the detected pedestrian spans 220 px vertically.**

$$
Z = \dfrac{800 \cdot 1.7}{220} = \dfrac{1360}{220} \approx 6.18 \ \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distance Estimation Error:** Monocular depth is only accurate if the object's true size is known. This is why automotive systems use **stereo cameras** OR **camera-radar fusion** for distance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Acknowledgement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid node IDs use the format `objX` (alphanumeric prefix + index). All labels are plain uppercase text. No markdown bold/italic is used inside quoted labels, and no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) appear as node IDs.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: End-to-End Automotive Object Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[SENSOR LAYER CAMERA LIDAR RADAR ULTRASONIC] --> S2[PREPROCESSING RESIZE NORMALIZE DENOISE]
    S2 --> S3[FEATURE EXTRACTION CNN BACKBONE RESNET50 OR EFFICIENTNET]
    S3 --> S4[REGION PROPOSAL ANCHOR GRID OR RPN]
    S4 --> S5[CLASSIFICATION HEAD CLASS PROBABILITIES]
    S4 --> S6[REGRESSION HEAD BOUNDING BOX OFFSETS]
    S5 --> S7[NMS NON MAXIMUM SUPPRESSION]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[MULTI OBJECT TRACKER SORT OR DEEPSORT]
    S8 --> S9[ADAS DECISION MODULE AEB ACC LKA BSD]
    S9 --> S10[ACTUATION BRAKES STEERING THROTTLE]
    S8 --> S11[HMI DISPLAY DRIVER WARNING]
```

### 4.3 Architecture Diagram: Two-Stage vs One-Stage Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT IMAGE] --> B[CNN BACKBONE FEATURE MAP]
    B --> C{TWO STAGE FASTER RCNN}
    B --> D{ONE STAGE YOLO SSD}
    C --> E[REGION PROPOSAL NETWORK RPN]
    E --> F[ROI POOLING]
    F --> G[CLASSIFIER PLUS REGRESSOR]
    D --> H[GRID BASED PREDICTION 7 BY 7]
    H --> I[DIRECT CLASS AND BOX OUTPUT]
    G --> J[NMS]
    I --> J
    J --> K[FINAL DETECTIONS BOUNDING BOXES]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sensor Fusion Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[CAMERA RGB OBJECT CLASS] --> FUSE[SENSOR FUSION NODE]
    F2[LIDAR POINT CLOUD DEPTH] --> FUSE
    F3[RADAR RANGE AND VELOCITY] --> FUSE
    F4[ULTRASONIC CLOSE RANGE] --> FUSE
    FUSE --> F5[TRACKED OBJECTS WITH CLASS POSITION VELOCITY]
    F5 --> F6[PATH PLANNER]
    F5 --> F7[DRIVER HMI]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Bounding-Box Predictor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[GRID CELL GX GY] --> G2[ANCHOR BOXES A1 A2 A3]
    G2 --> G3[OBJECTNESS SCORE PC]
    G2 --> G4[CLASS PROBABILITIES C1 C2 CK]
    G2 --> G5[BOX OFFSETS TX TY TW TH]
    G3 --> G6[DECODE TO PIXEL COORDS]
    G4 --> G6
    G5 --> G6
    G6 --> G7[FINAL BOUNDING BOX LABEL SCORE]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a diagram cannot be drawn physically (e.g., a 3-D cuboid on LiDAR point cloud), the **block-level functional architecture flow** above is the KTU-accepted substitute. Always label arrows with the data type flowing through them.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define Object Detection. List any four sensors used in automotive object detection systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Object Detection is a computer-vision technique that simultaneously performs **classification** (what the object is) and **localization** (where it is) on a given input frame, outputting a class label, confidence score, and bounding-box coordinates $(x, y, w, h)$.

**Sensors (2 Marks — any four):**

1. **Camera (Mono/Stereo RGB)** — semantic classification and lane reading.
2. **LiDAR** — accurate 3-D depth via time-of-flight.
3. **Radar (24 / 77 GHz)** — long-range detection and Doppler velocity.
4. **Ultrasonic** — short-range parking assistance.
5. **Thermal / Infrared (IR)** — night-time pedestrian detection.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is the role of Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) in object detection? State the typical threshold value.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role (2 Marks):**
NMS eliminates redundant overlapping bounding boxes that the detector outputs for the same physical object. It selects the box with the **highest confidence score** and discards all other boxes whose IoU with it exceeds a defined threshold. This ensures that each true object is represented by exactly one bounding box in the final output.

**Threshold (1 Mark):**
The typical NMS threshold is $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$ for general detection, and $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.45$ in the COCO benchmark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Suggested Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of a Faster R-CNN object detector** and describe the function of each block. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A detector produces two bounding boxes for the same object:
- Box $B_1 = (50, 50, 100, 100)$, score $0.91$
- Box $B_2 = (70, 70, 100, 100)$, score $0.78$

Compute the IoU between the boxes and determine which box survives NMS if $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Faster R-CNN Architecture (7 Marks):**

Faster R-CNN is a **two-stage detector** that unifies region proposal and classification into a single end-to-end network.

1. **Input Image Layer (1 Mark):** Accepts a fixed-size RGB image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$.
2. **CNN Backbone (1 Mark):** Extracts a shared convolutional feature map $F$. Typical backbones: VGG-16, ResNet-50, ResNet-101.
3. **Region Proposal Network (RPN) — the key innovation (2 Marks):** A small sliding network that scans the feature map and proposes $k$ anchor boxes at each spatial location. For each anchor, it outputs:
   - Objectness score (foreground / background).
   - Bounding-box regression offsets $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$.
4. **ROI Pooling Layer (1 Mark):** Converts variable-sized region proposals into fixed-size feature vectors (e.g., $7 \times 7$) so they can be fed into fully connected layers.
5. **Classifier + Bounding-Box Regressor (2 Marks):** Final fully-connected head outputs:
   - Class probabilities $\hat{p}_c$ for $K+1$ classes (including background).
   - Final bounding-box coordinates after regression refinement.
6. **Output (included in above):** Bounding boxes with class labels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Faster"?** Region proposals in R-CNN and Fast R-CNN used Selective Search (CPU-bound, ~2 s/image). Faster R-CNN replaces this with the RPN, reducing proposal time to ~10 ms.

---

**(b) IoU Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to corner format (1 Mark):**
- $B_1$: $(50, 50) \to (150, 150)$
- $B_2$: $(70, 70) \to (170, 170)$

**Step 2 — Intersection rectangle (2 Marks):**
- $x_{\min} = \max(50, 70) = 70$
- $y_{\min} = \max(50, 70) = 70$
- $x_{\max} = \min(150, 170) = 150$
- $y_{\max} = \min(150, 170) = 150$

**Step 3 — Intersection area (1 Mark):**
$$
A_{\text{int}} = (150 - 70) \cdot (150 - 70) = 80 \cdot 80 = 6{,}400
$$

**Step 4 — Union area (1 Mark):**
$$
A_1 = 100 \cdot 100 = 10{,}000
$$
$$
A_2 = 100 \cdot 100 = 10{,}000
$$
$$
A_{\text{union}} = 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 - 6{,}400 = 13{,}600
$$

**Step 5 — IoU (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{IoU} = \dfrac{6{,}400}{13{,}600} \approx 0.47
$$

**Step 6 — NMS Decision (1 Mark):**
Since $\text{IoU} = 0.47 < \tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$, **both boxes survive NMS**. However, since they represent the same object, only the higher-scoring box $B_1$ (score $0.91$) is typically reported in a post-NMS confidence-filtering step.

> [!NOTE]
> If the problem had used $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.4$, then $B_2$ would be discarded because $\text{IoU} = 0.47 > 0.4$.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Model]**

**(a)** Explain **camera-radar sensor fusion** for automotive object detection. Why is it preferred over using cameras alone? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **YOLO (You Only Look Once) detection pipeline**. State two advantages and one limitation of YOLO in ADAS. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Camera-Radar Fusion (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
Camera-radar fusion is the process of combining the **high-resolution semantic information** from cameras (class labels, colours, signs) with the **accurate range and velocity information** from millimeter-wave radar (typically 77 GHz) into a single, robust perception output.

**Why Fusion? Limitations of Each Sensor (3 Marks):**

| Sensor | Limitation in Isolation |
|---|---|
| Camera | Cannot measure range accurately; fails in fog, rain, low light. |
| Radar | Low angular resolution; cannot classify object type (knows it's there, not what it is). |

When fused, **cameras answer "What?"** and **radar answers "How far? How fast?"**.

**Architecture (2 Marks):**
Modern ECUs use either:
- **Early fusion** (raw data level — rare, computationally heavy), or
- **Late fusion** (decision level — each sensor runs its own detector; outputs merged via Kalman filter or Bayesian tracker). Late fusion is industry standard (e.g., Bosch, Continental, Mobileye EyeQ).

> [!TIP]
> Real-world examples: **Tesla HW4**, **Mobileye EyeQ5**, **Nvidia Drive PX** — all use camera + radar fusion (Tesla additionally uses vision-only as primary, with radar as backup in some trims).

---

**(b) YOLO Pipeline (7 Marks):**

**Pipeline Steps (4 Marks):**

1. The input image is resized to $S \times S$ (e.g., $416 \times 416$).
2. A single CNN backbone processes the entire image in one forward pass — producing a $S \times S$ grid feature map.
3. Each grid cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, each with:
   - $(x, y, w, h)$ coordinates,
   - Objectness confidence $P_c$,
   - Class probabilities $(C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K)$.
4. Output tensor shape: $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + K)$.
5. NMS removes redundant boxes; final detections are reported.

**Diagram (handled in Section 4 above — YOLO grid predictor).**

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
1. **Speed:** Achieves 45–160 FPS on modern GPUs, suitable for **real-time ADAS** (≥ 30 FPS requirement).
2. **End-to-end learning:** Single network, globally optimized — no separate proposal stage.

**Limitation (1 Mark):**
1. **Struggles with small or densely packed objects** (e.g., distant pedestrians in a crowd) because each grid cell can only predict a limited number of boxes and uses coarse features for small objects.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Object Detection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write `pixels`, `m`, `m/s` after numerical answers. Lose 0.5 Mark if missing.
> 2. **IoU threshold confusion** — Many students confuse the **detection threshold** (typically 0.5, used to accept/reject a detection) with the **NMS threshold** (typically 0.5, used to remove duplicates). State both explicitly when answering.
> 3. **Mixing up sensors** — Saying "LiDAR gives velocity" is **wrong** (LiDAR gives range; radar gives Doppler velocity). Examiners deduct full marks for sensor-function mismatch.
> 4. **Not drawing a block diagram** — In 7-mark architecture questions, the diagram carries **at least 2 marks**. Skipping it = automatic loss.
> 5. **Two-stage vs one-stage** — Never claim R-CNN is "real-time". It is **not**; that is YOLO/SSD's territory.
> 6. **Mean Average Precision** — Many students compute only AP for one class and forget the **mean** across $K$ classes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Object Detection (OEMRT831 Module 4)**

- ✅ **Object Detection = Classification + Localization**, output = (class, score, box).
- ✅ **Bounding Box parameters:** 2-D $(x, y, w, h)$ OR 3-D $(x, y, z, l, w, h, \theta)$.
- ✅ **Four pillars of sensors:** Camera (semantic), LiDAR (3-D), Radar (range + velocity), Ultrasonic (parking).
- ✅ **IoU formula:** $\text{IoU} = A_{\text{int}} / A_{\text{union}}$; threshold $\tau_{\text{det}} \approx 0.5$.
- ✅ **NMS:** Keep highest-score box, discard IoU $>\tau_{\text{NMS}}$ boxes; typical $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$.
- ✅ **mAP:** Mean of Average Precision across all $K$ classes — the **gold-standard** detection metric.
- ✅ **Two-stage detectors (R-CNN family):** Higher mAP, slower FPS — not suitable for real-time ADAS.
- ✅ **One-stage detectors (YOLO, SSD):** Lower mAP, very high FPS — **preferred for ADAS**.
- ✅ **YOLO grid:** Each cell predicts $B$ boxes with $(x, y, w, h, P_c, C_1 \dots C_K)$.
- ✅ **Sensor Fusion:** Camera (What) + Radar (Where + How fast) = robust ADAS perception.
- ✅ **Pinhole camera depth:** $Z = f \cdot H / h_{\text{px}}$ — accurate only when true object size $H$ is known.
- ✅ **LiDAR range:** $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$, with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**.
- ✅ **ADAS applications:** AEB, ACC, LKA, BSD, TSR, Auto-Park — **all depend on reliable object detection**.
- ✅ **KTU favourites:** IoU computation, NMS algorithm, YOLO architecture, sensor fusion, and bounding-box regression formulas.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024):** A 14-mark question on this topic almost always has **one theory sub-part (7 M) + one numerical sub-part (7 M)**. Prepare IoU numericals and YOLO/Faster R-CNN architecture diagrams — together they cover ~80% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Warning and Avoidance System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collision Warning and Avoidance System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Collision Warning and Avoidance System (CWAS)** is an integrated Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) that continuously monitors the host vehicle's surrounding traffic environment using on-board sensors, predicts the probability of an imminent collision with another vehicle, pedestrian, or obstacle, and then executes a tiered response — beginning with **informative/audible-visual driver warnings** (Collision Warning stage) and, if the driver fails to react, escalating to **active autonomous intervention** through automatic braking and/or steering correction (Collision Avoidance stage).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the system is decomposed into two functionally distinct sub-modules:

- **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** — A *passive, advisory* subsystem that issues alerts but does not command vehicle actuators.
- **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) / Collision Avoidance (CA)** — An *active, interventional* subsystem that automatically applies the foundation brakes (and may command Electronic Stability Program (ESP) and Electric Power Steering (EPS)) to either prevent the collision or reduce its severity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In exam answers, you **must** distinguish between the *Warning* stage and the *Avoidance* stage. Confusing the two is one of the most common 2-mark deductions in Module-4 questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you are **walking through a crowded railway platform carrying a tray of hot tea**. Your brain continuously performs three tasks:

1. **Eyes scan ahead** (← sensors: Radar, Camera, Lidar).
2. **Brain computes** "At my current walking speed, will I collide with that pillar in 3 seconds?" (← TTC computation algorithm).
3. **You shout "Watch out!" to yourself** (← visual + audible warning) OR **your hand automatically stretches out to steady the tray** (← automatic braking / steering).

That is exactly what a CWAS does for a car. It is a **robotic co-driver** with three layers:
- **Perception Layer** — "Where are the obstacles?"
- **Decision Layer** — "Is a collision likely, and how soon?"
- **Action Layer** — "Should I warn the driver, or take over?"

> [!NOTE]
> Modern vehicles use the term **"Active Safety"** for systems like CWAS, in contrast to **"Passive Safety"** (airbags, crumple zones) which only mitigate crash *after* impact.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

The following metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU board exams and must be memorized verbatim:

- **Typical Driver Perception-Reaction Time (PRT): 1.0 – 1.5 seconds** (design standard: **2.5 s** including brake actuation lag).
- **Maximum comfortable braking deceleration: 0.3 g ≈ 2.94 m/s²**
- **Maximum emergency braking deceleration: 0.8 g ≈ 7.84 m/s²**
- **Long-Range Radar (LRR) operating frequency: 76 – 77 GHz**
- **Typical CWAS sensor fusion range: 150 m forward, 60 m rear-corner**
- **NCAP Euro NCAP AEB test target relative speed: up to 80 km/h**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Layered Architecture

A CWAS is built on a strict **Sense → Think → Act** pipeline. Every KTU 14-mark question on this topic must explain at least these six operational stages:

1. **Sensor Data Acquisition (Sense)**
   - Front-facing long-range radar (76 GHz) measures range, range-rate (Doppler), and azimuth angle.
   - Mono/Stereo camera performs object classification (car vs. pedestrian vs. truck).
   - Lidar (when present) builds a dense 3-D point cloud.
   - Vehicle dynamics bus (CAN) feeds ego-speed, yaw rate, steering angle, brake pressure.

2. **Object Tracking & Fusion (Think – Layer 1)**
   - Kalman Filter / Extended Kalman Filter maintains a state vector $(x, y, v_x, v_y, a_x, a_y)$ for every tracked target.
   - Data association (Nearest Neighbour / JPDA) eliminates ghost objects.

3. **Threat Assessment – Time-to-Collision (TTC) Computation (Think – Layer 2)**
   - For each tracked target in the host vehicle's driving corridor, the ECU computes the **Time-to-Collision**.
   - If TTC falls below thresholds, the system progresses to the next stage.

4. **Warning Decision (Act – Stage 1: Passive)**
   - **Stage 1 — Visual warning** (dashboard icon, HUD): TTC < **2.6 s** (typical).
   - **Stage 2 — Audible "beep" + haptic seat vibration**: TTC < **1.6 s**.
   - **Stage 3 — Pre-charge brakes, retract seatbelts**: TTC < **0.8 s**.

5. **Active Intervention Decision (Act – Stage 2: Active)**
   - If driver has not braked despite warnings AND TTC < **0.6 s**, system commands **full autonomous emergency braking** at maximum deceleration.
   - Some systems also apply **Evasive Steering Assist (ESA)** if a clear adjacent lane exists.

6. **Post-event Deceleration (Act – Stage 3)**
   - After coming to a halt, the system holds the brakes for 2 s to prevent re-collision from a rear vehicle.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Design Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{\text{rel}}}$ | $\Delta d$ = gap (m); $v_{\text{rel}}$ = relative speed (m/s) | Valid only if $v_{\text{rel}} > 0$ |
| 2 | **Relative speed** | $v_{\text{rel}} = v_{\text{ego}} - v_{\text{lead}}$ | m/s | Negative ⇒ no closing |
| 3 | **Braking distance (kinematic)** | $d_{\text{brk}} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \, a_{\text{max}}}$ | $v$ in m/s; $a$ in m/s² | Ignores reaction time |
| 4 | **Total stopping distance** | $d_{\text{stop}} = v_{\text{ego}} \, t_{\text{r}} + \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2}}{2 \, a_{\text{max}}}$ | $t_{\text{r}}$ = reaction time (s) | $t_{\text{r}}$ design = **1.5 s** |
| 5 | **Minimum safe following gap** | $d_{\text{min}} = v_{\text{rel}} \, t_{\text{r}} + \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}^{2}}{2 \, a_{\text{max}}}$ | All SI | Comfort decel = **2.94 m/s²** |
| 6 | **Dynamic stopping with relative motion** | $d_{\text{min}} = v_{\text{rel}} \, t_{\text{r}} + \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2} - v_{\text{lead}}^{2}}{2 \, a_{\text{max}}}$ | m | Used when $a_{\text{lead}} \neq 0$ |
| 7 | **Radar Doppler range-rate** | $f_{\text{D}} = \dfrac{2 \, v_{\text{rel}}}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength (m); $f_D$ in Hz | 77 GHz ⇒ $\lambda \approx 3.9$ mm |
| 8 | **Maximum CWAS range** | $R_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f} \cdot N_{\text{FFT}}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | FMCW radar equation |
| 9 | **Kinetic energy to be dissipated** | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{\text{rel}}^{2}$ | Joules | Reduces with lower $v_{\text{rel}}$ |
| 10 | **Euro NCAP AEB severity reduction** | $\Delta v_{\text{impact}} = v_{\text{ego}} - a_{\text{max}} \, TTC$ | m/s | Aim: $\Delta v_{\text{impact}} < 5$ km/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vertical bar symbol** for absolute value has been replaced by `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Use $\vert x \vert$ in your own notes — never raw `|`.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CWAS technology, mandated in new vehicles across the EU (GSR 2019/2144) and India (AIS-184), has demonstrably reduced rear-end crashes by **38 – 45 %** according to Euro NCAP field studies. It is the gateway technology to **Level 3 Conditional Automation**, since the perception and decision stack is the same one later used by full autonomous-driving stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-to-Collision (TTC) under Constant Relative Velocity

**Statement:** Two vehicles travel along the same lane in the same direction. The ego vehicle has speed $v_e$ and the leading vehicle has speed $v_l$. The longitudinal gap is $\Delta d$. Derive the time remaining before impact if both vehicles continue at their current speeds.

**Step 1 — Define relative kinematic variables.**
The closing rate between the two vehicles is the difference of their velocities:

$$
v_{\text{rel}} = v_e - v_l
$$

**Step 2 — State the kinematic assumption.**
Assume the velocities are *constant* over the short prediction horizon (this is the standard CWAS assumption; curvature correction terms are added later for higher accuracy).

**Step 3 — Apply the fundamental kinematic relation.**
For constant velocity, distance closed per unit time equals the relative velocity. Therefore the time required to close the entire gap $\Delta d$ is:

$$
TTC = \dfrac{\text{Total gap to close}}{\text{Rate of closing}} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{\text{rel}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Check physical validity.**
TTC is only meaningful if $v_{\text{rel}} > 0$ (closing). If $v_{\text{rel}} \leq 0$, the vehicles are stationary or separating — TTC is set to infinity by the algorithm.

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**
A typical highway case: $v_e = 100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s, $v_l = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s, gap $\Delta d = 30$ m.

$$
v_{\text{rel}} = 27.78 - 22.22 = 5.56 \; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
TTC = \dfrac{30}{5.56} = 5.40 \; \text{s}
$$

Since 5.4 s > 2.6 s threshold, **no warning is issued**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Safe Following Gap for Collision Avoidance

**Statement:** Derive the longitudinal gap below which a rear-end collision is unavoidable, given the ego vehicle can decelerate at most $a_{\max}$ and the driver requires $t_r$ seconds to react.

**Step 1 — Decompose the stopping process into two phases.**
The ego vehicle will travel during (i) the driver's reaction phase, and (ii) the actual braking phase. Both must be summed.

**Step 2 — Distance travelled during reaction phase.**
During the reaction time $t_r$, the ego vehicle continues at constant speed $v_e$ (no braking yet):

$$
d_{\text{react}} = v_e \, t_r
$$

**Step 3 — Distance travelled during braking phase.**
Use the standard kinematic relation $v_f^{2} = v_i^{2} + 2 a s$ with final velocity $v_f = 0$ and deceleration $a = -a_{\max}$:

$$
0^{2} = v_e^{2} - 2 \, a_{\max} \, d_{\text{brk}}
$$

Solving for $d_{\text{brk}}$:

$$
d_{\text{brk}} = \dfrac{v_e^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total stopping distance.**

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = v_e \, t_r + \dfrac{v_e^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 5 — Adjust for a *moving* lead vehicle.**
If the lead vehicle is also moving forward with $v_l > 0$, the gap *itself* shrinks during reaction, and the ego must dissipate only its *relative* kinetic energy. Subtract $v_l \, t_{\text{total}}$ from the gap. The net **minimum safe gap** becomes:

$$
d_{\text{min}} = v_{\text{rel}} \, t_r + \dfrac{v_e^{2} - v_l^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

where $v_{\text{rel}} = v_e - v_l$.

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question).**
Take $v_e = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, $v_l = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s, $t_r = 1.5$ s, $a_{\max} = 7.84$ m/s² (0.8 g).

$$
v_{\text{rel}} = 25 - 16.67 = 8.33 \; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
d_{\text{react}} = 8.33 \times 1.5 = 12.50 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
d_{\text{brk}} = \dfrac{25^{2} - 16.67^{2}}{2 \times 7.84} = \dfrac{625 - 277.89}{15.68} = \dfrac{347.11}{15.68} = 22.14 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
d_{\text{min}} = 12.50 + 22.14 = 34.64 \; \text{m}
$$

**Interpretation:** If the radar reports a gap < 34.6 m, even a perfect driver with 0.8 g brakes cannot avoid contact — the CWAS must therefore issue the **Stage 3 pre-charge warning** well before this distance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TTC Threshold Required to Reduce Impact Speed to Zero (CWAS AEB Justification)

**Statement:** Show that the AEB sub-system must initiate braking *before* TTC reaches a critical value $TTC_{\text{crit}}$.

**Step 1 — During braking, ego speed decreases linearly with time** (constant $a_{\max}$):

$$
v_e(t) = v_{e,0} - a_{\max} \, t
$$

**Step 2 — Impact occurs when $v_e(t_{\text{imp}}) = v_l$** (relative speed vanishes just as contact occurs). Therefore:

$$
t_{\text{imp}} = \dfrac{v_{e,0} - v_l}{a_{\max}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}}{a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 3 — But by the time of impact the gap closed must equal the original gap $\Delta d_0$.** Using relative kinematics with constant deceleration:

$$
\Delta d_0 = v_{\text{rel}} \, t_{\text{imp}} - \dfrac{1}{2} a_{\max} \, t_{\text{imp}}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $t_{\text{imp}}$:**

$$
\Delta d_0 = v_{\text{rel}} \cdot \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}}{a_{\max}} - \dfrac{1}{2} a_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}^{2}}{a_{\max}^{2}}
$$

$$
\Delta d_0 = \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}^{2}}{a_{\max}} - \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the critical TTC.**

$$
TTC_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\Delta d_0}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical check.**
For $v_{\text{rel}} = 8.33$ m/s and $a_{\max} = 7.84$ m/s²:

$$
TTC_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{8.33}{2 \times 7.84} = 0.531 \; \text{s}
$$

This justifies the **industry-standard 0.6 s AEB intervention threshold**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a CWAS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RADAR 77 GHz] --> S1
    A2[CAMERA Front] --> S1
    A3[LIDAR Optional] --> S1
    A4[CAN Bus Vehicle Dynamics] --> S1
    S1[Sensor Data Fusion Unit] --> S2[Object Tracking Kalman Filter]
    S2 --> S3[Threat Assessment TTC Computation]
    S3 --> D1{TTC Threshold Check}
    D1 -- TTC less than 2.6 s --> W1[Visual HUD Warning]
    D1 -- TTC less than 1.6 s --> W2[Audible Beep plus Haptic Seat]
    D1 -- TTC less than 0.8 s --> W3[Brake Precharge plus Belt Pretensioner]
    D1 -- TTC less than 0.6 s and no driver input --> A5[Automatic Emergency Braking AEB]
    D1 -- Lane clear and TTC less than 0.5 s --> A6[Evasive Steering Assist ESA]
    A5 --> M1[ESP Brake Actuator]
    A6 --> M2[EPS Steering Actuator]
    W1 --> HMI[Driver HMI Cluster]
    W2 --> HMI
    W3 --> HMI
```

### 4.2 Decision-Flow State Machine for CWAS

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Monitoring
    Monitoring --> NoThreat: TTC greater than 2.6 s
    NoThreat --> Monitoring: Continuous scan
    Monitoring --> Caution: TTC between 1.6 and 2.6 s
    Caution --> Warning: TTC between 0.8 and 1.6 s
    Warning --> PreBrake: TTC between 0.6 and 0.8 s
    PreBrake --> AEBactive: TTC less than 0.6 s and no driver brake input
    PreBrake --> DriverOverride: Driver applies brake
    AEBactive --> CollisionAvoided: Gap stabilises or lead speeds up
    AEBactive --> Impact: AEB insufficient
    Impact --> PostCrash: Airbag and ESP hold
    CollisionAvoided --> Monitoring
    DriverOverride --> Monitoring
    PostCrash --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Sensor-Fusion Coverage Map (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERCEPTION[PERCEPTION LAYER]
        R1[LRR 76 GHz Long Range Radar 250 m]
        R2[MRR Mid Range Radar 60 m]
        C1[Front Camera 50 m Classify]
        L1[Lidar 150 m Optional]
        U1[Ultrasonic Rear Corner 5 m]
    end
    subgraph FUSION[FUSION LAYER]
        FU[Sensor Fusion ECU with EKF Tracker]
    end
    subgraph DECISION[DECISION LAYER]
        TA[Threat Assessment]
```
