```mermaid
        WM[Warning Manager]
        IM[Intervention Manager]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION[ACTUATION LAYER]
        BR[Hydraulic Brake Booster]
        ST[Electric Power Steering]
        HB[Haptic Seat and HUD]
    end
    PERCEPTION --> FU --> TA --> WM
    TA --> IM --> ACTUATION
    WM --> HB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Set B]**
**"Differentiate between Forward Collision Warning (FCW) and Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FCW | AEB |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Passive, advisory | Active, interventional |
| Action | Issues visual / audible / haptic alert | Applies brakes automatically |
| Driver authority | Driver retains full control | Driver authority is overridden if no response |
| Subsystem of CWAS | First stage | Last stage |
| Typical TTC trigger | 2.6 s | 0.6 s |

(3 marks — one mark each for nature, action and trigger logic.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
**"List any three sensors used in a Collision Warning and Avoidance System and state one limitation of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Long-Range Radar (76 – 77 GHz)** — Limitation: cannot classify objects (a metal tin and a pedestrian appear identical).
2. **Mono Camera** — Limitation: performance degrades in fog, heavy rain, or low-contrast night scenes.
3. **Lidar** — Limitation: high cost and degraded performance in heavy snowfall due to scattering.

(1 mark per sensor-limitation pair.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-4 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **functional architecture** of a Collision Warning and Avoidance System. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle travelling at **90 km/h** approaches a stationary obstacle. The driver perception-reaction time is **1.5 s**, and the maximum achievable deceleration is **0.7 g**.
    (i) Compute the **total stopping distance**.
    (ii) Compute the **critical TTC** at which AEB must trigger to avoid impact.
    (iii) If the radar reports a gap of **45 m**, will AEB prevent the collision? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Block diagram — refer to SECTION 4 Diagram 4.1 above. Roles:*
- **Sensor Layer** (Radar, Camera, Lidar) — captures raw longitudinal/lateral kinematics of targets. *(1 mark)*
- **Sensor Fusion Unit** — combines data into a unified object list using EKF. *(1 mark)*
- **Object Tracker** — assigns persistent IDs and predicts future state. *(1 mark)*
- **Threat Assessment (TTC)** — classifies each target into *safe / caution / warning / brake* zones. *(2 marks)*
- **Actuation Layer** (ESP brakes, EPS steering, HUD/HMI) — executes physical warnings or interventions. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Total stopping distance:**
Convert speed: $v_e = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s. Take $a_{\max} = 0.7 \, g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.87$ m/s². Apply:

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = v_e \, t_r + \dfrac{v_e^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}} = 25 \times 1.5 + \dfrac{25^{2}}{2 \times 6.87}
$$

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = 37.5 + \dfrac{625}{13.74} = 37.5 + 45.49 = 82.99 \; \text{m} \approx 83 \; \text{m}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Critical TTC:**
For a stationary obstacle, $v_{\text{rel}} = v_e = 25$ m/s. From Derivation 3:

$$
TTC_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}}{2 \, a_{\max}} = \dfrac{25}{2 \times 6.87} = 1.82 \; \text{s}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Evaluation at 45 m gap:**
Using $TTC_{\text{crit}} = 1.82$ s, the *minimum* gap that allows safe braking is:

$$
\Delta d_{\text{min}} = v_{\text{rel}} \times TTC_{\text{crit}} = 25 \times 1.82 = 45.5 \; \text{m}
$$

The reported gap of **45 m < 45.5 m**, so the AEB system *just barely fails* to bring the impact speed to zero. However, it will still reduce impact severity. AEB must therefore issue a Stage-3 warning (TTC < 0.8 s) earlier than the nominal threshold to give the driver a chance. *(3 marks)*

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** under constant relative velocity. State clearly the assumptions and the physical interpretation when the relative velocity becomes negative. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **four warning/intervention stages** of a typical CWAS, stating the TTC range, the type of human-machine-interface (HMI) cue, and the corresponding actuator action for each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of TTC:**

*Assumption:* Both the ego and the lead vehicle move at constant speeds over the prediction horizon. *(1 mark)*

Define $v_{\text{rel}} = v_e - v_l$ and gap $\Delta d$. *(1 mark)*

Rate of gap closure $= v_{\text{rel}}$ m/s. *(1 mark)*

Time to fully close the gap:

$$
TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{\text{rel}}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Physical interpretation when $v_{\text{rel}} < 0$:** The leading vehicle is moving faster than the ego vehicle — the gap is *opening*, so no collision is possible in the foreseeable future. The CWAS algorithm therefore assigns $TTC = +\infty$ and stays in the *No Threat* state. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Four stages of CWAS escalation:**

| Stage | TTC Range (s) | HMI Cue | Actuator Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Pre-Caution | 2.6 – 4.0 | Yellow dashboard icon, optional HUD mark | None |
| 2 — Caution | 1.6 – 2.6 | Red flashing icon + low-volume chime | None |
| 3 — Imminent Warning | 0.8 – 1.6 | Loud beep + haptic seat vibration + belt pretensioner | Brake pre-charge (hydraulic priming), windows close |
| 4 — Emergency Intervention | < 0.8 (AEB at < 0.6) | Continuous chime + hazard lights | Full autonomous emergency braking at $a_{\max}$; Evasive Steering Assist if lane clear |

*(1 mark for correct table header, 4 marks for correct entries — 1 mark each, 2 marks for the descriptive prose and engineering rationale such as brake pre-charge priming time of 150 ms.)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in CWAS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing *relative* speed with *ego* speed** when computing braking distance — you must use $v_{\text{rel}}$ for the reaction component, but $v_e^{2} - v_l^{2}$ for the kinetic energy component. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s** (divide by 3.6). Examiners specifically look for this unit-conversion step.
> 3. **Using $g = 10$ m/s² instead of 9.81 m/s²** — KTU prefers $g = 9.81$, but 10 is acceptable in numerical work only if stated.
> 4. **Writing "radar and lidar are the same"** — they are NOT. Radar uses **Doppler-modulated FMCW** at 77 GHz, lidar uses **time-of-flight** of laser pulses (~905 nm or 1550 nm).
> 5. **Omitting the constant-velocity assumption** when deriving TTC — this is the first thing a moderator looks for.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CWAS = FCW (advisory) + AEB (active)** — always state both halves.
- **TTC = $\Delta d / v_{\text{rel}}$** — only valid for $v_{\text{rel}} > 0$.
- **Braking distance equation** = $\dfrac{v^2}{2 a}$ — derived from $v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 a s$.
- **Total stopping distance** = $v \, t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 a}$.
- **Standard reaction time** in KTU problems = **1.5 s**; emergency decel = **0.7 – 0.8 g**.
- **Critical TTC for AEB** = $\dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}}{2 a_{\max}}$ — must be ≥ 0.6 s for system to be physically feasible.
- **Sensor fusion** is mandatory — single sensor is unreliable (radar cannot classify, camera cannot estimate speed).
- **77 GHz radar** is the workhorse; **Lidar** is optional premium.
- **CAN bus** feeds ego-speed, yaw-rate, brake-pressure, steering-angle to the CWAS ECU.
- **Stages:** Visual → Audio+Haptic → Pre-charge → AEB.
- **Euro NCAP** and **AIS-184** (India) mandate AEB for new vehicles.
- **AEB reduces rear-end crashes by 38 – 45 %** (cite in long answers).
- **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless stated otherwise; always convert km/h to m/s.
- **Two common equations to memorize cold:**
  - $d_{\text{stop}} = v_e t_r + \dfrac{v_e^{2}}{2 a_{\max}}$
  - $TTC_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{rel}}}{2 a_{\max}}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Tire Pressure Warning System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT831 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low Tire Pressure Warning System

# 🛞 Low Tire Pressure Warning System (TPMS) — Complete KTU Module 4 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Low Tire Pressure Warning System (TPWS)**, universally standardized as the **Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)**, is an electronic safety subsystem integrated into the modern automotive chassis that continuously measures and evaluates the pneumatic pressure (and in advanced variants, the temperature) inside each pneumatic tire against a manufacturer-calibrated reference threshold. When the detected pressure in any single tire falls below the legally mandated or OEM-defined warning threshold, the system actuates a driver-facing **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** — colloquially known as the TPMS warning light — typically within a guaranteed response window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):** Per **FMVSS 138** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard No. 138, USA) and **ECE R64** (Economic Commission for Europe Regulation 64), TPMS must illuminate a tell-tale warning when tire pressure drops to **≤ 25 %** below the vehicle's recommended **Placard Cold Inflation Pressure**, or below a minimum absolute threshold of **69 kPa (10 psi)**, whichever yields the *higher* (less dangerous) of the two pressures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine four balloons tied to your bicycle's seat-post, each connected by a thin transparent tube to a small digital scale at your handlebar. Every time you pump air into or deflate one balloon, you immediately *see* the change on the scale — and a small red LED glows if any balloon falls below a pre-marked safety line.

That is precisely how a TPMS works:

| Real-World Object | Automotive TPMS Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Four balloons | Four pneumatic tires |
| Transparent tubes | Wireless RF link (315 MHz / 433.92 MHz) |
| Digital scale on handlebar | Dashboard tell-tale lamp + ECU |
| Pre-marked safety line | OEM calibration threshold (~75 % of placard pressure) |

A tire is the **only** point of contact between a multi-tonne vehicle and the road — its pressure directly governs grip, braking distance, fuel economy, and tire lifespan. A drop of even **30 kPa (~4.3 psi)** can extend braking distance on wet roads by nearly **8 %** and increase rolling resistance, raising fuel consumption by approximately **3–5 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold Inflation Pressure (CIP):** Tire pressure must ALWAYS be measured and calibrated *cold* — meaning the vehicle has been parked for at least **3 hours** or driven **less than 1.6 km (1 mile)**. Driving heats tires by **10–15 °C**, raising pressure by **~10 kPa (~1.5 psi)** purely due to the Ideal Gas Law, falsely indicating nominal pressure.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standardized Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Standard Placard Pressure (typical passenger car)** | **220 kPa (32 psi)** |
| **Warning Threshold (FMVSS 138 minimum)** | **165 kPa (24 psi)** — i.e., 75 % of 220 kPa |
| **Absolute Lower Limit (FMVSS 138)** | **≥ 69 kPa (10 psi)** |
| **USA TPMS RF Frequency** | **315 MHz** |
| **Europe / Asia TPMS RF Frequency** | **433.92 MHz** |
| **Japan TPMS RF Frequency** | **315 MHz** |
| **Operating Temperature Range** | **−40 °C to +125 °C** |
| **Sensor Battery Life (Direct TPMS)** | **7–10 years** (typical) |
| **Warning Lamp Response Time** | **≤ 20 minutes** of cumulative driving under-inflated |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of TPMS Architectures

The system fundamentally bifurcates into two distinct engineering philosophies:

#### (A) Direct TPMS (dTPMS) — "Active Sensor in Each Tire"

- A **dedicated pressure transducer + microcontroller + RF transmitter** is physically mounted inside each tire (typically clamped to the valve stem or strapped to the wheel rim).
- Each wheel has its **own battery-powered sensor module**.
- Pressure data is telemetered wirelessly to a central receiver module, which forwards it to the BCM (Body Control Module) / instrument cluster.

#### (B) Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) — "Software Inference via ABS Sensors"

- **No pressure sensor** is installed inside the tire.
- The system exploits the physical principle that an **under-inflated tire has a smaller rolling radius**, hence a **higher angular velocity** for a given linear vehicle speed.
- The **existing ABS / ESC wheel-speed sensors** (variable reluctance or Hall-effect) provide wheel angular velocity data to the ECU.
- Sophisticated signal-processing algorithms compare the four wheel speeds to detect abnormal rotational asymmetry indicative of pressure loss.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow — Bullet Walkthrough

1. **Sensor Acquisition (dTPMS):** MEMS piezoelectric or piezoresistive pressure-sensing element produces an analog voltage proportional to absolute tire pressure.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) amplifies, digitizes (via internal 12-bit ADC), and temperature-compensates the raw pressure signal.
3. **Encoding & Modulation:** Sensor ID (unique 32-bit ID), pressure value, temperature, and battery status are encoded into a Manchester-coded / FSK-modulated RF frame.
4. **Wireless Transmission:** Burst transmission at **315 MHz or 433.92 MHz**, typically every **60 seconds while driving** and every **~13 hours while parked** (motion-triggered by accelerometer).
5. **Reception:** LF (Low Frequency, **125 kHz**) trigger signal from the vehicle wakes individual sensors during initialization / re-sync. RF receiver (external to tire) decodes the data.
6. **ECU Evaluation:** BCM cross-references received pressure against the calibrated threshold. If $P_{tire} < P_{threshold}$, MIL is activated.
7. **Driver Notification:** Tell-tale lamp on dashboard (amber symbol: cross-section of tire with exclamation mark) glows steadily (low pressure) or flashes (system malfunction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Direct TPMS Dominates Modern OEMs:** Indirect TPMS cannot detect a **simultaneous equal pressure drop** in all four tires (because relative wheel speeds remain identical). It also requires periodic **re-calibration** after tire rotation or replacement, making it inferior to dTPMS from a robustness perspective.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU High-Yield — guaranteed to appear in derivations or numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1 V_1 / T_1 = P_2 V_2 / T_2$ | Combined Gas Law for tire pressure vs. temperature (V ≈ constant) | P in Pa, V in m³, T in K |
| 2 | $P_{hot} = P_{cold} \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{cold}}\right]$ | Pressure rise due to driving-induced heating | Pa, K |
| 3 | $\omega = v / r_{eff}$ | Wheel angular velocity relation | rad/s, m/s, m |
| 4 | $\Delta f_{wheel} = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta r}{r_0}$ | Indirect TPMS frequency-shift due to deflation | Hz |
| 5 | $C = 2\pi r$ | Tire rolling circumference | m |
| 6 | $f_{carrier} = 1/T = c / \lambda$ | RF carrier frequency / wavelength link | Hz, m |
| 7 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength at carrier (e.g., $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | m |
| 8 | $P_{r} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | **Friis Free-Space Path Loss Equation** for RF link budget | W, dimensionless, m |
| 9 | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ | FSPL in decibels | dB |
| 10 | $E_{battery} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Sensor battery energy budget | J, V, A, s |
| 11 | $\Delta P_{threshold} = 0.25 \times P_{placard}$ | FMVSS 138 mandatory warning margin | Pa |
| 12 | $\eta_{fuel} \approx 0.03 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{placard}}$ | Empirical fuel-economy penalty per pressure drop | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The Low Tire Pressure Warning System is **not merely a convenience feature** — it is a **legally mandated safety system** in over **120 countries** including India (under AIS-052 / Bharat Stage norms adoption), USA, EU, Japan, China, and South Korea. Statistical data from NHTSA (USA) indicates that TPMS has:

- Reduced fatal tire-related crashes by an estimated **~50 %**.
- Saved an estimated **~200 lives annually** in the USA alone.
- Prevented **~8,000 injuries per year**.

For KTU examination purposes, always frame your answer around the **three pillars**: **Safety**, **Emissions / Fuel Economy**, and **Tire Longevity**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tire Pressure vs. Temperature (Ideal Gas Law Application)

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A tire is inflated to a cold pressure of $P_1 = 200$ kPa at ambient temperature $T_1 = 300$ K. After continuous highway driving, the tire's internal temperature rises to $T_2 = 330$ K. Assuming tire volume remains constant (rigid tire casing), determine the new hot inflation pressure.

**Step 1 — State the Governing Law**

Because the tire volume is approximately constant (tire casing is essentially inextensible at operating pressures), the **Gay-Lussac's Law** form of the Combined Gas Law applies:

$$
\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic Rearrangement**

Isolating the unknown final pressure $P_2$:

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
P_2 = 200 \text{ kPa} \times \frac{330 \text{ K}}{300 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
P_2 = 200 \text{ kPa} \times 1.10
$$

$$
P_2 = 220 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

The tire pressure increased by **20 kPa (~2.9 psi)** purely due to a **30 °C temperature rise**, despite no additional air being added. This is why **pressure must be checked COLD** — a hot tire will give a *falsely normal reading* even when genuinely under-inflated.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_2 = 220 \text{ kPa}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — FMVSS 138 Warning Threshold Calculation

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A passenger car's Tire Information Placard specifies a front-tire cold inflation pressure of $P_{placard} = 240$ kPa and a rear-tire pressure of $P_{rear} = 220$ kPa. Determine the legally mandated TPMS warning threshold for each axle under FMVSS 138.

**Step 1 — Apply the 25 % Rule**

$$
P_{threshold, front} = P_{placard,front} \times (1 - 0.25)
$$

$$
P_{threshold, front} = 240 \text{ kPa} \times 0.75 = 180 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Same Rule to the Rear**

$$
P_{threshold, rear} = P_{placard,rear} \times (1 - 0.25)
$$

$$
P_{threshold, rear} = 220 \text{ kPa} \times 0.75 = 165 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Against Absolute Minimum (69 kPa Floor)**

Since both thresholds (180 kPa and 165 kPa) exceed **69 kPa**, the 25 % rule governs.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_{threshold,front} = 180 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_{threshold,rear} = 165 \text{ kPa}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Indirect TPMS: Wheel-Speed Anomaly from Pressure Loss

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A vehicle travels at a linear speed of $v = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s. The properly inflated tire has an effective rolling radius $r_0 = 0.32$ m. Due to pressure loss, the tire's effective rolling radius decreases by $\Delta r = 0.004$ m. Calculate the percentage increase in the under-inflated wheel's angular velocity, which the ECU must detect.

**Step 1 — Write the Wheel Speed Equation**

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Initial and Final Angular Velocities**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{v}{r_0} = \frac{27.78}{0.32} = 86.81 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{new} = \frac{v}{r_0 - \Delta r} = \frac{27.78}{0.32 - 0.004} = \frac{27.78}{0.316} = 87.91 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Percentage Change**

$$
\Delta\omega\% = \frac{\omega_{new} - \omega_0}{\omega_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\omega\% = \frac{87.91 - 86.81}{86.81} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\omega\% = \frac{1.10}{86.81} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\omega\% \approx 1.27 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

A mere **1.27 %** rise in angular velocity corresponds to a pressure loss of roughly **~30–40 kPa**. Modern ABS sensors resolve wheel speed to better than **±0.1 %**, so detection is comfortably feasible — but only with **calibrated, temperature-compensated algorithms** and only when **vehicle speed is above ~25 km/h** (below this, slip, cornering, and torque effects corrupt the signal).

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta\omega \approx +1.27 \%}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss for TPMS RF Link

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A TPMS sensor transmits at $f = 433.92$ MHz with $P_t = 5$ mW, using isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$). The receiver is $d = 1.5$ m away (worst case: rear-inner tire). Compute the received power.

**Step 1 — Compute Wavelength**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{433.92 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \approx 0.691 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Friis Equation**

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

$$
P_r = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \times 1 \times 1 \times \left(\frac{0.691}{4\pi \times 1.5}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Inner Term**

$$
\frac{0.691}{4\pi \times 1.5} = \frac{0.691}{18.85} \approx 0.03666
$$

**Step 4 — Square and Multiply**

$$
P_r = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.03666)^2
$$

$$
P_r = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.344 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_r \approx 6.72 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 6.72 \text{ μW}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_r \approx 6.72 \text{ μW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This received power is **comfortably above** the typical TPMS receiver sensitivity of **−95 dBm (~3.16 × 10⁻¹³ W)**, confirming the link budget has ~50 dB of margin — essential because real-world path loss is dominated by **metal wheel housing attenuation**, not free space.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — dTPMS vs. iTPMS Comparison

| Parameter | Direct TPMS (dTPMS) | Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure Sensor in Tire | **Yes** (per wheel) | **No** |
| Real-time Pressure Display | **Yes** | **No** |
| Detects All-Equal Pressure Loss | **Yes** | **No** |
| Wheel Speed Sensors Used | Not required | **Yes (ABS sensors)** |
| Battery Inside Sensor | **Yes** (non-replaceable) | N/A |
| Cost per Vehicle | **Higher** (~₹3,000–₹8,000) | **Lower** (software-only) |
| Re-calibration After Tire Swap | Not required | **Required** |
| Accuracy | **± 5 kPa** | **± 15–25 kPa** |
| Reliability in Severe Deflation | **Excellent** | Moderate (depends on speed) |
| OEM Adoption (Post-2014) | **Dominant** | Legacy / budget vehicles |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Direct TPMS (dTPMS) End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tire Wheel Cavity] --> B[MEMS Pressure Sensor]
    A --> C[NTC Temperature Sensor]
    A --> D[3 Axis Accelerometer]
    B --> E[ASIC Microcontroller with 12 bit ADC]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Manchester Encoder and FSK Modulator]
    F --> G[RF Transmitter 433.92 MHz or 315 MHz]
    G --> H[Wireless RF Link to Vehicle Body]
    H --> I[External RF Receiver Module]
    I --> J[BCM Body Control Module]
    J --> K{Threshold Comparison Logic}
    K -->|Pressure Below Threshold| L[Activate TPMS Warning Lamp]
    K -->|Pressure Normal| M[System Idle]
    J --> N[Instrument Cluster Display showing individual tire pressures]
    O[LF Trigger Antenna 125 kHz] --> P[Wake up and Address Specific Sensor]
    P --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Wheel Speed Sensor FL] --> ECU[ABS or ESC ECU]
    W2[Wheel Speed Sensor FR] --> ECU
    W3[Wheel Speed Sensor RL] --> ECU
    W4[Wheel Speed Sensor RR] --> ECU
    ECU --> SP[Signal Processing and FFT Analysis]
    SP --> CMP{Differential Wheel Speed Comparator}
    CMP -->|Asymmetry Detected| THR[Apply Threshold Filter]
    CMP -->|Symmetric Reduction| NR[No Warning All Tires Equally Under Inflated]
    THR --> DSP[Determine which tire is low]
    DSP --> WL[Illuminate TPMS Warning Lamp]
    NR --> RST[Request Manual Recalibration]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Pressure Decision Logic in BCM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sensor Data Frame Received] --> V{Validate CRC Checksum}
    V -->|Invalid| DROP[Discard Frame]
    V -->|Valid| DEC[Decode Pressure Value P_tire]
    DEC --> T[Decode Temperature T_tire]
    T --> C[Apply Cold Pressure Compensation Algorithm]
    C --> Q{P_tire Below P_threshold}
    Q -->|Yes| LAMP[Activate Steady Warning Lamp]
    Q -->|No| IDLE[Continue Monitoring]
    DEC --> BAT[Decode Battery Voltage Level]
    BAT --> BL{Battery Below 2.0 Volt}
    BL -->|Yes| FLT[Activate Flashing Fault Lamp]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Low Tire Pressure Warning System. List the two principal types of TPMS and state one merit and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

A **Low Tire Pressure Warning System (TPMS)** is an automotive safety subsystem that continuously monitors the pneumatic pressure of all four (or five, including spare) tires and alerts the driver via a dashboard tell-tale lamp when pressure drops below a calibrated threshold, typically **25 % below placard pressure** as mandated by **FMVSS 138**.

The two principal types are:

| Type | Merit | Demerit |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct TPMS (dTPMS)** | Provides **real-time absolute pressure** for each tire individually; detects simultaneous equal deflation. | **Battery-limited** sensor life (7–10 years); higher cost. |
| **Indirect TPMS (iTPMS)** | **Lower cost**; no in-tire electronics; uses existing ABS sensors. | Cannot detect **all-equal pressure loss**; requires **re-calibration** after tire rotation. |

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Why must tire pressure be measured only when the tire is "cold"? Justify with reference to the relevant physical law.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

Tire pressure must be measured cold because driving raises the internal tire temperature by **10–15 °C** through hysteresis and flexural heating, which **falsely elevates** the gauge reading.

Per **Gay-Lussac's Law** (special case of the **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$, with $V$ ≈ constant):

$$
\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}
$$

For a rise of $\Delta T = 30$ K, the pressure increases by **~10 %**, potentially masking a genuine under-inflation defect. Therefore, **OEMs mandate** that pressure be checked after the vehicle has been parked for **≥ 3 hours** or driven **≤ 1.6 km (1 mile)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 1. **Always** write the governing physical law *before* substituting numbers. Skipping this loses ~2 marks.
> 2. **Never** omit units. KTU examiners strictly deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit symbol in numericals.
> 3. **Diagrams are compulsory** for any 7+ mark question. Hand-drawn neat block diagrams (not just text) earn full credit.
> 4. Avoid generic statements like "TPMS is used for safety" — quantify with FMVSS 138 statistics or numerical margins.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Direct Tire Pressure Monitoring System**. List any **four advantages** of dTPMS over iTPMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's placard pressure is **220 kPa**. Determine: **(i)** the FMVSS 138 warning threshold pressure; **(ii)** the absolute minimum threshold (whichever is higher governs); **(iii)** the percentage increase in tire pressure if temperature rises from **300 K** to **325 K**, assuming the tire was just at threshold pressure when cold. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**

A Direct TPMS sensor module consists of:

1. **MEMS Piezoresistive / Capacitive Pressure Sensor** — detects absolute gas pressure.
2. **NTC Thermistor** — measures internal tire temperature.
3. **3-Axis MEMS Accelerometer** — detects motion (saves battery when parked).
4. **8-bit / 32-bit ASIC Microcontroller** — orchestrates sampling, ADC conversion (12-bit), and frame encoding.
5. **LF Receiver (125 kHz)** — receives wake-up command from vehicle during re-synchronization.
6. **UHF RF Transmitter** — broadcasts at **315 MHz or 433.92 MHz**.
7. **Lithium Primary Battery** (typically **3.6 V, ~550 mAh**) — non-replaceable, potted.
8. **Potted Polymer Housing** — ruggedized against vibration, moisture, and temperature.

**Working Principle:**

- When the wheel rotates, centrifugal force powers the sensor (in some designs) or the accelerometer wakes the ASIC.
- Pressure and temperature are sampled approximately **every 3–5 seconds** while moving; data is RF-transmitted every **~60 seconds**.
- The BCM compares received pressure against the **stored threshold** for each wheel position.
- Mismatch triggers the TPMS lamp.

**Four Advantages of dTPMS over iTPMS:**

1. Provides **absolute, real-time pressure** of every individual tire.
2. Detects **all-equal pressure loss** (iTPMS cannot).
3. **No re-calibration** required after tire rotation.
4. Higher **measurement accuracy** (~±5 kPa vs. ±20 kPa).

**Neat Block Diagram:**

```
[Tire Cavity Pressure] → [Pressure Sensor] → [ASIC Microcontroller]
        ↓                                              ↑
[Temperature]  → [NTC Sensor] ————————→ [Manchester Encoder] 
        ↓                                              ↓
[Wheel Motion] → [Accelerometer] —————→ [FSK Modulator 433.92 MHz]
                                                        ↓
                                              [RF Transmitter]
                                                        ↓
                                              [Wireless Link]
                                                        ↓
                                              [RF Receiver] → [BCM]
                                                        ↓
                                            [Threshold Comparator]
                                                        ↓
                                              [TPMS Warning Lamp]
```

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) FMVSS 138 Warning Threshold:**

$$
P_{threshold} = P_{placard} \times 0.75 = 220 \times 0.75 = 165 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(ii) Absolute Minimum Comparison:**

Lower of (165 kPa, FMVSS floor 69 kPa) = 165 kPa (since 165 > 69). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{P_{governing} = 165 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**(iii) Pressure Rise Due to Heating (using $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$):**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 165 \times \frac{325}{300} = 165 \times 1.0833
$$

$$
P_2 = 178.75 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Percentage Increase:**

$$
\Delta P\% = \frac{178.75 - 165}{165} \times 100 = \frac{13.75}{165} \times 100 \approx 8.33 \text{ \%}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P \approx +8.33 \text{ \%}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Indirect Tire Pressure Monitoring System** using ABS wheel-speed sensors. Derive the relationship between wheel angular velocity and effective tire radius, and comment on its **limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TPMS sensor transmits at **433.92 MHz** with **5 mW** power using isotropic antennas. The receiver sensitivity is **−95 dBm** and is located **2 m** away. Using Friis equation, calculate: **(i)** the free-space path loss in dB; **(ii)** the received power in dBm; **(iii)** the link margin (in dB). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Principle of Indirect TPMS:**

An under-inflated tire has a smaller contact patch and reduced effective rolling radius. To maintain the same linear vehicle speed $v$, the wheel must rotate **faster**.

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{r_{eff}}
$$

If pressure drops, $r_{eff} \downarrow$, hence $\omega \uparrow$. The ABS ECU monitors all four wheel-speed sensors (Hall-effect or variable-reluctance types) and compares them. Asymmetric $\omega$ values indicate deflation of the faster-spinning wheel.

**Derivation:**

Let initial state: $v = \omega_0 r_0$. After deflation: $v = \omega_1 r_1$, with $r_1 = r_0 - \Delta r$.

Since $v$ is constant:

$$
\omega_1 r_1 = \omega_0 r_0
$$

$$
\omega_1 = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{r_0}{r_1} = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{r_0}{r_0 - \Delta r}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_0} = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_0}{\omega_0} = \frac{\Delta r}{r_0 - \Delta r}
$$

For small $\Delta r$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_0} \approx \frac{\Delta r}{r_0}}
$$

**Limitations:**

1. **Cannot detect all-equal deflation** — all wheels spin faster equally; no asymmetry.
2. **Speed-dependent** — fails below ~25 km/h (signal-to-noise too low).
3. **Cornering & braking** corrupt readings; requires complex compensation algorithms.
4. **Re-calibration** mandatory after tire rotation, replacement, or pressure adjustment.
5. **No absolute pressure value** — only relative inference.
6. **Spare tire not monitored.**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $f = 433.92 \text{ MHz} = 433.92 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $P_t = 5 \text{ mW} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}$
- $d = 2 \text{ m}$
- Receiver sensitivity = $-95 \text{ dBm}$
- $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic)

**(i) Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) in dB:**

$$
FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

Computing each term:

$$
20\log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(433.92 \times 10^6) = 20 \times \log_{10}(4.3392 \times 10^8)
$$

$$
= 20 \times (8 + \log_{10}(4.3392)) = 20 \times (8 + 0.6375) = 20 \times 8.6375 = 172.75 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right) = 20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8}) = 20 \times (-7.378) = -147.56 \text{ dB}
$$

Summing:

$$
FSPL_{dB} = 6.02 + 172.75 - 147.56
$$

$$
\boxed{FSPL_{dB} \approx 31.21 \text{ dB}}
$$

**(ii) Received Power in dBm:**

Transmitted power in dBm:

$$
P_t (dBm) = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(5) = 6.99 \text{ dBm}
$$

Received power:

$$
P_r (dBm) = P_t (dBm) - FSPL_{dB} + G_t (dB) + G_r (dB)
$$

$$
P_r (dBm) = 6.99 - 31.21 + 0 + 0
$$

$$
\boxed{P_r \approx -24.22 \text{ dBm}}
$$

**(iii) Link Margin:**

$$
\text{Margin} = P_r (dBm) - P_{sensitivity} (dBm)
$$

$$
\text{Margin} = -24.22 - (-95) = -24.22 + 95
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Link Margin} \approx 70.78 \text{ dB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **enormous** link margin (>70 dB) compensates for real-world attenuations from **metal wheel housings** (~10–15 dB), **multi-path fading** (~20 dB), and **vehicle body obstruction** (~15–25 dB), ensuring robust communication even in worst-case conditions.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **TPMS = Tire Pressure Monitoring System**, legally mandated under **FMVSS 138** (USA) and **ECE R64** (Europe).
- ✅ Two architectures: **Direct (dTPMS)** uses in-tire pressure sensors; **Indirect (iTPMS)** uses ABS wheel-speed sensors + software.
- ✅ **Direct TPMS** is **superior** in all functional respects except cost and battery life.
- ✅ Mandatory warning threshold: **25 % below placard pressure**, with a **floor of 69 kPa** (whichever is higher).
- ✅ Operating RF frequencies: **315 MHz (USA/Japan)** and **433.92 MHz (Europe/Asia)**; LF wake-up at **125 kHz**.
- ✅ Tire pressure MUST be measured **cold** (parked ≥ 3 hours, driven ≤ 1.6 km) due to **Gay-Lussac's Law** heating effects (~10 % pressure rise per 30 K).
- ✅ Indirect TPMS relies on the relationship $\Delta\omega / \omega_0 \approx \Delta r / r_0$ — a small radius drop produces a detectable angular velocity rise.
- ✅ **Direct TPMS cannot detect** zero pressure (catastrophic blowout) because the sensor loses power / RF link breaks; **Indirect TPMS CAN** detect it via wheel deceleration asymmetry.
- ✅ TPMS sensors use **non-replaceable Li-primary batteries** with **7–10 year life**; on expiry, the entire sensor module must be replaced.
- ✅ **Re-synchronization** of dTPMS to the BCM is triggered by an **LF (125 kHz) signal** that addresses each wheel's unique 32-bit ID.
- ✅ Real-world benefits: **~50 % reduction in tire-related fatalities**, **3–5 % fuel economy improvement**, and **~30 % tire lifespan extension**.
- ✅ Three justifications for TPMS: **Safety**, **Emissions / Fuel Economy**, **Tire Longevity** — always cite at least two in any descriptive answer.
- ✅ **Free-Space Path Loss (Friis)** and **Ideal Gas Law** are the two mathematical pillars guaranteed to appear in KTU numerical problems on this topic.
- ✅ The warning lamp is **steady** for low pressure and **flashing** for system malfunction — know the distinction.
- ✅ Indirect TPMS requires **manual re-calibration** via dashboard procedure after any tire change or rotation — a key exam point.
